A solid entry into the Sniper Elite franchise, Sniper Elite 3 takes the mechanics from Sniper Elite V2 and uses them in better ways. You play the role of a sniper working with the British military in North Africa during the Second World War. Really, you're equal parts sniper and spy as your missions have you single-handedly infiltrating enemy camps and bases as you try to find an infamous German general and piece together a plot involving Nazi superweapons.  Unlike Sniper Elite V2, the maps in SE3 are much more open and this benefits the game immensely. Where, in V2, I often felt as though I spent half the game playing run 'n gun with my submachine gun, in SE3 I probably used my SMG less than five times. Your silenced pistol will still get a firm workout as you move into positions unseen where you can begin to death upon your foes with your rifle. This isn't a modern era setting shooter though with super-scopes and giant silencers to turn your shots into whispers. If you want to avoid a straight up firefight, you'll need to time your shots against background noise from low-flying aircraft or sabotaged generators to prevent the enemy from knowing where you're at. The game manages a good balance by forcing you to be patient as you line up your shots without making the whole ordeal tedious. There are some stealth mechanics as well including sight indicators, the ability to hide in shadows, guards locating you via sound, etc. Much of the game is spent creeping around and avoiding conflict until you're ready to bring swift vengeance from your perch. The famous Sniper Elite X-Ray Kill Cam makes its return, now updated to show your bullet tearing into muscle and severing arteries as well as bone and organs. Curiously, for all the effort into showing me in graphic detail the damage I'm inflicting, every hit in SE3 is a fatal blow. Shooting someone in the foot was as fatal as expertly putting a round through an enemy sniper's scope and into his brain [Edit: Higher levels introduce much more frequent 'incapacitating' or wounding shots versus clean kills]. For those feeling squeamish or just bored watching bullet cams, this feature can be easily adjusted or turned off.   I only played through the campaign on the default difficulty [Edit: I've since played through on more difficult settings] which used light ballistics modeling but my understanding is that ballistics on the higher levels remain well done, requiring players to account for bullet drop and wind direction and making rifle characteristics such as scope sway and bullet velocity more important. It is unfortunate that there are only four weapons in each class (pistol, rifle, SMG) included in the base game. A wealth of others are available via DLC which, while something of a cash grab, the game redeems itself slightly by including a firing range where you can test fire all the available guns and see which you like before purchasing a weapons pack.  The story is nothing special and you won't feel invested in the plot or making sure that you succeed. The end in particular felt very weak. However, from a mechanics point of view the game shines and the story is just a contrivance to line up 200m shots and put a bullet through someone's temple as artillery fires in the distance, hiding your presence. If you enjoyed V2 (or disliked it mainly for its enclosed maps) or if you find yourself playing the sniper in shooters, you'll probably enjoy your run through this title.

Wasteland 2 is both fun and challenging while having it's nice funny moments while you're paving your road with blood and corpses. You start with 4 cool blokes who you can modify yourself or just go with... premade characters if you're into that. You can even do anything you please in their customization up to their biography if you are so inclined and since it is RNG based you can have your own little stories about your guys like my sniper 'Dead End' ambush sniping everyone into their own little dead end... in a bloody explosion that involves their head.  With the pros there's a solid combat system, lovely music score, solid story and properly fleshed out characters out there. Some love you on sight while others hate you, and of course there are some that just don't really care about you until you do something.   Most of all your decisions make an impact on the world while some not being obvious. Do you help this faction or that other one? Can one of your decisions is to do nothing and hope it solves itself? Yeah, you can choose to do nothing. And then you get to deal with all the consequences of those decisions.   Oh, and if you feel like it you can kill everyone you meet. Is this a good idea? Probably not... but this kind of thing is what I'm going to judge other RPGs on. Such as you don't like how someone looked at you? You can kill them. You don't like goats? You can shoot them. Don't like Mr. Boss Man telling you what to do? Yeah, you can shoot him to. Probably won't end well though.  Sadly with all games there are cons. Such as random encounters using the same map repeatedly. Slow on response, especially on the world map.  So yeah, if you like cRPGs that use Action Points and where character stats matter you'll like this game.

What a great game ! People says that this is 'the game that SimCity 5 should have been' but I'm not sure if they're right.  SimCity 5 had great aspects: day/night cycle, date, work and school hours; Monuments and events; traffic (I think); import/export between different cities in the same region -> MULTIPLAYER. Cities: Skylines takes some of these aspects but on some point the game can be disapointing...let me explain:  The vanilla Cities: Skylines has a very specific way to work, the traffic, the roads, the citizens, the tourists (mods can change it); I know that because of my 652 hours playing the game (yeah, that's pretty long). You can build a city that is 100km² and (with a mod) even a city than is 324km² ! But, the game has it's limits with buiding cities, roads and simulation of every citizens and cars.  The game provides a Steam Workshop where you can find (when I'm writing this) over 35000 Assets, Mods, Maps, Saves and Buildings ! You can fall in love with some ones ! But (yeah, another one) the way the game loads, subscribing to only 20 assets or mods, will slooooww down the loading of a city...(I wait between 7 and 15 minutes for my biggest city - 120.000 habitants - to load, and even loaded the game lags because of all the polygons in the map - don't worry, after loaded it's cool...)  Before the conclusion, Colossal Order makes great work on the free updates and the DLCs (After Dark: Leisure, tourism, day and night; Snowfall: Trams, snow, weather, map themes...) and I'm sure there will be more awesome things upcoming !  In conslusion, my review of the game can be pesimist and it can make you don't buy the game, but, really, buy it, sometimes I'm disapointed but I steel play. Just look, I have played this game 652 hours, some played it almost 1000 hours ! That's insane !  So, ifyou buy this game, you won't be sad of spending money for it, it's great game, sometimes disapointing and buggy, but it's a great, great game, with more building oportunities than there's assets on the workshop !

Quite a decent sci fi game with a lot of atmosphere. You play an astronaut sent with his crew to land on a distant planet but of course it all goes wrong. The astronaut wakes up to find his crew missing and the lush fertile planet he thought he was landing on is almost completely barren. To make things stranger he soon discovers a Soviet era settlement and the mystery goes from there. This game is pretty much a walking simlator though it does have a lot of sections where you have to jump across platforms and a few basic puzzles.  Good Points: - The atmosphere in his game is great and is brilliant in creating a feeling of isolation as you explore the games barren alien world - The story is pretty decent. There was enough mystery there to suck me in keep me playing. - Voice acting is good too and adds well to the games atmosphere.  Bad points: - As i mentioned ,despite a good few jumping sections, this game is pretty much a walking simulator and there isnt much here in terms of varied gameplay - Pretty short though to be honest its about the right length. Any longer and it wold have felt drawn out. Not much replay value either.  Overall its an alright game that might be worth picking up if you dont mind walking simulators. I would recommend picking it up on sale as it didnt feel worth the full price.

This game is what happens when Ubisoft listens to its fans.  Wait, wait. Hear me out.  For years, 'fans' of the AC series have asked for certain things: dedicated stealth buttons, more stealth gameplay, better variation in mission types, extra things to do, collectibles, achievements, better combat... and AC: Unity represents an attempt to give fans what they want.  There's a reason fans aren't game designers, however. Ask a kid what he wants for dinner every night, and you're liable to get ice cream as a response. But you know, as I do, that ice cream is not an acceptable dinner food.  AC: Unity, representing a 'crowdsourced' AC game, is ice cream for dinner. It's things you think you want, things that in moderation may be great, but when thrown together, end up just being bad for you.  Which is not to say that the game is bad, per se. The game is mediocre. Arno is a mediocre protagonist, akin to Connor or Altair. He lacks Ezio's roguish charm or Edward's roguish roguery. He doesn't have the personality of Shay or Aveline. In that regard, he's much more of a 'blank slate,' easy to step into the shoes of, but ultimately, a little flat.  The game also features very little bleed-in from the modern day story line, again something 'fans' have been clamoring for, and again, something that doesn't feel right for the series. The series' more annoying moments (tail missions, instant fail missions, anything with Desmond, etc.) served to highlight its truly bright points. The series has never been a 'stealth' game series. It's historical tourism with a side of stabbing simulator. It's a third person action game with wonky, yet familiar, controls. AC: Unity tries to change that, and in what ends up feeling the most like a video game, also feels like a shift away from what made the series popular, even if it wasn't always the toast of aficionados on gaming forums.  What good can be said about the game? It's beautiful. Obviously, Ubisoft put a lot of work into the new engine, but the edges are rough. This is a craftsperson's first try with new tools, and it shows. However, it's plain enough to see that as the engine matures and the technical bugs get ironed out, it will produce some simply stunning graphics and gameplay.  The game also has some innovative features: the murder mysteries, for example, are great. We've been master assassins and soldiers long enough. Let's have a Great Detective Assassin character, a rollicking mix of Holmes and Batman. The gear customization is also a nice change.  But what are we missing? Series mainstays like Assassin Tombs, variation in mission types (a problem with ACIII and AC:U, it seems). Rewarding combat (AC:U penalizes you for getting into combat. I suppose this is supposed to make you play more stealthily, but see above: stealth is not a strong suit of a game with janky controls and odd rules about line of sight). A well-written premise. I feel like we were cheated out of our Pere Dumas-inspired storyline for some crap about a murder we don't get to care about because we hardly know any of the characters. Even the star-crossed Templar/Assassin lovers storyline is downplayed for, what, the 30th time we've seen a bildungsroman about a young protagonist who totally doesn't want to follow Mom and Dad's creed, yo, only to realize by the end of the game that the Creed is worthwhile and the evil Templars must be stopped? It was cool when Ezio did it. It wore thin with Connor. Matt Ryan's performance as Edward saved that storyline, and now again we've got flat-voiced Arno learning that the world is a bigger place than he thought when he was young? Yawn. Yawn yawn yawn.   I think Ubisoft's heart was in the right place, but the execution fell flat. If AC:U is nothing more than a game hastily constructed around a tech demo for the next-gen AC engine, then it makes sense. If they meant this to be a full game, it's a sour note in the AC symphony. Still, there's enough good elements here to work with that I think the series still has a bright future.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: The best Call of Duty since World At War. Base Call of Duty mechanics with a more Arena Shooter style feel - The movement is easily compareable to a mixture of Unreal Tournament 4's Alpha (Q1 2016 release) (with a bit of UT3-isms) with a bit longer wall running that UT4; in the vein of Titanfall but not as overly long. - SP - C+/B- (CO-OP B+ due to how well thought out the Co-oP really was!) - - Zombies - A- - World at War still has the best Zombie Mode, and while I only have the retail map, I can say they did it proper compared to the recent installments; although I hope they bring the WaW and Initial Blops 1 Zombie Maps in DLC - MP - Playing Casually for Fun - A+ - dead on 97 CoD meets Unreal/Quake? Great Job Treyarch! - MP - Playing with your eSports Team as a group - A+ - Everything you want in coordination and tactics from eSports at the break neck speed of titles like UT/Quake that while UT4 will also heavily focus on CTF in eSports, and DOOM will have Clan Arena a serious eSports mode... Duels are what these titles are known for; to nail down that speed and movement in endless team based modes deserves the score! - MP - Playing Competitive Leagues or Pugs on PC platform = B- due to PS4 being lead eSports platform and difficulty finding good competition  - MP - Net Code - A- - Very Solid; only complaint is how the slight aim assisting that occurs seems to alter hit boxes in patches but it is improving - MP GAME MODE VARIETY = A+ 100/100 - BUT - Due to the fact nobody plays the amazing Demolition, CTF, Uplink, ... hell any mode that isnt TDM, DOM, and you can /find HardPoint / Kill Confirmed / Matches but.... sometimes you seriously can NOT outside the moshpit playlist. Thus PC Modes played Earns a rough C instead of the perfect A+ 100/100 - Player Base - Not as toxic as Counter-Strike which I cannot believe I am saying! Nowhere as helpful as Rainbow Six Siege. B+  Overall A-

The gameplay dynamic in Windward goes from calm, controlled and thoughtful (if you're looking for trade routes and quests to build up your towns), to outright mayhem and sudden and repeated death (if you've ventured into a tough pirate region, or a combat instance). These are some basic facts to know, if you're considering a purchase: The game begins with creation of a World, somewhat under the control of the creator, in terms of its shape, the challenges it will present, and some other global features. The world consists of a large number of rectangular regions, each of which contains a procedurally generated terrain and oceans, with a few coastal towns scattered in each. The next decision is to choose your starting faction. There are several Windward factions, all of which are allies by default, and each comes with a different set of advantages and drawbacks, which are described to you when you're asked to choose: maybe you want to get rich as an Exchange captain, or rampage the seas wiping out pirates with Valiant, or ... the choices are yours. You will begin in a Challenge Level 0 area (be thankful!) which will be peaceful enough to get familiar with sailing your sloop between ports around the map. If you haven't watched the introduction video to get a feel for the gameplay, do it now! Although the game is *all* about sailing, and it's called  Windward , this isn't a sailing ship simulator. Your craft will make its way directly into the wind, if you like to point it that way, although more slowly. There's a certain amount of wind variation, but you'll never get a flat calm, nor a storm to sink you. You have simple controls to speed up, slow down and steer to starboard or to port (right and left, for landlubbers :). You can even warp backwards at low speed, necessary if you're stuck up some creek, and your hull is taking damage from being aground. With limited gold at the start, and very much like Commander Jameson in the original Elite, you have few options for climbing to the top: you need to get some weapons, some experience with which to buy talent points, and to build or find more advanced towns which will sell you more advanced ships. Some reviewers have described the early phases of the game as 'tedious' and 'grinding', and if you're an adrenaline junky who doesn't want to take the time to grow your ship and your XP, you might agree. For my money, the calm and thoughtful elements are just as absorbing and rewarding as the maniac battles against fleets of pirates. Make no mistake, though, you will encounter piracy early in the game, and you'll get sunk by gloating pirates often enough to make you want to get back at 'em. The visual aspect of the game is excellent: the water and landscape are animated, there are flocks of gulls and shoals of fish, the clouds drift across the sky in the direction of the prevailing wind (which, incidentally, changes direction slowly but constantly - sometimes it's better to plan a journey that doesn't take you into the teeth of the wind). The sound too is well-chosen, with informative sound effects for game events, and music which matches the peacefulness or otherwise of your situation. Options for single-player play, multi-player on LAN or Internet servers and invitation-only 'instances' all exist, which means that you can be as solitary or gregarious as you wish. The developer has been very accessible and attentive during Early Access, updating  Windward  almost every day for the last six months, and there is a helpful community on the Steam forums to answer your questions. At the price, you won't find many better ways to spend enjoyable hours with a game. I thoroughly recommend it.

Wow, I just finished the game, and all of the alternate endings as well. What a great, bittersweet ride!  I read many of the reviews of this game, and most of them were very positive. Still, many were really picky and negative as well.  Let me start by saying I've been playing RPG's since the beginning (the Ultima series on C-64 type of beginning - yes, they were on Apple first... blah blah..).  While those games were groundbreaking, they also involved quite a bit of 'grinding'. It appears to be the norm from then until now.  There is a decent bit of grinding to be done to get your armor and weapons up to spec for the battle, but it's nothing like WoW grinding. All of the rooms appear to refresh after you leave and come back, so it's pretty easy to find a room that has several monsters in it, and not too many obstacles, and just go in, knock them out, leave, and repeat. What's worse, grinding to get up to spec, or getting trashed by the boss? ;)  Yes, there are grammatical errors throughout the game. Let's just say that Holy Priest speaks (or writes) better English than most of us can speak (or write) his/her language. If you're a nitpicky person that is deeply disturbed by grammatical errors, then just move along, after all, you wouldn't want to experience a fun and not too deep RPG with a nice story and great artwork that's also free, right?  I thought the story line, cut scenes, and overall gameplay were superb! It's meant to be simple, and even the toughest boss (for me, anyway) only took 3 tries to kill. There's enough armor and weapon crafting to make it interesting, but not overboard. Again, all pretty simple, and very entertaining. There are plenty of save spots, and other than not being able to make it to the 'exit' screen from in the game, I think it all went pretty well. I ended up hitting the windows key and ending the game once it was minimized by right clicking on the icon on the windows bar and selecting close. All of my saves worked fine, etc.  The music was nice, and it didn't get in the way, or get annoying, and the sound effects were simple and effective. The artwork was top notch, and the graphics are reminders of RPG days gone by, and I mean that in a good way!  If you're looking to play some RPG's, but don't have a lot of time to invest, then this game is a must. You can simply save in the save areas (which are pretty common), or you can do a couple of red-eye's like I did and finish it in 2 sittings.   I also appreciated that you can get to the other endings without replaying the entire game!  There is always room for improvement, and that's with all games. To think that this much work was put into a game and then offered for free - it's a really nice thing to happen for gamers. It's not ALWAYS about the money, and that's refreshing.  Big thanks to Holy Priest for taking the time, and making this little gem. I'd love to find a place where you could contact the author and send suggestions, alternate phrasing and words, etc. if only to keep the nitpicker's happy, or at least quieter...   I have not played Ragnarok Online, which this game is influenced by / based on, but I'm interested in trying it out now.  Anyway, this game was very fun to play, the grinding wasn't insane, (and it was pretty simple), and the storyline is happy and sad. Bittersweet. I enjoyed it thorougly!  8-13-2016

I was worried about how this game would turn out. id Software delivered a great sequel to the DOOM series. Badass powerful guns, fantastic level design, a great PC port, very good AI, and fun and fluid combat.   The campaign was the main reason I got the game, since I'm a long time DOOM fan. It went beyond my expectations of a new DOOM game, even though I've finished it, I've already found lots of reasons to go back and play it again. There are lots of stuff to look for in levels, mainly secrets, quite a bit that are well hidden. The glory kill system blended in well with the combat, saving me some ammo also plus a nice reward. I also thought the Chainsaw and BFG system were an amazing implementation. Weapon upgrades make the guns even more fun to use.  The multiplayer wasn't necessarily something that I'd see myself playing, even after a few matches, but that's my own opinion, if you like the more modern type of multiplayer shooters, this might be for you.   SnapMap was a fun tool to use, for a bit. I tried my hand at making a map and found it easy to use, but I found it quite limited. Especially with the maps not looking like they've been invaded by Hell, I'm also not too big of a fan of only using the AI conductor for enemy spawns. Another huge problem is the fact that you can only hold two weapons, the chainsaw, the pistol, and berserk are missing. I was hoping for another single player type of experience. This still adds quite a bit of content that should be checked out, but needs some tweaks, or just mod tools (which I'd prefer).
It's fun for a short amount of time, but after that you don't really find yourself coming back.
Maybe not quite worth the price, even with the opening 10% off, but still a very good little game.

app_name: Attack on Titan / A.O.T. Wings of Freedom
review_text: Did not expect this to show up on PC at all but frankly I'm glad it did. Its a solid enough port for what the baseline was, being PS4... Runs at 60fps and has an ok amount of PC options, but of course nowhere near a normal 'PC game'.   This game has one of the most unique gameplay engines I've seen in a very long while. The devs have tackled the issue of how to do 'small vs many huge' high speed combat VERY well. The scale and scope of taking down a Titan will seem daunting at first, but after a quick tutorial you'll be killing Titans left right and center. The controls feel extremely natural and the combat has an immense sense of 'size' and verticality to it. Traversing town using the 3d gear never felt so satisfying.  NOTE: Console game port = play with gamepad, idiots.

app_name: Warhammer 40,000: Dawn of War - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: I played Company of Heroes before I started Dawn of War, and I absolutely love both games. Disregarding the several very awesome standalone expansions to Dawn of War, I have to say the original is ♥♥♥♥ing kickass. When I first picked it up, I played it for the classic skirmish option, and set up my own custom games against the AI so I could have massive battles within this awesome universe. But when I really got sucked into this game was when I fired up the campaign with the nice little story, for an RTS game, and the varied missions that escalated the scale and availability of troops as I progressed. If you like RTS games, then Dawn of War is a must-buy. And I would even recommend it to those who've never played an RTS game before because it's very accessible given the less complicated base-building and resource system (no gatherer units, you just build energy generators with workers and capture points with infantry). I often have a difficult time playing the likes of Age of Empires and other games of a similar vein because I'm too lazy to manage everything economy after playing this game.

Well, where do I begin with the roller coaster life I have inhabited since beginning the two dimensional venture that has changed my three dimensional reality?  I suppose it started when I built a house after obtaining several life crystals in my traitorous cave ventures. With the new found power I had the ability to build a magnificent house in my land of the woods. To do just this had taken many hours from my day and I became winery during the process of playing this fantastic game that the pixels on my screen became a blur. I finished boarding up my house and shut down my computer. I lay awake at night thinking of what could happen on my adventures tomorrow as I stared into the slit of the night sky the curtain in my bedroom could not hide me from.  I awoke from my slumber and evacuated the bed sheets heading for the kitchen in my apartment. The box of circular whole wheat cereal had been left on the side with the inner bag’s corner torn and poking up out of the dented box. I poured myself a bowl and filled it with milk; I recall that the milk was nearly half depleted at this point. I ate the cereal as I loaded up my computer slurping every drop of milk before putting it to the left of my monitor. There it was, Terraria. With a few clicks I was immersed in the fantastic world I had the ability to live in as I wanted, to choose my own battles and travel to parts unknown.  I travelled east across the land to come across a horrific land of dark purple rock and open crevasses leading to God knows where. I tried to ignore it but I just couldn’t, the thought of treasures unseen lured me in as I found myself peering eagerly over the edge of the great pit before me. At that point a flying flesh-eating monster had risen from the depths, from a possible nest. I didn’t want to know if there were more I ran home but had stumbled before escaping the beast in the purple wasteland. It charged at me, I pointed my sword and closed my eyes. The beast had driven its head straight through, it tooth however surgery cut my leg. I didn’t think I could make back up that my adventure was over but a savior, an angle of God saved me as I was told that “Lucy the nurse had arrived”. I dragged myself home and was greeted by Lucy, her pixels were magnificent, her hair blond, her eyes blue. She noticed my leg and healed it within moments I gave her some silver coins and asked her to stay.

The Journey Down Chapter One is about two brothers adopted by a man off the street. They're running the gas charter in the bay and struggling with money. When a woman searching for a book arrives, suddenly, they're seeing 'dollars'.... but did they know that the book owned by their father was in their possession and sparked interest... in a powerful man?  The first chapter is set in the bay, where Bwana, your character, is working and living. As he needs to get Lyna across the ocean, he has to fix the airplane he's not flying since he was 12. That's why Bwana is messing with gas cannister, trying to get out of a freezer in a yacht, amazed that he can do a ladder with.... nah I'm not spoiling the puzzles.  I enjoyed that point-&-click adventure because of the atmosphere. There is clearly influenced by Afro-Caribbean (as the characters are inspired from African masks but the way they're talking and the music is more from the Caribbean). You ask yourself if Bwana hasn't smoked weed before the beginning of the story but well, he seems more like having a cool personnality. And that, dear gamers, that what I ask also from an adventure game: characters with a grey personnality as they're reflecting what we are: imperfect. Bwana isn't stupid. Some of his remarks show someone aware of his surrounding, feeling down because of his father's disappearance. Kito, his 'street' brother, is a genius when it comes to engineering, despite his cool attitude. Lyna, well-mannered, stays polite and provides information on what may happen in the background. She's never pressuring the boys, apart when the mean guys arrive. Besides, the fact that the ambiance is also leading to a comedy game is also doing that.  The graphics are also good: with scenes and rooms looking like in a comic. The only thing that I had an hard time to get used to is probably the characters design.  The music, well, is adapted. I really had the impression that I was in the Caribbean. It's awesome.  Apart one or two freezes, the game runs smoothly.  I've just a little complaint: the puzzles aren't hard at all, true. But sometimes, it's hard to imagine the solution for some of them. Like for the propeller used as a fan by Bwana. But also, as the puzzles aren't so hard, the game is short. As short as a Hidden Object game can be. A little more of 2 hours. I understand that the rest will follow (second chapter is out). But a little more would have been good too.  The Journey Down: Chapter 1 manages to entertain me. I'll play the second chapter very soon and I'll buy that third episode without problems.

I'm genuinely surprised by how much I like this game. I bought it thinking that I'd play it now and again for a change of pace as I used to play a game on my ZX Spectrum called 'Juggernaut' which had a similar theme, but I finally got around to playing it and I think I'm hooked!  I haven't explored much of the game yet, but I think the developers have done a great job of capturing the essence of the various locations and I'm seriously thinking of getting a steering wheel controller just to play this properly, plus a better graphics card so I can turn up some of the graphic options. Don't get me wrong though, even on basic settings it still looks great and when the rain starts pelting down it's tough not to really feel like you're in the cab.  I'm in two minds about whether the compressing of time and distance is a good thing or not but I guess it's a good compromise between full simulation realism and actually making it a playable game for most people. Perhaps a 'Hardcore Driver' option in future? :-)  The only problem I have so far is suicidal drivers on roundabouts and other 4-wheeled numbskulls, but I guess that makes it more realistic! Also, visibility of other traffic at night seems to be really poor (I'd expect to see their headlights way off in the distance) unless I'm missing something. It makes changing lanes in the dark a bit more stressful than I think it should be.

If you are looking for a rogue-like game to play, this game would be my first suggestion! It is a top-down, 2D bullet hell with minimislistic controls. Don't take the graphics as a sign for the gameplay though, this game will plough you harder than a farmer and you will love every minute of it! So many great reviews have already covered every detail of why this game is good but for me it stands out because every playthrough has the potential to feel so different due to the items. In other roguelikes I've seen, items just seem to give you more speed/damage/health with very little actually changing how you play. In Binding of Isaac however, you will have playthroughs where you shoot tears, playthroughs where you get lazers which penetrate a whole line of the screen, playthroughs where you shoot bombs out of eyes and have to bear yourself in mind, playthroughs where hordes of babies and/or spiders do most of the fighting for you as well playthroughs where you roleplay Magneto with your tears and so so so many more! If you love challenging rogue-like games and don't mind the retro graphics (I usually do but this game really does make up for what it lacks in visuals) you will not regret buying this!

This game. I haven't spent too much time in this game, but gosh is it hard. I've decked a good hour or so into the game, but it takes the rouge-like to a new level of hard. The screen is constantly descending deeper, and you're tryting to keep up, but you have to grab some more star-ammo, dare you risk going for the ammo? Or should you play it safe? Now, imagine you're a time lord, and you go for option A, you rush across the screen, and grab the ammo, but the screen passes and you die. Now, rewind time and go for B. You descend deeper succesfully, but are flanked by two flying foes, who basically bite you to death. This is Probability 0. The game has a certain old-school difficulty not successfully pulled very often today, and its by the creators of Starseed Piligrim. Overall, I'd say if you really like old-school titles, buy it, if not, wait for it to go on sale. It's a great game(Would be better with local coop.), but the price tag is a tad hyperbole.

7/10, would maybe play again.  The story is great while the writing can be poor at times, they removed a lot of what made Final Fantasy games special but also added a lot that can be considered innovative and valuable. The battle system was new, interesting, and kept me involved for some time. I was still learning new strategies at the very end of the game. The graphics, of course, are flawless, and since the update for PC they're even better. I never experienced any problems in terms of functionality. I didn't 100% the game because the incentives to complete the side missions were relatively nonexistent and traveling back and forth to complete them became tedious.  Overall, I would recommend that someone play through this game at least once. The story really is thought-provoking, and the character design is great; I grew attached to most of the characters and I really cared about the outcome of the game. Seeing as how my 1-hour-in review got marked as abusive by the devs (imagine that), let's try this again.  This game isn't finished. Whoever decided that this was 1.0 worthy made a very big mistake. Once you figure out how to make money reliably (which isn't difficult) you'll just be breezing through the then seemingly meaningless office and crew upgrades. Once you hire someone, they'll be with you until the end. (Unless they somehow get ♥♥♥♥♥♥ and leave, I guess.) You won't be able to fire anyone and replace them with someone more qualified. (Or maybe you can. Who knows with that mess of a UI.) Nope, not what the devs had in mind, it seems. Not that it matters much anyway. 'Leveling up' mostly consists of repeating dirt cheap training courses again and again. Wait until the game gives you another similar opportunity, repeat.  It's actually quite difficult to even remember all of the odd stuff going on with this game. You just sort of drone on after a while, repeating the same actions only to see your millions of dollars grow with nothing to spend any of it on. It's all a big, unfinished mess. The UI's held together with duct tape, many 'features' are blatantly strapped on as an afterthought and you'll be drowning in typos and grammatical weirdness.

Before this game was announced, I had no idea I wanted something like it. When Activision announced that Platinum was developing it, it made sense to be wary, especially after the last time they made a character-action game based on a licensed property. However, Transformers: Devastation certainly has a lot of great things going for it, which are listed below: PROS -Multiple playable characters (and so many weapons to boot) -Quite a few G1 voice actors come back (It's a lot like Ducktales Remastered in that way) -Cel-shaded artstyle (seriously, why has it taken this long to make a Transformers game like this?) -It's actually a mix of various G1 things (The cartoon, the toys, even the IDW comics) -Challenge mode -Multiple difficulties and level-up system ensure replayability, along with the character variety -Hidden secrets and easter eggs (They remenbered Kremzeek!)  With all that said, it's not perfect. There are quite a few glaring problems with the game: CONS -Autobots are only playable (A tad petty, but still...) -200-weapon limit -Story mode is WAAAAAAAY too short (I was hoping in vain that there would be a surprise last boss after the credits) -Some of the playable character choices are a bit weird (They play fine, but I don't know what Bumblebee is doing here when he's not in the Combiner Wars toyline)  It's still a great game, one of Platinum's finest; as long as fans know what they're expecting, they should be fine. Speaking of which, the only way I can recommend it is to hardcore Transformers fans, hardcore Platinum fans, or softer-core fans of either or both when the price is dropped. Bayonetta and Revengeance are more polished, but this is good for a few hours of mindless fun, taking a trip down memory lane, or remembering that there are good things about the Transformers brand (Bayformers and Kiss Players can't bring me down when I've got something like this!).

I personaly very much enjoyed, still enjoy I'll admit, these five mini-games that compose Rooftop Cop. Why for me is simmilar to my enjoyment of the Welcome to Night Vale podcast.  Both, at least to me, represent a subjective puzzle in which the player attempts to solve their lore, history, and story of how those systems(typically made of characters, objects, ideas) function in themselfs plus their interaction with one another- allowing you the player to project and stitch together the narrative: Through the five vignette* games you're given a vague picture where by its narrative evolution depends upon the lenses one analyzes it with.  Perhaps my comparriosn to WtNV sheds off here in that within its own meduim, you are not driving the ship, when unlike video games there's the epectation for direct and explicit rewards from effort; input then fallows personally impactful output. More so, if being someone who holds the epectation of a fully air-tight complete story [or general dislike for making 'narrative hypothesis' and self constructed story connections], that's not what Rooftop Cop provides.   It's a impromptu for you to fill in the gaps of the story; and, that's why I compare it to WtNV becuase that radio-podcast is the same in that both are subjective to your intrpetation, yet in WtNV progresses without imput. I mean beyond playing it and fallowed by listeing to it, WtNV progresses wither or not you've individually given its aspects thought.  Meanwhile with Rooftop Cop because its a video game I can see the un-ideal prospect of its mechanics being more of an obstacle; over that of additional layer for promoting contemplation, at least of the subjective story it evokes in each of us. _Side note, on that subject Idea Channel created a video in relation of 'Mechanics vs Nerrative' linked:          YouTube™ Video:  Are Videogames About Their Mechanics? | Idea Channel | PBS Digital Studios

This game was a great idea with very poor followthrough. You learn two or three tricks to bypass security, have to collect the appropriate scripts to defeat each of the half dozen interfaces, and then boom, you're a legendary hacker. It's all text-based with a super basic linuxy command line interface, which I'm sure makes some people feel like they're all h4xx0r... I'm probably being unnecissarily harsh, but I really was shocked at how repetative this game was.   It seemed like there were alot of mechanics that they planned, but never got around to implementing. All that hype about deleting the logs after you access a computer... maybe I missed something, but I couldn't see that it did anything. And the Trap and Forkbombs... were those actually useful somehow? Oh, and it was very easy to make game-breaking mistakes, like deleting a file you were supposed to steal. Alot of silent ways to ruin the game and be unable to recover... hope you didn't save after one of those.  There are alot of fun points. Getting a new script and seeing the new computers you can crack is exciting. The story in the beginning and end of the game are interesting. Also the little easter-eggs of emails and chat snippets were pretty funny. And I'm sure the game does look cool if you let people see you playing it.   My background in embedded sofware may have colored my opinion a bit, but the game felt awfully thin for $10. And from a gameplay perspective, it was awfully rough. Serious programmers should look at TIS-100 instead, although beware: Zacktronics games are not for the faint-hearted, and may feel a little too much like work to the professional programmer.

I've been playing this game for about 2 months on Android. At first glance this looks like your typical microtransactions game but really, it's not. There are tons of ways to earn gems (the premium currency) and even with them, there isn't a need to use them to progress. Sure, you can speed things up with them but let's face it, only a 10 year old running wild with mommy and daddy's credit card will use premium currency on speed ups. It would still cost way too much to speed everything up to the point that you get to the top even twice as fast as anyone playing for free. This game also has something more or less unique. You can actually pay gold, the regular free currency you use for everything in game to speed things up by investing in your city (more on that later). Whether or not you agree with it, paying doesn't make you progress that much faster. It's not pay to win either because there's no way a person who pays anything can inconvenience a person who never paid in any way.  This game also doesn't have flashy animated graphics telling you to buy gems or sales that you can't get off the screen (ala Game of War/Mobile Strike). At most, when they have a special package or sale, it's tucked way up into a banner at the top of the screen which on the steam version takes up less than 1% of the total screen and doesn't animate and drive you insane. They also don't have any extra popups that annoy you every time you enter the game or during gameplay other than the notification screen when you log on. The notification screen serves its purpose though showing you what has been going on within your city since you last logged off. It's half a banner that has news updates, sales, and social media stuff and on the other half, your list of notifications.  Within the game, you have cities of up to 25 players. A city serves an important purpose in the game. As you and the other players invest in the city, the buildings level up unlocking more workers, faster resource generation, different types of resources, more heroes to quest with, more quests, and more levels on the cooperative event held from Thursday to Sunday every week called City Raids. The game encourages cooperation with others but without too much risk, If you feel the need to leave your city, the money you invested in the city will follow you so you don't leave anything behind as long as you were a part of the city for 10 days or longer (less than 10 days, you will lose 10% - 1% for every day you were there so if you leave after 6 days, you lose 4% etc...). Investing in your city will give both an instant reward for yourself and a collective reward for your city. The collective rewards last for 30 minutes for each increment you invest and stacks for (I believe) up to 24 hours so all of your citymates will be able to reap the benefits of the collective rewards. Instant and collective rewards also increase as the building level increases. Instant rewards are things like resources, xp, and instant reduction of timers. Collective rewards are temporary, affect everyone in your city, and include things like boosted resource production, increased hero strength, increased worker skill, and reduced timers. Instant boosts stack so you can turn your timers from hours into minutes with enough gold!  The trade house allows you to trade items with other players. You can buy and sell stuff for both gold or gems. This is where many of the players make most of their money. You can also earn the premium currency (gems) here. Item prices are capped at 10x what they are worth to keep prices from going crazy.  You can send your heroes on quests to gain artifacts which are basically another resource to craft with. You can equip them with items you craft to make them stronger and allow them to go on harder quests later. Breakability of items depends on hero and item level. If they are too far apart, things will break quickly so you can't just pay money and buy the best stuff in the game in the beginning because you will lose it immediately (even though you wouldn't be able to see the best stuff in the game at a low level anyway, even in the trade house) also if a hero level is much higher than the equipment, it will break. Investing in the inn in your city can unlock more quests and artifacts along with its instant and collective boosts of reducing the quest timers.  There is an arena for PvP combat. This is mostly optional but can also help your city by winning crown jewels which can be used to purchase permanent boosts for your city. In PvP, if you win, you gain crown jewels, honor, which is used for matchmaking and leaderboards, and if you win enough times, you get a bag with a random item. If you lose, you lose honor, that's all. No one can harm your gameplay by beating you in the arena unless you're really hurt by not being at the top of the leaderboards. The only thing you can lose is honor and honor only dictates how many crown jewels you get if you win and what equipment bag you get if you win enough. The bag and crown jewels you win will not be at the expense of your opponent. You can use the same equipment on your heroes for the arena or use a special arena set. Some items give different skills so your items for quests may be different from the arena.  Overall there is a lot to do in this game but it does take a long time. If you're not into long progression games that will take you months to get to the mid and higher levels, don't bother. If you like a casual game that you can play at your own pace and you don't have to be on or have to listen 24/7 for notifications from, give this game a shot. The community is very nice. People are always willing to answer even the most basic questions and the developers are active and involved in conversations in the discussion forums on Steam and on their Website forum. I would definitely recommend.

I really wish there was a 'mixed' option because I appreciate what this game was trying to do (and in many regards it succeeded), but ultimately it was ahead of its time and felt lacking in some departments.  The game itself, while having an amazing story, complemented by top notch character development felt awefully linear. I often felt like I had to choose what the game wanted me to choose and wasn't allowed any freedom throughout the cases, such as when there are two pieces of evidence that could equally disprove someone, but the game only allows one to be correct, you cannot present both, or one or the other, only the one will be correct; that combined the 30FPS lock is what makes me have mixed opinions about this game.  I would still recommend to anybody looking for a crime-thriller, or just a great story driven game in general.  It was a great idea by Team Bondi, but unfortunately ahead of its time, I hope someone at Rockstar picks this title up again and we eventually get a sequel.

Holy ♥♥♥♥! The aliens are attacking and Will Smith is not here to help us! What are we going to do?! The ♥♥♥♥ do you think, dickwad?! We're going to start an anti-alien club and kick those alien asses back where they came from!  What is XCOM, you ask? It's the reimagining of the original XCOM: UFO Defense!   What do you do in XCOM? You go on the battlefield with your soilders and try to kill and/or capture aliens that are destroying everything!   Why are they destroying everything? Because they are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s! Technology and genetic modification did not remove their '♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ gene'!   How do you play the game? It's turn based! You move your soilders, shoot the aliens and hope their aim is worse when they have their turn!  And your soilders? They will die a horrible death by a plasma to the face, explosion to the face, rocket to the face or just friendly fire to the face when the rookie panics when s/he gets a paper cut! Or they will (wo)man the ♥♥♥♥ up when plasma hits their face and make that alien ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ regret pulling the trigger! Classes?! We got 'em! Assault - when you need to get close and personal and greet your enemy with a shotgun to the face! Heavy - machine gun and rockets, when you are overcompensating for something or just want to blow stuff up! Support - when you fall unconcious on the battlefield, nothing is better than cocaine to wake you up, from your friendly support! Sniper - you better protect this guy late game, because when your sniper hits the max level, aliens will have nightmares about the scary sniper called 'Moose'!  Now that the gang has saved the day (hopefully without casualties), it's time to go to the secret club house! Here's when the other half of the game takes place! Over here you build equipment and upgrades that the guys in the laboratory researched, plan your finances (which the council of nations give you each month), plan your soldiers weapons and wait for another mission in which those ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s will raid a taco restourant!  What's that? You want more? Good! Give your offering to our Lord Gaben and get the expansion Enemy within! The expansion includes some new aliens, two new classes and a new faction that wants to stop you, called EXALT! They're not aliens, they are humans and they hate you, because they couldn't join your club for the cool kids, so now they want revenge!  The aliens are not really worth mentioning, but the two classes make the game even more interesting! Throught the missions you collect MELD, which can be used in two different ways! The first one is genetic modification of your soldiers the other is chopping of their limbs and make them into a robot of death a.k.a. MEC trooper (google xcom kinetic strike)!  Overall, this game is amazing and will give you hours of fun, make you play the whole night, fail school and forget about life, just like Civilization! So grab your wallet, sacrifice your money and get the game with the expansion!

Have played alot of terraria which is great, and I am hoping that this game gets the same tender loving care that it did. As it stands right now, the game is quite stable, the only thing i have seen is it drops frames sometimes. The parallax and lighting in this game looks pretty awesome for a game like this. So far the mechanics and things have been working well, combat is a lil floaty as others have said, although there are some mods you can get to alleviate the problem. That gets us to the biggest pro the mods, and there is a crapton of them. Getting back to the meat of the game, the progression system is structured well although I think it should be a bit more broad in scope than it currently is *mods fix this too ;)*, but there is also the quests you can do for citizens in your colony which award random items from reward bags. The focus is mostly on armor which modifies various stats including attack, yet once you get good armor crafted it turns into a fashion show, as there are lots of costume pieces to wear. This leaves the weapons and so far about 3/4 the way through the story they have been pretty good, you seem to get alot of weapon upgrades but armor is few and far between. Crafting is pretty nice but there just isn't alot of variety on the armor/weapons side of things, there is tons of everything else but armor/weapons. Not to say that there isn't alot of them in the game that is, just that the crafting is kind of lacking in that regard. Was hoping for some hop in matchmaking for multiplayer or at least a browser of some kind, still more of the same ip plugging and hunting dedicated servers online. Other than that the game is a real gem, and one that shouldn't be passed up if you're into this kind of game. Its basically terraria/stardew valley in space, cept if you get bored of the world you're on you can just fly to another one there are 1000s.   Overall   Graphics: 8 (The parallax and lighting really makes this one standout, although I think that the game could use some optimization in some areas.)   Audio: 10 (Soundtrack is pretty good and there are many music mods to add more, even zelda music)   Mechanics: 8 (Floaty combat, can't be to critical of things like this because there will likely be more stuff added as we go along)   Mods: 10 (Some really good ones on the workshop for this game, some complete overhaul ones too. You'll not run out of things to play with for a long time)    Update

( With a couple of exceptions) - It's cool how you can kill your enemies with map interaction in a couple of maps. - Fun trolling players who take this game too seriously, and are total ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s.  - Mutaliting your friends limbs and toying with dead bodies is alway a plus - Modded maps are incredible and are way better than the normal maps that this game has to offer. - This game's ridiculousness makes it very humorous to play - Horde mode is pretty fun to play.  - Weapon kills now work fine. - Mods are what makes me still play this game time from time  Cons:  - Vanguard + Zweihander + LMB LMB LMB LMB LMB LMB LMB LMB = Guaranteed K/D of like 20-5 [Some people are using this to dismiss my review and say that I suck so I'll get this clear, It's what I see in lobby's. Likewise, I do not block and it still does anyway. (Without a shield ofc) - Obnoxiously tryhard community - Archers must be the most annoying people on the planet, according to the community. - Dragging is a mechanic that is important to learn and use in the game, why isn't in the tutorial? - Feinting is a fair mechanic, although I don't use it, I still hate it when people complain about it. - Animations are some of the worst i've ever seen, not medieval at all, looks like a couple of fat nerds playing LARP. - A spear stabs through a knight in full armor, and manages to hit someone behind him, Total sense. - The official servers lag ALL of the time, I get dropped from them very often. (54 mbps) - Hit a teammate once, you are offically the worst player on the planet. - Dev's won't do ♥♥♥♥ about balance and the bugs in this game, all they do is release new helmets and that stupid Warrior DLC, FOR MORE MONEY. Score: 5.5/10   Final analysis: Game can be fun, and i've sure had some great moments when playing with friends, but overall if you want a more balanced and fun game that doesnt have a bunch developers that dont do anything, and a community that doesn't have metal poles up their ♥♥♥, and just a good medieval game look elsewhere, Chivalry will offer you nothing. This is the biggest pile of crap in gaming. Forget that this totally abandons any last bit of suspenseful survival horror the franchise may have had left, but this crapfest doesn't even pull off being an average 3rd person shooter. All of its game and combat mechanics completely work against each other. Even on the easiest level ammo is way to scarce, reloading takes way to long, and swapping weapons is nightmare.   For a game centered on action they made combat so freaking moronic that it's nearly impossible to take part in the action. You'll spend more time running around trying to collect ammo and reloading your weapon than fighting monsters. And these monsters are the now trademark Resident Evil bullet sponges. You'll also fight he camera as its control is funky and unintuitive and will always do a lot of stuff not quite the way you expect it to.  The interface is god awful, and the inventory is the most insane system to navigate than any that's ever been. And the menus, god the menus. You will have to go through so many menus and button clicks just to do anything it's insane. Click to start, click to check DLC, clikc to acknowledge this game uses auto saves, click to start again, clik to play, clikc to play story, click for chapter, click for game settings, finally now you can play.  Then there's the extended boss fights. Spend 30 seconds shooting, then a minute of edgy combat cutscene, 30 more seconds of shooting, and then another minute of cutscene. Then action sequence where you're out of ammo and have to shoot something or die, die, reload, then have the smallest amount of time to do it again, but having to collect the ammo, select the weappon that uses that ammo, then reload the weapon, and hope you can still shoot that explosive barrel before you insta die again. No thanks.  The game has a lot for you to do, too bad it makes doing even the smallest tings a real chore. This game completely sucks as both a Resident Evil game and even as a generic monster hunt 3rd person shooter.

Gnomoria is the successful attempt to simplify the overly complex Dwarf Fortress, mix in some elements from other games and combine it with some basic isometric graphics and a very well-designed user interface.   Basically you get to build a more or less working little gnome village, containing living quarters, industry, farming, mining and so on. In addition to placing buildings and tools, the management of the gnomes and setting their priorities makes up the bulk of the gameplay - and it works really well! Especially at x2 speed, which feels good, there is always something to tweak and improve. Keeping your gnomes alive for a prolonged period of time is a real challenge and min-maxing all aspects of your colony is a really motivating task.   That being said, there are unfortunately still some issues and I feel like the game was declared to be finished a bit too early. My main bug bear is the combat system: For one, the way combat is shown is too basic - usually all your gnomes merge on one tile and sit next to the enemy's pile until gnomes retreat to bandage themselves. More importantly, the difficulty seems pretty off-balance to me. Often there are huge spikes in the amount and strength of enemies that arrive. Also, since you cannot micromanage your warriors and only order whole groups, I've often died because I happened to fight on multiple fronts at the same time or because some of my soldiers were sleeping during an attack. Alas, most of the time dying did feel cheap rather than fun. (The fact that most tips/forums recommend cheesing out enemies does not really help.)  That being said, I think that Gnomoria is an awesome game. While the game has its flaws, I've literally played it for hundreds of hours and highly recommend it to anyone interested in the genre. I've had a lot of fun with the game and would love to see the dev continue working on it or start working on a sequel.   Edit: Originally this was a positive review. However, I noticed that the support email address given on the game's homepage is no longer invalid. Since I cannot wholeheartedly recommend a game that is no longer supported, I have to amend my review accordingly. If the situation changes, I'll update this review accordingly.

review_text: Simple but fun concept where each analog stick controls each character.  The puzzles weren't difficult but amusing. The ambiance and atmosphere can make the game feel very slow at times. MOTION SICKNESS WARNING  : You'll use a (kickin' rad) vehicle to get around Brutal Legend's sizeable sand box world, but due to its Heavy Metal theme, terrain is very uneven and makes steady smooth driving difficult. You'll be accelerating and decelerating at different rates, and this gave me a lot of nausea. If fast-moving games give you the same problem, then Brutal Legend may have the same effect.  That aside, Brutal Legend is more an  experience  rather than a game. Set in an intricately-designed landscape inspired by metal album covers, you step into the shoes of Jack Black/Eddie Riggs as you headbang your way through your foes in defense of all things Metal and righteous. As you progress through the main campaign, you will recruit hearty groups of warriors to your cause, adding to an army that will help you rock your way to victory.  However, the gameplay is lacking. There are three main 'mini-games' that Brutal Legend will keep bringing you back to: Third-Person Hack&Slash Action, Real-Time Strategy, and Driving. Main Campaign missions will typically focus on one of these aspects, but frankly I never felt engaged enough with any of them.  With  Hack&Slash Action , I just waded in and mashed the attack buttons, occasionally circling to avoid getting hit and letting my health regen. Fights are chaotic masses of blood and special effects, making it hard for me to know what I did beyond haphazard slashing, or what can or did kill me and what I could've done to avoid that. With  Real-Time Strategy , I spammed unit-building and just charged en masse to whatever needed killing the most. That's my own single-mindedness at fault more than anything, but the unit-selection wheel was hard to manage with the mouse which led me to my less-involved approach. Finally, with  Driving , in addition to my nausea, control of my vehicle didn't feel smooth and made it even less enjoyable for me.  Also, there is a multiplayer option that focuses on the RTS aspect of Brutal Legend. I tried it once, but the same objections I had for the gameplay earlier apply here as well. Also, odds are good that you'll be facing a lot of players with much more RTS experience, so try out AI opponents first.  Overall, I actually  do  still recommend this game; despite my grievances, I did still enjoy the well-designed setting, and the story, and Jack Black as Eddie Riggs is a very relatable protagonist. However, I recommend this  only  as an interactive open-world movie that you only need to go through once, and  only  to fans of heavy metal and/or Jack Black, and also if said fans aren't prone to motion sickness.   Note for OCD Collectors : Brutal Legend also has a lot of 'collectibles' in the game, and fortunately once you finish the main campaign...    you can get an item that acts as a collectible compass. It won't mark them out for you explicitly on the map, but it will save you a few hours of squinting and searching.

Cut scenes cannot be skipped or even advanced, not even the closing credits, destroying replayability as there are many, many breaks in the actions for brief-to-long interludes of dialog.   The final level is a frustrating mess requiring 8-bit era manuevering and timing that does not play well with modern analog joysticks. Instead of a fun final romp to the conclusion it's a boring, awkward mess that feels out of place. I found myself rushing to try to get through it. And once there I was treated to a nearly ten minute long credit roll I couldn't skip past.  If this game gets down to $1.99 or lower I'd say go for it. Otherwise there are much, much better games for your money and time.

I'm writing this review with the fact i've had zero issues with the game, but i seem to be very lucky in that aspect, many have had a lot of issues graphical and performance wise.  My specs are Core i5 2500 (not k) 3.3ghz Asus GTX 770 2gb 16gb ddr3 Corsair Ram  Dying Light, for me i'm shocked to say this game is amazing.   I came into this game with very low expectations, and a month late thanks to pre ordering retail and being Aussie.   While i enjoyed the Dead Island games.. I found them hard to get into, which this game has some similarities too, But... it's a very different beast (pun intended), the game runs very fluid and the story feels much fuller, you feel like you're in a proper zombie flick, which is rare with so many bad games of the genre these days.   The movement feels really good and is quite easy to get used to, the weapons and combat feels meaty, the damage and difficulty ramps up well even on normal, this game is very much a melee orientated game even though there are firearms they are very niche bit still work well, aiming feels good.  The Characters are all diverse and well acted, especially the main characters, the main character also speaks which is a nice change, the world is large and is beautiful, even on singleplayer you never feel alone like some games, the npc are numerous and the npc missions while hit and miss most are really fun and progress the game well.  Night actually feels scary and as you progress you feel like your character also progresses.  For my cons..  I dislike the durability of weapons and the over use of fetch quests / collection type things console games are very well known for, the UI feels a bit clunky but lucky doesn't affect the combat at all.  I'm not a huge fan of achievements or competition missions, but if you like them this game has both.  Crafting while ok could have been far better.  A small annoyance is sometimes climbing qte fails and i fall to my death..  That said overall as a modern day zombie game it excels at what it aims at, the world is amazing, pretty sandboxie and a lot of fun, the mod scene seems to be going strong and it has coop.  Speaking of Multiplayer and Coop, i have not tested it out and have not tried 'Be the Zombie' at the time of writing this review.  Thanks for reading.

I can't say I really enjoyed playing this game. It seemed good at first, but the more I played, the more mind numbing, tedious and boring it became. I thought it was going to be good because it added a social element to a typical RPG. However, this game is nowhere near as good as the likes of Persona or Fire Emblem: Awakening. The social aspect is limited to making simple choices between two options which either impress or irritate the other characters in the game. Additionally, a lot of the choices look as though you're going to irritate the person you're talking to either way, which I personally don't like.  Yet, this isn't the only problem I found with the game. The equipment you have is fairly simple and there are only two ways to obtain more. Recycle your old gear for scrap metal and build new equipment with it or find it on quests. To find new equipment on quests is not efficient as you have several crew members who all need their own equipment and the quests yield nowhere near the amount needed. As for the recycling method, the amount of scrap metal needed is far too great (from my experience).  Finally, I find the combat to be very repetitive. A lot of the enemies are extremely powerful and take a lot of effort to take down. This results in you needing to go through long battles on a regular basis doing the exact same moves and praying luck is in your favour, since you really need a lot of luck in this game. It wouldn't be so bad if there were ways to grind against some weaker enemies, but as of yet, I have not found a single place where I can grind against waves of weaker enemies to train my team up.  All in all, it's not enjoyable for me. Especially since I feel the need to rest to restore my HP/PP at the end of every single battle. It makes me feel cheap for doing so but I know that if I don't, I'll end up dying in the next one. If you do decide to play this game, I have only one bit of advice for you. Save often. The autosave feature is buggy. I know I've lost some good progress just because the autosave deleted my save files out of nowhere.

Day of Defeat has a long history. I was playing Day of Defeat myself when I was about 16 asa a mod. I played right through to the introduction of Source. I'm 31 now and haven't played in years until I picked it up again in a sale.  The beauty is, you go right back into it. You remember instantly how it works because it is so naturally designed, if the two words can be put together. Even the abundance of Orange maps on the servers these days show how good the base gameplay is outside of the World War II setting. It doesn't matter if the map is all orange, or if the server is all Avalanche, because the base game is still fantastic to play.   Every role can play it's part. Teamwork is needed to hold certain positions but maverick behaviour can sometimes seal the deal and is often needed for the last flag. Mental strategy is involved, psychological warfare in a small shooter. Let's push left, when we actually want to go right. Tie everyone down in one direction, then storm up the middle. Sneak a wild card in near their spawn and tie them down trying to find him, meanwhile everyone capture the other flags.   Every single match has a million dynamic things going on like this which change case by case based on the people involved, the atmosphere of the server, etc. The experience in all is a fantastic one and if you have never tried it, I can't recommend it enough.

This game...I’m shocked as to how badly they screwed up a re-release of a classic tower defense game. If you're familiar with the original dungeon defender, then a lot of things will seem very familiar, if a little unintuitive at first.  However if you’re new to this series, then I feel really bad – you are thrown into a public tavern without going through any tutorial (which the original had) and you automatically start out with the MONK, a class that according its description in the original game is designed for advanced players…but you wouldn’t know that because the developers had the genius idea of not including ANY tooltips in this re-release. Tooltips and simple descriptions of the classes were in the original version – they can’t be that hard to implement (unless there are limitations to this mobile port).  This game needs a lot of work before I can recommend it, especially for newer players.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Short version: Don't buy, for two reasons.  1) Choices don't matter, the final outcome will always be the same; 2) The story has no ending.  Longer version: In the end you will feel mislead and backstabbed by Telltale Games for saying 'choices matter'. No matter your choices, the final outcome will always be the same. Most of the time, choices have no impact at all. The scarce moments that you had the feeling your choices did matter are very misleading. They didn't. The outcome of the main storyline will stay the same. You only get to choose if you like to end the story with person A or person B. No matter what, person A and person B will die next episode anyway and had no impact on the main storyline in the meanwhile.  So you might accept the fact that choices don't matter much and hope for it to be more like a good movie. Wrong again. Its more a TV series then a movie, as the game has no ending. You will need to buy the next season, and perhaps even more, to get to the end of this story. No one knows if that'll be 2, 3 or more seasons. They'll probally milk it as long as possible. In my opinion thats never okay for a game, but it would perhaps be a little accaptable if the storyline was extremly good. But suprise! The story isn't good. Its a mediocre story of which you'll know the outcome before you'll reach the end.

This is definitely one of the best games in the Assassin's Creed series and an absolute classic. The gameplay has aged decently. Combat isn't as good as later entries and at times Ezio seems to jump in a completely different direction to what you wanted, but overall it's a fairly solid experience. The story is the strongest point here, and the protagonist, Ezio Auditorie, is arguably the best in the entire series. The events of the game take place starting from his birth until he is about 40 years old, and you will notice him mature greatly as the story progresses. There's also a lot of different cities and areas to explore, my personal favourite being Venice.  Try to get the game in a sale if you can, but I strongly recommend this to anyone who wants to get into or already likes Assassin's Creed.

I honestly don't understand why this game has such a mass of negative reviews. Most of them aren't even about faults with the game and are rather technical issues to do with either outdated drivers or issues with resolution on immensly uncommercial monitors, I mean what do these people expect from a WiiU port anyways?  PROS: - Great atmosphere and pacing - Combat is fantastic, guns are slow but powerful, melee feels brutal - Graphics are amazing considering it is a WiiU port, no issues with resolution or fuzziness from me - Lots of missions with a semi-interetsing plot  - You feel weak, which makes you scared. Most zombie games make you OP to the point that a crowd of 50 zombies is nothing, in this game coming across more thatn 2 at a time is terrifying. Running from a crowd of 11 zombies after failing to land a molotov and then running into a local shop, barricading the door with planks and frantically grabbing supplies and loading your weapons before the zombies break through gives you a true rush of fear - Fast loading times  CONS: - Story is not that interesting, just engaging enough - Mission areas should be much more open, it would be truly magnificent if you could walk around an entire london neighbourhood and explore shops and flats while collecting supplies (if this had originally come to PC maybe this could have been, but i guess small areas is all the WiiU could manage)  Overall this game is 100% worth the buy if you like zombie/horror games, although i would recommend getting it on a sale - £3.79 is a steal for such a nice little game

I was a (very) early adopter for Overture and had a lot of fun playing for 20-minute stretches here and there initially, but the difficulty and general pointlessness got old pretty quickly and I ended up shelving the game. Now that I have a couch-gaming setup, I was eager to inaugurate it with something that wasn't going to be too twitchy (wireless lag being what it is) and decided to boot up Overture again to see what improvements had been made in the eight months or so since I last played it.  And that's when I found out that in order to upgrade my Knight, I'd first need to unlock all twenty-something other characters, many of which I have no interest whatsoever in using due to their playstyles. Given my gold collection rates, it seems like this would translate to roughly 20-30 minutes of farming per unlock, with very little chance of making it past the second floor with my un-upgraded starting characters in the meantime. In other words, the only reason to play Overture at this point is to collect gold to unlock other characters to collect gold to unlock other characters... etc. For 12+ hours before you can even start progressing through the floors.  I hope this restriction will be rolled back in a near-future update, because frankly, if I were eligible for a refund I'd have done it in a heartbeat. WTF, Black Shell?

One of the best games of all time, and I cannot believe how I loved this game. I first played it on PS3 back in 2014 and was horrified by how many times I would get lost. However, I must have been stupid! I strongly advise you buy this game, and in my opinion, it is pure gold. While the boss fights could use some work, the game is ultimately still a great experience. This is not exactly something I would call open world, but because it is such a small map it is absolutely CRANKED with detail! There are really nice riddles about the environment in several locations on the map, and figuring those out is one of the best parts of Arkham Asylum. The combat is stellar as you may have heard before, with countless games trying to imitate it but not always reaching their full potential. Well, to say the least, this is one of the most fun combat systems I have ever messed around with. There are gadgets at your disposal which severely impact the flow of combat, in both good ways and bad - depending on how you use them. The story is insanely awesome, and the graphics have CERTAINLY aged well. The story goes that you have captured Joker, and obviously ♥♥♥♥ never goes as it should be. Joker is absolutely insane here and is about to... well, I will let you find that out for yourselves. This is a great experience and in some ways better than City, in most ways better than Origins, and story-wise better than Knight. 9/10 from me. Boss fights could be better, but it is a load of fun, and the story is great for any Batman fan to enjoy!   Absolutely recommended!

Overall this is a fairly decent game. I personally wish that the devs spent more time on the character art (its honestly pretty bad) especially when they've showed off such detailed and interesting backgrounds throughout the game. The devs also beat you over the head with their rules of magic. They make sure to point out at every opportunity that something is powered or created using magic. They go into such depth about random clocks and carriages that it suspends the narrative to circle-jerk over their own created universe. As far as VN's go, An Octave Higher also lacks many of the bells and whistles that other VN's have such as gesture controls, smart skipping and as far as I can tell, branching story lines based on choice.  Now, on the positive side (and please bear in mind that I am only 25% of the way through). The story is good enough for what you pay. You take on the perspective of 3 separate and very different characters. Each one offers an interesting perspective on the brewing class-war, and each offers up their own unique feel and backstory. As far as choice, there hasn't been much, but I believe that the story is fine without it at this point. The world is interesting enough, the conflict (while slow to emerge) is an archetypal classic, and I am truly interested to read more and more into the story to see how life develops for the main characters.  7.5/10 Get it on sale and it's worth every penny.  EDIT: I have now played through the majority of the endings and this game is incredible. There is an entire second act that has to be discovered before you can fully experience the true story of this game. That story is engrossing, gritty and can end a few different ways (Although i personally don't prefer the true ending). I was wrong about the bells and whistles, the game automatically skips all previously read text, pauses at choices whenever they appear and there are a TON of them. The endings are truly different endings, not just 'slightly changed' outcomes (looking at you ME3), and each one is either an 'awe' or 'woah' moment.   After having finished this game through and through (still working towards one missing ending) I have to give this game props as well as upgrade its Llama rating.  8.5/10 Great game all-around and a wonderful story (if you choose the right paths)
