This review is based off my experience being a casual player with the Recruiter's Pack. I am a fan of the source material, but I will try to avoid any jargon where possible.  SUMMARY: Good FPS game, limited maps, likeable ability system, good community.  First thing to keep in mind while playing this game is that it is in Early Access so there will be problems every so often and you will see glitches. I have had some lag problems and issues while being connected to servers such as the server kicking me and keeping me in a frozen version of the map. The last one doesn't happen that often, but that's the nature of Early Access so deal with it.  The gamemodes are fairly standard for a competitive FPS game with some slight changes to fit the setting. You have TDM (Team attempts to reach a certain number of kills, players can revive), Demolition (Attack/defend two sites with/from explosives, no revives) and Terminal Conquest (Capture the flag, get to summon a customisable mech tank to help you every so often). All the game modes are fun to play, some more so than others depending on your play style.  At the moment there are two ways to join a match on a server; Quick Dive and another one I can't recall the name of at the time of this review. You will find 99% of players in Quick Dive as that is where most of the content is. In the other mode, one team is cloaked and tries to kill the other using their melee weapon only. This mode is only accessible when you're Level 7 and is currently in Alpha. When you join the lobby for this mode, it's fairly rare that you'll find a complete pair of teams.  The maps are well designed and more importantly well-balanced. By well-balanced I mean there are camping spots on both sides of open areas, a good mix of corridors and choke points forcing players into conflicts and the combination of map features mean that certain weapons work best in certain areas of the map. However there are a limited number of maps, so if you play for a couple of hours chances are you'll cycle through them. A new map was added recently, but more needs to be added to provide a good amount of content.  One of the most distinguishing features of the game is the use of abilities. Each character a player can choose has an ability. Some of the abilities can be shared with two other players in close proximity, for example sharing camouflage. This sharing aspect forces players to huddle together and cooperate to an extent. This is a welcoming change to the FPS genre where most focus on the single player.  Now to the unwelcome truth of F2P (Free to play) games; how it makes money. I haven't digged deeply into how you apply the special currency, but from the outset it seems it can only be applied to aesthetic items rather than functional ones. As a result, P2P (Pay to Play) players look pretty but unless they're several levels above you, it should be a pretty even playing field. Conversely, it does take quite a few matches to unlock new weapons and new parts to improve accuracy, stability etc. I didn't unlock my first shotgun until Level 6.  Finally, I'd like to make a note about the in-game community. I have several hours on record and in all of the matches I only heard one accusation of hacking and no racial slurs. A nice change of things. Keep in mind though, your experience may be very different from my own.  In summary, the current state of development is fun to play and a good way to kill a couple of hours. However the game is far from complete. There are a limited number of well made maps, the servers do need some work and it takes some grinding to get some weapons. However with regards to the final point, it means a good portion of the people you deal with will have the same or similar weapons. Also, the ability systems works best with people working together so it helpes reduce the number of lone wolves.

This game is basically the best (in my opinion) MMO. I came to RIFT from WoW, and I'll say... I'm not going back. Leveling is slow, but you can speed it up easily. There are many storylines to follow, so many you almost lose yourself. There are also 2 bonus leveling systems to get at 50, one for PvP, one for both PvP and PvE. And, hey. Free expansions. Not like you have to buy anything to go past level 10 or some other crap. I give it a 9/10. Here's why:  Pros -  1) Guilds, groups, raids are cross-faction (can be a con for some) 2) Massive class diversity 3) Such a custom character creation, you are literally a unique character 4) Graphics are outstanding 5) PvP is intense, balanced, fun 6) Dungeons are amusing 7) Can mentor down to join/help friends (up to 5 levels lower, however) 8) RIFT store is... Amazing. You may have to buy some stuff to unlock FULL functionality (Auction House), but if you really like the game, you will invest the money for Credits, or the platinum for REX  Cons -  1) Not as big as it should be 2) A LOT of currencies to keep track of  That is my review. Tropico 4  Story - Well, I haven't really finished it yet, but from the first 3 missions I played, lemme say it's really awesome. 'Well how come you have like 30 hrs and still haven't finished?' I like replaying the first mission and just playing around in the god / sandbox mode ;P 10/10  Storyline Length - Erm... I haven't finished the... idk, a couple days, maybe?  AI - Well, I'd count the tiny people as AI. Each individual has their own life. One can choose to be a doctor, a soldier, farmer, miner, construction worker, anything! Even a burglar, and a rebel, so watch yourself. You can view their thoughts (like in Rollercoaster Tycoon 3) The only flaw I see with the AI is that the construction workers take a bit long to even get to the building you want to build Like. Quite long. 9/10  Experience - Mate, I haven't even finished the game and this is already one of my favourite games! It's very cool, I recommend you try it out. Even if you don't think it's the game for you, please, the demo is like right there. You'll get sad because a tornado ruined your island tho ;-; 10/10  Graphics - I thought the graphics were bad. But then I turned them to the max and Jesus Christ it is one of the most detailed games ever made. I wonder what Tropico 5 is like Cx 10/10  Sounds / Voice Acting - Again, the amount of detail in everything. Most civilians (even the children) have their own seperate voices, the soundtrack makes the game lively, and you can even hear the sound of traffic and all. The game is just. Perfect. 10/10  Controls - Takes a while to get used to, because literally noone played the tutorial for this game (or is it just me? xD) But once you got the hang of it, you can build an entire airport in less than 5 seconds. Not exaggerating. 10/10  Overall - 10/10  I usually don't really like these kind of games where you manage your own thing. In this case, it's an island. After trying out the demo on my 360, I decided to buy this game and I love it so much. Go pick this game up. It is an absolute masterpiece of a game.

HANDS DOWN the BEST action RPG I have EVER played. The game is driven by the storyline. You do end up doing a lot of 'kill these monsters and collect these items from them' quests, but also many interesting ones. Each small quest adds a bit to the plot. The characters have original remarks, wants and needs, and story behind why they want these things. But there are also boss monsters, which usually aren't hack-n-slash. The game entices you to use your surroundings or alternate methods. Another HUGE feature of the game is the fact that, as you progress, you come to key decision points that, while possibly not blatantly obvious at the time (yet they may also be quite obvious, as characters may point it out) decide your fate and others' fates. Your actions for certain scenarios and how you handle them will decide who lives, who dies, how characters think of you, and what the world ultimately comes to. There are active story (plot) changes due to this. A MUST-HAVE! Cannot wait for #2!

The first time I saw UnEpic I thought to my self, not another one of those retro looking games. That was one of the major points why I waited so long to pick that game up. But then the Steam Sale came and for a few bucks I took the risk, because everyone and their mother praises this game and they are right.  Well for the most part at least. I mentioned already the graphics, but they are not that bad as they look in those previews. When you get a bit used to the look and feel of the game, they are rather pretty and make an excellent job to convey the 'dungeon feel' of the game.  The biggest problem I have with this game are the controls. They are not fluent and every now and then you are not fighting the game, but the controls of it (I played with a xBox 360 controller). After a while you know how the charakter handles itself, but even after close to 20 hrs of gametime, there are situations where I almost rage quitted the game altogether. The Moments were triggered mostly by two situations:  1. The Target Lock will not work for whatever reason. If you have, for example, a flying enemy and he is high up. You need the target Lock function to shoot him with magic or arrows. If the enemy is out of reach for your melee attacks and you cannot lock him on, you are forced to jump around and hope to agro it into melee range.  2. The Platforming is for the most part enjoyable, but here comes the controls you have to get used to. You have almost no control over you character mid jump and without a impulse in either side you just jump up. So to jump sideways, you need to get a bit of momentum and then jump. The margin for error in the platforming is rather small, but that is personell preference if that is good or bad. It only infuriated me in the later part of the game, when it took way to long to make that jump.  The pacing of the game is rather good, and except of the late game I enjoyed it quite a bit. Starting with 'The Halls' it felt forcefully drawn out. There are two Enemy types there that annoyed the hell out of me. The Harlekin mage that transmogrifies your drawn weapon in a joke weapon that does 1 dmg, if he hits you with his range Attack and the blobs. If the blobs make contact to you, they get you a debuff named 'slippery hands'. While this is active, you will drop your weapon. I have no problems with strategic situations, but if you have hallways filled with blobs, it is more a test of Patience to finish this section. But all of this is a thing of the past when you start the 'final level'. I do not want to spoil anything story wise, but this is a cakewalk and way to easy.  Even if I only complained up to this point, that does by no means mean that the game is bad. It is good. The music is enjoyable and compliments the feeling of the game and the situation at hand and never felt out of place. This game has a ton of replay-ability with all the secrets, challenges and different character builds and difficulty setting. Apparently it also a multiplayer mode, but I did not test that.  Just buy it.

app_name: 7 Grand Steps, Step 1: What Ancients Begat
review_text: This is a strangely compelling - and strangely moving - game. It's about a mysterious coin-op machine which represents the progression of time and a family's struggle to advance - and sometimes just to survive - the turning wheels of history. Their story is told mostly with the interaction of a few abstract icons, and the occasional snippet of text, and you'd think it would be a rather cold and abstract experience. It didn't feel that way to me, though. The struggle to find the best way to provide for your children's future has real emotional weight, for example. I still can't really say why I like this so much, but it's a totally different experience to most computer games. It seems very simple at first, and the complexity only creeps up on you very slowly, but as it does, the whole experience seems to grow with it. Highly recommended.

There is a lot of people that hate this game, and I know why:  TDD was a fad. The game became popular because of one thing, and one thing only: it was scary. Tens of thousands, if not hundreds of thousands likely bought this game because they wanted to get scared; they wanted a joyride complete with overwhelming fear. I don't blame them. The game is pretty scary, but here's the thing:  It seems that most people do not understand that Amnesia, and the games before it, were not just haunted-house amusement park rides like they like to act like it is. No, it's a lot more than that. The game has thick atmosphere, superb pacing and storytelling backed by a very dark story that keeps you curious from beginning and even to the end. To be able to truly appreciate TDD, you need to look further than the scare factor; it is not the only thing that made the game great. Guess what the Chinese Room capitalised on? The story. They wanted to tell a story, much like TDD did. MFP was much more of a psychological experience; the game did not need to scare you with darkness or spooky monsters, all it needed was an incredibly wicked story. This was fulfilled. I expected an atmospheric, thought-provoking horror experience that would keep me on the edge and this is exactly what the game did.  But what did everyone else expect? Probably something entirely different. They probably just wanted to get scared. Most of the fanbase is filled with people that appreciated TDD for nothing more than its scare factor, and therefor this is all they had expected from MFP. They are probably the kind of people that either skipped or skimmed through most of TDD's note readings, not givng a ♥♥♥♥ about the backstory whatsoever. They just wanted to get to the next 'scare.' The bad reception for this game purely comes from delusioned expectations, is what I believe. Horror is a much deeper genre than you'd think. Read up.

UPDATED REVIEW:  As fu cked up as this sounds, it's a LOT more fun on the second playthrough (TINY TINA! OMGOMGOMGOMGOMG HI)  Pros: 1. It's more Borderlands - if you loved BL1 and BL2, you'll at least 'be OK with' BL:TPS  2. F ucking Torgue. EXPLOSIONS!!!!!  3. It's an interesting story and new material, 'at least on paper'. Bits of the story (at least so far) really don't pan out that well, especially in regards to things already made explicit in BL1 or BL2. Kind of a problem when you want it to be a 'pre-sequel'.  4. The 'Grinder' machine really takes the bite out of the (fairly intrinsic) grinding for gear. At least on some level you're making progress, even if you're getting a bunch of garbage drops.  5. Aside from boss fights, the game (thankfully) lets you reconstruct and head right back into an ongoing quest, rather than resetting everything and making you start over.    Cons: 1. It's the most Australian thing to have ever been Australia'ed by Australians in Australia. Be prepared to: A) never meet a non-australian NPC that wasn't already 'supposed' to be a certain way (Moxxie, Jack, etc.) except in one or two 'hidden' missions, and (strangely enough) the entirety of the endgame. It seems pretty clear what '2kAustralia' was and was not involved in. B) put up with a slew of slang, jokes, euphemisms and phrases you've never heard, nor are likely to hear outside Australia. This includes limp-wristed jokes like 'Moxxie's Up Over Bar' (instead of 'Down Under', a euphemism for Australia) C) f ucking rhyming cockney slang with a grating Australian accent. ('Davis Pickle', an apparent attempt to cash in on Tiny Tina ... by making you want to jam screwdrivers in your ears.) D) be cured of your previously held 'f uck, aussie accents are sexy!' condition.  2. The new O2 / Gravity Slam mechanic is just as annoying as it is innovative or original. It can be fun to 'butt slam' enemies... but it also means dealing with weapon recoil and accuracy issues while tracking targets in 3D space (as opposed to 2D side-to-side, front-toback in BL1/2). There are also 'glitchy' situations where the gravity slam will deal no damage ... say if you don't hold down 'C' long enough, or if you land directly on an enemy).  3. The maps feel cramped, and you quickly fall into a routine of grinding for gear to quest, and questing til you need to grind for gear. It could just be nostalgia, but it doesn't feel as 'large' as BL2 in terms of places to go and things to do. That's pre-DLC BL2, by the way.  4. It's grindy (duh, it's Borderlands). The quests will move faster than your gear / level, making for some frustrating encounters and a moderate-to-large amount of farming for drops & xp. Conversely, there's also the issue that a large number of the quests allow you to 'speedrun' - that is, most of my first-time completions came not from me clearing the area and strolling to the finish line... rather from just dead sprinting to the net warp portal or quest objective and clicking it before I died.

If you're hungry, this is the place for you  Hans' Dutch Apple Pie Recipe  You need a Pie pan, a fork, and some sort of Apple peeling device -----------------------------------Ingredients:-------------------------------------------------    5 apples (Granny smith, crimson crisp, etc.) (Decently sized)    A stick or two of butter, depending on if you made your own crust    Some cinnamon (To Taste)    Some sugar (To Taste)    Pie Crust     3/8 cup of flour  ---------------------Recipe----------------------------------------------------------------------    Preheat oven to 225 Degrees Celsius (437 Farenheit)    Peel, Core and Cut all 5 apples in small cubes or thin slices (Soak in water with a bit of lemon juice to prevent browning)    Mix together a stick of butter, some oatmeal, a third cup of sugar, a pinch of brown sugar, a dash of vanilla extract, and a little under 1/3 cup of flour. Mix it together until you end up with a crumble-y texture.     Precook pie crust at 225C for 4-5 minutes    Mix sugar, cinnamon, and apple slices    Pour apple mixture into crust    apply crumble on top of apples, they should be piling over the top of the pie pan    Bake at 225 degrees C for 20-25 minutes    Add whipped cream or vanilla sauce to your hearts desire.   Note: I prefer to mix apples in a colander, then move them to a bowl after they've been sufficently sugared, so that they don't soak in the apple-sugar-cinnamon juice for too long. Buying this game gives you two versions, the original and a rebuilt redux version; I played the redux version so all opinions expressed here are based on that version.  I bought this game months ago in the sale and by the time I came to play it I had completely forgotten everything about it so I started playing it with absolutely no idea what to expect. This is an excellent way to appreciate the narrative; throughout the game you find notes and audio clips by exploring and solving puzzles which allow you to gradually piece together the story.  The levels are quite large and intricate. The visuals are pretty good and the voice acting is excellent. The exploration part of the game is great and some of the puzzles are great however many of the puzzles are overly obscure. But overall I am still giving this a thumbs up as the story more than makes up for having to look up a walk through for the difficult bits.  I did encounter a number of bugs whilst playing. None of them were significant but I am going to list in detail as the developers seem very engaged on the community hub and responsive to feedback.  - The game switches to Polish every couple of times I launch the game. - The texture detail reverts back to medium every time I launch the game. This is particularly infuriating as one of the puzzles near the start of the game requires    you to read a number off the back of a plate but the number is not visible at medium texture detail.   - Most of the achievements don't work. - The trading cards only drop when playing the original version not the redux version -   The miner's vigil projector wouldn't trigger after lighing the correct number of candles.   -   The piano won't play for the church projector, This is possibly because I saved my game whilst the tuning animation was running.   -   After triggering the plaque in Pinwheel village, I was stuck in the room with the red chair by the fire. I had to go back to the cage and travel to a different area and after that I was unable to jump.

I've had this game for awhile, but wasn't ever able to play the original version on my old PC. However, having played the redux and finishing the main story; I am left utterly confused, and rather heart broken.  An inexpressible experience.

I'm going to point out some pretty specific stuff in this game instead of just going broad or a general view of the game.   The Pros: -Very Easy and Casual Gameplay -Cash currency is earnable in game -Progessing in the game is quick -Equipment is easily accessible  -Not pay to be the best  -Subclass system is great -Friendly society -Great artwork -Skill animations are fancy -A good amount of classes to select from   The Cons: -Typical MMORPG  -Slow paced battle system  The cons are definitely something that would change someone's mind about playing this game, but if you ignore those two concepts of the game, you'll get to experience what Aura Kingdom truly has to offer.

The scale and scope of this epic game are incredible. I'll gladly conceed that this game isn't for everyone. It demands a heavy investment of time, but the rewards are so great for all of that. You start the game with a basic ship, enough currency to basically last your first 15 minutes (if you are frugal) and set loose in a galaxy. What will you do? Join the military? Become a trader? Work up to a station and trade? a pirate?  The good news is you can do all these things!  If you are looking for a casual game to kill 15 minutes while you wait for your bus, look elsewhere. If you want to spend months slowly building an empire to rival the largest corporations in the galaxy, with a fleet of freighters that number in the hundreds, and a personal amada that can jump into any sector and lay waste to everything it encounters, all while feeling a bit gushy over the amazing graphics, then THIS title is for you.

review_text: LOVE IT

Im a long time Deus Ex fan, and I consider this the best one yet. This is what Deus Ex is all about. It is badass, cinematic and atmospheric. Gameplay and story are top notch, and the environments and dialogue are great. The world feels alive, and Jensen is harder than ever.  There are some minor issues like lip sync glitching from time to time but thats really nitpicking, and the previous games were not better in this regard. The inventory management couldve been a little snappier and polished. There is also some controversy regarding the microtransaction content thats avaialble, which is sad because the game deserves praise. Id go so far as to say this is the best FPS Ive ever played, and Ive played games for 25+ years.  The Original Deus Ex was cool when it released, and still is in a retro kinda way, but compared with MK, it just doesnt hold up anymore.  You do need to have a decent rig to handle it though.  Also, I would not recommend the Season Pass at this time, since all of the DLC made my game crash. I refunded that without issue though.

Plagued with mild performance problems, made harder to excuse by sub-par overall visuals, and more severe mouse control issues - forced mouse acceleration, too high sensitivity even on the lowest setting, x and y axis have different sensitivities - and none of these issues can presently be fixed. My experience has been that mouse control in Mankind Divded feels altogether dreadful in its current state, and many others seem to be finding it the same. As far as I can tell, it's a pretty good game, just one that launched a bit too soon. Right now I give it a wait. Hopefully by the end of next month some major patches will have dropped and I can flip this to a positive review.

I liked the game, but it's just too short.  The devs have commented on other negative reviews that reflect my view. And basically their answer is that we should grind the same content over and over to reach an arbitrary 'maximum level' with no real incentive.  There is a bottomless pit wave mode. And challenges pertaining to each zone. But personally I'd prefer alot more story content to play through. It's just my personal taste, but I don't enjoy repeating maps just to complete challenges. If the devs had decided to go with an act system, adding acts after release, then the game would be alot better in my opinion. To me it just seemed like a first act playthrough and I'd liked to have seen another 2 or 3 acts.  Outfits, which are designed to be some kind of build changing mechanic, aren't very numerous. 5 I think at the time of my runthrough, just after launch. So there is no real replay value as far as builds go.  All in all, the game is fun for about 10-20 hours. But I wouldn't recommend it simply because I don't think it's good value at full price.

If you are thinking of buying this game, there are only 3 simple questions you need to ask yourself. 1: Do I like First-Person Shooters?  2: Do I like games that have no stealth elements? 3: Can I accept some (not all) unclear 'points of no return' in each level, in a game with many valuable secrets?   If you said 'Yes' to those 3 questions, then you should buy this game without a doubt!   (Nr 3 is a minor inconvenience for most people and can easily be fixed in the future without much effort or affecting level design. An easy fix would be if they allowed you to tick an option in the Options Menu which - if ticked 'yes' - puts a Portal at the end of each level that takes you back to the start of the level without restarting it or all spawns, for you to find your missing secrets without trouble before continuing on with the next level.)  [NO SPOILERS! Short review of basic information]:  This is easily the best First-Person Shooter in years. Besides the basics (which for some developers nowadays aren't even basic, since they ignore optimising etc) like that the game is running incredible well and the gameplay feeling amazing, it also manages to be one of the best games in a long time by its long list gameplay features.   I run it capped at 120 FPS, but otherwise I have between 100-140 FPS on Ultra settings at 1080p, the big difference coming from switching between no enemies at all to full on combat. Will mention that I'm not using any blur effects except DOF, since it makes the screen worse for less performance, which is especially noticable and actually affects your gameplay when you move alot while also panning with the mouse and you can't see crap. This is on a GTX 970 GPU with an Intel i5-6600k CPU, 16GB RAM and Windows 7 Pro OS.   The game also has an incredible depth EVERYWHERE you look! Level design, secrets, tons of different interesting suit, character, rune and weapon upgrades using different upgrade points (gained themselves from different interesting sources) or Challenges to upgrade them with, diverse enemy types and behavour etc. Easily one of the best games (of any category) in a long time! . . . [NO SPOILERS! TL;DR version]: Containing a list of all gameplay mechanics and non-spoilery info I can remember, each point being explained thoroughly. No story info at all. (If you have a Dark Souls mentality about all games and think gameplay mechanics are spoilers for you - because you want to go in completely blind - then don't continue)  -Complex level design with multiple paths to explore, paths either diverging from the main path to join back with it close to where you diverged or sometimes being completely alternate routes themselves, ending with you further up the main path.   -Valuable (gameplay affecting) secrets hidden everywhere. Somewhere between 10-20 per level, trying to find all of which will extend the gameplay extensibly. I have 24.1 hours played atm, all of which are only from my first Singleplayer Campaign playthrough (on the last mission now). Haven't touched any other mode in the game yet. This is mostly thanks to the many secrets and how many of them are very well hidden, but also a small part to me AFK:ing with the game tabbed down. My biggest time sink into this game applies only to people trying to get 100% of the secrets on the first run, I'll take why at the bottom of the review.  -Large arsenal of different weapons, all of which (except the pistol, a shotgun, the chainsaw and the BFG) having 2 different modes that can be unlocked by getting physical mods which you put on the weapon and can (if both are acquired) swapped between with a single key press, that often completely changes how the weapon behaves and it's area of speciality. Sidenote: Each type of ammo (except rockets, saw fuel and BFG) is shared between 2 weapons.  -Largest amount of different ways to upgrade your character I've seen in a campaign.  1: Every weapon can unlock 2 different mods that changes how the weapon works drastically (both mods can be obtained and switched between on the fly by pressing 'R').  2: Both mods on each weapon have their own unique upgrade options, upgraded by using Weapon Points gained in many different ways (cleansing a room of monsters, discovering a secret area, completing each one of the 3 Challenges you gain per new mission etc)  3: The last upgrade in each weapon mod-upgrade section can't be bought and can only be gained through completing a mod-specific 'Mastery Challenge', which becomes available after buying all other upgrades on that weapon-mod.  4: The suit you wear can be upgraded in 2 different trees, both using different points, none of which being the Weapon Points. One is using what we'll call Suit Points, gained from a computer chip inside the Red Armor Suits of the already dead and immobile soldiers hidden throughout the levels. Used in 5 different specialised trees, each containing 3 unique bonuses based on the name of each upgrade tree.  5: The other Suit upgrade (let's call it A-Cell Upgrade) uses something called Argent Cells (big orbs found in big rectangular chests) which lets you upgrade either your max Health, Armor or Ammo (for all weapon types).  6: You can get Upgrade Runes by finding hidden Rune Challenges, which tasks you with a challenge with a specific weapon, having infinite tries (which you'll need) to complete them. You can equip from 1 (at the start) up to 3 runes at the same time. Each changing how a gameplay mechanic behaves. For example: Glory Kills, Equipment (grenades), Pickups, Ammo drops, Armour drops etc.  7: Lastly, each Rune can be upgraded one level by completing a unique challenge written on them while having them equipped.   Rune note/tip: A favourite Rune combo atm combines one of the last runes you get - which makes you have infinite Ammo as long as you have over 100 Armor (75 if upgraded) - combined with the upgraded Equipment Power rune - which makes one of your grenades generate armor (the more demons caught within it's blast radius, the more armor you gain) - and Glory Kill Armor rune - which makes demons drop armor when Glory Killed.  -Diverse enemy types with different weaknesses, strengths, mobility, projectile types, attack patterns, specials etc.   1: Specials being everything from Stealth to Endlessly Summoning more creatures until killed, or a dangerous and swift Charge which if dodged gives room for you attacking a small weakspot, a dangerous and even more swift charge that CAN'T be dodged so the creature must be killed before or while charging etc.   2:Attack patterns are different between enemies, but they also have some enemies switching patterns on the fly, like changing between acting and attacking like a coward or being inquisitive or being aggresive depending on the situation (Imps), Idiots barely able to walk in your direction or swarming you (basic enemies), No-ducks-given monsters that will chase you to the end of the world (Hell Knights, Barons etc) or monsters staying back for their own reasons, etc.  [End note]: For people trying to get 100% secret completion on their first playthrough: The biggest time factor (and this is my ONLY - smaaall - con for this game) is caused by many levels (not all, but most) having unclear 'points of no return', which becomes a even greater problem because there is no set or average number of how many 'points of no return' there are per level (I've seen from 1 to around 8).   If you're trying like me to find all of the secrets on your first run, then this will increase your time quite alot. The fear of overstepping each 'point of no return' and not even having the safety of knowing that when you've crossed it, you got nothing more to worry about, makes you have to comb through each point of the level extra carefully, rather than skimming through each point until you reach the end of the level where you would have been able to check if you've missed something and get it.

One of the best horror games I've played in quite a while. It starts off seeming like a run-of-the-mill haunted house adventure but quickly becomes something different. It does a fantastic job of keeping you off-balance and really captures the feel of being trapped in a nightmare. It manages to be disturbing without relying on gore. The game looks insanely good and while the voice acting isn't the best, there's so little of it that it doesn't really detract much from the experience. About 4-5 hours to complete, if you take the time to explore every nook and cranny. Just about the perfect length for this type of game, imo. There are several possible endings and the chapter select option after you beat it the first time makes it easy to find the things missed and correct any mistakes you might have made. I definitely enjoyed my time with this and recommend it to other fans of the genre.

IT'S THE SAME GAME, so I would rather this game and this is why:   Story    Gameplay: 8-10 This game has a built-in radar system that detects moving enemies. It also has a cool feature to highlight who your friends are (yellow) and just ordinary players (green). The mechanics of the game run fairly smoothly on the lowest res and graphics and also the rolling mechanic is a dream if you want to relocate efficiantly and quickly and as a followup, you can lean and blindfire. Graphics: 9-10 It COULD be a little better due to the source-like graphics content. But it is a great look. This game allows you to immerse yourself into the tactical methods to take out your enemy. Replay Value    Overall Rating Value: 9.595-10 This game is a cool game and is JUST LIKE CS. RACE 07 - GTR Evolution Review  *This review is based upon the games DLC packs aswell, there may be mentioned vehicles and tracks not included in the base game.  RACE 07 is a brilliant package and offers a wide range of motorsports, all of which will be covered in this review. It has a wide variety of circuits and a very good championship career mode. There are many racing disciplines including: World Touring Car Championship (Official, Extreme & Classic), GT (Pro, Sport & Club), Swedish Touring Car Championship, Road Cars, Camaro Cup, Radical, Caterham, Formula (F3000, Formula Ford, Formula Masters & Formula 1), and Retro (Max & Cool).   The handling is ultra realistic and has realistic tyre and fuel depletion, which can be increased. There can be up to 36 cars on track at one time. There is a number of championships and has the ability to make your own chamionships up! The menus are much better and are far easier to navigate than GTR 2's.  Overall Rating: 9/10 Must buy for any Sim Racing fans

ARMA 3 is simply an AMAZING game! I am impressed with its attention to detail and the complexity of the AI. I have heard people frown upon Call of Duty and Battlefield, but now that I have played ARMA 3 it is extremely difficult for me to go back to either of those games. review_text: I was enjoying this game and I'll admit, it's very fun.  But you know what's not fun? 'Realm gold' is needed to buy almost EVERYTHING in this game so how do you get realm gold? With real money, of course.  Want a new character? Spend money. Want more bank space? Spend money. Want new character skins? Spend money. Want pets and other cool items? Spend money. Want character slots so you can play as more characters? Spend money.  You get the point. And those things listed above are just a few things that are required with real money but there are more.  I'd play the game more but I personally get annoyed with all of the completely unsubtle cash grabs and that has made me instantly dislike the developers. So I refuse to play and support the game.

Dear Esther isn't so much a conventional video game as it is an emotional and aesthetic journey through the limbo of ones mind. In the vein of walking simulator type experiences, Dear Esther keeps you strictly confined to a set path; without the ability to jump, interact with items, and so forth. The visuals are absolutely stunning, the narration absorbing, and the hauntingly beautiful score spectacular. Dear Esther's story unfolds within the protagonists mind, which after a life altering event is left reeling in limbo. The narrative is the progressive element to the story, with the terrain (visuals), reacting and representing concepts accordingly. Dear Esther is a truly beautiful and emotional experience, and although incredibly short, praise must be given to it's clearly very passionate developers.

Finally got my hands on this game as I'm a big fan of the TV Show. So, here's my review for it so far (reaching 6 hours into it) Final Score - 7 out of 10  Storyline ; ( 6/10 ) The storyline doesn't follow Game Of Thrones stories but it has some parts that will remind you of the TV Show. So you play as 2 different characters, one is a survivor and leader of the Night's Watch.  The other one is an old priest that returns to his city and tries to bring justice into it. So as for the story goes you will get yourself into the character's head, it's pretty fun. Dialogs are general I skip the conversations sometimes but I like the variety of ways you can play this game.  Gameplay ; (7/10) 1) Quests - After you create your character and give him his attributes,stats and class you follow the story that the game has given to you and move along the maps to complete the quests. You can do some side quests that will not take long and kill extra enemies. Nothing to explore really besides finding more quests/enemy and items. As for the equipment you have you can trade it (sell/buy) in merchants in each map. So, there's nothing much to do in this game. 2) Combat - As for the combat goes, if you played Of Orcs and Men and enjoy it you will enjoy the combat in this game as well. You have pause menu where you can choose which abilities to do with each character and then execute it on the enemy. The enemy A.I have different types of weapons/armor so it is challenging.  RPG Elements ; (8.5/10) 1) Skills - Skill Trees with upgrades for each character, and also to the character A.I assistant. 2) Items - variety of weapons to use, and in differnet types as well. Ranged/Closed , Blunt/Cutting , Short blades etc. 3) Equipment - You have 2 weapon sets in this game so you can switch from 1 set to another anytime. Example: [Set One] can be crossbow or heavy axe and [Set Two] can be 2 weapons in hand or sword+shield. You also have body armor (helmet etc) flasks (potions etc) and rings. 4) Character Build - in 4 phases. [Phase 1] Class [Phase 2] Stats [Phase 3] Skill Tree [Phase 4] Strength and Weakness attributes  So combine all of this in this game and you will enjoy your time playing as a badass ruthless warrior with strong weapons and heavy damage, or as the protector of the city with health and dodging abilities, or as a scouting rouge that has stealth moves. (yes, stealth is also a mechanic in this game).  A.I ; ( 6/10 ) 1) Enemy A.I - They are challenging because each has different type of armor and weapon. The come in groups so you will have to combine strategy and tactics to defeat them. 2) Character A.I - You control your A.I (ofcourse because it's a rpg fantasy game) just like in Of Orcs And Men. Each character has different abilities and different tasks. (control the dog of Mors or the soldier of The Priest).  Overall ; If you enjoyed games like Of Orcs and Men and Divinity you will find common combat system like in Orcs and Men and similiar rpg elements like in Divinity. You will enjoy the gameplay, cuz it's also repetetive as you can play in different classes, different builds/skills and making your character be a ruthless heartless ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ or a good 'governor' for the people of Riverspring. But if you've watched the show and expect the characters from the show and storylines from the show, you are to be mistaken. You should give this game a try cuz it's unique and good as it is. Thanks for watching this review.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: They meant to say 'Prepare To Die A Lot. Edition', especially on your first run. The game is truly fun, but it makes you want to rage quit many times. Recommended for the Skilled and/or Patient and/or Insane.

Inversion is your typical 'Take cover and regen' when damaged 3rd person shooter. Weapons will start to advance as you get along, thus standard weapons turn energized etc.  The different part is gravity however, as you get a device capable of manipulating gravity. At first it's the weak (Low Gravity) only allowing you to lift small things, but also enemies to drag them out of cover. Later you can throw cars and charge up using a classic 'Finger of Death' ability on enemies which instagib them. You can also throw lava... Last upgrade being 'High Gravity' allow you to make everything much more heavy. Enemies will be pushed to the ground, or groups can be insta gibbed after you 'Low' lifted them from the ground and then 'High' smash them into the floor turning them into a mighty puddle of goo.  Last but not least, the gravity is major in many areas allowing you to walk on walls, ceilings or fly through the air. It's like space floating just with breathable air and sound :P  The story is also quite interesting, and is a big twist compared to most games. I was sceptical with the game being released from Namco, but the game is overall a good experience.

app_name: Fallout 3 - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: This is a great game. Open world play and the freedom to do what you want. Good or bad, there's plenty there for all. Loads of companions and oodles of quests for whatever way you want to play it. It is quite gory, as a post-apocalyptic world would be, so be prepared for that.

In a nutshell, yes this is superior entry compared to Fallout 3. Though it still has the typical bugginess, FO:NV retains an entry that is more in tune to its old PC roots while using the innovations of the latter. It holds its place as one of the best PC games of all time. FO:NV also had the superior DLC compared to FO3. No matter what price you pay for this, it's worth it 100%.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: In a completely immersive and beautiful setting, Brothers does an incredible job of creating a world and putting you in it. From start to finish, you can feel the emotions of all involved. It's a beautiful tapestry of jaw-dropping environments and seamless gameplay. The gameplay is so cinematic that sometimes you don't even realize you're playing, it just feels like it's moving along as it should.  I highly recommend this game to anyone who loves a moving story, and looking at pretty things.

'Only my weapons understand me.'    If I had known the creators would release a remastered edition of this game I would have simply purchased that one. Make no mistake: I do not mind having played this classic from the end of the '90s I just know that once Postal Redux is released it'll be an immediate purchase for me.   'Let's blow something up.'    This is the original game with it's simplistic (and by today's standards: ugly) graphics. And while the pixelated characters certainly look aged the game's hand drawn levels still look good. In the roughly 2 dozen levels (in various locales such as farm, factory, shopping center, industrial complex, airport etc.) we control the so-called Postal Dude (do note that this Dude appeared before Lebowsky - they are not the same) who causes mayhem and destruction. We can kill everyone - all bystanders and even ostriches (?), but taking innocent lives is not mandatory in order to complete a level. Getting rid of at least 80-90% of the hostile cops is a must, though.   'I regret nothing.'    And this is Postal for you. An isometric shooter with constant action which now has full controller support. Without this I would have definitely given it a thumbs-down. I do not know how its 'remake', the so-called 'Hatred' fares but I do know this game's content is not that extremely violent by default. Also, the Postal Dude's one-liners certainly create a funny atmosphere. If you remember playing this under Windows 95 / 98 then this is a great purchase for you.
Lives up to all expectations I had and more. The game itself is extraordinarily fun, the art is beautiful, and the story comes together in ways I did not imagine. I can't wait for part 2.

This game is really great. A great story. Great graphics. Great battle system.  The best part is that you can choose how you want to play, without remorse. For example, instead of being a decker/hacker, I was able to sleuth my way through many missions with detective work. What I mean is, there are many ways to complete each mission, which was awesome!  Usually in an RPG you should never put points in charisma, but this game actually used my charisma for more story info. I also replayed charisma choices, picking the non-charisma choices just fine. Most of the time a charisma choice meant more money, which is something I don't go for, but the extra story helps make it fun and desirable.  Here and there I just wanted to kill stuff, but in the ShadowRun world, karma is most often what drives you forward. So there is often no way to just kill stuff. No hack and slash here! Killing everybody may sometimes be a possible way to complete a mission, but its often not the most interesting!

This game is awesome. You body slam a Metal Gear and slice enemies into tiny pieces. They understand how to make a good action title, and they know how to make you power trip so hard that you'll walk into work the next day and tell your boss exactly what you think of him.  This will get you fired, more than likely, but then you'll have more time to play games - so that's a win win. Congrats on growing a pair. You're now a Platinum gamer.  (I originally played this on PS3. I later got it on Steam to play again, though I never actually ran my playthrough - but I did have it running at 1080p/60fps on Windows 7, which is why I got it in the first place. Fast forward to recently: I wanted to play it again so I fired it up - Windows 10 has an issue with this game. I can't get it to run over 30fps, and I'm not having any luck finding a reliable fix. So keep that in mind.)

I'm still playing Episode 1 now, and I've played about 23 hours so far.   Most people are mainly complaining about 2 things.  1. Forced Online Feature - I haven't experienced any disconnection problems during 23 hours of play. If your network system is stable, don't mind about the disconnection. Even if the disconnection happens, it won't be a big problem cause the game allows 8 manual save files and 8 auto save files.  2. 6 separated episodes - It's a controversial subject, and some people even call it 'scam', but I think this new system is kind of an innovative attempt. It might not be perfect for now, but it'll gradually help to restrain illegal copyright infringements, and allocate financial resources to the rightful owners so they could make better series next time. As a completed season, I don't feel it's overpriced. I think it's totally worth for $50. I don't mind how they sell it. I'll pay for Full Pakage, DLC, 7 Episodes or whatever, as long as the game is solid.  For me, this is the most underrated game in 2016. If you're the one who likes to solve puzzles on your own, has a photographic memory and a good sense of direction, and also has Achievement OCD, this is your game.   Every time you open a new door, you have to remember everything. Where the wrench is, where the screwdriver is, where the NPCs are, which way they move, what's behind this room, how they work. Replan your assassination route based on those objects, people, barriers, shortcuts, different smuggling locations, different gadgets, clothes. It won't work as you planned at first. There will be restrictions, floors you can't go with your current disguise, it would be hard to get certain tools cause it's too far or there are too many guards, so you'll have to replan your assassination route from the start. The score varies from 50k to 250k, and you can get better scores as your kills become faster, more precise, and more quite. That's how this game should be played. Quality of the game really depends on how you play it. I bet those people on the leaderboard might have spent their best time with the game.   But beware, The game might become repetitive and boring if you don't have any clues, can't remember where they are, how the targets should be killed, or just rely on Youtube guides. if you're an impatient person and just want to shoot and kill, this is not your game.

Once you look behing the mechanic of the game and see that the KI for example is really stupid and the  mission goals are not really challenging,the game lost all its charme and it just made me rush the inmates instead of dodging/hiding since it comes out to the same result all in all but its way faster. If you're expecting an open world, Assassin's Creed-style game, this isn't the game for you. If you want an intense stealth game that tests your patience and creativity, go for it. On the easier modes, you can make it through without finesse, but you need a lot more skill to get through the harder modes. Loved the level design, very intriguing plot, NPCs are a bit unoriginal but that's not the point of the game. I loved the original Thief because you were constantly on edge, always about to be discovered. This incarnation taps into the same feeling, and does so even more intensely on some of the levels (e.g., the asylum.)

If you're reading these reviews because you're not sure you'll be interested in 'another 'inspired by x-bit classics' indie game' please do yourself a favor and just take the dive.  More than most, I fall into the catagory of gamers who are well and completely tired of the lazy, half-♥♥♥♥♥ 'retro' games being churned out of indie studios over the last decade. Nothing gets under my skin these days than having 2/3 of my friends gushing over some new meme-laden travesty that looks like it was slapped together in your little brother's 'game design' club in high school, only to find my low opinion justified once I begrudgingly give it a try myself.  This game isn't like those games.  Somehow, this studio managed to use pixel/sprite graphics as just a medium through which to make stunning, beautiful artwork rather than as a crutch to avoid paying an actual artist for their work. The story is told primarily through the visual medium, dragging you deeper into the decay of this breathtaking world with every subtle clue and unique tale (told through slideshow-like images, from the perspective of the NPC telling it). Combat is tight and satisfying, with enough variation in both enemies and mechanics to keep you interested and immersed.  Seriously, this is EASILY my indie game of the year. A gem, polished to a mirror finish by a developer who seems to have a real love and appreciation for their craft.  Buy this game. Buy the soundtrack edition. Hell, go buy one of the really badass hoodies they made. Worth every cent.  More in-depth review here:  https://youtu.be/qdLetFnW_ow

app_name: Left in the Dark: No One on Board
review_text: I realy enjoy HOG because they often have an interesting story and its enjoyable not to be stuck in game. This one is a bit of a disapointment really. This was outsourced and it shows. The Hidden object games are mostly hit and miss. Some of the items are the exact color of background scene making almost mandatory that you click the hint button.   The story isnt that well thought out and what should be a roller coaster ride ends up being a tame story in the end. I did finish the game and was expected you can expect 2 hours out of the game. Its very short. Also you can tell the outsource team was lazy as they have the same HOG scene that you have to do twice with the same object that you didnt click before. Now you have to click on the second set. They also look a little blury as well. Does it really take that much time to make another set of HOG scenes?  The puzzles are boring and mostly trial and error. One of the puzzles involves a family tree no less. I suppose that wouldnt have been bad if they gave you some hint in your diary. I couldnt find it. The price for this is crazy high for 2 hours. Thats $5 dollars an hour. If you can find the game cheap like I did. I paid $2 dollar for this and one other HOG game then its worth it otherwise save your money. Graphics while sparse are not bad and do look very good. Sound effects are also good and the voice of the women is the same that does Nightmare from the deep. A ok game if you can find it cheap otherwise their are much better games out their for the same price.  I own a lot of HOG's on steam and would recommend Abyss: the wraiths of eden. Nightmares from the deep: The sirens call. Nightmare from the deep: The Cursed heart. Clockwork Tales: Of glass and ink. All of these are much better looking and more interesting then this game. Let's start with the games is optimised fairly badly so if you don't have a powerful machine it will not run well. I have a high end graphics card with a powerful CPU and on max settings I still don't get a guarenteed 60 FPS. The game on launch is in Japanese but there is a community guide on how to change it on steam.   The price is also a big negative however as no game should really be at this price and not be able to last the test of time. If it is on sale I recommend it, but as it is I would say wait for a sale.   First of all if you have played the original Bladestorm it is fairly similar but looks better has another campaign as well as a few new mechanics and features.  Secondly, the setting. It is based in a very romanticised version of the 100 years war so it will have troops which weren't present such as ninjas, barbarians and magic. Do not expect a realistic game it is Koei Tecmo.  Thirdly, the game allows you to select units into battle and later on you got take extra commanders in and form armies.   Finally difficulty, as it stands there is almost no difficulty. If you want to speed through the game go through the nightmare mode untill you get guns and then go through a few missions with as many commanders as you can with guns form an army and victory is easy. You can use the same character in the other campaign and keep the previously gained levels books and pages.  Pro -  New army and commander system Large Maps Freedom to ignore orders and charge into random castles and villages to capture Many different unit types to choose from 2 campaigns to choose from Nightmare mode is interesting with the fantasy elements and still in the period of the 100 years war, not many games allow you to charge across the French country side with a horde of goblins  Cons -  Crap optimisation Sometimes units bug out and combat doesn't work right AI pathfinding messes up and some units will get caught on scenery or a random huge guy which I am sure is meant to be in nightmare mode appears in the normal game Overpriced

At times, there are some games that the themes are quite obvious – that’s a good thing however there are others that sends out mixed messages. Belladonna from developer Neckbolt forms the latter. On the initial onset, it promises a gothic adventure filled with various themes like who’s the real villain and morale value systems. Yet when completing the game, there were very minimalistic morale systems as quite frankly, there was no choices to be made. In addition, the dominate feeling I had was, this is some kind a high school / first year university project played on primitive philosophy.  The plot encompasses the story loosely based on Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley’s Frankenstein. That is, the doctor Wolfram von Trauerschloss had a vision to reanimate the dead. Of course he has his own reasons thus forms the plot. To enhance the plot, there are scattered journal notes that are extremely well written, yet a little too pretentious at times, along with the spoken dialogues that gives out an impression of superiority among the readers. Also, as much as I enjoyed reading the journal notes, this places too much dominance over the said gameplay style, which is an adventure game.  Yet the strong points of this ‘game’ is the atmosphere it creates and most of that was due to its musical scores. The music was indeed haunting and does loop eventually. However the looping is so well done that you won’t notice it for the most parts. Every dialogues are voice acted and does a reasonable job at it. Visually it’s simplistic yet effective. There won’t be any pixel hunting as there’s not much inventory management to begin with. The drawings are all displayed with great detail, especially the opening scene where the female protagonist wakes up from her ‘sleep’.  Depending upon your reading speed, this game can be completed within an hour or so. It took me about two hours however I was very meticulous with all of my actions. With a price tag of USD $6.99, that’s not a good deal. I was hoping there were morale choices or at minimal, two endings. The game, whilst promises various themes and morale systems, the game ultimately is linear. Other words, it really doesn’t matter what I think as the game has a starting and ending point so basically I’m taking the viewpoint of the developer’s ‘ideal’ choice.  The game doesn’t even have a thought provoking ending where it makes the player contemplate for the moment (for instance David Lynch’s ‘Lost Highway’) and that would be an acceptable ending to a linear game however it was not the case. Also the game’s mix messages it’s sending out leads for more confusion (for instance, looking at the trading cards, is it taking the mickey out of old horror films or is Neckbolt a fan of this genre?). This clashes to the ‘moralistic’ approach to this game. So it looks like Neckbolt had some sort of story to share however couldn’t think of an appropriate gaming genre so it uses the ‘adventure’ genre as the be-all-end-all.  If it meant to be a philosophical journey, as mentioned before, it’s coming across as pretentious. Neckbolt should take a page out of a great philosophical fictional novel ‘Sophie’s World’ by Jostein Gaarder. The author uses words that can appeal to the wider audience without sounding haughty that also gets the message across. Because this game missed the queue too many times, I really cannot recommend it at its current price. If the price is maximum USD $2.99 then it can be somewhat forgiven.  4 / 10

This game in its current state, is, in my opinion, one of the most watered down, shallow and simplistic Total Wars to date.  The lack of race options at launch gave the game for me, a very short lifespan, with Campaign replayablity almost non existant.  Whilst the game is aestheticall pleasing, the map is beautiful, the unit models are very nice also, but, the units themselves suffer from perhaps the biggest mistake they have made for a Warhammer title, being that they failed to put in unit officers, musicians and banners. This is simply criminal.  Back to the map, whilst it is lovely, it is way too small. You get zero sense of 'grande' in this campaign, you can basically city hop from one point to the next making the map actually feel smaller than it even is.  I do think the game will improve, but not until it is fully complete, which is going to be a number of years down the line with the additional two expansions fully added.   You cannot offer a Warhammer game, without all the races being present, and expect it to be any good. Until we can play as all races, it will never live up to its potential.

'They Ate My Clapper' is the name of the movie our hero Nate goes to see so often the cashier knows him by name. I do not know how or why the developers, phime studios LLC, came up with that name for a movie, but for some reason it reminds me of every zombie movie and game ever made. (They Ate My Brains immediately came to my mind.) This is especially true when the first thing our movie herione, Gabriella, says to Nate is 'The Mindhunters are after us.' No zombies though, unless being zombie-fied by mind control counts.  Story: Our hero, Nate, goes to watch the movie 'They Ate My Clapper' quite often. One evening he falls asleep and is transported into that particular movie's world. There he meets Gabriella, a detective of sorts, looking to stop the Mindhunter and find/rescue the movie's crew who are making the movie 'They Ate My Clapper.' (Paradox, anyone?)  Gameplay: In a word: challenging. This is more of a point and click game then a hidden object game, and full of logic. Everything is logical, the gameplay objectives and goals, and the puzzles. It is the most logical game I have ever played, in fact. No turning screws with a coin, there is actually a screwdriver! It is also quite challenging, and only the most ... insane? would try this game on expert the first time though. Some of the things that need to found are quite hard to find and I generally do not use game hints but for this game I highly recommend them when stuck. (Or use this game guide I found after I played -  http://www.phime.com/showing-tonight-mindhunters-incident-strategy-guide/) .   Despite some quite subtle paradoxes, the game itself plays out as a mystery, and not as the movie plot though that is debatable. There 7 chapters total, the last being what could be called 'the bonus chapter' and 8 locations plus 1 location added for chapter seven (but only accessible through another location). Each location is then divided into scenes which vary in number. For example, the first location has 6 scenes. There a map, a very nice 3D map for fast travel, but it is not really needed until chapter seven.   The Hidden Object aspect: There are only 3 actual word list Hidden Object scenes in the first 6 chapters, and 2 in chapter seven. What the hidden object scenes are, are silhouette hidden objects or SHOs as the developer calls them. (6 in the main storyline.) This is very different from the usual standard word list. A SHO is a group of pictures that must be located in the scene and once clicked on a 'shadow'/silhouette appears behind the object. The gamer then must rotate the object to match the 'shadow'/silhouette. This is quite the challenge, especially for those like me that lack skill with spatial reasoning. Also about 5 clicks or so into rotating the object a red box appears asking if you want to skip. Hmmm? I thought after a few of those, these little puzzles can be solved in less than 5 clicks?? It is true (look in the guide if you do not believe). Most, if not all, are solvable in 2 clicks. (They would have to be since 3 achievements (based on 1 minute time) are given for these SHOs.)  Puzzles: My, oh my, and OMG. This game's version of mini-puzzles include fixing a car engine, a driving test, check mating a king, geography, a crossword slider, launching a rocket and not 1 but 2 arcade games. Absolutely nothing standard here! I definitely suggest reading the game instructions.  In conclusion, one of the best games I have ever played and well worth full price. I did get a free key from the developers, phime studios, (which I was very surprised about) for supporting their Greenlight effort. Even if I had not received a key I would have still bought this game. If they ever make more of this series I am definitely going to buy them, (and phime studios might since the game ended with 'To be continued'.)

[Note: this review is only about the singleplayer campaign. I haven't tried multi.]  So I finally played Opposing Force, 13 years after originally buying it in the Half-Life Generation pack... and it was so much better than I expected! Actually, it was maybe even better than Half-Life itself. I thought it would be just like playing an additional map of Half-Life, and boy was I wrong. The gameplay is quite different, much more nervous and fast, in the fashion of Doom and Quake. There are new aliens, new awesome weapons, and even some cool puzzles. And it makes you feel like discovering more of the Half-Life universe; it gives you a better understanding about what is the Black Mesa facility and what happens in there. There are also some nice G-Man sightings. And the difficulty is up several notches compared to HL, which is not a bad thing. Your weapons often feel like they severely lack firepower, which is really frustrating at first, but becomes stimulating because of the challenge it generates. The only flaw about Opposing Force is the terrible AI of your squadmates, and the ridiculous dialogue lines they yell at random times. But even this becomes funny after a while. 'CHECK YOUR ZONE'!  Bottom line: hours of great fun, and quite indispensable if you enjoyed Half-Life.

This game is... strange, in a good way.  It's all about running an item shop and crawling through dungeons for items to sell, or ingrediants to make better items. The start is a bit slow and the combat could be better, but it picks up quickly as the days go by. The story of the game is all about you being the daughter of an infamous adventurer who left a massive debt in his name, and you're the only one who can clear it. With the help of the business fairy, Tear, you'll work through a month to pay off the debt... or you'll have to sell your house!  Good things about this game:  +Good music +Unique theme +Various items to find and a unique system for equipping your adventurer with items +Various characters to run through the dungeon with +Dialog is interesting, if not a little childish due to the age of the main character +Beating the game, or failing to do so, will allow you to 'restart' with all your items and 'Merchent Level' in tact. This lets you get up in money much faster than you would do so at the start, but you don't keep any of your money or your progress through events. +Decent end-game content that makes it worth while to try and get what you need +Haggling is a pretty neat concept, though it takes practice to get it down right. +Characters are interesting and have their own unique style, in dungeons and just in general.   Bad things about this game:  -Combat could definitely be better, with hit-scan and certain abilities. Some characters are just notably better than others. -Events are picky. You have to know when you need to go to places in town to get certain chat boxes, which can affect how you get items, characters, or unlock a new location. This causes you to lose a 'block' of time, too, and not all events do anything. -One girl will come in and sell you something and refuse to buy anything. She'll always refuse to sell at 100% of the item's price, too. 200% is the minimum for her, and there's no way to get rid of her after she appears. You can refuse to buy it, but this messes up your combo and makes you level up slower.  -Getting the 'true card' for characters takes too long. True cards let you use your adventurer without having to find them in the story, which can be helpful for clearing dungeons again. But getting the True Card requires you to beat the game and use that adventurer the most. (More than any other adventurer, that is, for that month.) To get another one, you have to play the game /again/ and use a different adventurer.  -There's little 'stats' to the game that you can see, at least in my experience. There /are/ stats, but you can't really see them except when your adventurer is buying an item that is better than what they have. You can't even see how much people like you to adjust your prices.  Despite the lengthy part of the 'bad stuff', I really just offered more explanation as to what and why it was bad. This game is still amazing to play, especially if you like dungeon crawlers. The 'bad' things are just my opinion, anyways, and the good things weren't as descriptive because I don't want to spoil anything!

review_text: 7.5/10 Pros:  -Nazi Zombies! -A pretty decent campaign that offers replayability value -Great multiplayer, but it's slowly dying :( -WWII shooter -Great selection of WWII shooters -Focuses on the Pacific Theatre of War for a change  Cons: -For both charaters (Miller and Dmitri) it starts off in 1942 (Makin Raid for Miller, Stalingrad for Dmitri) then all of the sudden it skips to 1944 for Miller and 1945 for Dmitri. So much happened that wasn't explored.  -Sort of branching off from the last con, there was little to no character development. Of course, it's Call of Duty and you play it to shoot down endless waves of bad guys but I think it would have been nice to show changes in characters.  -Historically awkward. For example, the only tanks you see for the Russians is the T-34-85, which makes sense because that was the most produced tank the Russians made but they had dozens of other tank models. They had at least 3 other variants of the T-34, the IS tank series and the SU TD's.  -Rock music in a WWII shooter. wtf? -Gets repititive very quickly  Buy it on sale, 20.00 is steep especially for its age.

Even though it is an RTS game, it reminds me of god-games. It might be the setting or the 'story', I'm not sure. It doesn't have a multiplayer mode (however, a separate multiplayer game called 'Multiwinia' exists).  What it does do, however, is mixing genres into something both unique and enjoyable. It doesn't fail to create an interesting atmosphere, mainly due to the soundtrack, which reminds me of Introversion's later title 'DEFCON'. Not having humans but rather 'abstract virtual digital lifeforms' who can't speak and who don't have any emotions (or do they?) probably adds to that fact, along with the 'cyber-world' that you play on/in, which does have water, hills et al, but, after all, also has a digitalized and abstract look-and-feel.  The featured game mechanics were not surprising, but overall very enjoyable. Along with an intuitive control mechanism the game was easy to handle.  The included 'loaders' (intro animations) are a nice gimmick. One of them parodies a typical 80s crack intro that looked so authentic,  a steam employee believed there had been a compromise  [forums.introversion.co.uk]  and thus delayed the game's Steam release.  In the end, I hope you have realized how this isn't your normal off-the-shelf strategy game. You might want to go watch some gameplay videos on your favourite video platform to check if you can cope with the game's style.

This is a pretty cool game that offers a slightly different experience from GTA but with the same basic metro-open world concept.  There's a reasonably complex melee system that is fun and acts as the basis for most of the combat early on in the game. What's interesting in this case, though, is that gunplay isn't introduced until later on but is equally satisfying and has its own little tricks to mess with. I thought it was a cool way to pace the game's mechanics.  Visually the game is a pleasure to look at; the hi-def texture pack is very much recommended. Though GTA IV should be given some slack since it was released 4 years earlier, graphically this game does everything right that it did wrong: it's well detailed and bursting with color.  The story is good and the soundtrack isn't bad either.

A game with appeal to the casual player (in normal difficulty).

app_name: Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon Future Soldier
review_text: Overall campaign is fun to play. Typical Russian bad guys vs the American good guys plot, typical ending, but no one really plays a tactical shooter like this for the story, it's more about how the game plays.  First off, you can definitely tell this game is ported from the console version as at times controls are clunky and button callouts are that of the Xbox controller instead of your Keyboard/Mouse callouts. This in itself does not make it a bad game but at times it can be frustrating because you're used to having PC functionality in a PC game.   In terms of gameplay, I thought it felt true to other games in the franchise and of course other Tom Clancy's games. Weapons are realistic, one-shot headshots are a thing, and you will die instantly if you peek enemies without thinking (playing on Hardcore difficulty). This is a game that relies heavily on your ability to think critically in the middle of a mission. You will want to stealth through but sometimes you need to take out enemies without alarming others, and this can lead to retrying at the checkpoint, or just outright dying because you weren't able to control your engagements.  I was able to complete the single player campaign solo on Hardcore (hardest difficulty) in about 14 hours of gameplay, with there being about 11 missions, that is just over an hour per mission, with multiple retries on some missions that are frustrating to run.  Overall I'd give this game an objective 7/10 simply because it wasn't bad, but it wasn't amazing. The gameplay feels rewarding when you complete missions, but the cinematics look grainy, the console feel of the game even when playing on a high-end PC. If you are into tactical shooters and have a couple of friends to run co-op on, I'd say grab the game for a cheap price as it will hold you over until Wildlands drops in 2017, if you are a Ghost Recon fan, I would recommend it as well but if you're just a FPS/TPS player who is looking for a new game - do not focus on this game, instead get Rainbow Six Siege or Wildlands when it comes out in 2017.

I have really enjoyed my time with this game even though it can be frustratingly difficult. What keeps me coming back for more is the fun and satisfying gameplay. Even when I am in the middle of a run that I know I can't win, I can still seek to unlock new ships and layouts. If you have ever liked  Star Trek  or any of the countless knock-offs and wanted to be the captain of your own ship, then this is a game you'll probably enjoy; that is unless you hate dying a lot. And you will most likely die a lot.  In this game you command a ship that starts out with only three crew members that has to traverse several sectors as you are running away from the Rebel fleet. Each sector is randomly generated and populated with a dozen or so beacons which mark where you can use your FTL drive to jump to. You need to travel across each sector from beacon to beacon until you get to the exit beacon which will allow you to jump to the next sector. The Rebel fleet sweeps along the whole sector that you are currently in trying to capture you.  At each jump point there are all kinds of random events that could happen. You may come across friendly, neutral, or enemy ships. There may be a distress signal that you encounter. Sometimes ion storms or solar flares make the system extra dangerous for you to occupy. You'll have to respond to these events and the outcomes may benefit or hinder you, based on your choices.  Speaking of choices, this game offers plenty. As you respond to events or combat enemy ships, you'll acquire resources, equipment, and crew. How you uses these resources will depend on your play style and your overall strategy. You can upgrade the systems on your ship to be more defensive (more shields, faster shield recharge, defense drones, evasion) or offensive (more weapons, faster weapons recharge< disables), or a blend of both. You can buy augmentations to give your ship buffs, or build up your crew and get a transporter so that you can defeat enemy ships from within. You'll have to also decide where to allocate the power from your reactor (each system on your ship needs a certain amount of power to function) and where to station each member of your crew. And even which beacon to jump to next is a decision that can make or break you. When I play this game I feel like I am caught in this tension of just not quite having enough resources to buy that one last system I need to make my build solid. I feel like good game design creates these sorts of situations where the player needs to make hard choices. For example, I finally save up enough to get that teleporter I wanted, but now I can't find that store that actually sells one. And even if I get the teleporter, I don't have a crew strong enough in combat to make it useful. Or, look at this, the random events I've encountered have made it so that I am sitting on a stockpile of missiles. But I sold my missile launcher to be able to afford the hull repair that I desperately needed. I love it; and hate it. That's good game design to me. The only frustrating thing is, even after making all the 'right' choices, one random bad jump might cause your ship to get blown to smithereens. I guess that's the nature of roguelikes.  Combat is really fun because you'll have to charge up your weapons, target different parts of the enemy ship, make impromptu repairs and possible deal with intruders who have teleported aboard. There can be a lot going on that needs your attention so I am so appreciated of the pause feature on this game. In the middle of a heavy fight, you can pause and still issue orders and commands; they just won't take effect until you un-pause. If a battle isn't going your way, you can simply try to stay alive until your FTL drive charges up and then you can flee. However, then you might jump right into another fight and you'll still have to deal with any damage you had from the fight you just fled.  Eventually, you'll get to the boss Rebel ship which is a long, tense battle; very difficult, but satisfying when you win.  If you like roguelike games and starship battles similar to what you would see in  Star Trek , then this is the game for you. Those who are easily frustrated or those who hate micromanaging need not apply. I am certainly going to return to this game from time to time when I want to play out a starship captain story that's randomly generated. There is certainly replay value in the fact that you can play again using different ships and layouts that you unlock.
