This game is a TRUE open world space RPG. I really love this game, despite is few shortcomings.  Freedom: Full freedom to do whatever you want, wherever you want, whenever you want is why this game is great. You can become a full time miner and collect minerals, you can ferry goods from station to station and trade for money, you can capture enemy ships and DRAG them back to your base to sell.   Combat: Perhaps the best part of this game is the combat system. It is really like a 3d dogfight in space ships. Do not expect a true space physics, but the combat system is one of the most fun I have played. You actually fly the ship yourself and shoot the weps yourself. Direct full energy to shields to survive, weps to destroy, or engines to run like hell!  RPG: You gain levels as you go. You can kill ships for small XP or do missions for more XP. You can level up each of your 4 special skills, get bonuses to your 'profession', and buy new ships at higher levels, each with their own special traits.  Glitches: I have had about 10 full crashes in 24 hours of playtime. I have many out of memory errors when I still have lots in task manager, although the game still runs, for a while... In game glitches are more frequent but less noteworthy.

Oh God, where to begin?  Let's start with the pros, shall we? The game looks nice, the game controls nicely.  Welp, that's it.  Cons? My word the bugs, I can't list them here.  DLC that gives you one tank engine for $20-you know what? Let's talk about the DLC.  In this game, for $25, you can get the 'GWR Small Prairies Pack', Small Prairies being the larger tank engines for the Great Western Railway that could take heavier trains than, say the Panniers or the 1400's.  In another simulator, Trainz 12, you can get that for free when you purchase the game, start up the download manager, search for 'Prairie Tank', you'll find some rather good looking engines that you can download, and drive around on any route that you load-up/create.  Oh yeah, the routes...  In this game, you can either buy an overly expensive route (Settle-Carlisle for $40) or make one using the horribly confusing route creator. In Trainz, you could buy their Settle & Carlisle route for $20 (Which still offers more in terms of route length and even gives you a plethora of locomotives) or you could make a route with a much more powerful, and overall smoother route creator.  Finally, third-party support.  The 3rd party support in TS2015 is abysmal, practically no notable or large websites for downloading actual engines, nor routes. (Reskins? Heheh...) Trainz 12, on the other hand, has entire websites and forums dedicated to making content for Trainz, whether it be routes, engines, buildings, you name it. (No, I'm not going to mention the large Thomas the Tank Engine community for either games.) Other than that, the Content Manager's Download Station has a vast, large database of over (As of April 12, 2015) 346,753 items in it. ALL COMPLETELY FREE. (With some exceptions, damn you Royal Scot!)  In conclusion; don't buy this, or anything that this game has to offer. Play Trainz 12  Edit 4/17/16; Help me please. I can't stop playing it. I've bought three DLC packs in the last week. I need help.  I like the game now. ;~;

Incredible game!  I felt compelled to write a review for this game because it has quickly become my favorite. It is such a simple concept but with unlimited potential for thrills on every game, especially when playing against human opponents online or locally.   Highlights: - Highly flexible options for accommodating split-screen and online players in a party or random match - Physics that just 'feel right' - Player has full control of the ball, in contrast to a traditional sports game like FIFA where the computer does the dribbling and you just make passes and shots - Challenges and rewards the player's sense of spatial orientation (great use of a 3D environment) - Potential for very simplistic run and hit playing or for spectacular aerial displays of skill - Abundant vehicle customization options - Workable AI and 'season' game mode that serves as good practice - The developer continually offers free content in the form of interesting gameplay modifications like basketball and hockey modes - Cross-platform play with XBox One and PS4 which makes for a very large pool of online opponents  This is one of those special games that causes its players to become addicted to it and are really hard to put down once you start playing. The length of each match is long enough to enjoy but short enough to 'go for one more', long after you intended to stop playing.   Besides the loads of fun RL offers as a party game, local or online, it also hosts a well populated community for competitive online gaming and a ranking system that entices players to want to climb to the towards the top. Even for offline play, the game provides AI opponents which, although sometimes are goofy enough to score on their own goal, they do provide a stiff challenge for beginner players.  You cannot go wrong with this game and I highly recommend it.

TL;DR - This is a mostly neutral-to-positive review. While i find it hard to reccomend at full price, i'd still do so.  Okay, first things first, what have you guys done to the name of this game? While it's safe to say the GRID series isn't nearly as closely related to the toca race driver series as it used to be, simply naming the 3rd iteration GRID Autosport sounds off to me. Having to explain to people which GRID title i'm referring to is time i could've spent talking about your title instead.  I have no idea what to say about the carreer mode at this point, so far, i'm under the impression Team Ravenwest is the hot stuff in town, and i'm not having any of it. I'm far too busy fighting it out with the track and the people right in front or behind me. Atleast they've let me play around with whatever discipline i felt like doing without forcing me to do something else for a change of pace just yet.  The handling and physics are pretty similar to GRID2, and while i miss RD:GRID's wacky instant handbrake drifts, i can sorta understand why they went with a slightly less agile approach. However, with every iteration of GRID, the CPU controlled cars seem more agressive and heavy. While i understand driving people off the track to gain the lead has always been a bit of a problem in racing games, CPU Opponents shoulden't be able to effortlessly get me into a wreck just because they don't agree with my driving line.  From a technical standpoint, there's a few graphical glitches when using AMD's CrossfireX, sometimes the grass flickers, but seeing how other games just completely fall flat in this regard i can look past it, since the performance scaling is all right. It doesn't change a whole lot, since even at 1440p, maxing out the settings leaves more than enough headroom to comfortable run this maxed out ( safe from some intel only settings, thanks). While the shaders and effects stepped up quite a bit from GRID2, the environments and roads seem left behind on most tracks, leaving you with an uninspired and uninteresting landscape at times. I have yet to see some of the tracks for other disciplines ( i have mostly seen the circuit-esque tracks at the point of writing), so take this with a grain of salt.

It's a good game at it's core. But some design decisions ruin it for me and I would not recommend it as it is if you are a similar player as me (gamer with a job and family, or just a gamer who cares about fun/time ratio). I kinda wish i bought Civ6 instead of this, in retrospect. Here's the details.  THE GOOD  - A real attempt at innovation. Things like immigration, robotic population, randomly generated races (including how they look), anomalies, enlightenment, uplifting, etc are new to the genre and interesting, and i definitely wish some of those ideas will spread out to the genre. - Great aesthetics. Love how planets, stars (sun), and black holes look. - A huge time sink. Which is good for a lot of people but not for everyone since it mostly comes from a diluted experience. But if you are a 'one game kind of player' then this is one of the game that you can stick with for a while. - Big on exploration. It puts EXPLORATION back into 4X. Finding anomalies, new alien races, ancient artifacts of extent races (they don't do anything but at least you feel like you are exploring space), many varied events spice up the discovery of the universe.   THE BAD  - EXTREMELY slow. I am 75 hours in my first game and i haven't won yet. Everything takes forever and is not necessarily fun. Keep in mind, I AM PLAYING ON MAX SPEED! And only pause to issue orders when necessary. Which is often because of so much micro... which is funny to see so much micro in a game that has the universe as a playground and a real opportunity to focus on macro mechanics. - Tedious and repetitive on some tasks, like controlling survey ships system by system and no 'automate workers' option like in similar titles. You will eventually unlock a technology to automate survey ships, but that's gonna be way down the road and an expensive tech at that when you have better options. Often too late to matter. Another tedious process is building and upgrading planets... If you have Mining network level 3, you can't just build that right off... you have to build the 1, then upgrade to 2, then upgrade to 3... each of them taking months of fixed time that can never be sped off, not even through buying the damn things like similar games. This adds again to the tedious repetitive tasks that just dilute the game and makes everything take forever. - Some extremely BAD GAME DESIGN decisions. One example is that building takes always the same amount of time, with no option to accelerate or speed up the process no matter your level of power of technology. A planet that has 20 population and every upgrade will build a new power plant in the same exact speed as that other new colony you just build and that has 1 single population. This means that the game starts slow and stays slow, no matter how powerful you get. - TEDIOUS victory conditions, and only 3 of them : 1. Own 40% of HABITABLE planets (not just habited, but all habitable planets! ... and the universe is quite huge...) 2. Subjugate all other empires (that's a lot too because war is extremely slow, i'll explain why in the next point) 3. Have your federation (allies) control 60% of HABITABLE PLANETS, meaning even if you ally and conquer absolutely everyone, there's dozens or hundreds of planets for you to colonize still. Colonization being capped by Influence, which generates very slowly and is capped at 1000 storage, meaning you can't even stockpile it for the times you want to explode your expansion. Tedious! And there are no creative victory conditions like science or cultural victory in civilization, just tedious conquest of the whole space one planet at a time. - Even warfare is tedious and plagued by slowness. First, travel is very slow and traveling back and forth takes FOREVER (your warp engines heat up and need to cool down at every jump, and they need also to build energy for a jump... MAKE IT SLOW is the keyword behind every design decision it seems). Second, you can't outright capture planets... even if you invade them the enemy will take them back automatically at the end of the war. The only way to capture planet is to say you want them UPON WAR DECLARATION, and there is a point system that limits how many you can demand. You can't demand more than 4 or 5 planets each war, which makes it problematic and a chore to take down the bigger empires. And you can't outright vassalize bigger empires because the score to vassalise is too big, you have to make them smaller first and that goes through slow conquest a handful of planets at a time. So you wage war, take a few planets, have to wait 10 years to be able to declare war again, rinse and repeat, and this for EVERY empire in the game. Tedious! Not to forget that even after you do this, you still have to colonize habitable planets that nobody took. Shoot me in the head please. - Little sens of accomplishment at every play session. Ok so you took a day off, you played Stellaris all day... and then you go to bed... feeling you did absolutely nothing. You wake up in the weekend, and do another full day of play thinking 'ok yesterday was slow but today i WILL make progress'... ... ... nope. Another day goes buy and you feel like you just flushed it down the toilet of 'survey this, and keep upgrading this planet that'. None of the 4X games (or even strategy games in general) make me feel like this. I feel like i am playing an MMO and grinding without getting that 0.02% drop chance item. Not a good feeling.   BUY OR NOT?  If sinking hundreds of hours in a single campaign is not a problem for you, and if everything taking forever is not a problem either, you will probably like this game. This seems to be the majority of player if i am to conclude from the Steam score of Stellaris. But if you actually want a game that has a pulse and want things to actually happen at a reasonable pace... OR if you have limited time and prefer shorter campaigns, this game is not for you.  I would say this game needs to be sped up by least least 3 times to feel decent. Or 5 times to feel like you are actually making a dent on your space conquest during a play session. It needs 3 to 5 times the current speed just to be on par with similar games in terms of progress and pacing. Right now it feels way too slow to be fun.  I would still get it on a sale just to see the innovations it bring. But I definitely don't see this game as having the staying power of better titles like civilizations and endless space. It is mostly something you would buy if you already played and are tired of better games and want something a bit different for a while, even if it is unlikely you will finish even a single campaign.

Trove is a good game, but it has it's draw backs. Many of which can be blamed on two things.   1. Some severe lag issues: For some people I hear the game doesn't even start. I haven't yet encountered that myself, and it runs fine for the most part for me, but there are moments where things just kinda break. Then again, try playing a game that doesn't have a glitch of some kind.  2. Pay2Win: Now this I can understand. I have seen the market place, and it is very restrictive on what you can get without pouring dollars into this game. HOWEVER: Like Warframe,(I know, of all games, why draw comparisons to that one. Hang with me, pls.) you can do just fine without spending a dime. Of course, it will take a helluva lot more time to do, but maybe it's worth it.  Despite that, I do reccomend this game, because of 3 things:  1. Friendly: It is a very nice game. In every way it is warm. All nearby players get the loot that drops, and there is no fierce competition over that Shadow lvl 3 staff; death isn't overly punishing, and the game gives you a pretty good start, and then encourages you to engage with the community.  2. Rewards cooperation: You get more together. When you're fighting with a team, all of you get XP, and all of you get loot. Even better, the punishment of death can be lessened when you have a team that can revive. Building becomes easier, and higher level characters can provide gear to lesser levels to help them gain rank.  3. Appeals to a very large audience: Everyone: young, old, builders, adventurers, people who like pretty places and people who like hell. Everyone will have a place in Trove. There are many different classes for people of many different playstyles, and each one can be tailored via the gear you get, and the gem system. Threre are clubs for the different types of people, those who like adventuring will be greeted very well by adventuring clubs, builders can show off there talents to eachother at building clubs, and so on. And the best part is that everyone progresses through their respective intrests in similar ways, resulting in a place where different interests collide and then are sewn together in a rather lovely way.  There is no way I could put this on the 1 to 10 scale and be accurate, so I'll give it a 89/100. Do the math, people!

First of all I will say that this is not a bad game per se. The gameplay at its core is good and it is a fun experience. However, as it stands at the time of this opinion, the game is unfinished. It feels like an early access game rather than a AAA title. Missing modes, not well tested mechanics, the ever adition of content which should have been there from the beginning, etc.   If this is your very first fighter it is ok, since it is so simple it will allow you to understand the basics of fighting games. The game is even intended to bring the casual audience along. However, this very principle makes the gap between well versed players and begginers much more aparent. Players with a medium to good understanding of the neutral game will crush begginers to medium leveled players, possibly making them leave in frustration. While begginers leaving the game because of frustration is nothing new in fighting games, in previous iterations it tended to not matter because the game was focused and designed for the hardcore crowd anyway. In this game, however, some tiers of the ranking system feel depopulated and artificial.   One very annoying thing is that characters have very few colors, and even though you can unlock a few by playing, some have to be bought. The alternate costumes also have to be bought, but in previous iterations you could buy packs with several for 10 bucks. Here, EACH COSTUME costs 10 bucks. Its too much money if you want to have all the alternate costumes.   Overall I really do not recommend this game unless, like me, you played ultra but now play this since capcom no longer supoorts USFIV and the mainstream tournament scene has refocused to this game.

EDIT: Added a list of game mechanics I enjoy.  Friendly visuals combined with its arcadey gameplay make this an extremely accessible baseball game on the PC!  But don't let the skin of the ball fool you, this game has meat! There's much more depth here than you may expect. A ton of super-solid game mechanics with the right amount of control to satisfy those of us who want to jump in and play but also feel like we're mastering a game and crave the feeling of progression.  Some of the solid game mechanics I enjoy:  BATTING - has both tap button swing and power swing (hold the button to build up power and release at the right moment) all the while moving your mouse/controller to position the bat. Increasing the EGO (difficulty) will make positioning the bat more important!  PITCHING - similar to batting, has a tap button pitch or power pitch by holding the button and aiming with the controller (or mouse).  FIELDING - while simple, the options to jump, dive, and wall jump all exist. On higher EGO ratings, you will need to guide your fielders more towards the ball.  BASE RUNNING - a nice feature here is you can tell all players to run at the same time OR you can control individual players (want 2nd base to run to third but 1st base to stay put? No problem).  EGO (difficulty settings) - It has a difficulty system that allows you to fine tune the AI to provide you with the pefect non-frustrating balance of challenge (the values range from 0-99!), it's really quite incredible. This allows you to tweak the game's difficulty as you get better but keep it from being frustratingly difficult, unless you want it to be.  To put it simply, Super Mega Baseball is brilliant and an absolute joy to play.  My only con: multiplayer is local only.  Super Mega Baseball came out of left field for me and seriously hits it out of the park!  Highly recommended.  Some gameplay footage:         YouTube™ Video:  Super Mega Baseball - League Championship Final Game [PC]

I've always wanted to write a proper review for this game but I still can't be arsed to write a lengthy, detailed writeup over a video game; everything I'd want to say has already been said again and again anyways. So I'll just get down to the important stuff.  This game has its pros and cons but the important thing to remember is that it -IS- free and if you play semi-regularly the grind doesn't really feel too hard. Everything performance-wise you can get with real money you can get with in-game money except for Hero mechs, which aren't even remotely P2W but do have unique playstyles and loadouts compared to their standard variants. Also, some of them look sexy as hell. (Most of the pay model in this game is cosmetic btw)  There are a LOT of mechs you can pick from and decent variety between the individual models. You can customize the loadout and there's a fair bit of playstyle flexibility for MOST of the mechs. However, this game suffers from a terrible state of 'forced' meta due to the way rebalancing is handled (or not handled), making things considered non-meta really hard to be competitive in. Still, if -you- like it and you can put in the performance, go for it and do you. All my clan mechs are ballistic-based and I love the unexpected facetime and carries I get against people expecting pulse vomit or streams or other meta Clan stuff.  This game hates AMD FX processors!!! Full stop. It's a known and somewhat long-term issue and, while not incapacitating, can be really frustrating because it causes small, infrequent hiccups/bottlenecking during gameplay. For everyone else, this game has the optimization of a flint axe! Bear that in mind if you crank up the graphics settings. However, the mechs on higher settings look amazing, as do the particles and shaders. The LODs are terribad but I guess that's the price you pay for super-sexy giant stompy robots with full first-person cockpit views.  Another big con for this game is that the developer doesn't always make the best or brightest decisions in regards to balance passes and content updates. But!!! Things have been way better in that department in the past few months/past year, and we actually got the new CW when they said they would put it out, so that's refreshing.    TLDR: Should you play this game? Yes. It's free. It's fun. There's no P2W. At least give it a shot before you decide you hate it.

Honestly I think the game is one of the most entertaining strategy games of recent memory. Sure, it's not on the complexity level of Paradox Interactive's offerings, but sometimes its nice to take a break from Paradox Games and play something with 'More Fightiness' as the Greenskins would say. I really love the Warhammer lore, and as someone who has always wanted to get into the tabletop, but never had the money, this is the next best thing. The factions, while not very numerous at this time, all feel really unique and fun to play. They are going to be adding all the major Warhammer factions at some point through DLC. If you enjoy the Warhammer lore, (before that Age of Sigmar crap) this game is a real treat.  The DLC has been a major complaint of recent Total War games as of late, and while I can see where the complaints are coming from, I don't nessessarily think it's as 'black and white' an issue as people make it out to be. The DLC is somewhat expensive, but I think if you invest enough time in the game it's worth it. I go by the dollar per hour rule, and if I get 1 hour of gameplay for every dollar I spend, I'm happy. While I wish they would be a little cheaper DLC wise, I don't think it's to say the game is bad just because of a lot of DLC. Look at Crusader Kings 2, my favourite game of all time, which has loads of DLC, but vast amounts of content and gameplay value. Just buy the DLC on sale if you dont feel like paying for more variety. Creative Assembly is reasonably fair with Total War Warhammer when it comes to the free patches. You are really just paying for different and optional ways of playing the game.

I enjoyed this game enough to finish it, and that took a decent amount of time. When I purchased such a cheap game, I expected much less from it.  I love strategy games and this one is fairly basic on the surface, but there's more than meets the eye.  The game does have some flaws, but overall it's quite solid in my opinion. I didn't think the game was tedious; I thought the missions made sure there's enough variety to keep things interesting. There's some griding involved, but that didn't phase me too much.  The equipment you find is also interesting. There's not a ton of weapons, but each weapon has its place. They're different enough that there are certainly different tools for different jobs. Once you figure out what the weapons do, you'll know that you need to keep several spares so you can swap them around when needed.  I recommend this game, especially for such a low price. I would love to see a second Star Chronicles game, or a DLC that extends the story.

Truly a spit in the face to any previous HOI fan. Takes all systems, even the most simple ones, and dumbs it down. This is all part of the Paradox 'Streamlining' process which is also going on in EU4. I understand that it opens up the games to a whole new audience, but it takes away almost everything that actually makes me love their games. And that is how hardcore they are. It gives you so much more immersion and a much more rewarding experience. However, this game, not only has major flaws in its game systems, but also takes a ♥♥♥♥ on what you would call the grand strategy genre. I would call this more of a you-take-care-of-that-while-I-sit-here-and-do-nothing game. And for a game covering the most devastating conflict in human history, that is quite bad. If you're looking for an easy, forgiving, simplistic experience, then this is for you. However I trust that many other paradox fans agree when I say that this one was just far too simplistic and dumbed down. Sure, future patches and dlc's will improve the experience alot, but the problems with this game would need an entire remake to be fixable.

This game is an abomination. More than anything else I've played recently, it's atrociously designed, moronic and utterly without redeeming features. I barely know where to start. How about with this? Half the time it's more expedient to let people kill you. Why? Because a feature called 'second wind' gives you a generous period after 'death' in which to finish an enemy off, rewarding you with all your shield and some health back. Fighting enemies is so laborious and repetitive, with so little opportunity to reduce the damage you take, that the best tactic is to repeatedly 'die' and then miraculously come back to life using second wind.  How about this? The game supposedly lets you play a sniper character. But can you infiltrate enemy camps stealthily, picking them off from a distance? No, you cannot. The rifles are so weak, and most of the enemies so preposterously over-armoured, that a direct headshot has little effect - except to alert every enemy in the area to your presence, causing them to immediately charge at you, screeching irritatingly.  But that's not even the worst of it - you can't infiltrate or snipe principally because most enemy camps are almost empty ... until, that is, you're inside them, whereupon they spawn waves of enemies out of black holes. You can wearily gun your way through them, but there's no real way to dodge, hide or retreat. You just have to weather their firepower and mow them down. Tedious. Extremely so. Here's a very common thing that happens when I play: 'Right, that's the last of them dead. Wait, I can still hear the faint rat-tat-tat of gunfire and my shield appears to be being chipped away at. Looks like I'll have to perform a careful 360-degree turn to try to work out which of these many black holes all around me has belatedly decided to spawn one more bandit. Oh, there also appears to be someone repeatedly hitting me. I'd better sort that out.'  How about this? The way ammo is dispensed/used up is unforgiveably stupid. Glowing green boxes, lockers, crates, barrels and toilets are littered everywhere, all containing guns and ammo, but since you have to empty about four clips of any gun into a single enemy, you soon run out. So a huge portion of the game is spent foraging and reloading, foraging and reloading. Opening lockers. Opening bins. Opening toilets. Why not just have less of this nonsense and have the enemies go down much more quickly? Do I really have to shoot twelve rounds from a high-powered sniper rifle into someone (three rounds at a time, with annoying reloading intervals) to get them to stop harassing me?  And that word, 'harrassing'? That's what it feels like. Nothing in this game feels particularly dangerous or testing. It's all just harrassment. They filled the world with hundreds and hundreds of fireamrs, but they might as well be spud guns. Enemies defeat you by mobbing you and filling you with hundreds of bullets while you reload every few seconds and gradually work your way through them. It's a war of attrition. An incredibly boring one.  The collision detection for melee combat is obviously woeful - it always is in games like this. I made the mistake of picking a character whose special talent is to project a hologram, them go invisible, allowing them to perform an assassination strike. A fat lot of good it is against any enemy who attacks with fists, since you can't actually come up behind them without getting hit by a punching animation that's directed the other way.  Why is there so much utter faffery around choosing which weapons to use? Each gun has about twelve statistics, plus additional bonuses and defects, which you're supposed to compare and contrast in order to find your favourites. And the game spits weapons at you at a rate of about one every minute. Enemies drop them. Enemy camps are flooded with them. Vending machines sell them.  Want to upgrade your character? Grind through dozens of boring encounters to level up and enjoy adding 0.6% to dozens of achingly dull statistics, like accuracy, reload time and zoom level. You will never notice a difference in how the game plays. It's still grinding, grinding, grinding, through the same braindead goons who roll up, absorb five clips' worth of ammo, then fall down slowly, only to be replaced by another identical enemy emerging from the same ♥♥♥♥ing bivouac.  What a load of ♥♥♥♥. Seriously.

This app does not tweak or optimize or change the registry. It can't, its a sandbox and whitelister. It uses the sandbox from the security app to construct the operating system without changing settings. This version does not come with the security bit.   How it was explained to me is Seventh Knight makes an additional step to any code being executed by the os to be whitelisted by the app itself. Updates, etc. the OS will be mostly halted entirely instead of turning off services. still be there but it wont execute code and most of it will be unloaded or gimped.    If you want to tweak your OS go ahead and skip this app since you can do that for free. Its not for you. Also if you stream or alt tab feel free to ignore this as obviously that wont work without whitelisting your app which defeats the purpose.   Personal Note: Ive done this review many times. I've taken a liking to this product and tried to do honest reviews since I saw no coverage for this product, but its seen a lot of hate. Ive deleted hateful comments in the past and I'm not proud of it, personal troubles unrelated affected me. My point is to inform the consumer.   I mean it by what I said, to skip this if you want to tweak or are looking for a tweaker. Its not what this does.    Well go ahead and comment on this one! For one time only comment literally anything you want. I don't care. I've made my point here as best ai could. The app works. And I like it.  ORIGINAL REVIEW: This program is very different from most other 'gaming optimizers' I've found. First off, i'm a big fan of system tweaks, unfortunately, because my first pc was a mac. I refused to use vista, and once 7 came out and I became a pc gamer, the operating system was very bloated and underpowered. I have since learned that merely tweaking registry settings, changing parameters, preventing startups and disabling services does more harm than good, and genuinely does not work at all. Which is why I found it interesting that this app, and its parent, are made by military contractors specializing in sandboxes. So I was genuinely interested in recieving a demo to try it out since I hadn't seen any reviews on youtube and, being a corporate solution turned consumer product, I couldn't find any info on it, so I decided to take it on myself to provide such information. What I found interesting right away is that, at no point did it tweak any settings, disable any startups, and change any registry settings when enabling this gaming mode. Instead, what it does it issue restart commands that prevent certain parts of the operating system from loading on boot. Being forced to restart, I found that the system wasn't simply faster, it was nonexistant. Using the max boost preset killed explorer entirely, leaving steam the only usable program. Upon learning that this utility is a gaming sandbox and not a true optimizer utility, I was stunned. Consoles completely disable their operating systems when a game is launched, apparently, but this was still a very interesting precident. Upon disabling my gaming mode, again being forced to restart, I found that no registry settings were tweaked, all my startup programs were enabled, and no services were turned off. It worked exactly as it had done before, and in lieu of making changes, the program simply prevented the kernel from loading anything in the first place. Its as if windows was completely out of the picture. Now in real life this makes very mixed results. In heaven benchmark i saw no impovement, and in fire strike I saw only a 120 point increase. However, in catzilla, a poorly coded and abandoned benchmark, I saw a 20% improvement. But what IS apparent is the smoothness. I saw no hitching and stuttering that I was used to at 144 hz and it was so smooth in fact that I decided to re enable lightboost. Even at 60 and 55 fps, my monitor sees no hitching or tearing related to laggy and uneven framerate. Such things I am not technically adept enough at explaining, but rest assured, this program does not optimize the operating system in any way. In fact it doesn't even touch the operating system. it prevents parts of it to not be loaded. Meaning to use anything in its max preset I had to kill explorer.exe and launch everything from task manager. So without repeating myself, do I reccommend it? yes, absolutely. This program has a very kind and professional customer support, ten years of military contractor experience, and comes with a very good security sandbox designed originally for environments that have to be working 100% all of the time and can't sacrifice slowdowns as a matter of national security. If you are a streamer, or like to use skype, I do not reccomend this over cpu cores or process lasso, because as those do not have nearly as much benefit, they do allow you to use more than just games when the programs are activated. If you are in any way skeptical just ask in the forums for a trial code, and they will give it to you. CONS unfortunately this app comes with disadvantages. The steam only downloads the installer and gives you a key. Currently you have to leave the setup utility open for 5 minutes in order to review the game as per valve's requirements. while the customer support is friendly enough to reset your cd key, it can officially only be installed on 3 motherboards. and while the games really do work better, they are not always going to be 'faster', as the store page only says that they are going to be 'smoother'. Also, because of how it requires you to disable or uninstall your antivirus to properly set up the program, because it disables and alters your how your system boots, some users may not want to risk the possible benefits. this is why it comes with a security program. CONCLUSION if you ever wanted to disable your operating system from loading in the first place, so that the only thing that would be loaded are the services needed for steam, or are in any way interested in boosting your game but don't want to change settings, or use high refresh rate montiors, and do not stream or record, then this app is for you. It sets an amazing precident in an industry invented by charlatains, and in this day and age with vulkan and directx 12 re-invigorating interest for 'close to the metal' performance, this app is a very welcome change. I rate this app 9.2 out of ten. Its a very well made, and surprisingly legitimate and well supported product, but comes with the requirement to disable your antivirus to install and requires you to restart to use it.﻿  VIDEO REVIEW:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ve12LZoAvkY

I have a powerful computer, and the above problems experienced in person - on yet another powerful hardware was the same.  Today, it is already well but minor bugs related to graphics (textures) sometimes happen.  - Okay now time to move on to the more pleasant part of describing the game.  Plots game is great, but i shall not say anything about it. :) Graphically, the game looks very good, beautifully but darkly, like a post-apocalyptic atmosphere. The music is nice, it's OK. Polish Dubbing, because this version I have the pleasure to have - is good, you can safely compare it to that of Fallout 3.  To summarize the above, the game RAGE had a difficult beginning to the PC, but now it is a game in which you need to play - is simply very good.  I surely recommend!

-Orcs Must Die! 2-  Orcs must die 2 takes on the tower defense genre, which to many incuding myself have always ignored and just seen as a 'play for ten seconds then wait around for a wave to go by while you watch your defences do the work.' But considering refunds are now an option I thought heck this game is ranked as 'Overwhelmingly Positive' I may aswell tryi it. And what a great decision that was.  The game is still a tower defense game but with all the fast paced third person shooting, and mindless comedic slaughter of orcs you could ever want. It breaks away from the bog standard tower defense where you feel you are controlling the outcome of the wave rather than the AI controlled defences. One minute you are blastiing down an ogre with your blunderbuss and the next you are trying to quickly decide within the time limit what traps to put down to get the best possible rating. This game is lovingly crafted with a quirky art style incredibly optimized gameplay creating a nicely packed game that suits even the the biggest haters of tower defense.  Oh and not only this it comes with an astounding co-op that allows you and a friend to take on the entire campaign together with th difficulty cleverly scailed to the amount of players in a mission. To ensure balanced gameplay among other things.Truly a star studded preformance from Robot Entertainment!  9.5/10

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops II
review_text: Absolotely amazing game! I'm not talking about the abomination that Treyarch calls 'Multiplayer' or the horrible single player campaign! I'm talking about the zombies mode... The starting 'Green Run' Maps are decent, but lack in originality with very little 'Exciting' or 'Interesting' features. Nevertheless the maps are fun and kept me engaged in the game for roughly 240hrs of total gameplay... But they did eventually get boring and I came to the realisation that the Green Run maps don't really compare to some of the other Black Ops 1 maps. So I decided to spend $5 and purchase the Nuketown Zombies map. What an amazing map! And finally some excitement! The perks fall from the sky in small meteors and they brang back the firesale power-up, best of all, the map is absolutely packed with easter eggs! But thats not where it ends! The other dlc absolutely blew me away! Buried, Die Rise, Origins and of course Mob Of The Dead. Overall an amazing game for zombies!

Probes, the Matrix, puns, references, sing alongs, and an at times overwhelming sense of power. What's not to love? Bounding through the streets, sending anything in your path spiraling through the air, jumping over buildings, slamming into the ground from rediculous heights... What a rush. Saints Row IV is not a perfect experience by any means. It crashed a few times, you quickly become so overpowered that your chances of dying are miniscule unless you fall asleep on the keyboard (I didn't buy any of the damage resestence or health perks until the end of the game and only died a couple times), and some of the characters in your party have very little development before becoming largely inconsequential. But it's fun. It's a lot of fun. Easily one of the most overall enjoyable experiences I've had in an open world like this. Your powers are fun to use, and you'll find yourself not bothering to get in a car most of the time cause you can run much faster than you can drive. In a series that started out as a GTA ripoff, yes, you'll probably not get in a car very often. I know, how far the series has come, right? Let's not fool ourselves, this series has spun way out of control into coo coo territory, but it's mostly to the game's benefit.  Plot wise, it's pretty simple but gets the job done. There's few surprises once you're numb to the chaos. You gather your friends, take over the town, and then fight the big bad. Several of your friends are amazing and some of them are just kinda, you know, there. They exist. Some characters end up being throw away summons with little novelty. But you won't care, because you'll be laughing and kicking alien butt and feeling like a badass. The DLC scenarios were good, too. This game makes you feel good, what more do you need to know? Best third act I've seen in an action game, loved the ending and even the credits. I can't recommend this enough. Set aside your desires for realism and ride this crazy train.

It's Call of Duty... don't be surprised. Many have begun ripping into the Call of Duty franchise for their lack of originality. I suppose one could make that argument, but I think they would be missing the point.   I've never looked upon the Call of Duty franchise as a game, at least not a regular FPS. I enjoy Call of Duty for one simple reason, it's an experience. Period. It's about 6 to 8 hours of interactive movie making whereby you are along for the ride in typically fantastic, far from reality, scenes of epics battles where even the action will slow down and wait for you should you decide to stroll off to the side somewhere. Not to worry, they'll call you back soon enough.  I had some optimisation issues, but my rig has begun showing its age, so maybe just me. Otherwise, graphics - solid, controls - solid, sound - fantastic, voice - good.  In the end, if you're in the mood for an intereactive cinematic gameplay rush. This game is for you. If you're looking for a cutting edge FPS, keep on walkin' soldier. I liked it for what it is, an experience!

app_name: Dark Messiah of Might & Magic Single Player
review_text: Dark Messiah has been out for more than a few years now, but the game still provides a solid single-player experience. The story may not be that engaging, but the combat is fun and allows you to finish the game in a number of ways: you can either develop yourself as an assassin, a mage, just go for a hardcore berserker approach or develop for some sort of hybrid. The nice thing is that all of these feel like they are actually viable and fun options.  The game was based on the Source engine, meaning it also uses the physics made popular by Half-Life 2 to its fullest, admittedly even going a little overboard on it sometimes (or those orcs sure love having dem dere spikey racks all over the place). The use of the Source engine also means that Dark Messiah´s graphics have aged quite well.  All in all, a good 8/10.

app_name: Defender's Quest: Valley of the Forgotten
review_text: Clever is the word for this game. The gameplay is tight, well-balanced, and clever. Tower defense games are rarely this clever and the RPG system rewards good play. The downfall of virtually all RPG systems is a sense of grind - you want the best stuff, you're going to have to perform repetitive tasks until you've got the prerequisite numbers. Grinding reminds me of a job. Defender's Quest lets you bypass that - anything you'd have to grind for is optional, and if you want the optional content, then you can toggle the exp and gold gain. This also lets you set the difficulty to as difficult or as easy as you want.  The amazing thing is that the gameplay is so good, and the rest of the game is also so good. The graphics aren't the modern blockbusters, but the character art is charming and clever, and the dialog is well-written and allows you to connect to the characters. Fun gameplay, thoughtful gameplay, good art, good dialog, fun plot. The game can even be replayed, and the new game+ has additional insight into the world if you're that hooked. It also ramps up the difficulty if you like a challenge. To top it off, the game is inexpensive. Why have you not already bought it?

Where can i start to review this game? On the surface it's an old shool point-and-click adventure, but the story, the voice acting, the art and the setting are simply amazing.  Set in a post-apocalyptic world where machines have became sentient and capable of build (and re-build) themselves. In this world, humans are only a fading memory, kept in partially corrupted data gathered by some and spread in a form of a religion. Most of the world is now covered by the desert, where most of the androids live in a single huge city. This is the background where Horatio, the protagonist, starts its (his?) journey to find a stolen component of his ship, revealing part of his (and our) past in the process.  The amazing art (all hand-made) and the attention to details of the world makes me heavily recomend this game to any point-and-click adventures fan with a soft spot for the post-apocaliptic setting :)

Dying Light,  Updated Review: Worth the money, and first I said 'especially on sale'. However I take that back with this current review. The reason I am editing my review is because they updated it was a patch, and made the DLC 'the following'-enhanced edition, just a patch for the regular game. That right there shows that this developer cares about the gamers and isnt looking to jip us off with incredible amounts of DLC, nickleing us for every penny. They created a great DLC and released is as a patch. That deserves respect, especially in this gaming world we have today. And because of that, I say this game is worth the FULL PRICE.    Fun open world game with a zombie theme, that I found to be very enjoyable to play, even with my high standard of games.  Long Review  From the creators of Dead Island, comes this gem. Very much different then Dead Island, and for me this was a good thing. I got very bord with Dead Island extreamly quickly. With that in mind I was reluctant to buy this game. I am sure glad I did. The best way I can describe this game is a Farcry 3/4 feel but with a zombie twist. The free running is super fun and for the most part smooth. The survival mentality is really there, especially if you decide to run at night, which I do for the fun. Its super crazy when you have 3 killer special zombies running behind you as well as a horde, and you know if you mess up once your dead. There can be some really heart pumping moments in this game. The story line is so-so, but the gameplay is solid. I would pay for it again, I will continue to play it, and enjoy it. Its a game where you will play it for a long time, put it down a while, then a few months later pick it back up again and enjoy it just as much as the last time. 8/10--Very Enjoyable

Mark L. Lester is an exploitation filmmaker. Even though he eventually evolved as an artist to take on more mainstream efforts such as Stephen King’s Firestarter (1984), the sleazy side of Lester’s filmography never seemed to leave his visual vernacular when directing. This is, after all, the man who helmed the Claudia Jennings sex and violence romp, Truck Stop Women (1974) and Class of 1984 (1982), the “fear the students” opus that is coated in a sheen of punk rock grime (complete with one of the more disturbing ♥♥♥♥/revenge finales ever put to the screen). Despite the fact that future mega-producer Joel Silver, fresh off three Walter Hill films (including one personal favorite) and the teen hit Weird Science (1985), financed the film, Lester’s background in B-Movies is still very much felt in every frame. He was a man brought in to do a distinct job — guide the Austrian Oak to maximum kill mode while keeping his dialogue to an absolute minimum.

I hired a few more drivers and went through every menu I could click on and still cant figure it out. The bad menu layouts lack of meaningfull starter info and sloppy presentation would leave anyone standing there scratching thier head and randomly clicking icons. I wonder if they beta tested this game at all. If they did i would bet they did it in house with people who were already too familiar with the code. Next time before releasing a game if you really cant't afford good outsourced beta testing put out a simple craigslist ad offering to pay teenagers to play a new game. Or hell just bring it home and let a family member or friend try it. Or even just follow the lazy trend of big dev's and release it in 'beta' let people pay you to beta test your stuff and you can blame the whole crappy mess on it 'still being in beta and requiring community feedback'.

I got this as part of some Humble Bundle, and at one point, I played it. And even though this game really suffers from an inherent lack of polish, I found myself really wanting to like this game. It bring some solid concepts forward to the table as a shooter, such as the hacking interface. It's sort of like Watchdogs as an FPS with this; scan security cameras to check enemy locations and numbers, set up environmental hazards up for remote detonated traps, and even use some of the machinery for surprise attacks. It has good ideas... BUT...  There's a lot wrong with this game. On a presentation scale, it's easily the worst part about the game. For a game released in '07, it looks like it comes from '02, and really doesn't have a lot of the aged visuals that can still look good in the modern day with games like, for instance, Unreal Tournament '99. The voice acting is atrociously bad, though it sounds more like bad localization and English dubs, and a lot of the sound quality is atrocious.  Gunplay is enjoyable, and some of the weapon designs like the gatling rifle and the sniper rifle are appealing, but the difficulty is extremely harsh, and the AI is pretty unforgiving. Even with patches to fix this, it still does put a damper on the gameplay as a whole.  So, I still do find myself really wanting to like this. It's a decently interesting package that does seem appealing, but suffers from some serious issues that drag it down hard. There are definitely other games out there more worth your money, but if it shows up in a good Humble Bundle or something, maybe give it a gander.

I had previously written a review for this game saying that it was amazing and that I loved it. Well, I'm here to change my mind. It was NOT worth $60. Maybe like $20 or $25. Even with lots of bugs and the possibility that it was falsly advertised, it makes it seem a bit questionable. I did enjoy it for a while, but then I noticed things getting very repetitive. Scan, discover, upload, repeat. Scan, discover, upload, repeat. Repeat. Repeat... And with a lot of the 'randomly created creatures' appearing multiple times on different planets, it gets annoying. And gathering resources for your ship: land, gather, refuel, take-off, warp, repeat. Land, gather, refuel, take-off, warp, repeat. Repeat. Repeat... And with no story or anything other than a destination to get to, it just gets boring and uninteresting really fast. After nearly 60 hours in this game trying to give it a chance, I just lost interest and uninstalled it.  If you want a space exploration game, this is not it. There's no mutiplayer. You can only craft parts, weapon, suit, multitool and ship upgrades. There is no building ability. Perhaps if building structures were a thing it would be a little more interactive. But then it would just seem like a glorified version of Minecraft.

review_text: Words cannot express the amount of regret I have for even getting this game in a byproduct of a Humble Bundle. With a plotline that could give a nonsequetor episode of Spongebob Squarepants a run for its money as far as the ability to engage anyone above a second grade reading level, calling this game a 'murder mystery' is akin to calling a go-cart a road ready, fully functioning vehicle.   If this had been a gift, I'd have stopped talking to the person to gift it.  Not only are the 'puzzles' shoddy at best, there's no question that this is a point-and-click game that does little more than engage you to blindly find the cat. find the rat. find the bat. Resist the urge to shoot yourself in the wallet and uninstall Steam entirely.   The voice acting sounds like someone pulled the most culturally offensive sound files of a drunken party in New Zealand and claimed it was British with American overtones. I'm not sure if the girl in the opening cutscene 'screamed' or if she stubbed her toe and let out a weak whine while running from a snake monster that looks like Voldemort got a little too friendly with one of his pets and created the abomination that is the antagonist.   This game made me want to die.

Overall I'm pretty satisfied with Obduction.  It's very much like its predecessors, where you find yourself in an alien world, and after exploring long enough, you will find you can teleport to a few other worlds. The worlds are very beautiful and imaginative, and are fully realized by the first person real-time rendering. Your progression in these worlds is based on your ability to solve logical and practical puzzles, typically by operating some form of machine in the right way and at the right time. Observation and attention to detail are very important in determining how things are supposed to work as there is rarely an operations manual for anything you find.   The game has a thin story to it, enough to lightly tug you from start to finish, and enough supporting lore to give you a general idea of what has happened in these worlds. It is on par with the other Myst games, with perhaps less emphasis on individual characters and personal drama.  My biggest complaint with the game is how short and easy it is. I read every journal I came across and got both primary endings, and despite feeling in general like I was taking my time, I still managed to clock in at 10 hours. Myst games can be downright frustrating, and Obduction is definitely not that. So, this might be a relief to some who do not look back as fondly as I do on them. I still found the game very enjoyable, but I feel like at least one world was almost entirely scrapped, and there did seem to be a lack of complex puzzles.   As far as performance goes, the game runs beautifully on my 980ti. I have a 120hz monitor and the game kept up 120 fps most of the time, which was really a sight to see. Graphically the game is very impressive, probably one of the best looking indie games. As an aside, I can't wait to see more games use UE4 if this was any indication of what to expect.  I definitely hope to see more from Cyan as well.

This game has amazing gameplay and new features that make it great for a sandbox experience. However the reason why I'm not recommending it, is because the story and characters are so underwhemling I can't take the game seriously. The idea behind this game is amazing. An assassin turning to the templars and killing them. It's something we saw and experienced in Assassin's Creed 3. However we see the turn for him is...easy. he turns into a templar quickly and without serious thought to reprocussions. He was raised to hate templars and we see our main character turning to them instantly? It feels like I was robbed of a fluid storyline. I pushed this off to continue playing anyway. The gameplay was fun and I couldn't wait to see how far they would go. Turns out not far at all. The game is about six hours long and ends without any real personal change on my part. Usually with assassin's creed games I feel like I've grown with the character. At the end of this one I feel the lingering thought of 'That's it?' and so is my experience with the game. The main character doesn't have all that much substance, I don't see enough of the templars and their plots. And the assassins seem like cookie cutter villains that pretend to be heros but it still doesn't work. You can't just make the good guys the bad guys in such a rushed way like they did here.  I'd say don't get it. Go for another assassin's creed game. This one is worth a watch but not worth your money. If you do want to see more of it. Check out the 20 dollar version. Don't bother with deluxe. But I recommend waiting for a sale if you value well spent money.

Playing this again, I realized that I still remembered some of the tunes, and a few of the puzzles The more expansive locations (especially rubacava) are just amazingly fun, while the smaller glue sections like the petrified forest seem like a chore these days. The graphics look the same as I remember them, which is a great result on a remaster of such an old game, because in reality when I switch to the original texturs I see just how pixely they really were.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: It starts out well enough with a rather good storyline and you feel like you actually have choices and you are a huge part of the story. However, should you ever replay the game (and you most likely will), you will find that what you do matters little. You will be screwed over either way and every episode makes it worse without taking any of your choices into account.   The last episode however is where the ♥♥♥♥ really hits the fan and the Game seemingly just wish to mock you to play it. In an attempt to recreate the Game of Thrones storytelling, they seem to forget its not about who dies, its about who lives and how. Its worth playing once but only if you are actually into Game of Thrones. Otherwise, feel free to give this one a pass.

There exist 5 routes to choose from and every route has can have one of two endings, a bad ending and a good ending. This visual novel is worth its price if you are looking to purchase one with a long playtime, took me 50+ hours to complete all routes and endings.   Pro's -Simple choice system. -Lots of content. -Nice visuals. -Lots of humour. -It removed the H scenes  Con's -It removed the H scenes. -Humour can sometimes come out cringey. -Have to go offline on steam to play so that people won't judge you. I’m not a “VIDEO games” person but a “video GAMES” one. You won’t see me praising a game like Heavy Rain because of its dull gameplay mechanics, neither over-crediting it for its TV movie-quality plot, what you would read from me about Telltale’s The Walking Dead series is that they are good interactive movies, perhaps calling them good “games” would be hard for me. But this one, this one it’s a bold act of rebellion, even when the game itself is nothing impressive, it is one of those few cases where I'm inclined to recommend a game I didn't enjoyed a lot playing to.  Spec Ops: The Line should be played, it deserves to be played, at least to experience what it does well and how it was a failed but respectable attempt to make a AAA game with an actually adult narrative and distancing itself from the cheap “play this to feel more mature” trend on gaming narrative that started more or less a couple of years after the 7th generation of consoles.   The GAME  In Spec Ops: The Line you won’t find a hidden gem with terrific mechanics, in fact, the game feels like a tasteless Gears of War clone, it feels like the genre of the game was chosen just to appeal to a bigger audience, if they had developed a top-down shooter or a strategy game a la “XCOM: Enemy Unknown” they would had saved a considerable amount of money, and even been able to invest more on its gameplay and get more or less the same sales they got (roughly 1.13M according to VGChartz).  This doesn’t mean the game is horrible, in fact, It’s decent, and even good at times, it isn’t boring and it’s A.I. isn’t bad, for example, in the hardest difficulty (which is the only one I played), it also has a fresh level design which helps to bring variety to the experience, using verticality pretty more often than third and first person shooters regularly do. So, in short, the game is not painful to play, but it isn’t also memorable gameplay-wise and you will feel no need to replay it.   The Portrait  One of the three things this game does right, and the first of them you will notice, is its environment, the ashes of Dubai are real, and not because the game is ridiculously photorealistic (which is not), but because it’s meant to communicate something, not a feeling, not a direct message, but a story, you know something more than a sandstorm happened there. The devastation and helplessness, the man-made disaster over the implacable overtaking of nature against a metropolis turning it into a no man’s land are tangible.   The designers did a good job (most of the time) trying to immerse the player by mixing the “COVER HERE PLOX” structures among the rest of the environment and they try to constantly draw the players towards what is left of the civilization to give us a break from the sand. The people designing its environment probable spent most of the time guessing: What would people do if “this” happened? The environment results believable and satisfying to watch too.  The Chant  The sound effects are decent and the voice acting accomplishes what it’s meant for. What the game does well is the music, instead of using an over the top generic film soundtrack, the game uses a soundtrack of rock music and its specter of derived genres, constantly portraying what is happening in front of you, sometimes even with some bit of irony, most of it has heavy feelings and a couple of tracks which could be considered as portraying an 'angry' or 'desperate' feeling, it counts with names as Nine Inch Nails, Deep Purple and Jimmy Hendrix.  The music also lets the sound effects of the sandstorms, broken glasses and guns take the main role when it’s needed. The radio signals also play an important role in the immersion and are a nice way of telling us what’s happening instead of leaving such task in its entirety to the cut-scenes.  Another interesting part related to the story is what I would consider as a 'cliche' being used many, many times in games to tell us the 'background' of the story, is the way of letting us get into the story by finding some intel recordings through the map, which may I say there is nothing bad with it, mostly because it's related to an already interesting story, but it's just another 'checklist' type of feature, similar to its completely unnecessary multiplayer.  The Book  The story of Spec Ops: The Line is based in a classic war novel called “Hearth of Darkness” written by the Polish Author Joseph Conrad (something the writers of this game don't try to hide) and they let you know that in the first moments of the game (I highly recommend the book, ONCE you have played the game).  The story revolves around the 33rd Battalion, a combat unit sent to help Dubai’s citizens to evacuate after a series of sandstorms downplayed or ignored by the high class and politicians of the city turned it into remnants of what it was. The 33rd was thought lost, but suddenly the US Government gets a looped message from the Colonel John Konrad about the situation of the city and the failure of its mission.   The game starts with the three-man operative from the Special Forces Delta conformed Martin Walker, John Lugo and Alphanso Adams entering to Dubai and trying to find what happened to the 33rd. The game has some twists, moral decisions, and many hard moments, it portrays war as what it is: murder, and a side trying to conquer the other, while its portrayal of the “occupation” is way lighter than how the book is based on portrays colonization, it’s very admirable to have people getting out of the safe-zone of bro-patriotic-BS found in most AAA war games, with silly “good vs. bad” plots. While it doesn’t goes deep about the political causes of a conflict, it’s a reminder of how distorted a leader’s mind can be and/or how harmful can people be by justifying their narcissistic needs by making up “the enemy” as “the evil” as an excuse for the urge to fight that “evil” which isn’t more than a result of our a need to rationalize the actions we commit or condone, probably our only scapegoat as simple mortals in one of the sides; a way of finding inner peace, perhaps full of emptiness, but enough to let us believe our own lie. First let’s take action; justification can come later as corpses can’t reply anyway. We are all heroes.  Score: 60/100.

Positive: -Really nice all-round arcadish game. It's desined for casuals imo, but can be setup to be fairly challenging/realistic. -The menu looks nice, and it's easy-to-use. -Gameplay is cool - carrier mode is entertaining -, cca 20-25 hours worth of variety. Completing the game though takes more than twice this time - calculation based on my progress -  Negative: -After every single race you have to watch how you did in the sponsorship challanges. Because of this, restarting a race after finishing it takes cca. 20 seconds, which can get extremly annoying after 3-4 consequent tries. -Too much camerashake and loud impactsounds during the listing of winnings / sponsorships. -Replay system is quite basic - no save, no option to hide overlay for recording purposes, no roundbased skip only fastforward-. -Drift scoresytem is weird, sometimes you get points for understeer slide, sometimes you get 0 for perfect drift.  About the ridiculous assists: -If you use keyboard, traction control is not an option, it's a MUST. Drift challanges are impossible with keyboard! -Stability control OFF means almost no front grip. ON setting makes the cars handle like they should.  More about Stability control: With OFF setting: AI will laugh at you in any corner. In addition they can push you out, but you cant do the same to them. Recommended if you like unfair challanges. With ON setting: It makes your RWD car handle like it should realistically with mild stability control. With this assist you can drive circles around the AI on higher difficulty levels.

A game that is well done. It lacks multiplayer, it lacks online features all together, but it doesn't matter. It's well done.  If you are looking for a game that puts single player immersion and great story telling first, this one's for you. If you are looking for incredibly brutal and violent shootouts, this one's for you. I've finished the game on the second hardest diffulty in about 14 hours. It's not incredibly long but it played like an interactive movie. I got to bond with the colourful characters and epic storyline. The game doesn't throw enemies at you for the full 14 hours. There is a nice balance between blood pumping action, some R&R at the make shift home base and story telling. The cutscenes are in my opinion movie worth. The only downside I found was the sometimes clumsy AI. Enemies don't always react the way you think they would react in a similar situation. But it's a small speck of negativity on an overall fantastic experience.   The game divides itself into two 'seperate' storylines. I put seperate in quotation marks since I don't find it fair to call them that. They are very similar although the enthousiasts among us (I count myselft to be one) will see the difference. The game has however only one ending.   Graphics are great . I run it on a GTX 770, 8 Gb mem, i7 cpu and everythings set to ultra without framerate drops.   Thank you for reading this. I hope you will enjoy this game as much as I have!

Pros: Great graphics Interesting story Great character delopment (They could've done so much bad if they'd made Laura helpless but she's strong willed, cleaver, and has great instinct.) Good progression (Only 4 weapons but as you upgrade them their appearance changes and they gain more functionality, skills give you more options in combat, even character progression as she learns to trust her own ability.) Options tombs if you want addition challenge (I didn't care much for other tomb raider games so I skipped these.)  Cons: Kind of short Quick time events (I hate them so) To many close calls (This seems odd but you know movies that try to have non-stop action, but as a result you kind of get used to the constant danger and it loses effect. There were so many times in this game she just narrowly escapes that it kind of reminds you it's a game and breaks immersion. You climb burning buildings 3 or so times so it ends up becoming commonplace. Still doesn't take much away from the overall gameplay.)   Definitely a good game to buy.

Guns of Icarus Online is a multiplayer online game that pits crews of airships against one another in the daring aerial combat that has made the series famous among youtube stars. The game revovles around the close-knit teamwork of four players working together to make their ship a combat threat to their opponents, while coordinating with other teams of four on their team.  Pilots learn to commnad their crews into full effect, managing the actions of their gunners and engineers to lead their ship to victory. Meanwhile the engineer has the job of maintaining the combat effectiveness of your ship by repairing the ship. Gunners do the heavy lifting in regards to firing weapons. Together you can help design a ship, crew loadouts, to lead your team to victory in a variety of thematic environments and scenarios.  Overall the game has an emphasis on teamwork and communication, and as such can be more of a niche game than the standard pick-up and play games that one would expect in this genre.  It must be noted however, there is a large skill gap in place, that the recently (10/12/2014) implimented match-making system is far from perfect, and that the issues of sexual harrassment towards women are very real. Overall a pleasurable eperience if you play with friends, or form bonds with crews that you can continue to play with.

Some bad failings aside this is the most innovative CoD game in years. If you're rating this game on singleplayer, you're a joke. The amount of players getting CoD or BF games for single player at this point is so small it's not even worth measuring.  With the major changes to the class system to contextual changes to objects in the environment over to the characters calling out enemy positions this game is just a lot of fun. Weapons feel a little more balanced compared to Blops 2 and MW3. Assault rifles feel a lot more on par with SMGs.  Lag compensation still needs to go and continuing to take choice away from players (no filters on matchmaking at all) are both frustrating.

Very good puzzles. OK game.  As said above, the puzzles are very good in this game. The concept is that you can make 5 clones of you and they move the same way that you do. Using this power, you have to hit switches, patches of no clones, and gravity patches in order to collect orbs to get to the next set of puzzles. The puzzles are very similar in style but each one has a unique approach which makes them very enjoyable. The puzzles are actually a challange (I had to look a few up) and are actually fun.  Some other positives are that the graphics are pretty damn good for an indie game. They arent the most realisitic and dont vary too much. However they look very nice. The music is also well put togther with soft piano riffs showing a calmness of silence and space and the silence of rocks (we'll get to it) with a bit of horror mixed in to show the death and destruction that happened abord the ship.   That being said. The rest of the game is kinda weird. The story is that people find rocks that are 'alive' and they murder everyone on the ship you crashlanded on. You encounter one other person who both shuns you and guides you to move to the next areas before you two die as well. The story is kinda out there and the other character and the logs you find also ask very specific questions about what is death, what is life, what is consciousness, and what is 'alive'. Although normally something like this interests me, it was just a bit too out there and not concrete enough for me. Maybe you'll like it. And there is a dual choice at the end which means you either youtube it or play the whole game again.  The character movement for this game was done with clay and it shows because its not very fluid or realistic but its minor. The controls are fine and simple but there is a problem with accuracy with it being hard to be on the edge of soemthing.  Overall, buy for the great puzzles or the (maybe) philosophical intrigue.

Overall opinion:  Pros: -Great graphics -Detailed interior of real locomotives -Real rail companies such as DB, ÖBB, SNCF... -Beautiful DLC routes with nice scenarios included -Quick drive -You can create your own train set -Some scenarios include real time timetable -Non stop realising of new DLC content -Quality sounds -Steam workshop where you can daily find new scenarios created by community  Cons: -Poor supply of routes, locos when buying vanilla train simulator - forces you to constantly buy more & more -Overpriced DLC contents -DLC locos include scenarios only for one specific route  General personal rating (8/10)

This game relies way too much on darkness to make its puzzles way more difficult than they need to be. For example, there is a section with a memory-type puzzle, where you have to step on a grid of tiles in the correct sequence to pass. However, the floor is completely dark, so you can't even tell which tile you're standing on. Even if you've memorized the sequence, you still have to make leaps of faith in the darkness and hope you land on the right tile.  If it takes you 2 hours to solve a puzzle that could be solved in 10 seconds with the lights on, that's a pretty poorly designed puzzle.

'In the Beast Boxing Leagues of Beasthalla, the strongest monsters fight for fame and fortune.' You are Char, a great fan of Beast Boxing. You dream abotu one day becomming a beast boxer yourself. But there is one problem: you are a human from the slums and humans are not allowed to compete. So you buy a monster costume and trick your way to a qualifying match for the Bush League, the lowest league there is. And that is where the game starts and you start to jab, cross, hook and uppercut yourself to fame and fortune. And eventually realize that youa re fighting for more than just your dream, but human rights and the place of humanity in a beasty world.  The controls are fairly simple, be it controller or keyboard, requiring only either an analogue stick and two buttons, or the arrow keys and two punching keys. As you progress through the game, you are tought to block, dodge, counter and preserve your stamina through a series of optional tutorial fights. There are 12 opponents in total to unlock and you can do exhibition matches against any opponent you have defeated. Every fight nets you cash. This you can use to buy better equipment and to train your skills. Sometimes you may need to take a step back and practice with weaker opponents, earn some cash and upgrade your equipment before being able to proceed through harder leagues. On normal difficulty, the story mode took me just under 3 hours. There is a New Game Plus though where opponents are tougher, faster and hit harder. And there is an Endless mode, where you can test your boxing skills and see how long you can last. There can be a bit of frustration when opponents seemingly hover just outside your reach and none of your punches connect. But perseverance will see every beast that gets in your way K.O. on the ground.  Overall a very fun little game that keeps entertaining far beyond a single playthrough.

I wanted to write a lengthily and detailed review about how much I love this game. But I don't think any number of words can truly captivate how much I enjoyed this compelling and beautiful experience.   Dislaimer: I did not play the Witcher 1 or 2. You can still pick up and enjoy this game with no prior experience, especially if you do a little reading on the previous games.   Breath taking environments. Atmospheric. Gorgeous.   Quests -- both main and side -- that make you think and make hard decisions that can shape how the game plays out. I've never been so engaged in quests. I paid attention to every single one I came across, even the seemingly insignificant side quests. Some were brutal. Some were tragic. Some were sad. Some were humorous. And some were even happy. Emotions were evoked.   Much of the dialogue is clever. The characters are well crafted and interesting. They often respond to your actions and choices with their own persona. The voice acting is great. And the colorful language used is hilarious in its own right.   Ciri. My favorite thing about this game was the vibrance and attitude of Ciri. And Geralt in the Feline armor set. And his hair. And Ciri's eyes. And the story. And the quests. And all the question marks on the map to find. And the fluid animations. And Roach. This game was my favorite thing about this game.  But truly. This is a masterpiece with so much thought and love and care put into its creation. It is master story telling. Gripping. Engaging. Provoking. Every time I took a 'wrong' turn I was devastated. At the very end I repeated to myself what I wanted the ending to be and hoped for it because it meant that much -- after 100hrs I needed a good ending!  My only gripes are minor. Some of the controls are clunky when you are in tight spaces or trying to loot boxes. Occassionally there is a graphic glitch -- like seeing people walking in the background during a cutscene with their hand's missing. Or Ciri missing her scabbard in some scenes. Or some decor on characters vibrating/jiggling. All of which are forgivable and don't break the game. The dev's release patches every so often, which is always a good sign. Speaking of signs, I will agree with others that the combat is a bit repetitive. But there are times it feels truly epic and challenging. And this combat isn't meant to be flashy with tons of flourish and effects and combo button mashing.   10/10 would spend another 100hrs lost in this game.
