I was excited to play this game. I love the concept and the art style. However, I did not enjoy it, as the controls felt sloppy and unresponsive, and the game looked oddly choppy, as if it was missing frames during most animations. This problem was large enough that I ultimately found playing to be a chore rather than a joy.

A solid story and interesting setting that are marred by the game's execution. The actual strategy mechanics are extremely basic and slow, the UI is sluggish and glitchy, and the engine has some significant problems. Graphics and animations tend to not be important (see: Avernum) but they have the same stupifying effect that they did in Wasteland 2. It's like playing with Lego men. The UI's problems really manifest in the boring virtual reality/rigging sections, where buttons, skills, and movements frequently freeze the game and require bizarre input sequences to fix.   The combat rebalance mod takes some of frustration and monotony out of the system but a Shadowrun game shouldn't need a day 1 overhaul mod like a Bethesda game. I enjoyed the story enough that I wanted to play the fan campaigns and main story again, but trying to restart the game with the terrible beginning characters was too droll to tolerate. The skills require heavy point investment to become useful so the builds lack variety. At some point you get three instead of two action points which makes combat less tedious. The downside is that the AI never gets this bonus and the combat sequences become too simple. Instead of making smarter opponents the game just throws waves of them at you.  For the price I paid for this on sale ($1.27?) it was a good game, but the dev studio has a lot of work to do if they want to cultivate a fanbase with this new series. This is the director's update of an improved expansion to the first game, which means I have no desire to play the original reboot and am avoiding the next installment (Hong Kong). People comparing this to strategy games like X-Com or Jagged Alliance in their reviews are really overselling it.

DB:XV is a solid Dragon Ball game that definitely takes its place as one of the best releases in the Franchise. I've logged 10 hours on PC with this game, and over 30 on the Xbox One version.  The style of this game feels quite familar to an MMO with lite fighting mechanics and a mission structure for progression. The 'MMO' vibe comes from the hub world that you move around between missions, visiting shops for items or skills, talking with trainers (character from DBZ) for side missions and extra moves, and interacting with other players when in a Multi Lobby.  You create your own custom character from a pool of 5 races, customize them to your liking, and then you're thrown into one of the most refreshing Dragon Ball storylines in recent memory. Instead of fighting your way through the DBZ sagas as we're done in games past, XV is an entirely new story focused around time travel, and dealing with 'What If' scenarios that could have happened during the story of DBZ. The story starts off strong, but then seems to lose steam during the middle act, before a satisfying and climactic finale. The fighting mechanics, while interesting, feel a little on the light side, with easy to memorize combos, and a selection of special strike or Ki moves mapped to other controls (Trigger+Button on controller). Unfortunately, after hours and hours of missions, the fighting system leaves more to be desired. That's not to necessarily say that it's bad, it just makes you wish there was a little more complexity to it.  I'd definitely recommend this game for any fan of the Dragon Ball franchise. Though, if you're aching for a game that is a little heavier on the fighting system, I'd recommend looking for the Budokai HD collection on consoles.

This is a Doom Clone set in the Star Wars universe.  Pros:  It has all of the good traits that Doom Clones have, such as no cover, more than two weapon slots, and non-linear maps.  The gunplay feels solid and gunning down enemies is satisfying.  The story is pretty good for a FPS released only a year and a few months after Doom II. It involves really good voice acting for the time period and some cut scenes that I imagine looked pretty good in 1995 but now have a certain retro charm to them.  The soundtrack really makes the most of it's MIDI music, and most of the songs still hold up today, especially the opening song. Unless you don't like MIDI music, in which case you probably won't like this game's music.  The environment is well designed, and does a good job capturing the feel of wherever the level is supposed to be set. This is an area that I always thought that Doom I and II frequently fell short, so this is rather big deal.  Cons   This is a FPS with LIMITED LIVES. You get three in the beginning, and you can find more hidden in the levels. This 'feature' is definitely going to make a few people not interested in this game anymore, but it isn't as big of a deal as it sounds.  You can't save your game mid-level. Even Wolfenstein 3D had that feature, and it also had limited lives, so I can't see why they didn't put that in this game.   As with many early Doom Clones/FPSs, to aim up and down, you have to press Page Up and Page Down. However when you aim at an enemy on a higher level than you are aiming, the game automatically detects that you are aiming at the enemy and your blaster shots automatically aim up. Sometimes this works, sometimes it doesn't. However, this isn't that big of a deal in the end. Fortunately, the game supports mouse control for looking left and right.  Like other Doom Clones, the game is rather pixelated. However, you can use XL Engine (a source port of this game and several others) to fix this, as well as the aiming up/down issue.  Easily the biggest problem present in this game, as well as many other Doom Clones, is that the non-linear levels act as a double-edged sword. On one hand, it can be quite liberating to play a fairly non-linear FPS, as you aren't being pushed down a corridor like in Call of Duty. On the other hand, it can be difficult at times to figure out where you are supposed to go next. This can result in a frustrating 'Where do I go now?' feeling compounded by the fact that you usually aren't doing a lot of shooting while looking where to go next.   Bottom Line  If you are searching for a retro FPS to wash the foul taste of today's ultra-linear FPSs, this is a game for you. If you like Star Wars, I can't recommend this game to you unless you also like retro first-person-shooters. Honestly, this type of game is rather dated in some ways, so it isn't for everybody. But if you do like Star Wars and retro first-person shooters, you should buy this right now.  Similar games: Wolfenstein 3D Spear of Destiny Doom Doom II Heretic Hexen Duke Nukem 3D Rise of the Triad Blake Stone Redneck Rampage Blood   TIP: Press F5 to turn on a flashlight. This seriously helps in some of the darker levels.

This game has a great idea and for the most part it is well executed but it is not perfect.  Combat and map design  The combat in the game feels satisfying and is fast paced. It does feel unbalanced at times and very unfair when you are easily cut down because your shotgun takes three hits to kill, I assume to force you to work as a team, but team play means there needs to be at least a two to one ratio of players if you want a reliable kill. So there is no “one person defends while the other diffuses a bomb” because you will both be killed.  Map design is my major problem with the game though, it feels like a mismatch with the style of game-play, the combat is quick requiring multiple hits to get a kill but the environments feel closed off like a twitch shooter like “Call of Duty”,  often leaving you trapped in a corner because there is no room to retreat.  Connection\\servers  I have an average router playing on relative low density server but I still have a problem with regular lag which happens two or three times a match which is unforgivable for a solely online game.  Corporate stuff  In-game purchases, I have to call this one out. The game uses a character rotation system similar to league of legends, where you have most of the characters only unlocked when they are on rotation. Unless you give them in-game credits or pay £5-8 ($8-12) for it, and the pay for real but is before the pay with credits button on the display page.  Overall opinon I would recommend this game despite its problems it is after all still in beta.

Firstly, alot of the negative reviews you will see will be because the game had a very buggy launch. It has now been officially three weeks since the game has been out to people who bought the early access bundles and the game is a blast. If you enjoyed Ragnarok but were disappointed in Ragnarok 2, then it is almost a guaruntee that you will enjoy this game!  This game is beautiful not only in the sense of its amazing art style and music but in the freedom you get with making a character specific to your playstyle. There are over 80 classes each which can be ranked up to three or two circles. Plenty of fun quests and dungeons, and even hidden quests and dungeons as well. Boss battles are suprisingly fun as well; some ofcourse more than others. I cannot talk about the pvp or gvg aspect because inorder to create a guild you need to be a specific class and that class is an end tier job. At first I thought this was a little disconcerting but then I realised that having the game like this makes it so that it is not overcrowded by thousands of guilds and ensures that the guild leader will competent seeing that he will be sacrificing an end tier class to dedicate to the creation and upkeep of a guild.  art/graphics 10/10 music 9/10 gameplay 8/10 replayability high  noteworthy cons: The game may crash or disconnect sometimes. It is more rare now but still happens. The game has just been released recently. If there are any major server issues the devs have been compensating the community rather well so far.

My potato PC could run this in 60 FPS with a few tweaks. - Elphelt Valentine - Good story, it's kinda cringeworthy in some moments, but overall it's really nice IMO. - Dual Audio, you could switch between English and Japanese Audio - Elphelt Valentine  But, the game currently is region locked for ranked match. What I liked: The graphics upgrade was fairly good in comparison to similar games, and quite a step above the previous offerings of Fallout:3 and Fallout:NV. The inclusion of the Lone Wanderer perk to give you a companion free adventuring option, even though that does gimp you from the story aspect of travelling with companions.  What I disliked: Companions are annoying. They never follow smartly. They are always either too far away or right in your way. And nothing gets old quite as fast as an NPC telling you they cant reach something that's literally right next to them.   The radiant quest system is a very poor attempt at making a static quest world appear more vibrant. Everybody has already seen the memes makig fun of settlers who complain about a problem 'right around the corner' only to have that problem actually be on the other side of the map. I'm also very bothered by how unintelligently the radiant quest system seems to operate. I can recall three specific instances where I was asked to do a quest for the exact same settlement several times in a row before I had even made it to the majority of available settlements. And, tbh, I really hate it when my log gets cluttered with a bunch of radiant quests, especially when they are repeats. I am a bit anal about keeping my questlog as clean as possible during my adventures, but if I've already handled Ghouls, Super-Mutants and Kidnappings at Oberland Station, I'm not really looking forward to going back there to find out what these useless ♥♥♥♥♥♥ are whining about this time.  Following from that, it's amazing to me how fast your quest log can get choked up with these mindlessly repetative radiant quests without having a simple way to remove or refuse those quests. If you're on a PC you can go through the hassle of using console commands, but god help you if you're a platform gamer.  But really, my biggest gripe with this latest installment of Fallout is how insubstantial the quests really are ultimately. The few factions that actually matter in this game (Minutemen, Railroad, Brotherhood of Steel, and Institute) basically trick and trap you unapologetically into getting into situations that no sane person would ever get into. You are locked into a linear, non-variable path with those factions until you reach a critical nexus where a quest for one of those faction requires you to betray 1, 2 or all three of the other relevant factions. That's not choice, that's the illusion of choice. What would have been truly inspiring is that if you go through the trouble of getting all these factions to accept you and depend on you, you could find some way to end things without being required to betray one (or multiple) factions that you've spent hours getting to trust you. Purge the Institute of it's elitist/sepratist faction and bring them back into the fold of the commonwealth, not nuke them from the face of the earth. Assassinate the charismatic militant leader of the BoS and find someone like Elder Lyons to take his place, someone with more concern about the well-being of normal people than their own agenda of avarice and fear, not blow up their fancy air ship.  No matter what you choose in the end it ultimately feels forced and unsatisfying.  Ultimately, the game feels like a fixed horse race. You pick your pony and then make sure he wins. End of story. Roll credits.  There are other petty gripes, mostly related to the forced gathering aspect of the game. Having to endlessly scrounge through ruins for aluminum cans and magnifying glasses just so I can make a scope for a new weapon is not compelling game play. It's filler. And whats more, it's boring. It's so boring that I usually stop playing every time I have to do it.  Perhaps I have simply set my sights a little too high and hoped a little too strongly for this game. After all, I've been pre-ordered since June, but I really wanted a lot more depth and story-telling(creating) from this game than I've gotten. I'm going to hit 'Yes' and recommend this game, but only becasue if you are a fan of the Fallout franchise you have to play it for yourself... even if only once, but I don't think I could bring myself to recommend this game to someone who has never played a Fallout game before. It does not feel like a complete, whole thing. It comes across as half-formed and barely breathing.

I am a huge Graphics Gale Fan and hesitated to move out of it for a long time. But I finally gave Aseprite another chance this year and it quickly became my favorite art program for Pixel Art. There is so many good things about this program and I think its trailer does a very good job saying its features. I used the offline version the most for 2 weeks straight, 6 hrs a day, to create badges for RPG Maker MV. So I have played around most of its features.  If there are some things I enjoy about it the most:  > The color wheel (the option to be not square) is greatly appreciated. Especially since I find it easier to pick colors from there.  > The way it handles palettes is a lot better than any pixel art program I used. It's easy to use and doesn't require me to go through much hoops to save.  > It has an animation timeline for animation. Similar to flash/toon boom/other animation programs. That is very much appreciated. Additionally, it has a separate preview for animation. I can edit the frames themselves, similar to graphics gale, than adjust the canvas position for the next 'sprite sheet frame' and edit that. I hate it when other art programs physically stops me from touching the canvas to do adjustments while watching the motion. It is infuriating.   > Adjustable grid sizes and it doesn't have to 'constrain proportions' all the time. If I want a 43x50 sprite grid I am going to get it and I love this program for that. Other art programs doesn't let you do this and it drives me crazy.  > It feels great to draw in asesprite with a tablet. Which is a big factor for me. I do not use the mouse when doing pixel art. There is no dumb delay whenever I draw as well. Overall, it's sweeeeeet.  > The UI feels great and fun.  I could go on but if you happen to have the same workflow as I do, give it a go.

I suppose the obvious thing to say is that if you liked the original Dead Space then you will enjoy the sequel. There are a couple of differences in the gameplay. Where in the original, you were wandering around a spaceship and could 'revisit' certain areas by using the map, so if you needed ammo, you pulled up the map and found the nearest store (or bench or whatever you needed) In the sequel you just head on forward to the next save point in the style of a more traditional arcade shooter, so the game does lose something there.  What the game does really well is to make you feel like a badass with a gun, fighting, shooting and chopping off limbs of various aliens is very satisfying indeed, sadly the progression of the game itself lets the whole thing down. You basically head from one point to the next only to find out that you need to fix/repair/switch on something... and when you have done that, you head off to the next thing that will need to be fixed/repaired/switched on before you can continue on your quest.   At around 12-15 hours, the game surprisingly starts to wear out it's welcome at around the 10 hour mark, as it begins to feel like the story is stretched out unnecessarily to make the game just that little bit longer. Just when you think you are done, you have to find yet another switch, or head outside and switch more things on. Luckily, the shooting mechanics are great as are the graphics and sound, so that helps make the experience much more enjoyable. This game is fun at first, but it gets very repetitive by the time you're halfway through.  It would be more accurate to call this a stealth game than a sniper game, though chances are most of the time you'll get discovered and it'll be a straight up shoot-out anyway. The game flies in the face of game logic that bringing a sniper rifle to a firefight means insta-death for you, with it expecting you to use the rifle in close-quarters combat, as the regular machine guns are the useless weapons this time round.  What you're left with is basically a shoot-em-up, going from point A to B, and repeat, plowing through firefights as you go. You might be expecting some sort of sniper simulator, but even in this game you're a super soldier single-handedly winning the war by wading into battlegrounds, walking up to enemies and shooting them no problem. On harder difficulties it instead turns to frustration as you have to restart the entire part several times because of a hidden sniper taking you out when the coast was supposedly clear.  With no variation, it gets boring fast, if not infuriating.  A Steam game review without any forced jokes. I bet you did Nazi that coming.

OK, why was I so disappointed with this game? Three main points spoiled this game for me. I won't go on about graphics, they're fine. Decent engine, decent artists etc. Game mechanics were fine. The menu system was a little annoying but you get used to that. Music was typically horrible but thankfully you can turn it off unlike some games(yes Halo 4, I'm talking about you) So what didn't I like? Read on.  Firstly, cinematics...lots and lots of cinematics. The annoying kind where you can't move and the characters talk seemingly forever when all they have to do is say, 'go out the door and shoot everyone you see.'. You can hardly travel 100 meters without being dragged into some scripted action sequence or some long boring dialogue. Judging by the amount of positive reviews this game receives I assume most people don't mind this. I don't understand. I find it very tedious. Don't get me wrong, I like storylines. I'm happy to sit and watch little cinematics every now and then. I just hate the game being interrupted every 5 minutes just so someone can tell you to walk down some stairs or whatever. OK, finished gripe no.1  Second gripe; the demon, serpent, tentacle, ♥♥♥♥♥ things. I assumed they were something you could bring up to use when you needed them. Nope, they're there the whole time. Fine if you like that sort of thing, IE great big dangly appendages cluttering up the screen, and I understand that some people do like that sort of thing, just not me. Oh, and apart from their basic intrusiveness, they're just silly. A gangster with tentacles, spare me please.  Thirdly, the sidekick. Again, some people obviously like annoying little halfwit sidekicks running around saying annoying half witted things. I just happen to not. The last half hour of my abortive attempt at playing this game was spent shooting it. The only joy I had in this game was discovering that you can shoot the sidekick, though regrettably he does regenerate.  So there you have it. I wanted to like this game. I loved Sandman and Hellblazer and other stuff back in the day. I knew it would be silly. It's graphic novel/comic book type horror, of course it's going to be silly. Unfortunately excessive cinematics, non-retractable tentacles and a sidekick modelled on a brain damaged teenage metal head made this game unplayable for me. Sorry Digital Extremes. I tried.

This is a review for 'Hold Right Arrow Key to Win Game' in which you ...hold right (perhaps left occasionally) to win. Thats.. basically it. You may think I am kidding, but thats the actual game. There are speed up and slow down powers, anti gravity powers and some other neat stuff that breaks up the fact you just hold right... but still, 90% of the game is holding right and knowing when to not hold right. There were screens and puzzles I never even had time to figure out because holding right just beat them for me. Sure, there are some 'nice' puzzles and some 'fun' challenges... but ..honestly.. I didn't like those parts. It felt kinda like those terrible moments in Sonic where the fluid of the level design stops and you have to struggle to regain it. Its awkward and you just want it to end so you can get back to holding right.   I beat the game, it was painful to do. I have fond moments of holding right..and I have many many bad memories of this game. I have labled it terrible and buried it in the graveyard of games I wish I didn't own. If considering purchase, please skip over this game. There are plenty of similar games that are much better examples of what you really want.

DOOM 2 probably was my very first game on PC, back when it just came out. I've played the first part a lot too, but the very first scene in Doom 2 is something I always get reminded about, when I hear 'Doom'. I've spent so many hours playing it back then, although it took longer to figure out where to go back then, although I was replaying the game a lot (mostly because I didn't have my own PC back then and played it whereever I could install it). When Doom 3 came out it was a big thing for me, as a fan of the old parts, but I didn't get into it... Firstly because I wasn't (and still not quite) into horror games and secondly because my PC wasn't good enough for a smooth gameplay back then. But a few weeks ago I've got a nostalgic mood and decided to replay Doom and Doom 2 among with Duke Nukem 3D. So I couldn't resist the BFG Edition (especially because I wanted to give Doom 3 another go).  Some might say that I should play vanilla version of Doom 3, because in BFG you can use the flashlight and your weapon at the same time and because they did something with the brightness levels. I personally find it way too ridiculous to not being able to use the flashlight and the gun at the same time (one of the reasons I stopped playing the game back then). I mean, of all the 'unrealistic' things this is probably the most weird one. It's like all these people in movies that go to check the dark corner where they heard some creepy sounds instead of getting a hell out of there - it might provide the better effect for you as a viewer, but it's unlikely that you would do it yourself in such situation. And connecting a flashlight to your gun is quite simple to do in real life. As for the brightness, I'm not quite sure what people mean by that, since you alway can reduce the brightness and the game is dark enough for me (so that I really have to use the flashlight most of the times to even recognize an enemy in the dark). So for me the bundle was a really great deal.  First I was a bit disappointed by some aspects, like disappearing corpses (what I especially liked in classic doom that the levels felt persistent in a way that if you kill something there then it will stay there forever and not just disappear into the air) or, even more, by the fact that headshots almost never kill with one shot (depends on the weapon, of course). But then I just took it as a game design in it's very meaning and...  Who cares that you need multiple headshots (and some of them take plenty) to kill an enemy? It sticks to the classic games, where there were no headshots.   Who cares that you can reload your gun while climbing on a ladder? In the old games you didn't even need to reload!  But back to the other aspects... The sound of the game is just incredible! I like ambient music and here it plays through the entire game. I mean, all these background noises out there in the dark... they all are rythmical. They add to the horror element, but also bring joy to the listener.   Speaking of horror element. Of course the game is quite old now and it won't be that scary (since graphical realism stepped way forward since then), but it still quite scary at times. In my opinion, they actually made a really good balance that still works: sometimes it's quite scary and you are kinda afraid of checking the next courner (and sometimes I even shouted out loud when something jumped on me), but sometimes it's just a lot of fun shooting. I don't know what is the key to make a really good shooter, but DOOM developers seem to know the secret. It's hard to explain why, but it's simply a lot of fun to play it.   Also I'd like to thank them a lot for keeping the old inventory in which you can carry all kind of guns (not that I think that it should be like that in all FPS games, but here it's definitely a must).   Conclusion: a MUST for those who liked the old parts, quite recommendable overall, but probably already too outdated for most gamers.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: This game is pretty great. The developers managed to do better characterization without any real dialogue than most games can manage with hundreds of lines of dialogue. The visuals and music are excellent as well, and they give the game a very distinct atmosphere.   My only real complaint is that this is a barebones console port. The beatiful graphics would be well served by options to increase the graphics quality, but unfortunately there are only resolution and v-sync settings. Also, although this game does not actually *require* a controller, you don't want to play this with a mouse and keyboard anyway. In the end this game is absolutely still worth your time.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: Indeed, being an indie game, I could see the ways the storyline would 'subvert' your expectations a mile off. It would build you up to knock you down, grind at you with grime and supplant you with achievment. What I could not forsee is how the level and puzzle design would be so wonderful that however I thought the story would go, it went with a sense of overcoming and good gameplay. Despite the lack of graphics options, short length and unashamed controller exclusivity, there are moments in this discourse you will not easily forget.

So I was a little bit worried about all rewiews that are not recommending this game, but anyway I managed to buy this game on sale, and I am happily surprised. A lot of people say that this game has a lot of issues, but I dont find them that big. Here is a little list of pros and cons about game:  Pros:  1. Really massive, tons of units and structures to build 2. Grahics are very nice, cartoony 3. ANNAAHAAALAATEEEE !!!!! 4. Ground, Air and Orbital Units. 5. AI is suprisingly intelligent, does something even on lowest difficulty 6. DA NUKEZZZZ. 7. Good resource gathering system.  Cons: 1. HW Requirements, I have pretty average notebook, I5 Dual Core, AMD Radeon 7500 HD, 6GB RAM, but I was able to run game without problems, FPS dropped when there was a lot of units, but still playable. 2. Price, I got in on sale, I wouldn't buy it for full price, thats kinda overhit. 3. Basically no Storyline, you are stuck with Multiplayer and Skirmish, there is also Galactic War mode, but you will see it's almost the same as skirmish. 4. Devs should look more on the engine optimalization.  Conclusion: Well, thats simple, If you are fan of really fast and hardcore RTS this is clean choice. Try to get it on sale, it is not worth of full price. 7/10 for me because of price and requirements.  Some performance tips: If you are having FPS issues try to download Razer Cortex, which will turn off unnecessary services and therefore reserving more processor time for game. In task manager, assign maximum priority to the Game Process. You might also try to run CCleaner to collect garbage, but it won't help much tough. If you are AMD GPU user like me, definitely set up maximum performance in Catalyst Control Center in exchange for quallity (it wont affect display quality at all, but it will heavily increase your FPS). Finally, configure your AMD Switchable Graphics for the game it might help a bit.

Period Wayyyyy too many of that ♥♥♥♥.   Pros : - Great designs; both monsters and the first half of the game - Character development / Backstory - Gameplay  Cons : - Cheap instant-deaths - Also the gameplay. I'd say false advertising lol - Final chapter level design

I just got this I can already tell that it's going to be one my all time favourites.  A lot of modern games glorify this post apocalyptic existence whereas this one brings it more down to earth and adds the humanity aspect back into it. The reality is that this game actually does very little, it finds its complexity in its simplicity. This War of Mine certainly doesn't hold your hand through, it gives you 3 people and a house and says 'go.' Whatever happens is random or it's because of what you've done previously.  It definitely makes you question your morals. We say that each life is as valuable as the next, but sometimes you simply can't save everybody, and surprise surprise, it's primarily your choice who lives and who doesn't. It's definitely a 'hate the player not the game' scenario because ultimately, everything is down to you. Do you give your medicine to that ill kid? Do you kill the eldery couple for food? Do you trade that pistol you spent days collecting parts to build? It's all questions that you'd think to be deeply unsatisfying considering that no choice is 'right.' You don't get points, trophies or even a pat on the back...you get to live another day, where you'll have whole new challenges and moral dilemas thrown at you.  I highly advise anybody to get this game. What it somewhat lacks it action it makes up for in immersiveness.

Although 0.0 hours are shown on the last 2 weeks of my profile listed here, I've played this game on my iPhone everyday, several times a day, for about a month. The i5 processor on the laptop (I'm using to type this review with) does not work as well. Too much lag. But my phone plays this game very nicely (iPhone 6plus). However, the play time does not appear to transfer over. This game is, as other players have already noted, is geared more towards a phone or tablet. It's terrific for down time away from home. It is a free download. However, the money this game generates for the company is by selling 'gold' which can be used to advance your position rapidly. That's ok with me. I like to work slowly and later decide to make a purchase with these type games. Because I loved the Total war games that were published, I wanted to give this game a try. First thing I noticed was the lack of instructions and no user manual that I could find. So starting off was mostly trial and error. That was a little irksome, but I continued because I was still enjoying the experience. I did find a forum on line that gave suggestions for play based on the authors' experience. I used them to improve my game. So, here's my first complaint about this game (1) Why the secrecy on play. There may be players out there that like the challenge of exploring the unknown, but I find it tedious making the same mistake over and over again, not really knowing what I'm doing. The instructions offered are pitifully brief. I would have found it more interesting having a basic manual that showed what did what. Like the classic PC games did. The strategy can still be left up to me, but at least I wouldn't be fumbling around. I am sure there are players who do enjoy that approach. I don't.  Now, in spite of the initial trial and error period, I still found this game very interesting and started following the advice of the online forums. As I said, I found myself playing this game every day, several times a day. I was enjoying the process of slowly building my Kingdom. This leads up to my final complaint: (2) So, as I said, I'm slowly building up my Kingdom with farms, roads, castles, houses, blacksmiths, etc, and just got to the point of having 4 towns in 4 regions. This was a long time in the making. Then, I log in after an update today and find 2 of those towns completely destroyed. No explanation. Instead, there were 2 large large patches of icons, each indicating 'ruins' where the building once stood. This, in short, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ me off. No aspect of the game gave me a hint of this coming. OK, again, maybe there are those who take this in stride and take up the challenge, but I personally play games on my iPhone for immediate comfort and gratification. There may be others out there, like me, who are not looking for unexpected massive tragedies, and very challenging play. I just want to expand my own Kingdom at my own pace. It would be nice if the publishers of these games would provide options to cover varying degrees of game difficulty for different target markets.  That being said, I wish there was a choice for the above recommendation beyond 'YES' 'NO' if I recommend this game. I would definitely recommend this game for those serious players who enjoy the challenges this game offers. I wouldn't for the vanilla players like me. It's a shame. I was really starting to enjoy this game. I'm curious about the statistics of the purchases made by this game's players (of 'gold'). If there is any correlation to time span played vs purchases. I can say that I've lost my interest in continuing under these circumstances.   By the way, the graphics are incredible.

The problem with games that rely on RNG is that people to don't recognize that the game isn't 'spin to win,' it's about managing the RNG. Everyone complains that RNG games determine win or loss based on the roll of the dice, but in reality, that's not the case. In Tharsis, you can manage your resources and prepare yourself for bad rolls. Even if you got horrible rolls, there's a chance you could survive if you strategize. All in all, great game. Don't let the RNG-haters skew your view on this game.

Played about a dozen hours so far, and It's pretty fun.  I've played most of this game's kin (TF2, Counter-Strike, Call of Duty et al), and while this one doesn't really bring anything all that new to the table, it does a good job of incorporating the notable elements from other games like it. It has your TF2 style level flows escorting 'EV's (carts), it has your cooldown artillery abilities, it has Quake's fast, frenetic, circle-strafing, it has Counter-Strike's long-distance sniper battles, and somehow or another it all just sort of works.  Right now the game is lacking any sort of meaningful competitive aspects. At the time of writing, it doesn't track competitive stats and there's not an appreciable difference between a competitive match and a normal one other than people get angrier when you lose in competitive and the competitive mode is locked to one game type.  Visually, the game looks very accomplished. Every aspect of each level looks hand-crafted and unique, though you may be hard pressed to realize it since you'll likely be traveling past the scenery at the speed of sound. Technically, it's pretty sound also. Although, there are times when I do get the occasional anomolous frame drops. I've not noticed any major gameplay bugs, though there are a few minor ones (not being able to return EMP charges, odd dangling HUD elements etc.)  I'm not the type of person to put money into these types of games anymore (learned my lesson with PS2), so it's disappointing to see that there's loads of 'Mercs' I'll never be able to play with, since the in-game cash you buy things with accumulates at a snail's pace.  Overall, it's definitely worth a shot.

This game is fantastic. It brings together the best ideas of many, many games. Most notably the Champions system from League of Legends, where you can choose to play a character in combat (but you get 3 in a squad, and can swp them out situationally while inside of a match) and the competitive system that CS:GO has. You can play Comp. after level 5; and the casual is not an untalented bunch; there is some clever play to be had there.   They have adapted the claver pay system from Ghost Recon, the aim-is-super-important ideal from CS, the 6, 7, 8-man DYNAMIC squad from the likes of LoL and MMO raiding, where everyone fills a seperate and distinct role (In CS:GO, everyone fills very similar roles) and the map design and run-and-gun tactics from Insurgency and big-map games like Battlefront, CoD.   Most unique, however; is the Loadout system. You can buy with points or be awarded at random Cases which you then open to reveal a random Loadout card. The card has BONUSES for your Mercenary (character that you play) that effect your in-game playstyle. New and better weapons; actual bonuses. The cards have names like Close-Range Assault, Standard Rifleman, Premium Rifleman, etc. and supply you with the weapons for your niche as well as actual bonuses such as: increased volume of nearby enemy footsteps, increased # of magazines, ability to pick up and throw live grenades, increased health for deployables (turrets, health stations, ammo stations) faster reloading, sprint while reloading, increased ironsight focus while being shot at. The list goes on and on and every single bonus is as useful and clever as the last. the game is matching the right bonuses for your character and your playstyle. This changes everything. Managing my finances outside of matchmaking isn't just worrying about how slowly I collect points: It's spending them wisely on my Loadout cards, new Mercs to play, upgrading current cards. This is my favorite feature. that and missions: REvery 3 hours, you get 3 missions for bonus EXP and points. Wicked. They have done a great job of designing how I think about the game when not in a match. It keeps me feeling like I'm in control of my own destiny, that my progress isn't just about practice, and playing at the right times so that the matchmaking algorithm doesn't ruin my ranking (Im looking at you, every single eSport Competitive game). Progress is more than timing and an algorithm. It's managing Mercenaries and Loadouts. Awesome.

Worst playerbase I have ever seen. People on skype calls cooperating while being on opposing teams. People completely arbitrarily helping someone who is supposed to be their enemy, even when they have a good chance of winning. People refusing to learn most basic and essential mechanics (wills, invest clues). Almost every game, 2-3 people leave right at the start.  Perhaps it's different in ranked. However, I've been unable to join any ranked games. Client disconnects after about 30 seconds of staying on home screen without joining any games (some culling of inactive connections, I presume - just have to restart for it to work again) - not an issue in non-ranked play, but queues for ranked are long enough for disconnection to occur, so it can never join a lobby. Might just be a very specific issue only I have, idk.  The game itself is quite good and can be a lot of fun, but not with such a playerbase. I hoped it would get better some time after launch, when those who have more interest in trolling and derping around than in the actual game quit, but nope. Still the same, perhaps even worse.  It's still recommended if you have enough friends with some free time to play only with them. But not otherwise. Unless you just want to annoy and grief people or enjoy that being done to you.

review_text: Summary:  a fun dirt bike racing game   Pros:     a lot of distinct tracks    Crashing the bike looks realistic (also sometimes quite funny).    The graphics are decent (although spectators do look a little bit weird).    accessible handling    There's a demo.  I think that every racing game needs one.     The price is right.    Cons:     After falling of his bike, the rider clips through the dirt.    Sometimes the audio stops working after a race, and you have to restart the game.    This compact version doesn't include a career mode. I usually skip that mode in racing games anyway because I prefer setting up races exactly the way I want them to be. If you only wish to jump into a dirt bike race on the track of your choosing once in a while, this is the version of MXGP you should pick up. In case you would rather race in a career mode with your own customised rider, I recommend checking out the full version.

A simple, somewhat old-school strategy game, Vietnam '65 maintains a highly replayable experience due to it's unique take on counter insurgency. Though there's nothing new in terms of it's approach as a turn based hexagon grid strategy game, the scenarios themselves offer an extremely appropriate and realistic game translation of the actual vietnam war. There isn't much to it; a tutorial and a battle mode, it keeps itself interesting enough to just keep replaying with different settings.   A must have for those interested in the Vietnam conflict.

ARMA 2 depicts real militry gear very accurately.  Arma 2's command menu is partially hidden, and won't initially display all options as in ARMA CWA / OFP and ARMA 1 / Armed Assault. AI in ARMA 2 have trouble deciding on stance and maintaining formation, often running out in front of a player like Left4Dead AI teammates. In addition to isuing commands, the player can switch between units similar to Rainbow Six or Ghost Recon. Single player can be frustrating because of the bad AI, so if you want a better single player experience get ARMA Cold War Assault or ARMA 3.
Campaign is around 5-8 hours depending on how fast you run through it. Very entertaining if you like me enjoy these types of games.

Watching people still defend this steaming pile amuses me greatly. Braben is up there with Molyneux in terms of snake oil salesmen and honestly should have had the “are you a pathological liar” treatment thrown at him at the same time ol’ Pete did. Elite Dangerous took a great platform and proceeded to do virtually nothing with it, instead selling promises which have managed to con a great number of people out of a great deal of money (first on kickstarter, and then via horizons) which have kept Frontier afloat. Braben is trying to do the *exact same thing* with the Planet Coaster mess, an always online game that you’ll not be able to play despite being an ostensibly single player game in the past. All in the name of wringing out more monies for Frontier.  When the whole thing comes crashing down, I will be stood with a bag of marshmallows and a cup of hot chocolate. Frontier deserves no less, along with 22Cents. Both of them represent the absolute worst of current dev practices. Over-promising, under-delivering, treating their consumers as disposable money presses to be bilked and having no respect for the larger gaming ecosystem.  The faster they collapse, the better for all of us.

I don't play a lot of shooters, but this is one of the best tactical shooters I've played, without being enormously complex or requiring a steep learning curve.   1) Wide range of characters with their own skillset and equipment.  2) Lots of cool maps that need to be manouvered differently.  3) Every game plays different, it doesn't get boring for quite a while.  Plus, the graphics and physics in this game are beautiful. Ubi's shop system is only for aesthetics. Most players are nice to deal with. I would almost say that this is the best that ubi did so far. Experienced no glitches or dealbreakers.

It's unfortunate that so many reviews focus on the rocky development period and first release of PA. As true as some of these comments might be, the fact is, PA TITANS is fun. If you like RTS games and give it a fair chance, you'll agree.  For me, it's the little things... Intuitive hotkeys/shortcuts, area patrols, build queues, rally/production orders, etc. This is a new concept for RTS, with a good design and great style. The new units TITANS brought in have been a great addition so far.  If no one had the rocky release on their mind, and the backers didn't feel cheated, this would have far fewer negative reviews, because it is simply a fun game to play.  This game a perfect example of good developers making a good game that got trashed by the business side of things. Uber screwed up badly, but this is a good game. Period.

The author has got his inspurations from legit sources like Death Rally, Interstate'76 and GTA1 and the mix is not so bad, just there's not enough of presentation and it is all made up on Unity engine. That's why it only manages to reach the level of free browser gaming fun: there is no story and/or intermission art, runs start suddenly and finish abruptly, maps are flat and look alike, flamethrower animation is horrid. If you could tolerate such primitivism then you look at a nice car-shooting rogue-lite.  Автор вдохновился отличными примерами типа Смертельного ралли, Межгорода-76 и Великого Автовора 1, и смесь получилась не столь уж плохою, просто не хватает требуемого антуража и сделано все на движке Единство. Потому это всего лишь уровень бесплатного браузерного развлечения: нету истории и/или заставок между миссиями, которые просто внезапно начинаются и заканчиваются посреди дороги, карты плоские и выглядят одинаково, ужасна анимация огнемета. Если вам удастся взглянуть на сей вящий примитивизм сквозь пальцы, то перед вами прикольный автомобильный рогалик.

These are the actual, old-school bards tale games. I have no idea why this ia 4 gig install. If you have nostalga for them, then I guess you should buy it. (I am not talking about graphics here). For example in combat to cast a mage spell, I need to type out the code for the mage spell I want to cast. One additional problem is, I have NO IDEA what spells my mage even knows. There is no way to tell as far as I know.   Then combat: You can face 4 stacks of 99 monsters each. On round one you need to give orders to your party, each member. You might do 10 hp to 1 monsters in rank 1, out of 99 total. Then you go to round 2 and you give orders to your party again, and manage to kill 1 monster, then repeat like 600 times and you fnish the encounter.  I didn't get very far, I just couldn't take it. Maybe back in the day when this was the ONLY choice, but not today.

Summary: Wait to see what the first DLC offers.  I have to admit that I reversed course on my opinion of this game. Originally I had recommended it, but that was as a Civ junkie still in the first flush of playing a new Civ game. Now that the dust has settled and I can take a more objective look, I have to give it a thumbs down. The factor that changed my mind is simply that I'm not playing it any more. If this game were of the same caliber as previous Civ releases, I'd still be playing it, but I got tired of it weeks ago. Ironically, though, other than this first paragraph, I don't have to change a single word of the rest of my review to fit my new conclusion. That in itself should tell you something.  Make no mistake, this is 'Civ In Space' and not a reincarnation of Alpha Centauri. It's obvious that the old Civ V game engine is under the hood, and the gameplay is very similar. In fact it's almost more of a 'total conversion' of Civ V than a whole new game. Other than the superficial window dressing (alien landscapes and units and a UI overhaul), there are only a few significant gameplay additions -- as well as some glaring subtractions.  On the positive side, the most significant change is the new tech web, a huge improvement whose significance I don't think will be truly appreciated for some time. In previous Civ games -- as well as in most every other 4X game ever made -- the tech progression is more or less linear. There are often different 'lines' of research, but they're usually highly interconnected, and by the time you get to the 'modern' era the chances are that you'll have researched *all* the 'primitive' techs already, or at least be able to acquire them in a few turns of hasty catch-up research.  Not so in Beyond Earth. Research progresses outward from a central hub, with each new node in the web consisting of a 'branch' that must first be researched, as well as 1 or 2 optional 'leaves' off that branch that you don't have to research to be able to progress to the next level. The leaves you do choose to research will depend on your chosen affinity (see below) and how you want to tailor your gameplay. Ultimately, by the time the game ends you'll probably have only about half the techs under your belt. The effect is that for perhaps the first time in a 4X game, you truly have the ability to customize your faction's technologies in a way that makes a significant difference to gameplay. I suspect that tweaking the tech web will be the major focus of the modding community, and that a web format will become the de facto standard for technology research in future 4X games of all stripes.  Another major change is the addition of a 'quest' engine, which not only adds a narrative to your game, it mandates the necessity of making choices that affect gameplay, albeit in a minor way. When you resolve a quest -- which involves anything from constructing a certain building to killing a certain native alien unit -- you are given a reward, and often also asked to choose between one of two bonuses. Individually they're small -- typically a +1 modifier to the effect provided by some building -- but if you choose the same type of bonus consistently, they can add up,  The last major change is the addition of 'affinities'. Depending on the techs you research and the choices you make in quest resolutions, you gain points toward one of three affinities. Higher levels in an affinity provide unit upgrades and significant gameplay bonuses, such as immunity to the 'miasma' terrain scattered around the map. You'll generally want to focus on a single affinity (+3 in two affinities is not as good as +6 in just one), so the net effect of this mechanic is to encourage a certain style of gameplay that further customizes your faction.  On the negative side, there are some game mechanics from Civ V that are glaringly absent in Beyond Earth. First and foremost is the dramatic nerfing of natural resources. Luxury resources are completely gone. The happiness system from Civ V has been replaced with a functionally equivalent health system, but the luxury resources that provided happiness in Civ V haven't been replaced with resources that provide health in BE. Strategic resources are still present, but you don't really need them: it's nowhere near as debilitating to be without titanium or 'firaxite' in BE as it was to be without coal or oil in Civ V. The net effect is that (a) international trade and therefore diplomacy is pretty much *entirely* absent; and (b) there's little incentive to find and settle (and fight over!) strategic map locations because everyplace is pretty much the same. IMO this is a crippling loss of gameplay that needs to be fixed at some point.  Another negative change is the nerfing of the culture system, although I have to admit this may just be perceptual on my part. There is no culture-oriented victory in BE, and although culture points are still used to purchase upgrades as they were in Civ V, somehow they don't seem as important. In the games I've played so far, I didn't see much difference between those in which I focused primarily on maximizing culture, and those in which I completely neglected it. I intend to look into this one more carefully, so for now take this assessment with a grain of salt.  Wonders have also been nerfed -- or again, at least it seems that way. They're still present, but the perks they provide don't seem anywhere near as substantial as those in previous Civ games. More to the point they just aren't as interesting. Maybe it's the fact that they're made-up techno-babble rather than something rooted in the context of actual human history, but the wonders just seem really underwhelming. In Civ V, you'd often base your whole early-game strategy around being the one to build, say, The Oracle or The Great Library, but I don't see myself caring enough about any of the wonders in BE to bother doing that. The last game I played I won easily without building or capturing a single wonder (other than the victory wonder).  Less significant but still mysterious in their absence because it seems like they would have been really easy to have are natural wonders. That has GOT to be something they're saving for a DLC!  A more abstract but still very important issue is that the pacing seems off. There's no question, at least, that games are much shorter. Maybe that was the intention, but at the point where you'd be just getting into the meat of the midgame in Civ V, you're already building the victory wonder in BE. It's like there's no midgame at all, which for me was always the most fun part.  All in all, I welcome the change of setting, but there are some things that clearly need fixing, and since they're gameplay issues rather than technical problems I doubt they'll be addressed in a patch. The cynical part of me can't help but suspect that the omissions were intentional and that Firaxis is already busy on the DLC to re-add the missing features... for another $29.99 plux tax.

If you have been growing tiresome of the continuous flops that Sonic Team has been shooting out over the past decade, Sonic Generations will serve as a blunt reminder that the Blue-Blur still has some life left in him.  Sonic Generations is a monumental tribute to fans; boasting large and beautiful recreations of iconic stages from past games. (FYI, said games are Sonic the Hedgehog, Sonic the Hedgehog 2, Sonic & Knuckles, Sonic Adventure, Sonic Adventure 2, Sonic Heroes, Sonic 2006, Sonic Unleashed, and Sonic Colors). The nostalgia will be strong with this one; but make no mistake, the actual game here is genuinely good. You alternate between both modern and classic versions of Sonic throughout the course of the game; each level featuring both a classic and modern section.  Classic Sonic plays as you might expect. Entirely 2-D platforming and the lack of a boost successfully replicate the formula that made classic Sonic the Hedgehog installments so great. Modern Sonic plunges you into EXTREMELY fast-paced mayhem that made it's debut in the form of daytime stages in Sonic: Unleashed. Quick reaction time is needed to take full advantage of numerous shortcuts that riddle the maps.  Also, the mod community has actually managed to find ways of adding new content to Sonic Generations on PC. As a matter of fact, there is a mod out now that allows you to play nearly all of the levels from Sonic Unleashed, despite the fact that it is a console exclusive.  Overall, I recommend this game with complete confidence that most will enjoy it. If you have been discouraged at all by recent attempts to reignite the gaming icon that is Sonic, you NEED to restore your faith right now by playing this game. In all honesty, the story IS slapped on and completely laughable, but it's refined gameplay and undoubtedly nostalgic nature makes Sonic Generations a crowning achievement for Sonic Team.

WARNING: This review may contain what some people might see as minor 'spoilers'....  The Two Worlds II Game is a great RPG game that has a lot of adventure game like elements in it, I think it will appeal most to PC game players that likes to use character to run around and explore and do a lot of battles along the way. For full enjoyment of the the game I must recommend using a Game Pad such as the Logitech F510/F710 or similar. The game offers large maps to explore and if one prefers one can simply roam the maps and gather loot and ressources taken fromn enemies, animals, plants or found in maze like caves, chests or if skill good enough from places in buildings entered by picking locks or getting money from gambling or pickpocketing (both difficult unless high skill or great luck) - of course one can also get new objects both through quest and trade and one can trade objects/loot away also. High Lock picking skill is a must in this game. Also don't overlook that you can also scroll on teleportation maps so that you can travel to previous locations.  A prominent feature is also the ability to, after breaking down loot into elemental components, being able to combine components and to upgrade items (both demanding aquired and spent skill points for abilities). Though the player have a map of locations then in some cases in can be difficult to find a few of the locations (or to get there).  The game is to large extend non-linear but as it is story driven one ofcourse must perform some tasks to unlock other tasks or possibility of access to places...  I have played the game for more than 200 hours and I have yet to finish the expansion. But with repspect to the Two Worlds II base game itself then I think that there were only two things that really sucked for me.  One is that one at a time gets the task to have to participate in a horse race to advance in game and that can be rather difficult for many (however one can cheat by placing personal teleportation stones in advance and use these to get around, this will leave you with map markers that won't go away but at least will solve the horse race problem)(Placing of personal teleportation stones on selected places on some maps will help the player a lot getting around with greater ease).  The other thing is the final 'boss' battle which in my taste is too difficult...  But all in all the Two Worlds II game will offer many hours of entertaining game play for those liking the genre and style that this particular game offers.  Personaly I think it is a great game that I will recommend other people to try if it appeals to you through the description given...

If you're highly used to open world games like Skyrim, then you may feel a little shut in. Travelling between areas can only be done by using a single way which will be unavailable when you progress to next area. You can revisit previous areas by using fast travel option which you can find at firecamps. Also, those locations will respawn some of it's items and sometimes enemies.  Every area is very richly detailed, so it's easy for you to feel confused where to go or where an objective / item is. Regarding this, you can use 'Survival Instinc' ( the key Q by default ) to show objects ( appear as bright yellow ) you can interract with, these objects can help you progress further. But still, some useful objects may not 'viewable' so it's advised to also use your own instinc.  As you progress, you will obtain new weapons and tools which you can upgrade at the camps. Upgrades are done by using salvages found in crates or dead enemies. You can also unlock new skills which enhance the usefullness of your weapons / tools at the camps.  Many cutscenes are actually interractive ( require you to push the right buttons ) which oftenly decides whether you're alive or dead, so be ready when you see one.   Bottom line, it's quite enjoyable. But if blood, violence, gruesome death and crazy cult theme are not in your 'OKs', then other games are advised. Also, don't bother with Link Square Enix Account option if you plan to buy this game on Steam, because it doesn't work.

Thomas was alone is a beautiful game. A great game. A fantastic game.  You play as a cast of rectangles and squares, inside a program dedicated to creating artificial intelligence. They all have names. The one you start out with is Thomas, and he is alone. In the beginning of the game, you realize that Thomas would meet an untimely end, in some shape or form, but you play the game to get a feel for it's controls.  And then, the game takes a pleasant turn.  The narraration is beautiful. The voice tells you what all of the characters are thinking and expressing, without a need for voice actors. It makes each character, while different in play style, relatable or at the very least, understandable. For being two dimensional objects, they all form three dimensional personalities. The fact that they have different and unique abilities changes up the puzzles and makes the game much more fantastic. It really feels like you're exploring a virtual world born with artificial intelligence. And the game does this without cutscenes or character voices.  The puzzles are fun, the gameplay easy to pick up, and even though the levels are fairly basic, it has it's share of moderate difficulty situations. I wish it had some harder ones. The controls are fluid and the game is solidly built because of that. Nothing terrible complex and no bugs as far as I can tell.   (SPOILER ALERT)  As you continue through the game, there's a point at which Thomas and your original cast... just aren't there anymore. Maybe this was to make it so that you valued the characters more, but it simply made me upset. The ending that I was expecting for my main cast didn't exist. They didn't go out in a blaze of glory or defy the original premise of the game or become true heroes. The game just pretended like they never existed.  And that's what drew out the last few levels. The storyline drew me in so that I could recite the orginal cast by name and what their personality was like, even though the game is just a series of rectangles. The game took that story away from me. The game cheated me out of all the friends that I had fallen in love with. It was no longer about a story, but getting to the end. That seemed boring and almost repetitive.  Overall, this is a good game, but the story will aggrivate you to your very core. My heart tells me to rate it less and my brain tells me to rate it more, but it's a solid 8/10.

The biggest issue that I have with the game comes from networking troubles, and that while all other games were working over my LAN just fine, GodMode just didn't want to stay connected.  The game is (most of the time) a co-operative game where you last against waves and waves of enemies before moving on to the next area to try to last through waves more. Each enemy offers a different kind of challenge, and during each wave modifiers will randomly activate to make things more interesting. The 'Oathes' system is great because if you're having an easy time with a particular level you can turn up the difficulty a little bit with each oath, in exchange you earn more rewards.  Its tons of fun, and honestly doesn't require much of a system to play. I wish my friends played more often instead of just letting it rot in their libraries.

Well, to start I want to clarify one thing – the game is not bad, it’s just mediocre.   While I have played all previous instalments (and The Dark Mod) I’m not going to judge Thief developers for the level of fidelity to its predecessors. I don’t mind changing the backstory and getting rid of the known and established lore, the conflict between the Pagans and Mechanist etc. The thing is – new Thief fails in creating something equally interesting on its own. It just has this generic XIX century steampunk vibe, that didn’t manage to tackle my interest.  I will just try to explain why I’m rather disappointed in this game. There is no single game feature that would make it mediocre in my opinion, it’s just the sum of little things, that create this rather bland experience. I will try to explain it giving just some points as an example.   Lack of the ability to jump – it’s not that limiting as I initially was led to believe, because you can mantle practically on any surface you would like to, with only few exceptions, so I’m ok with that. Contextual rope arrow – I can live with that, but implementing that solution causes the rope anchor point to stand like sore thumbs and screaming invitation – “look, here you can climb, this is the way further to something interesting”.   Sound design – now this is a big letdown for me. Original Thief games had wonderful sound design that let you place characters and objects in 3D space. Judging by the sound of footsteps you were able to determine the surface someone is walking on, but also whether someone is closing up on you, or getting away further. In new Thief it’s hardly the case. Footsteps really doesn’t give you good sense of direction, and to make things worse – they are often muffled by generics speeches that persons give. About ♥♥♥♥ rings. From the other side of the stone wall. With audio repeated twice. And alas, that happens frequently, especially when you load new area (which also happens frequently). So often what would be a tense moment of hiding from a patrolling guard will be ruined, because you will only hear repeated generic guard chat (to loudly, even from the other side of the wall (sic!)) ad nauseam that will muffle the footsteps. As for the random chats and guard lines – they have a big variety of them, but it seems like every person you encounter has only 2-3 limited lines, that they repeat constantly. So you creep for long minutes around the guards that are having the same conversation over and over. Occasionally you will encounter a scripted exposition conversation that shed some light on the world, characters, or even gives you clues about hidden loot. But Karras forbid you raise their suspicion level, because if you interrupt them, there is no coming back (except for a load long before you encounter such situation). Staying with that – voice acting is pretty decent, albeit there are some issues with lip-sync, and Garret on liners are sometimes over the top.  Speaking about conversations we go to another element – the writing. It’s not that good. I don’t know how the story was butchered and changed in its turbulent development process, but the outcome is average at most and sometimes straight out laughable (so the protagonist is a Thief – lets name his nemesis Thief- Catcher General). Conversations imply sexual things in not very subtle style, like it was written with teenage audience in mind. Plot is uninteresting and cliché-ridden, not to mention that it bears unhealthy resemblance to Dishonored (city dying of plague, you are the only saviour, your allies betray you).  Going back to gameplay – City hub is divided into many districts, all separated by loading zones, and sometimes having loading zones in them. It’s painfully boring to navigate, especially if you have explored most of it on the beginning, because some side missions are available only after finishing main story “Chapters”. So, one mission requires you to go through 3 separate loading zones before you get to the mission area, and there is nothing to do on the way. You have to sneak (or fight) past the guards with, well known by that point, patrol routes, listening to the same conversations. The City itself seems lifeless. I didn’t expect crowds on the streets by night (and the plague is a poor device to justify keeping NPC’s of the streets), but even if there is a public event, like hanging there are 2 persons performing it, and just 2 citizens watching.  Main story is divided into Chapters, but they are designed rather linearly, with multiple loading sections (cleverly disguised, I admit). They just feel like a scripted set of arenas you have to complete in order to move forward, to the next arena. And what’s worse, they feel even more limiting than Dishonored. Admittedly, there are some missions, where you can freely roam big mansions, and they gave me big enjoyment and this “Thief” vibe I was longing to feel. The plot sometimes acts as just to give you a pretext to move your character to a different setting.  The graphics are nice, and lighting is especially good looking, but not always working well. For example if you hide behind a crate with only your head sticking out and the passing guard will have a lantern, the screen will flash to indicate that you are fully visible (another not good design decision), but you will still remain hidden. Or you will be spotted on the other side of the fence, even if you were hiding there (maybe the can see you through the space between planks?). But those are rare instances. Other than that, the bluish tint in everything, and the repeating window textures for rooms that you cannot get into got me bored after some time. On the other hand, interiors are sometimes exceptionally well designed, and underline the differences between mission locations.  As for exploring the City – almost every breaking and entering will be proceed by a loading screen, which takes form of your hands working a crowbar animation, paired with a QTE. Also – your hands are almost constantly visible, and you can see the animations for doing almost anything – looking for a hidden switch, working a lock pick, or taking the loot. But since the most common loot is a pair of scissors laying precisely in the middle of a drawer, it quickly gets boring, and rather brakes immersion, because you know that behind every pair of golden scissors there is an 1-2 second long animation, that you can’t do anything about. I don’t know why they just didn’t go for stacks of coin here and there.  The AI – while they seem competent, when alerted, it’s painfully easy to lose the chase, even by going around the corner and hiding behind a crate, or changing the climbing up or down. Other than that, the change their patrol routs on the basis of your current location. So even if they have no indication of you moving to a different area, they will go and do their routine over there, because the game says so – also a poor design decision. Other than that they react rather properly to you dousing the lights, or making too much noise.  Now for the good things. Side missions proved to give me most enjoyment so far, and some have really brilliant moments. They are well thought out, loading screens are better incorporated (talking about bank job mission here), the gave me the thief experience I was hoping to get.  But, alas, those few great moments drown in the sea of mediocrity.  And that is why I don’t recommend buying this game for its current price. I’m rather disappointed with the experience, and while I’ve spent around 30h, I had to fight boredom on much more occasions that I wished.  So, to sum up – while not a bad game, it’s not good either, I would recommend waiting for a 50% sale before buying it.

app_name: The Typing of The Dead: Overkill
review_text: Good but not great  Its a fun niche game with a ridiculous (in a good way) story. Typing out 'okey dokey doctor jones' while fending of a horde of zombies is a great feeling. However I must compare this to the previous Typing of the dead game.  Much of the uniqueness of the first game are not present here.   The boss fights are poorly suited for typing in this game.   Gone are the sentence drills or answer a question fights of the first game. The boss fights are not much different from the regular zombies of just typing the word(s) before they attack. Another problem I feel with the game is that   it is too easy . Disclaimer: only ran through once on normal mode. Throughout the game the difficulty between levels was not noticable. On the last level I was still getting words that were 5 characters long. Maybe there were some marginally longer sentences to type out but i would not say it was significant increase in difficulty. Compared to the first game, the difficulty was very noticable with some zombies having very long words to type out. Also in the first game, when you die the difficulty changes dynamically.  The difficulty in this game is not dynamic compared to the first. The game also has some general buggy-ness . I had to restart once because when I finished typing out a zombies string, it disappered yet the zombie was still there rendering it impossible to me to beat him. Also the audio cut out many times when I had to face rage zombies. Probably the biggest knock I have for the game is that I came across   repeated phrases in a single level   (occured multiple times). I have never seen this happen in the previous game but there should be no reason I see the phrase Killing Twins twice in one level.   This too happened with the DLC I bought  . I believe the dlc is much much smaller than i expected it to be.  As many problems the game has, it just dumb and fun. Not many other games you can type and kill zombies. Buy it when it goes on sale. Or torrent the superior previous one. I highly recommend the latter.

Rock on, Risk of Rain! I sometimes lose patience with roguelikes, but this game has a lot of style, and a really good combat feel. Weapons seem powerful in proportion to their charge time, and the various mobs, elites, and intermittent horde attacks keep the game interesting.  Story is kind of thin--there's a transport ship, which a big bad guy and his gang mess up. You were on that ship, and now you're p'd-off! You progress through the levels for some reason (revenge? reparations? lulz?), hacking/shooting/dodging the increasingly nasty baddies to get to the end.  It's simple, there's no character management, you level automatically as you kill baddies and open chests. Surviving to the endgame is a matter of both skill and the RNG gods giving you good items and manageable foes.  Thumb up!
