I stopped playing this game when my first mate started running in circles with a turd in his hand and a turtle on his head. He seemed confused as to whether to join his crew in the tavern or practice swords in the cabin. Or throw the turd away. Or set the turtle down. Yes, this is a problem, and I doubt the excuse of the crew having freewill really addresses this. But more on this later.  Initally it was a fun experience, though the lack of intuitive tutorials made it a little rockier to start than most games. The overall look of the game, the sound of the music, and the grunts of the pirates were all quite welcoming as well. As I conquered each island or pirate ship, things got progressively harder in a maneagable sense. I hired my first mate and named him 'Vance the Lance' in honor of the hulking spear he carried. I got a kick out of his former job as a fantastic male model. Qualities I definitely need in my pirate crew.  Whatever the case, Vance and I soon had a larger crew to manage, and that's about when the problems started to happen. Arnold the Brick (because he is dumb as a bag of bricks apparently), had decided he was no longer going to cook the fish he had become so good at catching. I decided to teach another crew member how to cook expecting this to solve the problem. Ironically, this crew member nearly died to starvation because he too refused to cook, despite having both the skill and knife to do so. Meanwhile, Arnold kept providing me fish which I as the player kept placing in hopes of SOMEONE actually cooking it.   The solution to this odd problem was for me to dock at the nearest town and frantically buy fruit for my famished crew.  Now, some would say the real problem here was that the morale must have been poor. I wish I could say this was the problem, but I had my crew loaded with Rum and Gold, thus spirits were high. Nope, this was just an example of poor AI. Which brings us back to my poor first mate.  Poor Vance. I wanted to help him, but he was so absorbed in his turd/turtle ritual...it was bad. And that wasn't the only thing I saw. Many of my pirates couldn't handle things like 'crawl over up that ladder' or 'walk over there.' I was excited to get cannons, but soon I realized that the AI was very prone to getting choked on that as well. My cannoneers would frequently take too long to load cannons. Some would be more inclined to playing cards than loading the cannon. Your game of solitaire needs to wait guys! Cannons PLEASSSE!    Then the fights would happen. I liked the chaos of it all well enough, but sometimes stupid junk would happen. Like swimming. My captain has the swimming skill, but he never seems inclined to using it when he innevitably gets knocked into the water. The knockback can get kinda crazy in the fights too. Thank goodness there is a reloading button..but sheesh.  Then there was sailing. I get the use for it, but I kinda wish there were more random occurences that would just happen to break things up. Something like, mermaid attacks...or giant white whale attacks...or seagull raiding parties... I don't know...Just something! Break up the humdrum of pooping pirates derping back and forth on a fictional ship.   The morale/hunger is the last thing that I think needs a bit more tweaking. Gold is used to pay your crew, which instantly puts them at full morale. Gold is also used to feed the crew when your sailing. You can raise their morale and their hunger (how well fed they are) at taverns. The hunger stat raises reaaaaally slowly, so unless you want to wait all day, your better off buying food for them at the ship (which costs even more gold), or fishing it (which means dealing with finicky AI). They also have to have gold to spend (apparently) to make use of the tavern...but with no way to track what gold each pirate has, it's kinda difficult to keep track of (i.e. When did I last pay them?). It also bothers me immensly that my captain never seems to eat when he is at the tavern. It seems he is damned to eating food on a ship instead of with his crew.  Ah well...I bought the game on sale at least, so I'm not OVERLY disgruntled.  Don't get me wrong. I've enjoyed my time with this game so far...but I think I will stop and wait for them to address some of these issues (hopefully) before I continue my foray into Pixel Piracy.  I'll gladly update this review in the future if some changes actually occur! Until then, Vance and his turd problem shall remain.  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- EDIT:  Thought I might throw some more positives into what I thought about the game:  -Combat is frantic, but I enjoyed the chaos of it. It isn't so noticeable with small crews, but with larger crews it can be a chaotic blast. -Creating your own boat is an interesting concept as you progress through the game. You always have the option of simply taking the enemy boat, but I enjoy plundering them and slowing adding various parts to my own ship. I'd love to see more cosmetic damage to the ship as you take bombardment shots eventually! -Charming: I know I said it once before, but I really do like the look and sound of it all. I'm not a fan of all the shanties/tavern songs, but I do think that it is a nice touch. Visually, I enjoy the pixelated nature of the characters and the locales they inhabit. -Looting makes me feel good, and there is always the chance you'll find something very nice (like a diamond which sells for quite a bit) I'd love to see more of this expanded, but it seems good enough for now. I'd love to see plundering expanded (i.e. attacking TRADE ships vs pirate, or attacking cities, or stealing the food stores off a ship)  And lastly, the management is a positive when the game works as I think was intended. When the AI actually does what you want, and you get into the groove of managing moral vs gold, it is fairly enjoyable. I would tweak it in different ways personally, but I think the framework in place works well enough. (i.e. I'd make taverns more effective at curing hunger, I'd make paying the crew worth less morale to place more emphasis on buying/finding rum or visiting taverns) ------  1.7 Update  The Good news:  -My first mate finally got his life straight and decided to join us on the shore. -For about 30-40 minutes, the AI felt decent/better and I was enjoying myself well enough. -Pets are way easier to catch now! No more grinding! -My captain actually finally decided that bar food is OK. It may have only been a nibble, but that is progress in my book. I sincerely hope they address taverns and make them a little more useful in regards to hunger.(and on that note, I really wish I knew how much gold each crewmember had to spend!)  The Bad news: -After about 40 minutes, my new pet chicken (called ROBOCHICKEN) decided he is part of the crew now. I caught him practicing with swords and playing cards in a corner. I didn't notice if he was also eating, but I definitely caught the bugger taking a dump next to the larder. I've got my eye on you chicken! -The AI has gone back to being a reluctant mess. Shovelback Ray (my chief shovel carrier/treasure hunter) refuses to leave the boat for anything. -The moral system went haywire. I paid my crew as usual, but seconds later everyone went from super happy to 'KILL THE CAPTAIN' rage. I dunno...maybe the chicken is promoting a mutiny among my men?  -Eh, Better luck in 1.08 I hope! ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Hexcells is a puzzle game that feels like a sort of combination between minesweeper and picross. Puzzles are pre-made, can all be solved without guessing and combine the hint systems of minesweeper and picross, along with the hexagon twist. The concept works very well and I found the game quite enjoyable, but it is unfortunately quite short. There are 6 groups of puzzles, 30 in total, that gradually introduce new concepts and become more difficult. I was able to finish all of them without any mistakes in just over an hour, though I'm generally faster than most with these type of games.  It's a fun puzzle game and you can't really go wrong with the price. I've been told Hexcells Plus is much more difficult so it should be a great continuation for those who can solve these puzzles quickly.

::10/22/2014 UPDATE:: So approximately a week and a half after the game launches, Square Enix has quietly introduced a patch. What does it fix? We now get a confirmation that we want to close the game when we press escape in full screen.  Excuse me if I'm too overwhelmed to edit anything else about my review just yet. (Less cynically: at least it's SOMETHING and the patch notes say they're working on other stuff. I'll be holding my breath until I pass out from lack of oxygen. Stay tuned, and, as always... Please look forward to it.)  Original review:  Locked 720p resolution ESC key is a shortcut for Alt+F4 in full screen mode Literally no graphics configuration options (whoops, I mean, you can choose full screen or windowed) Best 60 GB I ever spent 10/10 would fellate Squeenix again  ::10/10/2014 EDIT::  60 GB is stupid. The 'reason' this game is 60 GB supposedly is because fans clamored for Japanese languages. To maintain everyone's super deep lip sync immersion, this means that the Japenese language pack doesn't just consist of extra audio files, but slightly tweaked versions of every pre-rendered cutscene. So the game installs two versions of every cutscene on your computer. And no, the Japanese pack is not optional. Why not do as Dynasty Warriors 8 did and make the Japanese language pack free optional DLC? I'm not the only one who isn't going to play with Japanese voices (though I know many are). Perhaps they could even offer two separate downloads, a 30GB English install and a 30GB Japanese install. (I don't see the need for having both installed at the same time, since I figure most people would play through the game using one or the other language.)  Locked 720p is stupid. Note that this isn't CAPPED 720p, it's LOCKED 720p. There are literally no resoultion options. The game touts 60 FPS but many people experience stuttering and inconsistent framerames (myself included). Some people have suggested certain workarounds revolving configuring video drivers, with mixed results. Some say it works, some report it doesn't. (I'm in the 'it doesn't do anything' boat.) And before the mindless white knighting fanboys tell me to upgrade my toaster hurr durr (in the same breath they tell me to stop being an entitled PC master race elitst for wanting resolution changes), my specs are:  Time of this report: 10/10/2014, 01:58:56  Operating System: Windows 8.1 Pro 64-bit (6.3, Build 9600) (9600.winblue_gdr.140723-2018) Processor: Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-4930K CPU @ 3.40GHz (12 CPUs), ~3.4GHz Memory: 32768MB RAM DirectX Version: DirectX 11 GPU: 2x NVidia GeForce GTX 780 3GB VRAM in SLI  The inconsistent performance is happening for people across the board regardless of their specs.  Once you get past all of that, the game runs and you can play it. So that's a thing. It hasn't crashed on me. But it should be mentioned there are several reports of people who can't launch the game, can't get full screen working, have the game crashing after loading the launcher, and other similar problems.  BUT HEY AT LEAST IT'S ONLY $16 LOL WHAT DID YOU EXPECT??????  I dunno, maybe a port like The Last Remnant which has all the standard configuration options, and even went above and beyond the call of duty by adding extra content and features that make the PC version even better than the console versions for $6 less than what the same company is asking for Final Fantasy XIII?  Lastly, yes, there is a pre-alpha community developed mod headed by Durante (of the DSFix and DPFix fame) that lets you tool around with resolutions but also reportedly breaks menus and has other graphical glitches and such (hence: pre-alpha). Also it has no impact on performance (FPS dips, etc) problems. Forgive me for being old fashioned but I kind of think it falls to the devs to offer these kinds of basic functionalities, not modders.

This is a bit of a mixed bag, but not for the reasons you'd think.  As a freemium title, you'd expect it to be rife with pay-to-have-fun features and short on content. It is neither of those things. What you get instead is a fun little throwback to old school AD&D, complete with a perfect mimicry of the art and writing styles of that era of game design. And my god, it even plays like old DnD - just a simplified, reasonable version, dressed up with some crunchy deck-building mechanics. It's accessible, it's charming, and above all it's fun. It's a gem of a game.  So, why is Card Hunter a mixed bag?   The answer to that is Gary and Melvin.  Campaign mode in Card Hunter is basically a three-character story. Gary is your GM, Melvin is his older brother, and then there's a girl from a pizza place. The pizza girl is totally fine as a character. Gary and Melvin make me writhe in my chair.   Imagine, if you will, a hobby shop. Imagine this hobby shop is in a crappy part of your city. Imagine its windows are so heavily plastered with comics and action figure ads from the 1970, all of which are faded and peeling and stuck on with tape. Imagine that you've gone inside this store, wrinkling your noise at the powerful funk of old books and spilled snackfood that fills the cramped, dirty space within. Imagine there's only one customer, fat and unwashed, paging idly through Bikini Mail Magazine. And now imagine that behind the cracked, ancient counter, lounging in a pool of his own adipose folds, is a forty-something man in a pit-stained old t-shirt and sweatpants, hyucking softly to himself as he optimizes his WoW build on his laptop.  Now imagine you've collapsed that entire experience into a single person. That person is Melvin.  Gary is not much better.   Thanks to Gary and Melvin, Card Hunter is a wild roller coaster of highs and lows. You'll fight a sharkbear, and that'll be pretty cool. Then Gary will say something cringing and miserable and you'll want to claw your own eyes out. You'll perservere, though, and get to see some righteous module art about cannibal yetis living in a blizzard. And then Melvin will open his mouth and you'll dream about just punching him. Punching him until he stops moving. Punching him FOREVER.  I've never seen a game ooze social awkwardness like this one, and it's a damn shame, because everything else about it is great. The devs clearly are familiar with old school DnD. They get what tabletop culture used to be like.  And then they choose to make the faces of Card Hunter a distillation of all the worst parts of the people who play the hobby.  Maybe they hate themselves?

I don't know if I've quite played enough to write a review for this, but here are my 2 cents anyway.  Enter the Gungeon is a great game. That's not much to go on, but that's the basic core of my review. EtG is a good game.    Pros:  Tight controls: Every error of movement is player error Strong visual style: Everything looks like it belongs in the same world Realistic environment: Kick over a table and the stuff falls to the floor and breaks. Wrecking bookshelves sends paper everywhere. Nearly everything reacts to you. Gun Puns: Everywhere. Every single thing is a pun or a reference to DnD Great music: The Soundtrack is really solid Diverse characters: Yes, I give a ♥♥♥♥ that two of the player characters are black and two are women.  Different characters: Everyone plays differently and has different perks, which shakes things up Building the Gungeon: Every time you unlock a Cell, there's more to do, more to do, more to do Learn via Gameplay and Observation: One of those games that you can improve at by both time played and by watching more skilled players   Cons:   This game is hard. Like, frustratingly hard. Half of being able to beat a floor is skill and the other half is RNG. Sometimes if you don't get a gun good enough, you just have to restart or resign yourself to die. And while you can, as a player, improve via observing enemy patterns and being careful -- this game acclimates to you. It gets harder as you get better. And, despite the game still being fun while I'm playing it, feeling like I'm unable to progress can really make the game unenjoyable in the long run. I do believe that this is the reason why, at time of review, I've only put 30 hours into the game...because I can only play two or three rounds before I'm too ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off to continue.   If the game had an easy mode, I think it would be a lot more accessible to a lot of people. If there were modes in general then I think it would be better. Being able to start at an easy level and then ramp up the difficulty at my own pace would make it so that I could play for a lot longer without getting so frustrated. I wouldn't even mind if all the achivements were just locked off to Normal/Hard mode or something -- I just feel like it would be a better game in general if there was a mode that wasn't so...punishing.   Another Con: There's only Couch Co-Op. I hope a future update brings in proper Co-op, as playing this game with friends that don't have to literally be in my house would be awesome, and take some of the edge off of the difficulty, I think.    Overall   The game is good and definitely worth buying if you like this style. If you enjoy The Binding of Isaac or similar games, then you'll like this -- it's basically the same game without the grotesque theme. I really enjoy playing EnG -- and seeing myself get better at the game is really fun. I can finally get to the third floor, where when I began I could never defeat the first floor boss. So, again, while I do think the difficulty handicaps the game a bit, I won't take off more than just a few points for that.  Overall, about an 8/10

I have to say this game has one of the most bizzare plots I've ever seen. 'Squishy the Suicidal Pig' is a game about a little pig who's parents have been taken to the slaughter house to be made in to bacon. Squishy decides he wants to join them in piggy heaven so commits suicide. Instead of going to heaven, he goes to hell where the devil makes him an offer and gives him a second chance to go to heaven.   In every level, Squishy has to complete puzzles and find ways to kill himself at the end of each one before progressing onto the next. I can't tell you how the game ends (because I haven't completed it yet), but I am very much looking forward to doing so. The games graphics are relatively arcade like and the controls very basic so anyone can play, the music is surprisingly sweet but can become a little irritating after a while, and the levels continually get harder as the game progresses. Overall I find 'Squishy the Suicidal Pig' rather entertaining and amusing, whilst challenging enough to make me think about how to complete the current level.   If you like puzzle games with a quirky backstory, then look no further!

I thought this game needed some more love. The obvious highlight is being able to play as dinosaurs, and although the human gameplay is pretty standard-issue-FPS, being able to fight dinosaurs is pretty fun. The dinosaurs are fairly overpowered one-on-one, which pretty much requires you to work in a group if you're playing human, which can be annoying. But being able to switch classes (and even factions, when there's space) mid-battle lets you keep things fresh and unique if the experience starts feeling stale. This is even better when playing as a dinosaur, because you can go from stomping around as a Tyrannosaurus to weaving through the undergrowth and spitting venom as a Dilophosaurus to soaring around and dropping people off cliffs as a Pteranodon, all within the course of one match.  While the experience is fairly limited beyond multiplayer deathmatch, the developers are constantly adding and expanding to the game. Overall, I'd say it's the best dinosaur game around right now, right next to ARK.

Girlfriend Rescue  is a turn/round-based RPG through and through which starts out with a conversation among friends about approaching your crush, but you find out that some recently escaped prisoner has taken her! You have to form a party to find a way to save her. You start out with choices you can make about your gender, name, crush’s name, and once you can move around, you have a selection of up to two other members you are friendly with that can help you out as you start your quest to get your girl back. I would have liked to have been able to try them all to find out what each possible party member could do, but I would not really know future abilities as they leveled up anyway, so it is probably not a very important issue.   As you begin, you get a lot of nice tips from the local police NPCs on various aspects of play. This was helpful for me as I began journeying around. There are a lot of various original things about this RPG game that I like, such as the various debuffs and what can change when things happen. They are atypical to what I am used to seeing, which is pretty interesting and refreshing to me. I mean, a lot of it involves various stats, so it’s not too different, but the way it is done is not the typical type of stuff. I like what I’ve encountered so far in this game. The combat system is a standard turn-based style where you have attack and defend commands along with special moves you can learn as you level up, which you pick for the next round, and watch the round unfold.  The actual gameplay outside of combat is roaming around a map, trying to find X amount of enemies on the area you explore, and taking out the last guy on the map. I have not tried finishing an area with less than the target number, but I am guessing your bonus reward at the end would change if you got less than the number it shows, which finalizes after the last “boss” character on the map, as far as I can tell, ever since the first area I explored fully. Reviewing this game is simple.  Want Game of the Year with a gripping story, revolutionary gameplay and hundreds of hours of content? Go buy something else.  Want the video game equivalent of the most sugar-filled but delicious bowl of kids cereal you can find? Buy Transformers Devastation. It's a bowl of Fruity Pepples with a side of a Saturday morning cartoon. And everyting about it is stupid and fun.  Don't believe folks that say it's a 4 hour game. Beating the campaign once (on medium difficulty with one character of five) is 4 hours. If you want to beat it with all the characters, on all the difficulties (the 3 base + 2 hidden harder difficulties), do all 50 challenge modes, craft the best weapons , and get highest rank in everything (SS), it's considerably longer. Probably closer to 100. It's a real grind though. But a fun one.  Basically this game is what you make it. Want a pure diversion... a simple game that is just a joy to play and is nothing if not lovingly done? Play it in short intervals. Want to play this game for many, many hours? You can do that too.  If nothing else, you'll have a game that you can sit down and play for 30 minutes if you're bored or feeling down that will make you grin ear to ear every time with how fun it is. Deep? No. Fun? You'll find few games more fun. Extremely recommended.

Great game, great presentation. Thoughtfully desinged tables. At least one table works great (Epic Quest)! The problem is that two of the tables that come out of the box are exhibiting performance issues on an overclocked 980 Ti paired with a 4.6GHz 980X. I don't think it's a hardware issue, since this is the only game in my Library that exhibits such problems. Even Raw Data runs great, and looks much, much better.  I guess if you don't mind the occasional dropped frame, this is fine. I would wager that on average, the framerate is probably very close to the 90fps that VR demands. But the second it dips below that, it totally ruins the experience --at least for me. YMMV.  I tried lowering the graphics settings to the absolute minimum, and while this made the game LOOK substantially worse, the performance remained the same. What a shame too, because with a better framerate, this would be one of my favorite Vive games. As it stands now, I'm on the fence about whether to return it. It's that good (and that bad).  Now, I'm not saying that I expect a John Carmack level of graphical efficiency out of a $15 game.... I'd just be happy with lower IQ settings to make it run well. If I were writing this game, I'd build in a way to reduce the shader detail (and possibly geometry) to whatever extent is required to hit 90fps on a GTX 970, which is min-spec for the Vive. Given that this game is inherently set in an isometric view, resolution and anti-aliasing are much more important to overall IQ than shader detail and geometry. Computationally, these are also easier targets to hit, and would make the game more marketable. My 2 cents...

I haven't liked a shooter this much since UT2004. I'm generally not an FPS fan, but sometimes the combination of theme and game mechanics, along with some decent voice acting can make a shooter a TON of fun. That's Left 4 Dead 2 for you. I hate tactical shooters because I just want to wade in and shoot the hell out of everything (or knock heads off with a cricket bat ala Shaun of the Dead), so thankfully this isn't a tactical shooter. It's also not a mindless shoot everything in sight game, either. Well-made, goal-oriented maps make for some awesome Zombie slaughtering fun with the very capable NPCs or with your friends online.  There are a great variety of weapons, lots of explosion potential, and the characters and voice acting are really well done in my opinion... much more entertaining and easier to 'bond' with than the gang from the original L4D. Director AI keeps things fresh and challenging, but not impossible. 5 Stars.

I personally never played this when I was younger, but my brothers sure as hell did! One day, one of them sent me this, saying that I should play it since it was such an important part of gaming history. I did and... Eh.  Although it's confusing at first, the controls are rather simple and very smooth. It's just... Not what you'd expect nowadays. For earlier DOS standards, the graphics are pretty good. May as well get if if you never played it.  Watch out now, here comes a load of text for each of the games in the collection, this is gonna be a doozey!   Episode 1: Marooned On Mars Billy Blaze, eight year-old genius, working diligently in his backyard clubhouse has created an interstellar starship from old soup cans, rubber cement and plastic tubing. While his folks are out on the town and the babysitter has fallen asleep, Billy travels into his backyard workshop, dons his brother's football helmet, and transforms into...   Commander Keen - Defender of Earth!  In this episode, aliens from the planet Vorticon VI find out about the eight year-old genius and plan his destruction. While Keen is out exploring the mountains of Mars, the Vorticons steal vital parts of his ship and take them to distant Martian cities! Can Keen recover all the pieces of his ship and repel the Vorticon invasion? Will he make it back before his parents get home? Stay tuned!  Keen's mission in this game is to find back the stolen parts of his ship. The evil Vorticons have stolen four objects from the ship and hidden them all in different places on Mars. Commander Keen's Megarocket was constructed from objects found around his house, which have been modified into starship parts. As a manual flight controller, he has used a joystick from his brother's video game. As for electricity, he has used a car battery from his mom's car. A heavily modified vacuum cleaner from their kitchen has been used as a ion propulsion unit. And as for fuel, he has used everclear from his dad's liquor cabinet.   Episode 2: The Earth Explodes In this episode a Vorticon Mothership is poised above Earth, preparing to destroy every major city within their deadly Tentalus Ray! Keen must sneak aboard the ship and stop each Tantalus team from their terrible task. If he fails, the Earth explodes!  As you already know, your mission is to stop these evil ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s from blowing up the earth. In order to do this you need to find and destroy each X-14 Tantalus Ray, powered by cells from the big purple Space Amoeba. You need to find your way in the ship to each Tantaulus Control Room. You start in the ship's galley, and and must find items to help you on your quest. You already have a Vorticon Hyper-Pistol, which has almost no ammunition at all, and of course, the pogo stick from your mars adventure.  Episode 3: Keen Must Die In the last episode commander keen disabled the Vorticon Mothership, and now in this final episode in the trilogy commander keen is making his way to the planet Vorticon VI. Then he lands on the island of Vortville, from where he tries to travel to the city of New Vorticon, which is very well guarded by a huge vorticon army. Then he must pass the impossible maze of the Caves of Oblivion. When he passes it, he will finally reach the Castle of the Grand Intellect. Just who is The Grand Intellect and can you stop him before he destroys earth?  Now your mission is to make your way to the Castle of the Grand Intellect on the planet Vortiocon VI, and discover the secret of the Grand Intellect. The Vorticon Elder on the Vorticon's Mothership told you two things. Firstly, the Vorticons are not evil - the Grand Intellect is controlling them with his 'Mind-Belts'. And secondly, the Grand Intellect is from Earth! Who could he be?  Episode 4: Secret Of The Oracle Billy has been working on a new invention in this episode. His latest invention is the Photachyon Tranceiver, a radio that can reviece signals from anywhere in the galaxy. After playing around with his new invention, he suddenly hears a strange message: 'brrzzz ... giggg ... oment of great triumph ... frzzt ... ast the Milky Way will be ... huzzzzz ... terly destroyed ... pyuneeeeeeg ... can stop us now. We will remake the galaxy in the name of the Gannalech. Power to our race! power to the Shikadi!' After zapping his parents, Billy heads to The Oracle of Gnosticus IV, who will help Billy with his mission.  But after arriving on Gnosticus IV he discovers that the Shikadi have already been there. The Shikadi seems to have kidnapped all the members of the council, and taken them all to the shadow lands in the west side of the planet. Sounds like a interesting mission for commander keen.  Episode 5: The Armageddon Machine The story in this episode continues from the 'Secret of the Oracle' episode. In last episode, Keen rescued all the members of the council. And now in episode 5, Keen finds out about the Shikadi's enourmous ship, which has the largest weapon ever built. Now it seems like the Shikadi have built a huge machine to destroy the world. And now Commander Keen flies to the Shikadi's ship in order to save the world.  Your mission in this episode is to find the various power stations and break them with your pogo stick, and then to destroy the main power cell and make the armageddon machine harmless.  Surprisingly, episode 6: Aliens Ate My Baby Sitter isn't in this collection. I dunno why but they must have their reasons.  (I told ya that was gonna be a doozey, didn't I?)

With no reservations, I highly recommend playing The Secret World. It's unlike any other gaming experience. Oh god the zergs. Nobody can withstand the thunder of 2000 tanks.     Ok seriously. This game is really fun. I mean, if you can run it. While most games don't require too much cpu power this one is very cpu hungry. Its no fun when you lag to 15 frames per second when you're supposed to be enjoying a huge fight with explosions everywhere running through the desert. Other than that, the only real hinderance this game has is the huge zergs. The team balance is huge sometimes! I played on matherson and once there was 40% vanu, 40% NC, and only 20% TR. Losing just makes people switch, and it just avalanches from there.     Factions.  The faction balance in my opinion is a meh. TR is good at cqc, but most of the game is in wide open areas. IMO TR does not get enough love NC is good at range and the scattermax is a force to be reconed with in cqc. I don't care if you're a heavy. If you see a scattermax, shoot rockets and run. Vanu. These guys are (gay) terribly op. At night there (gay spandex) purple and black armor blends in, making it impossible to see them. Also did I mention they have no bullet drop? Oh yeah and they have a significant decrease in recoil when compared to the other factions. Then there's the dubstep gun..... Vanu really gets too much love, but I can understand it because they are 'more technologically advanced' than the other factions, but please SOE, for the sake of balance, could you buff the 2 other factions a bit?  Overall Opinion Gameplay 9/10, zergs can ruin fun, but when you get in a battle its really great Graphics 10/10 its not cryengine, but with physx and everything on max, the lighting and the particles really make your jaw drop. Especially because you know its all real people out there :O Lasting Impressions x/10 If you play with friends, this could be THE game you guys play. Its like a budget battlefield on steroids. Overall 8/10. Nothing really new besides the humongous battles, but I think that's enough ;D

CAN YOU ENJOY THIS GAME IN 2016?  Morrowind may have not aged well with its clunky combat, dumb AI and poor animations, and while mods can improve on many of its flaws, it will still be hard to get into it for those that started with Skyrim. However, if you give it a chance you will discover that there's a lot more to it.   Exploration is rewarding, bits and pieces of lore are hidden and scattered around the map and not every detail of the story is laid out in front of you, and discovering it on your own is part of the experience. The lore is intertwined with the plot.  Magic will open a world of possibilities. Want to enchant a pair of boots that will make you jump over mountains? You can do it. Want a pair of pants that shoot fireballs? No problem! Alchemy, Enchanting and Spellmaking will turn you into a god in no time, experiment different combinations and you will feel like you can do anything.  Skyrim is a bloody good game, but it has given up a lot of the older TES freedom for a balanced and polished gameplay. Morrowind isn't polished or balanced in any way, but that's actually what makes it special: it's a giant playground where your creativity is the only limit.  Anything I could write would never bring justice to this game, it's a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ masterpiece!

AMAZING!  I've been playing PC games for 15+ years and I am not going to lie, I am a HUGE graphics junky, I'm pretty picky when it comes to them. Hammerwatch is a step back into the classic top view DOS graphic game world. When I was younger I'd always play a PC game called Gladiator and GOT (God of Thunder) if you liked either of those, then this game will be a hit. I've played quite a few hours of Hammerwatch so far and honestly playing it Multiplayer was the most fun I've had in a long time. It's a very simple control scheme that is easy to master, however, the game itself has some challenging parts. I've been playing the campaign with my Non-Nerdy girlfriend and for the first time in her life I heard her say the words 'I can't wait to upgrade my mana...' Granted I'm not a Magic User type of player, however that brought tears to my eyes. Hammerwatch has a strong level editor and there are few to no complaints about anything in this game. I picked it up for $2.00 on sale and immediately bought a few more copies for close friends and family. If I would have played it before for the sales, I would of paid the $10.00 asking price. The classes of characters are well designed each with their own perks, they vary greatly in attributes and the feeling reminds me of a MMORPG Raid group where you have Tanks, Healers, Ranged, etc. I highly suggest Hammerwatch to anybody looking for a quick jump in, low requirements yet addicting game.

In 2012, an old strategy franchise returned from the dead and showed the gaming community just what real difficulty and real pain is. That game was XCOM: Enemy Unknown, rebooted from a series once known as X-COM and it went on to win not just the pleasure of old fans but gain new fans as well.  XCOM 2 is the sequel to Enemy Unknown. Storywise, everything that happened in Enemy Unknown was a fake. The Commander was really a prisoner to the alien invaders and Earth has been conquered and ruled by the aliens with a faction of apparent human symphatizers called ADVENT. As the Commander, you're freed at the beginning of the game and once again, it's up to you to lead the forces of Earth against the aliens, only this time you'll be the attacker.  From a gameplay perspective, XCOM 2 is mostly the same. You try your best to keep your soldiers in the best cover you can find and launch attacks on your enemies. What changes is that this time, you start out in Concealment mode. The enemy won't know where you are until you attack or get spotted by them, so this is where strategy comes in as you'll have to pick a perfect opportunity to ambush the enemy. Sounds simple? Well, it wouldn't be XCOM if it was simple now, wouldn't it? This time, certain missions also feature a turn timer which shows how many turns you can take before you fail the mission. With that in mind, you have to get to an objective as quickly as you can while also keeping an eye on the timer. While it may seem like a rush, the timer is mostly fair. You do have enough time to get to an objective, you just need to make the most of Concealment mode. Another new element to the game is the more destructible environment. Putting all of your soldiers on top of a building may not always be such a good idea.  And stopping you from reaching those objectives would be the aliens now bolstered by ranks of so-called human volunteers. Some aliens make a return like the Sectoid and Muton but even the ones that returned have completely evolved or have been upgraded. Sectoids are no longer cannon fodder, Floaters have been upgraded to the angelic-looking Archons and the dreaded Sectopods return in a slimmer yet more powerful design. One way or another, the aliens are going to ruin your day, each of them with their own unique way.  All is not lost however. Your soldiers may be outnumbered and outgunned, but they're no pushovers. The classes are both similar and different from Enemy Unknown. Snipers now known as Sharpshooters not only make use of Sniper rifles but can learn to use pistols like a gunslinger from the Old West. A new class called the Ranger employs a high-risk, high reward combat style to slash the enemy with swords in melee combat. With the destructible environments, the Grenadier basically becomes the most powerful and valuable class you can have in your squad as you can blow enemies off buildings or those hiding behind cars and other explosive objects. Most importantly, you also can blow up walls and obstacles so that you can reach a objective faster. Rest assured, you're going to have a lot of fun doing that.  While XCOM 2 doesn't disappoint in its gameplay, it does in it's optimization. You'd think that having it as a PC-exclusive would make it easier for Firaxis to release a more polished game but nope, that didn't happen. The game is buggy like hell, at least it's mostly visual bugs but still it can ruin the experience a little bit, especially for the last mission. There's also other weird problems like the aliens' turn taking too long, sudden framerate drops and the like but most players didn't experience anything game-breaking. Hopefully, Firaxis fixes the game soon.  All in all, XCOM 2 is a fantastic game and is a textbook example of what devs should do when making a sequel (not the bugs though). If you're one for great turn-based combat and you can stand the pain of watching your men and women die horribly in combat, then you'll love XCOM 2. Just remember when you miss a 95% shot, that's XCOM, baby.

The first chapter of Reversion was a mildly entertaining, if rather exceedingly traditional point and click adventure game with the undeniable benefit of being entirely free. Chapter two, 'The Meeting', now carries with it a price tag but also comes along with rather significantly less charm, cascading the first's amusing silliness for a melodramatic plot full of outrageous performances and exposition with any movement.  From start to finish The Meeting accomplishes almost nothing in advancing Reversion's plot, spending most of its time forcing you to solve inscrutable puzzles typically only difficult in so far as the clunky interface is proficient at making simple tasks intuitive and buried within interaction menus. I was leaning on a guide for much of the hour or so long chapter, as the in game hint system is fairly useless, and everytime I reached for a better walkthrough I was met with a terribly simple solution which usually involved clicking on an object more than I had reason to believe I ought to.  Amid the tedious fiddling with each and every random item The Meeting is stuffed full of agonizingly tone deaf performances of the abysmal dialogue, which would be almost delightful in its mediocrity if it wasn't so intent on being absolutely serious, outside of some groan inducing attempts at humor. Characters are practically caricatures of caricatures, with no personality outside of their cliched roles and grating voices, with nothing to say save a continual chorus of 'there's no time to explain.'  I'm not sure exactly what happened between chapter one and two. Maybe it's been too long and I didn't enjoy the first as much as I thought I did, or perhaps I simply expected more from the sequel as it was no longer this random novelty but actually trying to create a full season length experience. Whatever the case 'The Meeting' was an unexpectedly dreadful followup to something that seemed if not promising, than at least a potentially enjoyable diversion, but which I now cannot see myself coming back to regardless of whether or not chapter three ever shows itself.

Very fun and interactive, and keeps you guessing. You can shallowly explore nearly any object, which makes it even more interesting. This game is more about the thrill of exploring stuff in an emersive environment.  https://youtu.be/sPLJqZBUdvk  This video just shows the first area, which gets a good feel for the game. Picking up objects, moving them. Trying to figure out who we are, and why we exist.  CONS:    Basically, there are objects everywhere, and you have to find the 1 object on that board to continue. Once I realized this, I started clearing boards much faster.     There are very few enemies and enemy types. But since they can all kill you, this seems fine.   Strange bugs with video, which may crash anything but the best of systems. Such as,   when you trigger the first water action in the base, the game screams to a halt, but somehow continued past it on my system. My system specs include 8core 4.7Ghz chipset, 16GB DDR3 Ram, and a Radeon HD R9 290x with 8GB GDR5 Ram.     No glass for reflections. It seems the only thing to reflect is the surface of water, which only reflects light.   This is most likely a plot device, so don't judge too harshly.     No use for ANY items other than what is the point of the board. Finding thumb drives, but can't insert them into a pc. Finding drills, but unable to use them.   Find robot parts, but can't put together a robot. Find coffee machines, but can't make the food item.   These things would have made this hollow world more in depth. I do applaud on the depth already, but its a hollow depth.   Not much replay value. There seems to always be at least one choice per board, of 'do this or don't do this'. I suppose this gives a slightly different feel to the game, but over all I am unsure if the choice matters for future events.   Couple small bugs.   PROS:  Really nice graphics.   Lots of time to leasurley explore.   A perfect idea to explore of 'What should I do?'.   The game tells a story, which makes you feel like you are solving a mystery.   A great mystery story!   So good I'll say it twice!     The Pros out weigh the Cons drastically, even though its list is short. 7/10  Two thumbs up! Get this leisurely game and enjoy the full story!  It will make your heart pound!!!

What a beautiful culture.  Not only is this a charming and interesting game based on the Iñupiat people, but included are “insights” that you can collect which unlock various clips from a documentary with said people.   I played through the game first and watched the insights afterwards and although I didn’t really fully understand the game, it was very enjoyable to hear these people speak so passionately about their culture and their love for their way of life.   Whoever made this game put a lot of love and respect into what they were trying to teach us about and I actually love the fact that the documentary side to this was included. You can tell that this game was made to be as true to the culture it was portraying as possible.  I played this game as a local co-op and honestly can’t really imagine trying to play this on my own. Given this games theme of community, companionship and togetherness, I don’t think it would have been as fun to me playing on my own.   The music and narrative is great and the graphics are beautiful. I especially like that the narrative was in the native tongue and translated with subtitles, as to give it a more genuine feeling. The general glow and contrast is really nice and with all these things together it gave off a nice atmosphere.  There are puzzle elements to this game and it’s at these parts especially that I can’t imagine playing on my own. Me and my boyfriend had to co-ordinate and get certain timings right to be successful in some of the puzzles. Although they weren’t all that difficult, they still take some planning and quick execution.  As an overall, the game itself I can’t say was one of my favourites or particularly powerful to me, but I love everything that was put into this game and having those insights definitely redeemed it for me.  Last of all, achievements. Unfortunately this is a game that you actually need the DLC to be able to 100%. Apart from that, it’s an easy one to complete. It took us around 4 hours to get through and is a fairly enjoyable game.  All in all, this is a beautifully charming game and if you enjoy “for the experience” kind of games I think this is definitely worth trying. If you want something with a deep, plot twisting, life changing story, or something with crazy mechanics or puzzles, than you are better off elsewhere.   TL;DR : Based on a beautiful and interesting culture, a (best in) local co-op game with beautiful graphics and a short unlockable documentary on the people it’s based around. An easy 100% and a fairly enjoyable game.

I love Prince of Persia (the old 2D games), Flashback, and all of the other gameplay inspirations for Inside. I think the gameplay for Inside is god-awful. The puzzles are frustratingly simplistic, and executing many of them once you know the answer takes too long. I'd often find myself thinking up elaborate solutions to puzzles that actually boiled down to 'have a running start when you press a button.'  The game's aesthetic is neat, but the narrative it frames is delivered in an overly obtuse manner that opens the game up to many possible interpretations. Many artistic works utilize this device, but it doesn't really work here. There's a cool moment near the end that I will not spoil, although there was no ah-ha moment for me when I discovered the game's twist. It was mostly just weird and cool. Ultimately, though, there was not a single moment where I gave a ♥♥♥♥ about what I was doing besides walking to the right side of the screen.  The game is worth $20, because video games should not be measured by length. It's pretty and the animations are really impressive.   I didn't like it all that much. I expect that it will be quite popular within the YouTuber and games journalism communities, because it is easy to play and obtuse enough to make cool clickbait that copy/pastes forum posts from people who overthink the story. Personally, I wouldn't reccomend this to fans of the jump-around, one-hit-kill genre. I wish there was an 'Acknowledge game as good' button, but there isn't so I guess I'll click the 'no' button. Sorry to all who will downvote this as unhelpful as a result, and please continue to enjoy this game if you currently do.

review_text: Haven't played this since it was patched on Steam. Apparently it works, or at least works better now.  In general, a fun classic SW game.

I'm generally a fan of this genre of game, notionally the 'privateer' style game where there is space based action/combat along with RPG elements. I played about 18+ hours prior to this review.   This game contains a bit more of the RPG than some, but it's presentation is extremely basic...a dialog screen that list who at a given location you may talk to. Interaction is fairly straightforward after that.   There exists a very large skill web to populate, but not a great deal of content to use to level up...so its the same 4-5 quests (kill the pirate, get some ore, deliver a message, rescue some stranded civies, or buy and deliver some goods) over and over, or mining ore for cash and xp. The music is repetitive, the AI voice (computer on your ship) becomes anoying fairly quickly, and the main quest requires digging through a good bit of text (repeated by a large number of 'characters') to comprehend and follow. There are a number of factions, and I've focused on one thus far (order).   The route planning is attrocious, and given the dependance on autopiloting built into the game, the route planner has gotten me killed MANY times, mostly due to the fact it can't seem to go around an asteroid, so that the most boring parts of the game require your attention. Ultimately, this along with the repetitive nature of the other elements makes this a 'not recommended' game.   If you're looking for a similar game, done much better, go play Rebel Galaxy.

Barring a few blocky character models, this game has aged remarkably well and has such a great use of colour and style that it's still a joy to look at today. The soundtrack is mostly atmospheric but adds so much to the charm of the game.  It's fairly standard RTS fare but for the ability to create your own armies - you may take parts from two of the many stock animals to create a combined creature, and then take nine of these combined creatures into any game you play. The range of strategies is far more impressive than I think the developers could ever have hoped for, with creatures of various sizes, stats and abilities available to each player.  The only downsides I found with the original game were with the balance - some creatures are inherently better when combined with certain stock animals, thus making others effectively redundant but for their novelty value. In the past few years this has been addressed by player-made mods such as Tellurian, which also adds a range of new creatures to play with. It's nice to see that these mods are still compatible with the Steam edition.  All in all, I reccommend this :D  EDIT: I've nominated this for the Steam 'Test of Time' award!

app_name: School of Dragons: How to Train Your Dragon
review_text: Having played for a few hours, I'm still not sure how I feel about this game. The greatest issue I am having with it is the loading times just to get in. Once the game was downloaded, it took over half an hour just to gain access, and then another half hour after logging in for the game to actually start. Once in the game, it was fairly straight forward for an MMO. To gain access to the extras, you need to subscribe and it constantly reminds you of this, but you're guided through the games and quests, which are fairly simple. For my kids, the wait times are insanely frustrating. Not too many kids are going to wait around for half an hour or more to play a game before finding something else to do. Wouldn't recommend this to anyone who is looking for something to do on the fly - chances are, you'll never get in during your free time. But it is entertaining once you actually gain access...

Decent enough walking simulator; explore a valley about to be hit by a meteor in super slow motion. I think it could have been much better if the meteor actually impacted, rather than the entire area fading to white when it was still a good thirty seconds away from the ground. Terrain deformation and shrapnel would add a lot; although there are a lot of mysteriously broken things scattered around, falling in slow mo. It could be that this is meant to represent an air burst; if so it would've been nice to see shrapnel from the meteor flying everywhere rather than the aforementioned white screen.  It also would be better if I could figure out a run button, or if the interesting sites in this place were less far apart. And possibly more numerous. Though I do like the texts that are scattered here and there. Opening the main menu also does not pause the game, so there's no way to halt the meteor for a minute while you answer the phone or the door. The timer is strict, and about ten minutes long.  It feels like someone's first game, and that's alright. Buy if you want to support a game maker.

These are my first impressions. I received the game as part of a Humble Bundle. I'm recommending it only for particular people and on a deep sale  The best comparison I can draw is to The Vanishing of Ethan Carter. You walk around in a beautiful atmospheric world looking for story clues. That said, VEC is a better game with a far more engaging story. The visuals and sound in Kholat are impressive, but many criticize the poor story. Other points of criticism include the infrequency of saves, cheap deaths, and lack of marker on the map  Save points can be ten minutes away, and some deaths seem unavoidable. Moving slightly off the edge is death. Being caught by the enemy is death. The map has no marker so you need to rely on your compass (C key) and the geographical coordinates scattered around the world. Periodically you will run into coordinates written on rock which you can check you map for. I'm not having trouble navigating, but it's a turn off for some players  Don't buy this at full price. Try The Vanishing of Ethan Carter first, then think about Kholat when it's on deep sale. Right now it's just $1 on a discount keys site, which is a steal for this atmospheric journey

review_text: So plants. Wow. Much internet. Very zombies. Wow.

I own the starter edition but regardless i think my experience would have been the same if not slightly different if i owned the original $60 version. For starters the starter edition is cheap but the catch is you need to really grind in order to get your operators. My issue is they made the cost way too expensive because you would spend countless hours as your free characters. I dont mind that they made it more grinding but having to get 12k of whatever seige's currency is to just get 1 new guy is too much. Now lets get into the real meat of the game, yes it has no single player campaign but it doesnt need one since mainly this game deals with breaching and securing. It has training missions teaching the player how to do specific tasks that are required in the game either for pvp mode or the pve mode (terrorist hunt). Multiplayer is heavily on team work and strategy, that is fine but my issue with the multiplayer mode is that the game has quite a bit of hackers and trolls. That really kills the game for people. Terrorist hunt is a nice team training just like the single player situations. They are all right, pretty fun to do but there are so little map selections that it will get old pretty fast. I want to say their are about 8 maps that are unique and have a morning/ night versions. Lastly i can not recommend the game in its current state, internet issues, hackers, and lack of any real long term life span of the game with such a small selection of maps.

A solid entry as a runner game, but lacking the polish that is oh-so-hard to achieve these days. Worth a pick up unless you're strapped for bandwitdh, considering it's free (Ironic, isn't it?)   VISUAL AND POLISH NITPICKING The menus feel stilted, and look strange on 16:9 monitors, the graphics options are limited, the game is poorly optimized, leading my generally mashing machine to struggle around 15-40fps, but look a lot worse (due to the lack of freesync and/or inconsistent frame timings).  Now, I can't stress this next bit enough,   Bloom and distortion are not objectively good in any context, especially not in 3D  .  HDR and esposure adjustment? sure! objective around 95% of the time, a few exceptions. bloom shouldn't be used as a non-toggleable option. It's bloody blinding for some people.  Now the distortion is interesting in that it seems to only occur when taking damage, and that is nice, but may need an option for people with iffy eyes. BUT... The game itself either handles AA awfully, or just doesn't handle internal resolution well. sometimes it looks like nearest-neighbor scaled 720p AT ALL TIMES, it doesn't make much sense at all.  --END NITPICKING--  's pretty neat.

Actual Review: Fallout 4 is a great game, but it feels like most of the rpg elements were thrown out the window in favour of FPS elements. (Im lookin at you, the wanderer.)  Cons: -Characters are a bit bland. -Almost no variety in repeatable quests. -FPS, as stated above. -Graphics look like fallout 3 from up high (to me, anyways).  tl;dr: It has its problems, but its overall a good game.

I am writing this review as a winner of their Halloween contest, wherein they painted my portrait and put it in the game (it is in the Ring artifact room with the first combination code of the game!)  Even though I have such bias, I the bias speaks to how well the game was made. Without their ingenuity and artistic immersion, I would not have entered that contest in the first place and desire to win so much. Their artists (especially the one of my portrait, Andrzej Dybowski) are fantastic, the marketing and PR Manager Rafał Basaj is also a great guy. They are obviously a great team and I look forward to seeing more of their work in the future.  The only downside of the game is that it is short, and I think that is the only reason it is not a $60 game. I guess the only downside is when you have a small break during gameplay, like in the workshop (like a safe room), you have thoughts that creep into your mind...'I hope this doesn't end' and 'I am getting really close to the end now, dang.' It's a game that compells you to see what is next.  My only hope is that we see more of their work in the future, and hopefully DLC or some kind of additional content, but for now, I am content. This is a finished and well-made game. No bugs or anything.  For those that haven't played since release: they changed a lot of the rooms. It's a new experience. I played through pre-release several times and always had trouble remembering what was coming next, but post-release, it's a whole new level. Really great stuff!

Hearts of Iron 4, I have so many hours into this game but I couldnt be more split on what kind of score to give it, To explain why I will give you a breakdown what is good and what is bad about this game here.  +-battle plans make it much easier to plan attacks. In previous games you could give the ai focuses on what to attack and nothing more but this battle plan works much better but it isnt perfect. it has its flaws, like the AI doesnt know when to retreat, how to reinforce possitions and for some reason somtimes 1 unit goes forward on its own, like in a straight line. Also battla plans can be dangerous for the AI ignores things like forts (the maginot line) which can make your troops mash their heads upon this reinforced line forever.  + airplanes: now this point is very possitve for me for in hearts of iron 3 I always forgot I had these but now with this map mode and better ui contoll of the planes no more of that! This is a flat out improvement   ° National focuses, even though many nations do have a generic focus tree (a shame) those who dont can have a lot of fun and even the generic focus tree can make things change a lot, like changing china to fascist or Germany to democratic is all a thing you can do. THe national fous tree does need some more work though as some decissions (the atalntic wall as germany) are missing.  - The ai: My biggest bug bear with this game, the AI. You remember the AI from hearts of iron 3? that guy retreated out of pockets or reinforced weakspots, well not naymore. Tha aia doesnt recognize pockets or dangerous situations. better said it doesnt know what to do when it gets pocketed, AT ALL! this is a major issue for a strategy game and they have to fix this. Also AI germany loves to be at war with denmark, france and the USSR at the same time :S.  - naval invasions: they have fixed this a bit, the time has been reduced but dont expect to see japan island hopping, or do any naval invasions at all! This is an issue that needs to be fixed. not to mention how easy it is to get inot |England, just invade Galloway, its never defended and fight 3 english divisions.  +- nukes: while they do lower national unity and the explosion is amazing, it doesnt damage the unit in the tile and the industry in the tile is hardly damaged and population doesnt go down, what the hell?!?! this needs to be changed for this isnt how a nuke works.  Overal this game is... well inbetween for me. with mods this game is amazing and much better then hoi3 in my eyes but the vanilla game needs lots of work. So right now I would reccomend you to lower your expectations and look for some good mods or wait untill, at least the ai is fixed.

There are things to like about the game - it's quite pretty, and the way Lara can take a shitkicking reminds you of some of Indiana Jones' knock-down drag-out fights. But the game itself doesn't have much respect for you as a player, and everywhere it could be trying something new or bucking a trend, it just doesn't.   It's loud, it's flashy, and it's completely toothless. There are countless scenes where you're escaping something exploding or falling apart by the skin of your teeth - except it's not by the skin of the teeth, because you're not actually doing very much; it's, for all intents and purposes, a cutscene with the occasional need to press a button. Like a very, very easy Dragon's Lair.  Now, certainly this is preferable to a normal Dragon's Lair, where there's absolutely no tolerance for error. But it feels like the more cinematic a game gets, the less comfortable it is actually being a game, and we wind up turning the clock back to Laserdisc times.   One of the biggest things that bothered me about this game was how it was a game about being a 'survivor', yet it's far more 'First Blood Part 2' than 'First Blood'. There's one very good scene in the first half hour of the game where Lara is attacked, and winds up killing a guy. A few minutes before, she tells someone that she'd rather not become a murderer, and there she is, just having killed a man, and she just about throws up. It's one of the few times I've seen a game actually acknowledge how serious the act of killing is - then she immediately proceeds to brutally kill a dozen more guys without so much as batting an eye.  I feel like if the game really wanted to show Lara's transformation from someone who is scared, battered, and alone into a hardened survivor, it really should have left the mass slaughter out, or at least left it until much later in the game. Fighting should be hard, supplies should be scarce, and you should be trying to avoid combat as much as possible. Instead, you're constantly being assailed by guys coming out of the ♥♥♥♥in' walls and having to shoot the hell out them en masse. If they had maybe saved something like that until the end, to show the raw determination that Lara has, that might work. But it's so constant that it becomes routine, and lacks any sort of emotional resonance. The fights are very easy - so there's no tension, no panic, nothing to justify their inclusion beyond 'you have an AK so we have to give you something to shoot with it'.  And the puzzles - the game has great mechanics and clever design to tell you what you can and can't interact with, but it has no faith in its design. If you get stuck for more than a minute or two, the game will prompt you to go into 'Instinct view' where Lara will basically tell you straight out how to solve the puzzle. Saves you needing to run to GameFAQs when you bang your head against something obvious in retrospect for an hour, but completely excises the revelatory joy of discovering anything on your own.  The game is enjoyable in a 'let it wash over you' kind of way, but it's too timid and conservative to be great.  Oh, and when Lara got that chunk of rebar through her side at the beginning of the game? She NEVER GOT THAT TREATED. I'm surprised there's going to be a sequel because that's going to get so infected...

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Games for Windows LIVE is supposed to be removed from the game in November. Therefore I am changing this review to 'recommended'.  This is an excellent example of great game design. This is an action RPG that features very little exposition in terms of story. You won't be forced to watch a ton of cutscenes in order to discover what's going on. You're given pieces of the story as you go along and it's up to you to put it all together. Combat is difficult but fair. Music is reserved mostly for boss fights, you won't be hearing the same tunes over and over. All of these things help create a wonderful sense of immersion, and I highly recommend this game.

This in my opinion is the best Elder Scrolls Game. Morrowind was the game that introduced me to the fantastic world of TES! (Steam doesn't accurately show how much I've played. I've played many hours between the xbox version and a disc version for my pc. I only recently got it off Steam.)  Map: Vvardenfell is where you will be having your adventure in Morrowind. There is beauty and wonder to be found nearly everywhere. From the murky swamps and coastal villages to the barren ash lands and blight covered Red Mountain. There is plenty of variation to keep things from getting stale.  Story:  Without going into the actual plot (the plot is as good as ever though) the mechanics for the story requires reading and using your head. Unlike Oblivion and Skyrim (both are still incredible games), Morrowind doesn't hold your hand by giving you quest markers. Once you begin a quest it is wrote down in your journal and requires the player to read the journal and npc dialogue; not just skip through and run to the objective. The game has you ask around and talk to people to get information.   Gameplay: You playin first and third person just like the sequel. There is obviously skills, attributes, birth signs, spells, powers, races, and character creation like the other games. The combat, lockpicking, and probing can be frustrating because it's based on an RNG system. For example if you have a low long blade skill when you swing at something you have a low chance to hit and cause damage (even if your sword physically touches them when swung). If combat skills are low it can be a pain to fight enemies until your skills get higher. One other thing that's not necessarily and bad thing but there is no fast travel like in Oblivion and Skyrim. The only kind of fast travel is by taking a boat or silt strider (which both cost gold) and you can only travel between places that have either one. I like not being able to fast travel because it makes my journey feel log and epic, plus there are plenty of chances to go exploring as you're walking to you're destination.  Conclusion: Overall this game in my opinion is a fantastic open world game. This game made me fall in love with The Elder Scrolls series. Morrowind and the other TES games are just slammed full of content from the rich lore and people to the epic quests and stories that allow the player to feel like they are a part of the world of Nirn. Would recomend this game to anyone who loves open world rpgs.  10/10 would get killed by cliff racers again

Freakin' finally. Let's just admit it, 2014 was a crap year for games, especially on Steam. Finally here's a game that has me thinking about it when I'm not playing and always anxious to get back to my PC. Hard to put your finger on that magic *something* that makes this game stand out from the rest but IMHO, and with all due respect, the dumbing-down of PC games to appeal to the console set appeared to be an irreversible trend - until this game came out.   Yes kids, in this game you have to *think* before you act. The writing is mature without being dry, nothing is dumbed-down, nothing is overly streamlined.   As everyone else has already said, if you enjoyed the early Fallout games and have been itching for something just that smart and immersive, look no further.   It is absolutely the Best Game of the Year 2014. Bravo Devs, and THANK YOU for making a game for adults in a year of games designed to appeal to the lowest common denominator.

I can't speak for the version on Steam since I haven't played this game since 1999... but I have very fond memories of it back in the day. It was a very ambitious game that would have been quickly forgotten if it hadn't been for the awesome David Bowie soundtrack and secret David Bowie concerts to find.  The main game is a 3D free-roaming exploration with some basic puzzle solving (not just how to navigate through doors and avoid swearing at the third-person camera), with a couple of completely different game-modes thrown in for good measure - a not-completely-awful one-on-one 3D fighting game and a fairly awful first-person shooter.  It was a great place to explore, it had an amazing soundtrack and it was quirky, sexy fun whilst also being delightfully awful and unbelievably cool. Don't expect a perfect triple-A game - expert a nostalgic slice of body-swapping, lizard-feeding gaming history.

This Pre-Sequel is not the same quality as the previous Borderlands. It feels cheap and untested for the majority of its game play and has quite a few outstanding issues: Geometry problems (vehicles going off the map), mobs shooting/running/flying through structures and various terrain glitches just to name a few engine based issues. I personally on SteamOS have problems with some Guns' rate of fire suddenly changing in the middle of a fight for no apparent reasons. For the AI, enemies love flying and they also love getting stuck while flying or they just stop doing whatever they were doing to give you time to shoot them down. (pretty)  This release of Borderlands also adds a few mechanical gimmicks, the main one being the low gravity and the absence or presence of an atmosphere. Unfortunately it just doesn't work very well, Your character is supposed to benefit from the low gravity using an Oz kit. Using the Oz kit you can apparently fly, move quickly across terrain etc... This is probably true, if you are an enemy. You on the other hand don't fly or move quicker. It feels like you're stuck with Marry Poppins' Umbrela and all your enemies have Tribe based jet packs. The low gravity/lack of atmosphere thing ends up turning combat into Stay Puft Marshmallow Man vs An annoying bunch of prepubescent kids.  Combine the slow motion fights with the few vehicles in the game and you've got quite a contrast and it's not to say that vehicle combat is any better. In most fights with vehicle weapon systems, you can stop, aim, put the vehicle in reverse, step back a little, aim, kill and you're done. The combat is just massively disappointing in Borderlands: The Pre-Sequel. It is slow and not exciting at all.  A few reviews out there have been stating that level design is confusing, well I disagree. It's just dumb. So if you approach levels the dumbest way you can, you shouldn't be confused at all. One of the most frustrating feature from these levels is how checkpoints are distributed and how some of it forces you to walk an entire map back if you die while others are right next to each other.  Really disappointed with this game, I was expecting better quality, but it looks like it was basically thrown out to fans just to wrap up the Borderlands franchise. I'm happy I got the title at a discount and I wouldn't recommend this game at full price to any hardcore Borderlands fan.

There's a lot to be said for Not a Hero, the most recent (as of this writing) game by Roll7 and Devolver Digital that puts players in the shoes of a group of people working to clean up crime on behalf of an anthropomorphic rabbit named BunnyLord.  I was part of the beta testing team for this game, and the first thing that stood out to me was how polished the game is. This is a gorgeous game that feels like the offspring between Broforce, Super Meat Boy and Hotline Miami. The controls are incredibly responsive, there were no apparent bugs that I caught (although there are a couple ways to exploit movement speed and the dive mechanics) and the death animations were equal parts amusing and brutal.  The game takes place in the three weeks leading up to the U.K.'s mayoral elections, and the first character, Steve, is chosen by BunnyLord to hunt down and destroy various criminal elements in order to up their approval rating and win the ballot. As time goes on and the player's rating rises, BunnyLord brings in new staffers to help out on missions, including a shotgun-toting redneck, a slovenly man with an afro and gay Jesus. Not making this up.  Each character has their own pros and cons - while Steve is the everyman character with no major advantages or disadvantages, others have different skill sets. Samantha is a Welsh alcoholic who speaks in an exaggerated accent and moves very quickly but has poor accuracy. Jesus runs fast but can't shoot straight while moving. Mike has a powerful shotgun and can do quick executions, but only gets two shots per reload.  A lot of the levels throughout the game favor specific characters. In fact, the time-based challenges are nigh-impossible to complete using the same character - you'll have to mix it up in order to complete the stages, which are graded based on how many of the objectives are completed at once.  There's a few interesting concepts I liked about the game. The post-mission chat with BunnyLord changes depending on which character you've picked, how many of the objectives you completed and how much damage you took during the mission. Likewise, there are some extremely amusing level objectives - the one where you escort BunnyLord's aunt, Ruby, back to her apartment is hilarious - her nonstop chatter is mixed in with the soundtrack, and she proves to be just as capable with a weapon as the other characters are. That said, the humor can come off as forced at times, and I never really felt there was a need to listen to the pre-mission briefings.  If there's one real negative I have about the game, it's that the later stages devolve from a sort of freeflow experience (where you can either rush through levels for speed or take your time and pick off enemies one-by-one) to complete trial-and-error. The last five levels are filled with samurai and enemies who will kill you in one hit, and it is extremely frustrating to go all the way through a level, get within distance of the exit and suddenly get impaled through the chest by an enemy who can run much faster than you.  Having finished it, I don't think the game gives much incentive to go back and replay levels for 100% completion. I was just fine steamrolling through the game, and although the ending seems to guilt-trip the player into going back and doing all the optional stuff, I just didn't care enough to replay it.  I would still recommend Not A Hero to anyone looking for a solid, polished 2D shooter, and I enjoyed the experience.

This game is meant to be a full simulation of flight, and in no way is a simulation 'game'. After this past weekend and around 10 hours playtime, probably only 5 which were spent in the sky, I feel like I could legitimately fly a Cessna 172. I honestly don't believe I would fly highly efficiently, but yes I could get off the ground and land.   You NEED a controller or joystick to make the game reasonable in difficulty. There are no tutorials so learning will be a lot of trial and error(hence the 5 hours on the runway). I used an Xbox Controller, but don't bind acceleration to a thumbstick, bind it to A and B. If it is bound to a thumbstick it will naturally rest in 50% throttle, you can fine tune thrust in small increments with keys.  I do plan on buying a HOTAS setup(ideal), but they are pricey for good ones. The graphics are decent and can easily be modded with free content downloads(100's) on the xplane forum to suit individual tastes. Modding is easy, took me less than an hour to put in 4. I will most likely replace more textures/meshes later for further realism. This game will kill your harddrive space, especially when putting in UHD or HD texture meshes(google alpilotx), its free.  The tons of freeware, mods, plugins, textures you find for this game easily double the worth of it. Please not though there are no objectives, money, progression in this simulation. IT is purely a SIM. Travel time will be accurate as you fly, I spent an hour or more flying over Lake Michigan from Ohare to South Haven, MI in what has to be realistic travel time(Cessna 172).  Don't buy this if you want a game, if you want to learn - buy this.
