app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: Haven't touched Call of Duty since 2007 but I got a free weekend of Black Ops 3 so I thought why not play it, maybe it's good...  ------------------------------- After playing it...  The fact that people actually pay REAL money for this is astounding, I've heard COD is a terrible game for casual console players BUT MY GOD this game is  awful!   [ONLY PLAYED MULTIPLAYER] I'm not touching singleplayer it'll give me cancer.     The gameplay is repetitive and mundane. Very little skill involved and 90% of the time you'll die because someone spawned behind you, most of your kills will be because you spawned behind someone else.    The maps are tiny and uninteresting.    Graphics are pretty bad compared to a majority of the games that came out in 2015. I also noticed some really low rez textures, just like Aliens: Colonial Marines had on release.    Reloading animations and all animations in general are complete crap. I was using the weapon that looks like an AK47 and the reloading animation for it had me laughing for about 5 minutes, its unatural and comes a sudden stop when he puts the magazine in.    Sound design is straight up terrible, especially when a character talks because it clearly plays over the top of everything else and it sounds incredibly unnatural. Nothing blends together to create an atmosphere its just lots of single sound effects with clear stops and starts playing over each other.    Ragdolls are as stiff as wood, this is just a clear sign that this game has no attention to detail.    This futuristic stuff is uninspired, probably the reason for why everyone hates it so much.    The whole operators thing feels pointless.    Microtransactions...   So who is to blame for this monstrosity? Well isn't it obvious? The absolute morons who bought it, people have complained for years about how Call of Duty sucks but at the end of the day they f*cking buy it at full price and the company makes there money.  The people who make this aren't to blame. YOU ARE.  YOU ARE THE REASON FOR WHY PRE-ORDER BONUSES EXIST.

as it stands i can not reccomend Steredenn. The combat is fun, but the way the weapons and bosses are balanced right now make every death feel like the game cheated you. it lacks the depth of any of it's competing 'rougelites' so the game kind of boils down to 'this weapon is good' and 'this weapon will get you killed'.  The prizes for defeating bosses feel really shallow and lame, some of them will give you an interesting advantage like a familiar bot which shoots enemies, but nothing feels substantial. helper bots do very little damage, damage upgrades feel almost non-existent, actual gameplay-effecting upgrades are very sparse.  almost every boss has at least one attack which is nigh-impossible to avoid, you can do it with enough practice, but at the time of facing it for the first time it just feels like you died because you weren't expecting something, and not because you didn't perform.  there's nothing discoverable in this game outside of the patterns of bosses, you face an enemy, you will face it a thousand times. obviously no secrets, no currency which means no buying upgrades which means choice is limited to the one you make after killing a boss, on which crappy powerup you will take.  the game is very close to being good, i do enjoy it at times, but i feel like it should have twice the content it currently has to compete with objectively better reccomendations, being other schmupps or other roguelites.  take your money, and buy enter the gungeon.

So, it all begun with simple 'I'd like to try something different.' And I'll be completely honest with you - this game sucks. More on that matter a little later, but be advised, there's a good reason for me saying that.  Now, when we know what made me buy 'Her Story', let's just focus on something a little more different - my experiences with this game. Feeling like solving a puzzle, burried deep down is some PD archives? Check. Wanting to move your brain cells a little, don't really want all elements on the table from the start? Check. Want to grab a pencil and an old-school notebook you were hiding for occasions like that? Check.  This game has it all - surprise, not-too-linear story, element of fiding a secret and solving it completely. This is why it took my heart in an instant, and left me craving for more. Now, you've been lured by me saying that this game sucks, and promised to shed some light on it: Remeber the amazement you felt the first time you've watched 'Sixth Sense'? BUT THERE IS ABSOLUTELY NO GOING BACK FROM KNOWING THAT BRUCE WILLIS IS ACTUALLY DEAD. The same problem is with 'Her Story' - I want more, much, much more; I want to bury myself in that story again, feel it once again and have that ':O' face while watching the clips and typing new words into the search box with my cheeks red. Knowing how it ends (although a big plus is the 'opened ending', leaving you free for interpretation: basically, there're two theories and both have their point) makes you wanna run and cry into a pillow, because you already know all the secrets. That's the only problem with it, honestly. 3 amazing hours to get to know the story, one more to make it 100% to get the 'bigger picture'. Worth your time, worth your money. Now, make yourself a favor, make a cup of coffee or your fauvorite tea, (grab a cig if you smoke and you're 18 or older), take a small notebook and a pencil. Sit in front of your computer. Give up the cinema, spend 3 comfy hours at home, while being a detective, the audience, the judge and a director at once. Thank me later.

Firewatch  is one of those games that I feel went through a tad few too many reworks. The world is fine, artstyle terrific, music pleasing, and I think it's definitely worth a buy, but there's lost potential here.  Delilah and Henry are overall both written terrifically, and I have nothing but praise for the dialogue and performance of their VAs. These two were organic, convincing, and overall just  human  in a way that many game characters fail. During the first few days their relationship developed in a sweet way, and I'd be content if the game focused on that the entire time. Nevertheless, the story presented always had a feeling of that there was supposed to be something more, past the point of an intentional red herring and into where it seems that the last arc especially was altered with not much time left in development.   What made the characters so great was dropped at the end - they no longer remained logically consistent and failed to behave as an emotional person would. The game spent a huge amount of time building up to a seemingly certain climax, only for it to never come. Events occurred, sure, but the reaction to them by both Henry and Delilah fell completely flat. The last scene suffered from the same issues.  Regardless,  Firewatch  succeeds in both developing and keeping player attention, and while I'd have preferred a different ending I also wouldn't say that the current spoils the game entirely.

The atmosphere seems fantastic? I went into this feeling really positive, but I was kind of flabbergasted by how egregious some of the bad qualities are.  1. The controls are pretty bad. Part of that is a gameplay gimmick that I just happen not to like. You are controlling two characters with a single input. One of them walks faster than the other. The girl walks at a steady rate. The boy either walks slower than the girl or, if you press the run key, faster than the girl. A lot of the challenge (and in my opinion incredible frustration) derives from this mechanic, as you are guiding the characters over narrow paths that they aren't allowed to step off of or else you die and have to start over. This might not have been quite so bad if there were smooth controls, like the ability to move with a joystick, but you are limited to using WASD, making the character movement clunky and jagged, so the WASD controls (sorry PC master race) actually make things significantly worse instead of better in this case.  2. The camera angles can be antagonistic, especially when dealing with the above controls.  3. The above two gripes I probably could have had patience with for a while, but really the worst of all is that the game is just bad at communication. It doesn't tell you what you ARE supposed to do. It doesn't tell you what you're NOT supposed to do. You just start moving these characters and you don't know where you are moving them to or why. Then you will die and you won't understand why. Then you will die again with zero understanding why. It's kinda hard to win at a game that doesn't bother to tell you its objective or its rules.  If you like a game that says 'die until you figure out what the point of me is' then have fun with this one, but it didn't take me long to go from optimistic to just plain annoyed.

Dear god, not you too. Only the dead can know peace from this hell. Kid, I'm sorry. I'm so sorry.  The Moon, it had seemed like an easy mission to all of us. Simply fix some pipes and go back home to Earth, that was 5 years ago. This place is where NASA sends all of the people they want rid of to exist in this hellish land. There is no escape from the moon. Houston will never radio back in. They've abandoned us here, everyone you see here has met the same fate you have. I just wish it wasn't so.  At first, we had fun. Using the speech program to say silly things while still getting the job done, but after a while. We tried sending word that we had finished the job, but there was no response. It wasn't till about 3 hours later that we discovered the ship didn't have any fuel to send us home, because it never was going to. We are simply junk thrown away by NASA. We are doomed to ours fates, no matter how creative they are. Last week, a member of the first crew that got here went insane, it was awful. He died sitting alone with foam coming out of his mouth, repeating over and over. 'I love you, Mom.' Dear god, I can still hear his screams.  There is no escape from this place, I can't stand to see more arrive here, we can't even kill ourselves with our sanity intact. Everything we tried didn't work, blow torch to the face? The torch suddenly shut off. Ran over by the rover? That stopped by itself too. Nothing works, we are doomed to an existence of exhile and madness. I'm sorry you have to go through this too. But, there is one thing we can do. I never wanted to do it, but now I don't care. Take this wrench, we are going to tear this entire station down, break apart the solar relays, destroy the oxygen supplies. Tear the whole damn thing down.  Godspeed, son. Hopefully, we will die as heros and send a message to the swine known as NASA.

If you're not easily emotionally manipulated or otherwise do not get emotionally invested in a piece of media, LIMBO is not worth the purchase.  Outside of that highly subjective criteria, LIMBO's positive points are few and far between. It is 'Another World' with a monochromatic art style, ragdoll physics, and absolutely none of the tight narrative focus that, while lacking in dialogue, drove 'Another World' to the status of cult classic. It is a cinematic platformer without the cinema, relying on the 'feel' over the execution.  On a technical level, LIMBO is not just competent but surprisingly well done. The platforming is precise, the hitboxes are accurate, the many different platforming mechanics the game throws at you all perfectly executed and without a single glitch or snag in sight. But on a design level it is atrocious - long stretches of simply heading right, stock puzzles and cliche mechanics that aren't even explored particularly indepth before being forgotten about. It quickly becomes a chore for anyone who's touched a small-budget platformer before.  The game does have its high points - with the secret level in particular actually exploring the mechanics indepth and being well worth playing, but those are few and far between the boring standard puzzles that make the game feel like it belongs on Newgrounds.   On the whole I can't recommend paying for this game - if you want superb platformer, play Super Meat Boy or Dustforce. If you want a well-done cinematic platformer, play Abe's Odyssey, Flashback or Another World. If you want to play a simple, fairly boring platformer whose redeeming points have to be actively sought to see, play LIMBO.

You'll fail a lot. I did in the beginning. Learning to build off simple mechanics can help solidify your footing in your world.  When creating a new world, it'll create large patches of one of two kinds of nastiness. First is the Corruption: Full of flying worm-like creatures in a land of shadows, Shadow Orbs pulsate with pure evil. The other is the Crimson: A living land of ichor, eyeballs, flesh, and bone where Demon Hearts throb with intelligent hunger. Both will slowly spread through Ice, Sand, Stone, and more.  Aside from those lands of evil, you'll encounter deserts, forests, sky islands, snowy lands, a jungle at one end of the world, and a dungeon at the opposite. The jungle is teeming with life that wants to end yours. The dungeon is teeming with death that wants to bring about yours.  Deep within the world, you'll find riches galore and danger to match. Deeper and deeper you'll dig, straight into the fiery underworld...and if you're willing to sacrifice your Guide to the boiling lakes of lava, the underworld will try to devour you, too. If you manage to emerge victorious, the world will be blessed with a land of Hallow...but such purity must be equally balanced by evil.  The creation of such extremes throws your world into Hard Mode...and Hard is an understatement. Every challenge you overcome will increase the danger...and the rewards...

Do I recommend this? yes, yes I do, hands down. unless you're the sort of invalid that thinks Call of Duty is Mindblowing.  XCOM: Enemy Unknown is a faithful and excellently designed reboot of the 90's PC strategy Franchise that started with UFO: Enemy Unknown. I really don't know what I can say about this game that hasn't already been said, it's a good game if you're sick of the same typical FPS excretion year in, year out. it's interesting it's well made, looks great considering it's a strategy game and I was playing this with low graphics and it still looked great.  GAMEPLAY: solid turn-based strategy that reminds me of the old Warhammer 40k games I played as a kid like Chaos Gate (only reference I can make having never played the original XCOM games). The game is one of those 'easy to learn, hard to master' affairs, controls are intuitive and well designed without holding your hamd like you're a fecking 5 year old. Onto my gripes with the gameplay, the difficulty curve is ridiculous and will punish you for getting ANYTHING wrong in some way or another. The enemy will advance their technology regardless of whether you do or not so slow and steady wins ♥♥♥♥ all it appears. some of your own tactics appear to be a little broken too, I hardly ever had to leave the LZ with my team entirely made up of 5 snipers and 1 mec, at top rank snipers can see like a hawk and then shoot that hawks head off from 2 ♥♥♥♥ing towns away. there is also some imbalancing on the Alien side, particularly with a type of late game enemy called a sectopod that seems to literally have no weakness, it has 30 HP which is a hell of alot on this game and as if the high health isn't bad enough it's main weapon does ridiculous ammounts of damage and it can fire more than once in it's turn, and after that can still take reaction shots which normally cannot be taken if a shot was taken in your own turn. it also likes to completely ignore damage, I had my team outfitted with plasma weapons which are supposed to do a minimum of about 8 damage and up doing about 2 to 5 on this monster.  STORY: it begins as a typical alien invasion story but as you learn more about your enemy it becomes far more compelling and complex. in game cutscenes move the story along without forcing you to move the story on, the story moves at the pace you do because the story based missions are hard as nails. it's a solid story, nothing special, but it's solid.  OVERALL: 8.5/10 I wanted to give this a 9 but that is reserved for the best game ever that is yet to come out and I call 10/10 impossible to aquire since that would be the perfect game that will never exist. it's one of the most interesting games released in recent year and does an excellent job of bringing this franchise into the modern generation and manges to do it without completely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ising it. it's a good game, it's an excellent game for the thinking man or woman. the game really drives home the consequences of command and I felt a small ammount of attachment to my squad that when members died I'd have a luttke funeral at my desk for them and leave roses at the side of my monitor.

*BEFORE YOU CONTINUE* I've been in Games Dev for a while now, so everything in this review comes from an industry-grade perspective. I'll try to be as objective as I can, so you can decide for yourself whether or not it's worth it.  If a classic top-down RPG is your goal, by all means, get this. It has all you need to get started - the default build even comes with a main AND pause menu!  So, let's play pros and cons: Pros: - Simple to navigate UI - A LOT of pre made stuff to make prototyping quickly - Documentation for the programmers - Reasonable price (when I bought it) - People make stuff for this game in Workshop - Some cool stuff in the workshop Cons: - It's easy to tell when someone has made a game with this program - Most of the community made graphics conform to the grid of RPG Maker - making most of the stuff look 'generic' - Want to make a turn-based strategy? Best you find something else. - learnerFirendly = preMadeCode ? No : Yes (programmers will understand this);  - Documentation isn't as in-depth as some of the meatier engines out there - People make stuff for this game in Workshop - Pre-made events make it easier, but what if you wanted to make a game in a different engine?  TL;DR Good for top-down RPGs, not so much for other games - but not impossible

What’s this game all about?   Since there is no established genre which Reus would fit in and it’s not easy to intuitively understand what the deal with the game is, I’ll explain the core gameplay loop in detail.  At first you’re presented with your barren 2D planet and you’re given control of Reus’ four trademark giants. With them you create your planet’s biomes and resource nodes, which in turn leads to establishment of human settlements. Each settlement’s most important attribute is its resource count, split into three categories: food, wealth, technology. Resource count will gradually rise from nothing at founding to some maximum amount dynamically determined by composition of resource nodes within settlements’ borders. Resources don’t have any traditional functions (i.e. food isn’t used to feed the population), they are simply gathered for two purposes: they count towards overall score and are used to complete so-called ‘projects’.  Projects are the core gameplay concept in Reus. They are buildings, such as a granary or an observatory, which spontaneously appear as construction sites within settlements’ borders and they are completed once settlement gathers certain amount of resources. Each project provides a unique passive bonus to its settlement, so players are usually forced to adjust their resource composition once a new project pops up. Completing projects is rewarded with ‘ambassadors’ each of whom is assigned to a giant thus upgrading it. Upgrades allow giants to create more advanced resource nodes which in turn makes it possible to complete increasingly demanding projects.   Who’s going to like it?   I’d recommend Reus to anyone who is going to enjoy getting familiar with characteristics of a large number of resource types, combining them in a manner that will extract the most value from each land patch, tinkering with secondary resources to power up their settlements’ growth, analyze what upgrade path will best suit their current situation, etc.  As far as presentation goes Reus is pretty strong. Graphics are simple but stylish. Music is calm and pleasant even if it becomes slightly repetitive after a while. Interface is tidy and responsive, although some of the tooltips are not informative enough (luckily there’s a wiki website which describes everything in detail).   Any red flags?   This is not a god game, so if anyone is looking for that particular fix - look elsewhere. Humans of Reus have extremely limited autonomy and the game requires lots of micromanagement.  There is no form of time compression so sporadically players will have to sit through a period of nothing to do. Generally pace of the game is rather slow with plenty of waiting for cooldowns or for giants to move between settlements. Players with little patience might want to think twice before plunging in.  And completionists beware – top tier projects require some serious analysis and calculations and on top of that there are tons of unlocks available. Generally, you won’t be able to just breeze through the content.

Verdict: Strider is fast-paced side-scrolling slasher which hits all the right notes. The combat system is fun and constantly evolving with each new upgrade. By the end of the game your character has 3 powers, throwing kunai, 4 sword types and more than enough mobility to move around feeling like a real pro cyber ninja making this game a blast from start to finish. Also a lot of fun and hard boss fights are a real plus.  Some downsides/complaints: My experience was ruined a bit by the game's perfomance. Even with the resolution turned down the game still dropped FPS consistenly when there were a lot of enemies and clutter on screen. Also navigating the map is a struggle by its self. You never really know how to get where you want to go unless you try and fail. And the 2 powers you unlock help you move around the map to specific places but don't indicate you where so only way to know is try and see which is really annoying. Last and least is the story is basically non-existent just acts as a tool for throwing at your way a ton of ridiculous enemies so it serves it's purpose.

app_name: Dysfunctional Systems: Learning to Manage Chaos
review_text: Visual novels!  Learning to Manage Chaos is only the first chapter of Dysfunctional Systems. Because of that, it's not very long (you could easily get through both main routes in a day) and offers virtually no resolution or closure in order to set up future installments. If I had to pick one thing about the story to criticize, it would be that they really, REALLY emphasized how sheltered Winter's upbringing was. The story does, however, have a very interesting premise (complete with moral dilemmas, which is always nice), and I look foward to further exploring its universe.  I think five bucks is a fair price, especially considering that the (great) soundtrack is included.

This is the game that made me say 'never again' to Early Access. Other reviews have detailed the bugs here, so I won't go into all that's wrong with the game. But from the Early Access perspective, as players we buy into Early Access because we believe in a concept and want to financially support independent developers. It's always a risk that the game won't turn out the way we hope, but we expect a genuine effort on the part of the developer to try to deliver on their promise. The promise of this game sounded great, and there are some solid gameplay ideas in here, but the developer took the money and abandonded the game. I learned a great lesson here; never trust a game developer on their promises. In the future, I will wait for a finished product before I put down my money. It's unfortunate that developers like this are going to ruin the crowd-funding paradigm, but it's inevitable that, with experiences like this, more and more players will refuse to take these risks.

This is surreal and charming--and overpriced; it's worth getting on sale or in a Humble Bundle pack, but unless you have a fetish for flowers, probably not worth paying full price for. (I suppose it depends on your available leisure money.)  That said, the game is fun for people like me who like games with low-pressure win conditions; it's not at all clear when you start playing what you're supposed to do--but it doesn't matter; you click the pretty flower and it explodes into color and moans at you.  Flower. Moaning. And saying 'yes' and 'more, faster, please,' and similar comments. The only thing subtle about the eroticism in this game is the lack of human-shaped genitalia. The actual game mechanics are subtle: after the first flower event (not sure what else to call it), you get progress bars, and more flowers open up; you can replay each of them, trying to maximize, erm, 'color,' which is not the same as speed--go too fast, and you'll be told to slow down.  ... Maybe it is worth full price.

Absolutely loving this game :D It is what open ended space exploration is meant to be like. No hand-holding here, no rails; only you, your space ship and your sense of wonder.  Runs smoothly thusfar, on max gfx settings on a 780GTX 16GB Ram. Haven't had a single crash; but I realise other players' experiences may differ depending on system and setups; these are all issues that will be ironed out in the near future, I'm sure; it's a small indie team, be patient.   Is it worth $60.00? If you love space, exploration and just a relaxing game where no goal in particular is the goal, absolutely! If you're looking for Battlefield style handholding fps shooter actionz, then sadly no - your expectation will no be met here (and for this I am glad).   There are a few frame drops here and there, but nothing extreme in my experience. The sounds and atmosphere are spectacular. The creatures can range from frightening to adorable, rng-be praised. My starting system was a bit bleak - nearly froze to death on my first planet :D Was magnificent to lift off and exit the atmosphere.   Having a blast.  Put away your short attention span, your lust for immediate gratification; jump in, explore and just enjoy the worlds that are yours and yours alone to explore.

Sad, but true: Put 'Japan' in your game title and the game will sell.  But I could let that 'slide', because it's a puzzle game after all... PUZZLE - yes! Puzzle. Though the creator went out of his way to do everything to take the PUZZLE element out of the game. What's left? Well... do you know the experience of cutting & pasting multiple lines in a textfile? That's what this game is. Let me eleborate:  1) You do not 'rotate' the pieces, they are pre-fixed to be alligned correctly the moment you 'fish' them out of the pond.  2) Double clicking somewhere around the area automatically fixes the position of your puzzle piece and thus takes away the challenging element. They stay fixed in that position also, so you already know you got the puzzle piece at the right spot. 3) The shapes of the pieces are so easily distinguishable that you don't even have to look at the overall picture to arrange them together.  What's fun about puzzle games? Not knowing which direction the puzzle piece goes, not knowing where to put it and having the joy of finally fitting one with the other after a long time of 'puzzling'. The creator took all those 3 elements away and what's left is a drag... litterally dragging the pieces to the board and be done with it.  Verdict: FAIL 2/10

Cosmic DJ is, all things considered, indeed more of a toy than a proper game but it's a pretty fun one. As the titular protagonist, your task is to help people and fight bad vibes by filling a bar, you do so adding instruments to the track that's playing thus creating your own songs.   PROS:   -while extremely simple,the gameplay is very fun  -nonsensical yet entertaining story  -top notch sound (created by you aswell)  -good amount of different instruments  -intuitive controls,whether tapping or using a grid it's easy to choose instruments,their tone and when they'd appear  -charming graphics, they're simple overall but there's nice little touches, like the various instruments changing the scene whenever they're active during the track  -good sense of humour through the whole game   CONS:   -very simple gameplay, you can just click randomly and win, if you're so inclined  -very short, if you try and make some decent music it'd take you about an hour and a half  -screen flashing is sometimes overdone   http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=450843453   Overall I found Cosmic DJ to be a fun albeit limited game: creating your tracks is an interesting experience and it surely tickled my creative side while the funny story was enough to carry the game through its duration. For 80 cents on sale I think this'd be an interesting experience for most people, even more so if you're interested in music.

app_name: Darkness Within: In Pursuit of Loath Nolder
review_text: If you're a fan of classic Point-and-Click mysteries in the vein of games like 'Scatches', this one is definitely for you. A variety of beautiful, elegant environments and a rich, haunting soundtrack. Great atmosphere! You play as a police detective searching for the titular Loath Nolder amidst a slowly developing, Lovecraftian-inspired plot. A complex and compelling story builds around you in the form of newpaper articles, books, notes and your interaction with various strange and interesting characters. The puzzles are not terribly difficult, but do require you pay attention to the information you gather on the way.   A few gripes: There is an item and information combination mechanic present that isn't explained very well in-game, so some Google-fu is required. Some of the areas are also a little too dark (even with Gamma adjusted) and you may miss things if you're not careful. This is also the first in a trilogy and while you can find the first two on Steam, it seems the third game was discontinued in 2014 (having played only this one so far, I'm not sure where that might leave off the conclusion of the story). As a standalone though, this one is worth it.

A little history lesson. A couple years back there was a free game called Ace of Spades. Think of it as a mix of minecraft and old shooters, it was more of a trench warfare type game. Then Jagex came alone, and purchased Ace of Spades. They shut the entire thing down and made a worse paid version, which died rather quickly. Now we come to this game, it's Ace of Spades again but with a different name and a few new features, even the font is the same! Everyone who bought AoS has been abandoned and now they're cashing in with an entirely new product that could have just been an update to the original.  As soon as the player numbers for this game dwindle they'll end all support and probably work on something else they get make money off of. If this game was free, I would be alright with it. But abandoning a game and a paid update is not something we should be supporting. If you want to play a game like this, play the original at  buildandshoot.com  This is not worth your money.

Essentially a tower defense which utilises a party-based combat system and takes a rogue-like approach to map generation. The game's scope, while limited, is novel and worthwhile for tower defense fans who want something a little different.  Map squares contains a randomly generated buildings which provide upgrades and are discovered as the player explores, however with each building unlocked there is a chance of battle, with increasing difficulty commensurate to distance from the starting base. While there is an element of skill involved, luck plays a larger part in the party's survival, before inevitable failure.  While some of the screenshots may look similar to  Plants vs. Zombies , Hunters of the Dead plays differently, with party members using action points upon placement on the combat board and subsequent movement.  Permanent upgrades can be purchased between games, however once all the upgrades have been unlocked the game turns to being essentially a high-score chaser.  - Skinny from the Fat B*stard  (curator link)

Five Nights at Freddy's is my first ever horror game. Nearly everyone knows about, thanks to the fame of many Youtubers, such as Markiplier and Game Theory. I've followed the series through both of these people, but when I finally bought the original game, I wanted to experience this game first hand. Overall, I really enjoyed my playthorugh of FNAF, finding it a mix of challenge and skill. Yes, the RNG system did get me a few times, but from night 1-6, I died only three times, and none on night six. Not lying, I didn't die on night six. From where Scott Cawthon is now with Sister Location, it's interesting to play the game which began, with the mysterious Golden Freddy, a few lines of dialogue in game and phone guys recordings. Whilst the gameplay is easy and simple, and only a few screens for images, the sounds make the player tense and alert, whilst the cameras prevent too much freedom. We also can't forget the iconic animatronics, who could be considered to be as many triple A video game characters. To finish, this game is something you should play through. I've had quite an easy time, and probably I'll play this on off as the game really immerses you. I'm looking forward to playing all, even if I am rather late (like two years)

This is my first review that I am aware of. If this is your first time playing a HOI game, then you should be happy with it. It's interesting and you will invest a lot of hours into it.  However, if you have any of the previous HOI's, this game is a joke. Once again, publishers feel they need to dumb down games in order to make more money. This HOI is incredibly simplistic and takes away a lot of the fabulous complexity in previous versions that made the game stand out. Research is simple and visually looks stupid with the fat buttons to make sure you follow the obvious linear trees. By 1944 in my first game, I had all national focuses, and 90% of technologies filled. The AI is dumb and very direct. They removed the complex diplomatic/intelligence structure. Warfare is simplistic with the front system, although some of it was interesting. I quickly ran out of generals, only to find out as I 'bought' new ones, the same face pic came up for several of the generals. I had 4 with the same face. Why do you buy generals with diplomatic experience versus army/navy/air experience? I had 400 army experience, and could not use it for anything really. I didn't want to create a new template, so it just went to waste. You should be able to use it to get generals, improve technology speed, get combat bonuses, etc. No air commanders??  I did like the way they designed Air combat. I thought overall it was better than the cumbersome air system from previous versions.  Verdict. I am giving it a negative review based on my experience playing previous versions. If taken without any previous knowledge, the game would be enjoyable.

Well After finally deciding to pull this game back up after some time. and i'll safely say that I don't regret it.  As much as i don't want people to think that i'm reccommending this game just for the simple fact of... oh i don't know... FURRIES LEL... this 'graphic novel' if you want to call it that, actually redeemed itself thanks to the added chaptures. Although i am keenly frustrated with the kinda obvious ending. and i don't mean by story wise. the story is pretty unique in of itself. i'm just not saying what it is for the sake of spoilers. it's the cliff hangers.....  the game is going for a furry graphic novel with branching paths, that much is clear. but due to the fairly recent controversies this game carries on it's shoulders, and the cons this game carries, I can safely say that if the developer just went with the simple route of turning his content into a book that anyone could own, he could of easily avoided all of this nonsense that's lead up to this point.   I understand that He was mainly going for an interactive story that can be told in a multitude of ways depending on what you, the player chooses to make. It sounds good on paper, but what he failed to realize is the little subtle details that Make graphic novels good. and that's just simple things like more expressions on the characters, or small animations attatched to the characters, you don't neccessarily NEED voice acting, but it certainly can help with the quality of the product.  being able to have more ART effort into the game WOULD OF BEEN NICE. little distractions like those can go a long way. seriously, the art is just mainly reused busts of the characters mentioned within the game. EVEN THE GOD DAMNED ACHIEVEMENTS ARE REUSED. If that's not lazy design i don't know what is. and i'll mention this one more time....  If the developer just went with the simple Idea of turning this graphic novel into a book that anyone could read, with an interesting front page and all that. he MORE THEN LIKELY wouldn't have all these people trying to sue him. Or having people making an effort to personally attack him.  with that said, I hope the developer see's his mistakes on this journey of his and does his best to make the right decisions to not only make his content better, but to also protect himself from legal issues. The story is interesting enough to stay entertained, but I wont say it's a FANTASTIC story. it's a fairly unique one, something worth reading to pass the time, but everyone has their own tastes in stories. But as much as I know, every story out there has already been told time and time again, cliche's and or cheesyness will always be there. But not once in my own opinion did i notice any cliche's and or cheesyness. just a whole lot of confusion and questions that are unanswered.  It's totally up to you if you want to play this game. but note that it's mainly the story that keeps it held up. ( sense thats the point of novels ) the art is alright, but it's seriously lacking.

This review is written in Bahasa. English version is written below.  Magicka adalah game aksi dengan mekanisme sihir elemen yang cukup asyik dengan tampilan gameplay yang mirip DotA. Tugasmu dalam game ini cukup simpel, yaitu hancurkan monster-monster dengan kekuatan sihirmu, kalahkan boss, dan selesaikan setiap stage yang ada. Yang paling menarik dari game ini adalah mekanisme  magic -nya, di mana kamu dapat menggabung-gabungkan 8 elemen sihir yang ada untuk menciptakan sihir yang kuat sesuai situasi dan selera--simpel sekaligus kompleks. Ditambah mekanisme MP/mana tak terbatas, kamu bisa merasakan menjadi penyihir yang kuat.  Cerita Magicka--yang sedari awal sudah konyol, banyak dibumbui parodi ikon  pop culture  seperti Star Wars, 300, Castlevania, The Hobbit plus bahasa lucu (English+Swedish+gibberish) sehingga menjadikannya semakin konyol lagi.  Sangat menyenangkan untuk dimainkan sendiri ataupun multiplayer (biar bisa saling bunuh antar teman). Oh ya, ingat-ingatlah mantra sakti 'QFASA' dan kalau suntuk jangan lupa 'dunka dunka'. Happy gaming!   English Version   Magicka is an action game with interesting elemental magic mechanism, with gameplay which looks similar to DotA. The objective is fairly simple: defeat your enemies with your mighty magick power, defeat the boss and clear the stage. The most interesting thing about this game is its magic casting mechanism where you can combine the 8 magick elements to produce many kinds of spell best suited to the situation and your personal taste. With unlimited MP/mana, you can truly experience what it feels like to be mighty wizard in fantasy RPG setting (with comedic touch).  The story--which is already funny, is made even funnier with pop culture icon parodies such as Star Wars, 300, Castlevania, The Hobbit, etc. The Simlish-like language (or maybe English+Swedish+Gibberish) is also very funny.  It's a very fun game to play alone or with friends (so you can kill each other 'accidentally'). Also, never forget QFASA and try to do some 'dunka dunka' whenever you get stressed. Happy gaming!

WARNING: Requires a pre-requisite of an advanced understanding of English vocabulary and you WILL find yourself getting lost often, even with 'Free Roam Mode' turned on.  By definition, this is technically a game. It's an interactive, just not in the way where choices impact end results, but more in the way how a puzzle works. You activate switches outlined by  italicized text  and build up an inventory of keys, lamps, records, compass, etc.. However, it is more of a book than a game as a number of negative reviews have pointed out.  Now I will admit that within the first 7-10 areas, I didn't know what the hell was going on. Still don't. After being unable to understand what was going on, I did what most people would do when choosing not to put a game down due to achievements....click around hopelessly in hopes that something will happen. (Something's gotta give if you hit it hard enough)  The story...Well, from my understanding of the ending (which is really the only thing I could understand), it has to deal with the Great War (World War I) and....stuff happens. The 'Guide' who indirectly guides you (by letting you explore the area and seeing memories/flashbacks) and leaves you with a cliffhanger as to what happened with her (at least I assume it's a her) afterwards.  I suppose it is rather well-written, but I can't get into it. As my warning suggests, the vocabulary is far beyond my own. I would require constant use of a dictionary just to understand what's going on. (Kinda' like the d*uchebags that use a new term when talking to you, but pull a d*ck move by saying 'look it up' rather than explaining what it is. Similar to that.)  PROS: Free Achievements Short (Took me 100 minutes to click somewhat aimlessly to get to the end)  CONS: Boring Easy to get lost (Difficult to understand what's going on) Advanced English used (Be sure to have that dictionary handy) No visuals Music only presented in flashbacks  Personally, this was an eyesore on my end. I mean this dev no disrespect, but I would personally not enjoy booting up this headache again. If you want achievements or want a long story, be my guest. Otherwise, I advise you consider alternatives you would do with your time. A headache for 3 achievements? Well...at least it's another 100%.  1/10 To burn in memory? To burn in hell.

Good stuff: You can customize your horse and rider with lots of differently colored items (lots of colors, not lots of items). You can race against other people on horses. It makes a nice plinking noise when you successfully jump over an obstacle. If you are tired and bored of more interesting games, you might enjoy wasting a few minutes playing this. You can get rid of your extra money to spend a few more minutes playing. Grooming your horse is sort of interesting the first few times.  It closes really fast. It doesn't have overly intrusive ads (as in ads that interrupt you while you are riding). It is free to install, run and play, if you are willing to wait to regain energy. I haven't seen any spelling errors. I haven't experienced any crashes. It encourages the use of math by enticing one to calculate how long it takes refill the energy bar. It encourages you to go ride a real horse rather than sit and wait for an hour to ride a fake horse for five minutes. I could go on, there is just so much great stuff about this game!  Bad stuff (the main two for me): First, the energy/aciton point depletion rate.  You have an initial maximum of 100 points, which are consumed at a rate of 4 points per jump and 1 point every few seconds of running or standing within the paddock (even while paused).  These points are restored while resting (on the main menu) at a rate of 1 point every 35 seconds, which works out to 100 points in an hour.  This means you can play for about 5-10 minutes every hour. In fairness, you can upgrade certain clothing items to offset this, increasing the rate of recharge to 1 point every 17ish seconds and a max of 200 points. But the amount of currency to upgrade the items needed is ridculous.  Second, the currency rewards are pitiful.  There are two kinds of courses, jumping and agility. Either way, if you knock stuff over, you incure penalties.  Once you complete the course, you receive 100 coins, 5 diamonds and 100 xp (after gaining 9 levels, these rates have remained unchanged). These rewards are unaffected by how well you ran the course, which means you could knock everything over and still get the maximum reward. To put these numbers in perspective, an apple that restores 100 energy costs 400 diamonds. The most basic of the upgrades mentioned above costs 5000 coins and 500 diamonds. To get this much currency, you would have to run 50 courses.  In the end, to play this game, you will either be paying lots of money or spending a lot of time waiting to recharge your horse so you can grind your way through a few minutes of play.

Here is a shooter thats rewarding for the short and long term. You get to level up your perks which can become very effective once you get to high levels, while they take long to achieve, its worth it. For the short term, every game rewards skill play, headshots are very satisfying and effective; hitboxes are a good size so its not too difficult to nail but small enough to require focus and attention. You also have to mind your back at all times as enemies can creep up on you quickly. With different perks there are different tactics to employ and you learn the weapons for each perk and how to deal with all classes of enemies. It makes a game where you never feel cheated, deaths are always clear and you know you could avoid it next time with a new position or tactic. It can be a good challenge at times but even if you die, its the kind that makes you try again for good, instead of rage quitting. The coop element of the game adds to the experience a lot as well. The fact that you can share money and weapons, heal each other (with incentive to level up the medic perk), make it so people who are friendly and cooperative can enjoy this game even more. You really feel the difference when the players are working as a group helping each other versus everyone going off for themselves. Most shooters lack this coop spirit and I think its a big reason why I keep playing this game. Another is the great shooting experience.    TLDR: very satisfying shooting mechanics, hitboxes are on point, perks(classes) are fun and varied, lots of weapons, leveling takes long but rewarding, social element of game encourages teamwork and is often experienced in game where players share resources and heal each other. 90% of players are nice in my experience, which is phenomenal compared to other big name fps'. Overall I'd get it if you are a somewhat serious gamer who cares to go for headshots, because things like that matter in this game; there is a moderate depth to this game where casual gamers might find it too complicated.   extra: runs well on a macbook pro, graphics are not the best but good gameplay certainly overshadows that aspect. Hack, Slash, Loot is a top down roguelike, this game will kill you over and over again to begin with. But be paintent, the game will reward you immensley with a lot of fun and characters. The more you die, the more characters and items drops you unlock.   The classic pixel art works really well in the top down view. The game play is very simple. You play with a mouse, and that's it. Turn based movement/actions makes this perfect for playing with a trackpad on a laptop too.   There is a nice 'compare' feature here which makes picking and choosing what equipment to use easier than most games I've played previously. Plenty of different classes, I specifically enjoyed playing the Mage class, as it is super powerful towards endgame. I also found that the levels do not get progressivly harder, for instance, the first map you play is by far not the easiest, the difficulty will change on each playthrough. By completing certain other maps, you might also unlock 'artifacts' which carry over to your other playthroughs as well.  Whilst the game is very RNG, I still ploughed plenty of hours into HSL and enjoyed every minute of it. Worth the £4,99 for sure.

I can't really say I've enjoyed playing this game.  Levels you play are incredibly repetitive and chaotic. One thing is a bullet hell when you know exactly where your hit area is. Another thing is this game, which is maddeningly fast and crazy. You have to kill incredible amount of enemy ships while trying to collect bonuses each of those countless ships drop. But the ships keep coming and sometimes you're not able to get them.  These bonuses also repair your ship. By the way, everytime you get hit, you lose a weapon until it's repaired. So, once you do just one thing wrong, bam, you're not closer to die, but it also gets  easier  to die. Ok, you have infinite lives, but when you die, you stay dead for like 5 seconds and the field gets even more crowded. So it's hard to get back on your feet after you've died once.  Another major issue is the navigation menus. You're encouraged to play with your owned songs, the ones you have in your computer. So, yes, you have a long list of songs as big as the songs you have on your computer (mine has 5k), but the filtering system is terrible! Each time you complete a song, it becomes attached to an item. After you get around 10 items, you probably won't need any more. But when you want to upgrade your weapons, you have to find them in a long list of items, or in a shorter list of items you have. Thing is, I bought more items than I needed and I can't sell them so I can navigate through them better. Really frustrating! Ok, you can upgrade them from your ship editing menu. The problem is that you don't know the price of the upgrades when you access them this way! How stupid can it be?!  Also, you have two kinds of points - inspiration and kudos. They're both basically the same thing, only they're not. Just like an RPG asking you to collect gils and zennies, for examplo. Doesn't make much sense to me. Oh, by the way, whenever I complete a song, I'm told I got a lot more (like 10x or 20x) kudos points than I actually got.  The only thing that kept me playing this were my own songs. So, to sum it all up, I'd advise against you getting this game. It's a nice excuse to listen to the music you like, but what's the point of buying a game whose best feature is already installed in your computer?

Great game, in my opinion, if you manage to setup the microsoft live account. Just search within the game forum and you shouldn't have much problem with the game's functionality.  Campaign: You play as the Wolf pack, Umbrella's Delta team, special force and must seek and destroy any evidence of Umbrella's experiments following the Dr.Birkin unwillingness to cooperate. External forces are involved with the good doctor, no one must leave alive.  Secondary missions (optional add-on): You play as the Spec-Ops, sent by the goverment following the total radio silence of Raccoon City. Local info of the population status is sketchy at best, but an epidemic seems to be involved. Investigate and establish the current situation within the quarantine zone.  Single Player : Pros: The atmosphere is incredible, from the bloody streets to Umbrella's ressearch center. From the simple zombie to Birkin's mutant form, a well defined fan service. There's a wide range of close combat combos you may master and, in time, you can unlock around 30 different firearms. Every character has different specs, each playing a key part within the team's survival.  Cons: Your afliate teamates A.I. respond well, but it isn't the cadillac of programing. Their lack of tactics truly shows during the last mission when you must face an endless horde of Crimson heads while the Spec-Ops have a fortified forteress with (unidentify suspect) shooting you with a scoped rifle.  Coop: Pros: The game is made for teamwork, as espected and its communication mechanics work like a charm.  Cons: With Microsoft Live, no Lan, only online games which might cause some headaches to people who haven't a swift internet connection and wants to play with their siblings. Just a side note.  Overall : Clearly needs more support from the 3rd part company or Capcom, but it's still a nice hidden gem. I reccomend it if you work past the Microsoft Live situation (Look for the solution on the forum).  Have fun people! :)

Been playing Starbound for years now, ever since it was early alpha and while it used to be a prettier but more broken Terraria, it really has grown into something else entirely. Something vastly superior, at any rate.  The integration of the story mode greatly improved the fun I had playing. I wasn't just building things anymore; I was doing it for a specific reason. The only drawback, of course, is that once the story mode is over, you're left with another building game with no real plot. I wish I could say there's enough story to go around not to feel like this but the main plot is rather short. Still, if I were to chose only one building game for the rest of my life, Starbound would be it.  With workshop mods and differents species and a ridiculous amount of worlds to explore, there's enough replayability to last forever.

Overall, the pieces for something really big are here, it just doesn't flex all it's muscles. Most importantly, I had fun. Characters were cool, combat kept me going, choices lead me to being nice to some, a rare puzzle element suprised me, and it's worth the not-as-short-as-most-people-who-complain-about-length you put into it.

Cyberpunk has never looked this gorgeous! The art direction of this game is just stunning and probably its biggest selling point. This indie game blows many bigger productions right out of the water with its vibrant use of color, hand painted artwork and fantastic character designs. The gameplay mechanics are also quite creative, with the combat system consisting of a mix of real-time action and turn-based strategy, and the progression system allowing for a great deal of experimentation and a diverse set of playstyles. Furthermore, instead of only leveling your own character, you can also upgrade the enemy faction in exchange for more experience points. This way you can adjust the difficulty of the game in a more refined manner, as opposed to the standard easy/normal/hard settings of most games. Once you completed the story, you can even “recurse” through the game while retaining your level and powers, further increasing this game’s replay value. The game’s soundtrack is also worthy of note and is quite enjoyable even out of context.   Transistor ’s writing is probably its least memorable aspect, and while it isn’t bad by any means, I simply didn’t find myself to be heavily invested in this game’s narrative. This is probably a result of the story being delivered mostly through stream of consciousness style comments from the titular Transistor and complementary text passages that have to be found or unlocked. Cutscenes, dialogue and additional non-player characters to interact with are almost non-existent due to the game taking place in an evacuated city and featuring a main protagonist who has lost her voice. However, the overall experience is still an incredibly satisfying one and I have found myself replaying the game immediately after completing it for the first time, which is probably the highest praise I can give. Analogue was a pretty good visual novel, how does the sequel compare?   ...ehh..?   It's alright, yeah. It's just... sterile. Poorly presented overall. None of the characters really have any weight in the story, it's all reading about things that happened in the past that neither character was there to experience, so it doesn't matter much who you pick because the end result is about the same. Why couldn't we, maybe, have a story told from *Mute's perspective leading up to the event that caused Year 0? It would make it a lot more compelling than reading through a lot of logs with an AI companion making comments on stuff. Meh.  The story itself is alright, there's a lot of characters to keep track of and they all have names that are kind of hard to remember but there's a few characters than stand out as more important, so I guess it's fine. Nothing ever really gets resolved in the side-plots though. I mean there are implications given the nature of what comes next, but... ehh. The central story is pretty decent though, a lot of politics but not without a point, and the characters are pretty strong even without any direct interaction between them and the player.  The forced wait between days is also possibly for the better as I don't know if I could stomach just reading through logs for too long, I'd probably be waiting anyways. It's not a game for long sittings anyways, unlike Analogue which could easily and most likely would be completed in one sitting. But that had a variety of gameplay elements, reading over (shorter) logs, interacting with the console, interacting with the AI... that's the thing, there's not much to interact with here, compared to Analogue. I don't know why that's the case.  Overall I'm left just feeling very meh about this. It feels haphazardly thrown together because the author wanted to continue the story but didn't have any ideas for a game. I'd give it a slight recommendation if you've read Analogue already but otherwise there's no reason to get this one.

While it may SEEM like a re-skin of Assassin's Creed with a Batman: Arkham Asylum combat system, Middle-Earth: Shadow of Mordor soars above and beyond, combining a satisfying combat system with unique enemies within a beautiful world.  The main word of this review would be NEMESIS. The nemesis system in Shadow of Mordor generates unique and sometimes hilarious enemies that will remember previous encounters, gloat at you, flee from you, and sometimes generate a feeling of vengeance which leads to a thirst for revenge, making killing a previously encountered captain extremely rewarding. Each playthrough is completely unique, featuring different enemies with different strengths and weaknesses that make no encounter ever the same.  Shadow of Mordor is influenced by other games, but not defined by them. Instead it takes pieces from some of the best games of the last few years, augments them with RPG mechanics and the new Nemesis system, and integrates them all so well together that it's sometimes hard to tell where one system begins and another ends. It's a remarkable achievement.

A very good surprise ! Based on the trailer, i was expecting a basic roguelike or a 'roguelike-like' (like FTL or Binding of Isaac). So i was expecting something interesting, but not very original. But it turns out Dungeon of the Endless is a completely unique mix of roguelike, tower defense and ressource management.  The game asks you to explore a dungeon with a group of 1 to 4 heroes. But each time you open a new door, it starts a new 'turn'. At the start of each turn, each dark room may spawn monsters. You have to clear all the monsters of that wave to 'end the turn', and get automatic and full healing of your heroes. When the turn is over, you're free to build more stuff, change the inventory of your heroes, etc. The game is in a suspended test until you feel ready to open a new door.  I talked about building stuff, because that's the second part of the game, the tower defense aspect. Your heroes do not have to face enemies all by themselves, you can build offensive and defensive structures, that will hopefully stop the monster waves. You have many available buildings, so figuring out the best way to lighten the load for your heroes is of critical importance.  The last part, the resource management, comes from the fact that 3 buildings are here to produce 3 resources. Industry is what you have to pay to build. Science is what you have to pay to research new technologies. Food is what you use to heal and level up your heroes. The last ressources is gained automatically but is in very short supply: Energy is what you use to power a room. A powered room is automatically lit, and therefore can't spawn monsters, but also is required to build structures. A structure doesnt work unless it's in a powered room.  So, with a limited amount of resources, and building spaces available, you have to figure out how to maximize the resources you currently need and also prepare for the future, as the latter floors will be deadly. The goal of the game is 'simply' to reach the exit of each floor, until you get to the last floor and can finally escape the dungeon.  As i said, Dungeon of the Endless was a very good surprise, i wasn't expecting that. Once i got the hang of the system, the game became very addictive, it definitely has that 'One more turn' aspect that all turn-by-turn gamers know well enough. You always want to open one more door, until you notice it's 2 AM and you should have been to bed a long time ago. It's hard to leave the game when your in the middle of a run, and want to know what will happen to your crew. Somehow, i've logged more than 30h in my first week of play, even though it felt like half that, because i was so engaged in the game.  Dungeon of the Endless also manage to be fairly accessible. Though it doesn't explain all its mechanisms and you will have to figure some stuff out it offers two difficulty modes 'Too Easy' and 'Easy' (yes the developers are cheeky) to cater to different groups of people. Too easy difficulty should please people who aren't too familiar with roguelike mechanisms, or people who just want to enjoy the game without having too much trouble. Easy difficulty is definitely 2 steps above that, and should please people used to roguelikes, or games such as FTL.   Moreover the game has good replayability. First you get many different characters, who are all interesting to discover, unlock and play with. Moreover you will be able to unlock new 'Escape pods', which change the game rules, and therefore the playstyle you will have to use. One pod gives you stronger heroes and weaker buildings, one pod is the opposite, and others pods mix things even more. Some will even up the difficulty quite a bit for those looking for a greater challenge.  An interesting twist is you can also play the game online, with others people, which change the rules a bit and will also increase the replayability.  To sum it up, Dungeon of the Endless has a pretty artstyle, a unique and interesting gameplay, a very diverse and well made cast of characters, and has good replayability. Definitely a big thumbs up for me !

app_name: Batman™: Arkham Origins Blackgate - Deluxe Edition
review_text: It's like they took every single feature of Arkgam Origins (already the weakest game of the series) and made it even worse.  This game is a very clear demonstation of why Batman: Arkham series won't work in 2D.   First of all, the combat is very similar to the one in 3D Arkham games. But since it's in 2D, you never know what guy Batman will hit. And, of course, he always hits the one with the shoker stick or the one who can block. This makes fighting the generic enemies really frustrating.  In contrast, the bosses are too easy. I've defeated Solomon Grundy on the first try in less than a minute.  Secondly, looking for secrets and navigating levels in general is boring. Many people find the map in the game confusing, but it was pretty easy to understand for me. The real problem is the level design itself. The levels are too small. and it's like the same rooms/ventilation shafts/tunnels all over again.  Thirdly, the story is really lacking and even the Joker feels boring and dumb.  Finally, my gamepad (360 controller for windows) is switching off every 20-30 seconds when playing this game for no reason. Since it works fine in other games, I believe this has something to do with the way the game itself. And to make things even worse, the default keys when playing on mouse/keyboard are not remappable. For instance, I cannot bind 'crouch' to C because the key is bound to something else.  I really tried to love this game, but the problems with the controls were the final thing I'm giving up on this.

It Is NOT the same as Return to Shadowrun. Very basic system. Combatsystem is closer to jagged alience than RTS. So beware if You think you will continue you adventures from RTS in better graphic's. They have very little commen ground. Also importaint to know its aimed at multiplayer co-op. If you Tend to go solo you will have to struggle with a very lowbuged henchman system were you take 1-3 (i guess either playermade or random generated) henchmen with you for that mission. Theres no party and you cant manipulate the henchmen. They are equipped pre-teamup, and use to very poor equipped. You yourself have 2 itemslots, but only players can utilize that option, hence the npc-henchmen never have a granade or a medpack. Not even a pill to help them. The enemy does. Your party have to share the 2 things you choose to bring. Theres is also no revives, stabalizes or anything in that direction other that the medkit. Did I say your party have to share those 2 things. Your combat options are also very basic and constricted. You have a main and maybe a secondary, most npc's only the main which makes them very specilized to handle enemys. Some are immune to magic and hence the mage have to find other targets to engage as his or her attacks cant harm that enemy and they havent got any options to use melee or alternative weapon. So very arcade'ish. Hopes for the future depth of the combat system complexity isnt high.   If you want online player co-op turn base and dont mind the low complexity this might be fun. If you have standards regarding that you will be happier with the top-down RTS:dragon fall or other. Ufo-series (not x-com). X-com enemy unknows is good though!

By god this game. In comparison, Resident Evil 4 is a b!tch and this is the master.  Sure, this game takes a lot from Resident Evil 4, but it does those things, SOOOO Much better.   Compared to RE4: You don't have to shoot an enemy 5 times in the head to kill them anymore. The story is absolutely incredible. Controls are smooth. (Well compared to RE4, any modern day game controls smooth) Just, this game imo is so much better than RE4.  Pros: You shoot people. Storyline is absolutely amazing. Flexible weapons for different kinds of players. Stealth is good, could use a bit of tweaking. It's made by Bethesda. Bethesda gave out a bunch of console commands and info on how to turn on console commands if you just wanna have fun and ♥♥♥♥ around. Enemies heads explode with headshots. The game is very fun. High replayability. There are some scares in there.  Cons: Melee is useless. It takes 8+ hits to kill someone. (NEVER INVEST IN MELEE, Enemies scale with you so basically even if you have 5 bars of melee, it will still take like 8 hits.) It's not really scary. (For me personally) (Hell I didn't find Alien Isolation, The Excorcist, or even FEAR scary, so maybe I'm just immune) The tips in the loading screens are very useful, It would be nice if they told you those tips while you are playing the game... The ending left something to be desired, like a lot of unfinished business, but if there is a sequel to this game, then everything should be all right. The 2 Dlc's you play as Kidman, personally I hate them. I just hate playing as side characters who are basically the bad guy being manipulated because they are too ♥♥♥♥ing stupid to think for themselves. But hey, maybe that is just me. Playing as Sebastian in the main game was like eating your favortite food, you took your time to savor every part of it, and to remember the experience. However, playing as Kidman was like 'Okay let's just get this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t over with so I can learn more about the story.' It was the equivalent of ripping of a bandaid fast to get it over with. I'll tell you if I ever finish the DLC's. They are terrible.

Do you remember playing dodgeball in school? Do you remember that one nerdy kid sitting in the corner trying to avoid being pelted by balls? Well Roller-Derby Disco Dodgeball is for you , its a fast paced arena shooter that can run on a lot of PC's.   Besides its only 10.99 and that is a steal for a laugh and a good time. You can also buy a four pack so that you and your friends can play the game together. Be warned the game is region locked so your friend will have to purchase a copy from their region. That isn't the dev's fault that is just steam being steam on a regular basis.   The graphics aren't the flashiest thing ever , but its not supposed to be a AAA FPS With a complex storyline entailing the journey of Dodge McBall a rouge agent or some wierd stuff like that. But its a game about playing dodgeball and the lower graphical settings mean more flashing lights and less time trying to spot an enemy from the background of the arena.   The sound is awesome having a pumping soundtrack really can get you in the mood and rythym of picking up the balls and knocking your opponents out. Did I mention a KO entails a little particle explosion.   Seriously this game is insanely fun and definitely is worth the pick up and hey sometimes it's on sale for an even lower price. So don't miss out on some cheap chaotic fun and pick it up today.

If you're like me, Gamespot and Metacritic are my resources to get game reviews. I rarely buy games that get below a 70% rating with either of these reviewers. That being said, I bought this game despite the 64/100 on metacritic, I played it not knowing what to expect, and thankfully, I really enjoyed it.  As many of the other reviewers will tell you, this game is not for everyone. I purchased it because I loved Hotline Miami and seeing the previews for this game I was drawn to the brutal violence and artistic style. It's a very challanging game, and it forces you to pick up on little trends and nuances during the gameplay if you want to win.  To explain further, this game requires patience. Just as you start getting really mad because you're getting your tail handed to you, you realize that something you're failing in isn't just RNG, it's something you just haven't been paying attention to. Sure there are parts that are randomized (which you can disable if you want), but the changing factors with each restart keep you on your toes.  The story is interesting enough to follow and it actually kind of makes you look at the way you play ruthless games such as this one that force you to die or fail over and over again in order to discover a solution. Things you do will make you feel bad, they'll make you feel like you could have done more, and they certainly make you feel helpless.  Bottom line, you feel like you're in a Cormac McCarthy novel. Despiration, violence, and death on death on death. Love it.  Play it for yourself, but with one stipulation: DO NOT LOOK UP WALK-THROUGHS. FIGURE IT OUT BY DOING!

SHORT REVIEW: Submerge your head in your local zoo's fish tank and get a similar experience with less base-building frustration. LONG REVIEW: I bought this game on release day, thinking 'Underwater Minecraft' or 'Undewater Planet Explorers' or 'Underwater Starforge' but no. First, there is no deformable terrain. Mining involves pointing at a hill with a bunch of flecks of metal in it, and holding down the mining button forever until you've got all the materials you could want. Next, base expanding is a huge pain in the ♥♥♥ and I ended up locking myself out of my base and just making it worse with every attempt to fix it. I spent over a half hour googling and trying to figure out how to build my base the right way but it wasn't worth it. Finally, the lack of crafting options makes the game feel very shallow -- you can build maybe a dozen things, and in the case of scuba-suit upgrades you can skip many of the tiers of gear if you point your drill at the right metal-flaked mound for a few minutes. This game is basically a first person aquarium simulator with a couple extra toys to give you the illusion of depth. I'm also under the impression devs will no longer be updating this game, due to some forum conversations. It's not the worst game, but it feels unfinished and kludgy. There's a serious lack of fun, and I cannot recommend spending more than $5.

I have been a fan of 4X games for a while and coming from Civilization, I was interested in this game due to the fantasy setting. I was completely blown away. I have left 4X games for a few months at a time and keep coming back to this game before Civ. If you're a fan of 4X games, get this one. Be prepared for a steep learning curve.  Pros: - Each faction has very unique play, not just a few unique units. ****  coming from Civ where every Civilization I play pretty much the same, this was huge for me.  - Can customize your units and heroes to have a more powerful army or faster scouts. - Can micromanage combat to your advantage, or play auto and watch the battle unfold. - Can really customize the playing field to make interesting games. - Great DLC.  Cons: - Can get slow at times (as do most 4X games) - Steep learning curve (if that's a con?) - You will find that important things like chores, work, school, whatever seem less important and get put off for hours as you keep saying 'just one more turn...'

If the genre 'Arcade Management' makes any sense, Kingdom belongs firmly to it. It's a fairly simple game, that derives most of its challenge from the fact that the player knows very little about the possibilities of the game. You are explained the basic mechanics, only to be left to explore the possibilities of the game. It gave me a sense of mystery and exploration, as i forced the wild forest back to make place for civilization and agriculture. It also meant that each game i made it just a little bit longer, and learned a bit more about how to play the game most efficiently. The atmosphere is a cross between the grim everything-is-about-to-topple-angst from Dwarf Fortress, and the cosiness of a game like Reus. The pixel art, music and sound effects are perfect. In my opinion (as I see there is a bit of controverse about it) the mechanics are solid. The game certainly won't hold your hand, but in the couple of hours and about 7-8 attempts I've made at this point, I have at no point been left with the feeling of being cheated by the game. Whenever something went wrong, I could see why and trace it back to me making a mistake somewhere. Being a bit of a simbuilder myself, I would have liked to see more options in the game to allow for more strategic variance. Given the limited number of units, tools and structures, your approach to the game is pretty much the same in every game. This keeps the game simple, and forces you to consider what to do with the few possibilities you have, but at the same time it means that one game won't stand out from another, and I can see this making it bland after a while.  All in all I think the game is solid, and found it highly enjoyable - especially during the first few playthroughs when you had no idea as to what to expect. I'd recommend this to friends and others.

I have been playing video games for almost the full time I have been alive, owned more consoles than years I have been alive, and invested more time into gaming in general than any other activity in my life.  With all this noted, I can safely say that Teddy Floopy Ear - The Race is the best game I have ever played.  The character development in this game is truly outstanding, and the story is both inspiring and breathtaking at times, you will feel every emotion you have even known plus more; joy, sadness, anger, regret, fear, shock, surprise, disgust and true love. Decisions have consequences and you need to take your time as well as make quick decisions. After a some time you start to feel for your characters and develop various bonds with them; you rely on them for various tasks and you will start to worry taking them into more dangerous areas. Personally, I have never felt such a true bond with any person, living or dead, as I have with the characters in Teddy Floppy Ear - The Race. When you lose a race, you will feel you have personally failed your friends; I have broken down right in front of my PC due to this more times than I can remember.  The roleplaying in this game is truly intense, and the replayibility is on another level. Games like the Witcher 3, Skyrim or any other mid-tier game have nothing on the time you will spend in this. As a great man once said, 'Easy to pick up, hard to master'. That man was Teddy Floppy Ear.  The over-the-top graphical power of this game may be hard for some players to handle, as the photo realism of the vast green plains, glowing sunsets and life-like characters may scare you with its beauty. Yet, the optimisation is years ahead of its time, and the game will run beautifully on almost any PC. The developers of this game are true geniuses.  This is by far the greatest soundtrack in any game ever, the compisitions left me in true awe and the only things even comparable to the sounds that will fill your ears are The Beatles' Sgt. Peppers Lonley Hearts Club Band; Mozart's Symphony No 41, ‘Jupiter’; or the sound of a child being brought into this cruel, cold and unforgiving world. Note that these works sound like fingernails on a blackboard compared to what you will experience in Teddy Floppy Ear - The Race.  This is by far the best money I have ever spent. I urge you with all my heart and all my soul to play this masterpiece. 11/10. Game of the millenium.  (Note I have over 6,000 hours in this game in offline mode, as I would frequently have to shut myself out from notifications due to the emotional trauma and/or euphoria I experienced so frequently in Teddy Floopy Ear - The Race.)  Thank you for reading.

Monument is a old-school fps in the vein of Quake that contains quite a lot of verticality - there even is a kind of double-jump/climb mechanic in there to further facilitate this. Navigation around the sprawiling, mazelike levels is it's very own skill mechanic, and it's yellow-brownish temple ruins contain a lot of secret areas, reachable by the more complicated movement mechanics.  If you like simple, oldschooled FPS with quite some platforming in it, Monument is a real treat.  Of course it's weapons' kinaesthetic leave a lot to be desired, and you shouldn't expect gunplay that's anywhere near Doom or Quake. At least they sound decent.  All in all - for a good 90s flashback it's most definitively worth it's asking price.
