TL;DR - If you're up for a new take on the JRPG experience, give this a try.  Disclaimer: I was given a Steam key for this game so that I could try it ahead of time and write a review on the release date, but I did so because I support this developer, not for the promise of future keys.  This is a pretty good game, but it has its flaws. Before getting into the gameplay, it took a little while just to get comfortable with the controls. They're not complicated, and there was a (very) brief explanation at the beginning, but I was expecting to just start playing this like other JRPGs I've played, and that didn't work. There are other things that confused me at the beginning as well, like how the coins that I was collecting weren't accumulating in my gold pouch (they turned out to be 'points', not 'gold'), and how I couldn't walk through shady parts of the grass (though I think this is fixed now). This game is interesting in that it is both very similar to other JRPGs yet at the same time very different.  The combat includes features present in many other games, but has one defining feature that makes it play in a completely new way. Each battle will award one to three stars for winning. This is a game without random encounters, just enemies that you can see and choose to fight, so that means that there are a specific number of stars obtainable in the game. Monsters can be refought for XP and gold, but stars are only awarded during the first battle. One star is awarded just for winning the battle, one is for winning without taking too much damage, and one is for winning in a set number of turns. These limits are set per battle, so boss battles are more gracious with the turns and lost health allowed. With these restrictions in place, you are no longer just fighting a battle to win, but you are fighting a battle to win it quickly while taking few hits. At first, this might sound like it would be really annoying, but luckily this feature comes with a second: you may restart a battle at any time without consequence, even a boss battle. So if you're fighting and you miss and have to take an extra turn, or the enemy crits and does too much damage, you're not automatically out a star, you just have to restart the battle. Restarting also helps for being able to try out new skills and test out new enemies/bosses. This game changes combat from grinding to efficiency optimization.  As I said earlier, the controls take some getting used to. I should point out that I've only tried using a keyboard (and a mouse a little bit) so far. Everything works well enough, but it's not super intuitive. For example, to equip weapons or accessories, you have to go to the Miscellaneous submenu. To cancel or go back, you have to either press Escape, which isn't reachable without moving your hand, or right-click. There is no diagonal movement, and if multiple directions are pressed at once, the vertical direction wins. (This may be a problem with the engine itself.) There are actually two main menus that are opened by two separate keys. It all works, it's just not normal.  That's the thing about this game: it's not normal. If you want a normal JRPG, look elsewhere. This game has all of the features of a JRPG, but it's very light. There are weapons, but you'll rarely actually have to make a choice as to which one to use. There are optional sidequests; well, one or two, at least. (I've only played through the first two stages so far.) There is hidden treasure to find, though all treasure  chests  are marked out on your minimap. There is combat, but it doesn't feel very complex. As I explained above, the complexity to the battles is a meta-complexity rather than mentally calculating numbers or massive item use. This is a fun game, but you have to be willing to explore something new.  I've mostly talked about what's awkward so far, but now let's cover the simple stuff. I like the art style, personally. You can see for yourself from the screenshots what it's like. This is a very light-hearted game, and the art styled matches that feeling well. The music is pleasant, but nothing spectacular. The broad storyline seems pretty commonplace, from the little I've seen, but the details to the storyline keep it interesting. The humour is very enjoyable, though it probably wouldn't be for everyone. There is an option in the settings to eliminate suggestive humour, but that seems to be about all there is. ;) The main character is pretty mundane, as most main characters tend to be, but the other party characters are all pretty creative and interesting.  I'm glad I started playing this and will continue to the end. In an age where so many video games are made to fit a mold, it is refreshing to watch this developer try out new things - some that work in really cool ways, and some that should probably be scrapped. What I love about how this developer implements ideas is that he always keeps things easy for the user. If you want to explore a new take on JRPGs, give this game a try.

I felt like writing a lot of reviews this day, and since logging in over 300 hours, mainly Napoleonic Wars (sorry reg if you're reading this, internet has forced me to postpone online gaming)  Anyway Mount and Blade where to start? Being a 6 year old game, as hype for the ever growing Mount and Blade Bannerlord comes, this game creates one of the most ultimate sandbox feels if you play singleplayer. Although it may seem long and repeatable, seeing your army go down in one battle as you go down in one blaze of glory taking on 30+ AI bots brings depth and strange euphoria as you get captured. This game is able to create your own story, be the ultimate protaginist, or the ultimate 'Black Knight' so to speak. The graphics may be outdated, but the battles commanding your forces and fighting along side your troops is a gamestyle that has not been hit on in recent gaming history.  In terms of replayability there is countless ways to become Ruler of all Caldaria and this game also has many memorable moments. Including mods ranging from Napoleonic Wars to A Lord of the Rings mod, you can have countless hours in this game.  Just believe me when I say if you're looking for a game that still has a large community, look no further because mount and blade is here. It hits in all the right areas, singleplayer, multiplayer, sandbox, and moddding, even if you don't want the mods you can easily put 1000 hours into the game.  10/10 Would marry daughter of Lord who would later betray his king, to take his Town and Fiefs.

Reviewing a 15 year old game is really challenging, considering the fact that everything about gaming has changed ever since, BUT if I say that this 15 year old game is what single handedly paved the way for what shooters are today its bold statement. HALF-LIFE needs no introduction, out in 1998 it had a very powerful impact in the industry, back in a time when FPS was all about running and gunning through narrow corridors HALF-LIFE single handedly changed that trend, it was a revolution of its time and was a direction for game developers.  The story is very simple, you are a scientist who works for a top secret research facility, one day an experiment goes horribly wrong and it rips open a portal to another dimension, the base is riddled with alien life forms and in order to confront the situation the govt. dispatches soldiers, their mission is not only to wipe out the threat but also the evidence which includes the entire Black Mesa science team including you, it’s all about survival. Simple story but the game has a very powerful sense of narration, story-telling and presentation.   Its crafted so well that it stands the test of time even today, the ones who played this game when it was released know why HALF-LIFE is praised so much, the ones who play it today find it stale, I don’t blame them but fact is that this was the first game to implement narration and ingame story telling without the player leaving control, no cut scenes or anything that takes you away from the player, even levels have a cohesive transition, they are merged by loading screens so the game feels like a smooth flow. Games today lack that kind of flow. The premise of the game is so well designed it feels like a living breathing world with an ecosystem.   It’s the game that merges old school shooters with new or lets just say it brings shooters to a whole new direction. there is a wide variety of weapons at your disposal, each one of them behaves so perfect that shooting is very satisfactory, also there is a wide variety of foes who have different behavior, today these things are very common but imagine all of these things in 1998, it bagged more than 60 GOTY awards.   The level design, the sound design, the atmosphere, the feel of claustrophobe and isolation, the desperation of survival are some of the attributes that make HALF-LIFE.  If there was no HALF-LIFE, there wouldn’t have been VALVE, this is the game that made them, gave them this legendary stature.   For the love of gaming play this. You owe it to yourself.

I personally like FC3 more than 2, and I haven't tried 4 yet. Most folks will say that FC2 is the best. It is a great game. It has a huge open world and a lot of stuff to do. The guard posts along your routes respawn very quickly, which can be a pain when you're trying to do one of the main missions. I'm currently 54% done with the main quest and I have over 30 hours in.  FC2 is set in a war-torn unnamed African country and it is up to your choices to see who comes out on top. You can recruit 'companions' that will assist you in the main missions (they don't follow you around, but will meet you at mission locations and provide you with alternate tasks in quests). There are a handful of ground vehicles that you can use to get around the country as well as a few hang gliders and boats. Gun stores throughout the country will allow the player to upgrade weapons, vehicles, and the player. There are also a bunch of campsites all over where you can rest and save the game safely.  FC3 has more of a story and FC2 can be repetitive, but it is a great game and very much recommended (especially when it’s on sale for $2.50!!)

Pros: Good action, excellent enemy AI, nice graphics, challenging puzzles, something always happening, good voiceover work, Multiplayer was pretty fun  The action only lets up for you to find treasure or scavange for weapon parts and puzzle sequences, other than that you are fighting a group of enemies or escaping a collapsing building or narrowly escaping death via action sequences.   The enemy AI is challenging and engaging. They will use their numbers to their advantaged by sneaking around you with an enemy in the distance trying to pick you off with a rifle or an enemy with a pickaxe will charge you while others fire bows etc. Every so often, if you have the higher ground, they will retreat, luring you into an ambush. I thought they ran away, so I walked around cautiously to see where they went and was gunned down from all angles. That was a surprise the first time it happened (about 5 hours in)  The graphics are nice, nothing groundbreaking but it is clear Eidios/SquareEnix/Feral spent the most effort on graphics. The voiceover actors are believable, I like that there is swearing (a believable amount of swearing) it makes the characters reactions seem real as anybody caught in these situations would be cursing.   Multiplayer. It was fun. Nothing genre (re)defining or game changing. Basically, you vs bad guys in deathmatch or others (I only played 4 or 5 rounds, who's got time for that with CS:GO/S or BF4?)  Cons: Lara's hair, horrid cutscenes, Lara's choppy movement,   Lara's hair will randomly gust in the wind in a goof manner, nothing too extreme but it is bothersome.  Cutscenes: Let's take a director from B-Movie or C-Movie slasher flicks and put him/her in charge of cutscenes. Honestly it felt as though that was the case. Everything moved so quickly as though the cutscenes where after thoughts or just bumpers between action/exploration sequences. Nothing is fleshed out. One particular scene stands out, Lara gets caught by the enemy along with some others, she sees a guy get struck down to the ground and instantly stabbed, like a split second after he hits the ground, and then it jumps to the next cutscene. Everything is so fast paced it is hard to care or keep track of who is who in the story. You'll meet a character and after a few minutes of gameplay he is killed by animals or dies in a building collapse. The cutscenes give you no reason to care about any character. As of this review I've played for 7+ hours, and I couldn't tell you who the enemy are or any of Lara's friends names, all I know is you are Lara and bad guys are after you while you tomb raid and try to escape the island with your friends. And that's not for trying to pay attention to the story, but the story seems like an afterthought just to make the progression of the game, from one action sequence to the next, make some sense. Like 'oh yeah this is the main bad dude and he wants to kill you because he is bad' and thats about it. No exploring of motives or backstory of any kind for any character; even Lara.  Lara's movement is kinda fluid, it is hardly fleshed out. You be running and snap to jumping in any direction or roll in any direction at a moments notice. Other than that, Lara seems like a typical 3rd person action character.  Conclusion: I stayed away from comparing this to the Uncharted series who are the de facto leader of 3rd person explorer/action/platformer/indiana jones-esque gaming, but the comparasion are glaring if you have played any Uncharted game. Tomb Raider is nothing like and almost exactly like Uncharted with a female lead. Which I guess is kinda unfair because without Tomb Raider, Uncharted wouldn't exsist. The jumping, rolling, ledge clamouring, shoot-em up, escape disaster, puzzle solving are all parallel to the Uncharted series, but not nearly as polished or thought out or fun really. BUT if you've never played Uncharted, this game is quite a bit of fun. Hell, I've played all 3 Uncharted extensively and I found this fun.  If I ever played a demo I would have never paid the $60 full price sticker (Survival Edition). This is definitely worth a bargin bin find and it is on sale via steam for $29.99 GOTY Edition (which came out a week and a half after I bought it, so that was fun) and I'd definitley recommend it.

I died having sex before I had a child, ending my dynasty (i had executed my sister). I had a 90% chance of living.  10%/10% would heart attack again

Addendum: As of Nov 2016, I nominated Avernum for the “I Thought This Game Was Cool Before It Won An Award” Award.  Second remake of the classic Exile series' first part, Avernum: Escape From the Pit is classic roleplaying at its best! Basically, you play as a party of adventurers, who were exiled into a huge cavern, Avernum, far below the surface of the world. You have to find a new purpose here, which basically means doing all kinds of odd-jobs and quests for mayors, wizards and kings of the underworld and, while you're at it, trying to escape your dark prison.  The controls have been streamlined for the 2010s. Like in Avernum, the first remake, the game is still isometric and tile-based. However, you can finally control the characters efficiently with a mouse. This works splendidly, though the controls have frankly lost some of the function of the earlier games: e.g. you can no longer close doors (unless I've yet to discover how). Also, the pathfinding algorithm does some weird stuff sometimes when your preferred path is blocked, making characters run into wrong direction (and at worst, their DOOOOM). There's still no music (except in the title screen and the few cut scenes): use your favorite epic adventure music instead.  Skill system has basically been remade, though the comedic art of the skill descriptions has been appreciatively retained. Skills are now learned in trees, with a basic skill in the bottom and every subsequent skill requiring the below skills before they can be developed. Also, characters gain bonus traits every few levels gaining small bonuses to different skills and stats. It's a matter of taste if these are good or bad changes, but the whole works nevertheless quite fine.  Combat has always been a rather important part of Avernum series. Turn-based, the basics are still there. However, some additions and changes have been made. Warriors have gained special skills called battle disciplines to supplement their fighting ability, making them a bit more interesting and tactical than before. Spells have been remade as well since original Avernum. Particularly, all the blessings and curses have become area effects, and multi-hit spells are now cone or area attacks instead of simply hitting a number of targets.  Frankly, the hard-coreness of the series has dulled quite a bit with this latest installment. No longer are the dark dungeons truly dark, as you don't benefit from bringing a torch or casting light spells (which were actually removed altogether). There is no need to eat to satisfy hunger nor deal with dangerous diseases, and death is no longer so much a set-back as party is automatically resurrected and healed at a friendly town, removing the need for inns and renting rooms. You may no longer jump down ledges (which was never that healthy anyway). Identification of magical items has also been removed (albeit hilariously explained by 'adventurers having better training nowadays'), and potion making has become a trade secret of alchemists, so your party can't learn it itself any longer. Spiders no longer say 'hi!' in a high-pitched voice audibly. Along with these changes Avernum has lost part of its legacy and spark.  What is left, however, is an excellent RPG adventure in a unique fantasy world. The streamlining makes the game more accessible and lets the player concentrate on what's really important: dialogue, exploration and questing. And their implementation is simply brilliant. There are tens, if not hundreds, of characters in the game who all have their own dialogue trees, and several tens of dungeons and towns to explore, everyone of them extremely detailed. Enemies are still dangerous, and mini-bosses and bosses are refreshingly difficult to beat.  Game is not only high-quality, for there's also quite a lot of it. My first play-through, on Hard difficulty level, lasted about 80 hours. Want to just experience the story? Play on Normal or even Casual difficulty, and you may even change it mid-game. However, the highest difficulty level, Torment, provides an amply named challenge for an experienced player, requiring advance planning and lots of re-exploring in areas that were previously too dangerous. Seriously though, I recommend playing your first game on Hard difficulty at max, as Torment can get tedious if you don't know where to go for the next admissible challenge.  TL:DR?  Excellent dialogues. Robust tactical combat. Addicting exploration. Epic questing to become true heroes.  Not convinced? You may as well give it a try. The demo is available at  http://www.avernum.com/avernum/index.html , letting you play through, I believe, about one third of the full game.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Knights of the Old Republic™ II: The Sith Lords™
review_text: The short version: 7.5/10. A worthy successor to the first KotOR, but could be better. Cons first: Influence system is a tug-of-war; lightsabers aren't future-proof, necessitates constant upgrades; and clarity of objective is murky at points. Pros: Lightsabers more modular, game responds even more deeply to how you play, and how you played KotOR 1. Long version: I'm not done with this game yet. Not by a long shot. I play offline, which doesn't allow Steam to really count the actual amount of time I've spent on it, so I'll go ahead and say I've spent at least 24-36 hours on one playthrough so far, and that was mostly getting off the first couple of scenarios, which you're basically railroaded into/through. Sometimes your objectives and how to achieve them is a little opaque, so it took me a while to even get through the first act. Plus, now that I'm given a choice, I'm not really doing things in the optimal order. Took a long time to get my lightsaber. Once I did, though, hoooo boy, I was stoked -- oh, I need what skills to build something to actually make my 'saber effective? Aw, phooey. Good thing I'm speccing Sentinel. I have ALL THE SKILLS! Bring it -- wait, huh, you can't future-proof your 'saber, either? Geeeeez, this deal's getting worse all the time! At least you're close-to-literally drowning in the parts to make the upgrades. I do like the added modular elements to the lightsaber customisation, though. Since inventory space is basically unlimited, you can hoard ALL THE LIGHTSABER PARTS. The influence system makes me want to drink until I can't feel my mouse. You will not be best pals with everyone in any one playthrough like you could in the last game, unless you cheat. That's just how it is. Wanna play light-side? Oh look, this guy likes it, but your other buddy ain't happy with you. Stand with a rebellion? Now Thing 1 hates your face and you've gained brownie points with Thing 2. Like KotOR 1, there's an optimal character build for this game, but I'm playing (nominally, anyhow) for fun. This will make my inevitable fight with the final boss probably very difficult to impossible. Still, though, I'm a Star Wars fan to the bone, and I do enjoy the chance to paddle about again in the Old Republic setting (it's one of my faves, dating back to the Tales of the Jedi comics half my lifetime ago).

app_name: The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings Enhanced Edition
review_text: The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings. Developed by CDPROJECKT RED and being a sequel to the first Witcher game, which was based off the writings of Andrjez Sapowski 'Wiedźmin' series, The Witcher 2 is the more ambitious game from #1, which has the problem of having aged terribly. The game was ambitious and got a bunch of acclaim once it came out, though sadly I wasn't interested in video games at the time and when I was my PC was ♥♥♥♥ and I already packed away my 360, so I had to wait until my new PC. I finally installed it and booted it up, then started playing. Well what did I think? Well, I guess I'm going to tell you.  STORY: Your name is Geralt of Rivia. After King Foltest is killed on your watch after the siege of a castle, you are accused of murdering him. You didn't, and you convince your allies enough to get them to help you escape the dungeon to clear your name and kill the man who did so. Along the way, you are forced to interfere with the politics of the Northern Realms, initially with a minor trading settlement but eventually with a whole kingdom and all of the realms. Someone once called the Witcher series as the Game of Thrones of video games, and that's a pretty good comparison. The political talks are deep near the end of the game, there's a lot of killing, there are boobs and sex, I'm surprised George R.R. Martin doesn't have a writer's credit on this. Like Game of Thrones, The Witcher 2 handles these pretty well. The story is interested, it keeps you going and you want to conclude the story. Very well put together.  GAMEPLAY: 3rd person perspective, use swords as weapons. You have two swords, one silver and one steel. The steel one is good for killing humans, elves, and dwarfs, while silver is better for monsters and wraiths. You have a light and heavy attack for either, with the usual differences. You can parry attacks or just move out of the way with a spin dash (for lack of a better term to describe it coming to mind). You also have 5 magic abilities: Igni, which lights your enemies on fire, Yrden, which traps enemies down, Axii, which has enemies fight for you for a small period of time, Aard, which pushes your enemies back and sometimes gives you an opportunity to instakill them, and Quen, which surrounds you with a protective barrier. All of em are useful in their own way, though Axii isn't very effective half the time and I only used Yrden for boss battles that required me to. There are also some bombs, daggers, and traps in the mix. You can brew potions or apply oils to your sword to increase damage or give you extra benefits. And there's a skill tree to level up yourself up and give you extra stuff. Deep enough, kind of hard if you have no idea what you're doing or just try to button mash, but if you take a few steps back and breath, you should got it.  GRAPHICS, WORLD DESIGN, AND PERFORMANCE: Ran it on Ultra, and ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ it looked beautiful. Ran pretty well, 60FPS, some drops but think it might be Windows 10. There was an annoying thing that whenever I died later in the game, it crashed, though that turned out to be an issue with having too many saves since I'm a bit of a frantic saver. Had to turn steam cloud off to get it to work normally but it worked just peachily once I turned that off and deleted some older saves. If you have an issue like that, it's probably because of that. World design is varied, you go to 4 different places through the main story and they're varied enough, look nice.  CHARACTERS, VOICE ACTING, AND AI: AI is a bit derpy sometimes, sometimes when an enemy charges it just stays there and let me get backstabs, though that only happened once or twice so I guess I can excuse it. Voice acting is pretty good, Geralt is well done, one of your possible allies Vernon is done well, your love interest for this game Triss is good too, some of the NPC VA's are kind of lame but overall it's top notch. Character design for NPC's is kind of redone, with different colorations varying a few NPC's if there is one, but for the main guys it's good and varied enough.  SIDE ACTIVITIES: You can arm wrestle, fight, or play dice poker wherever you go. This is fun enough, and you get money that you can use to buy weapons, armor, potion materials, sex, and the usual stuff. There are a lot of side quests, some just being monster contracts while others have some more depth into them. I'd recommend you do them to get some more money and XP to level up, since it makes everything easier afterwards, but it's up to you, is it not? :P  FINAL THOUGHTS: Phenominal game. Try it if you haven't. 9.5/10, worth a look at without a doubt. At this point it goes on sale for 2 or 3 bucks, and that is an absolute steal. You run out of excuses every day to not play this game, people. Just get it over with :P     Drinking alone is as bad as ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in company

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: A quick edit to my review, as part of the Steam Game Awards, after nominating this game a time or two.   Where can I begin with this game? It's one of the few that don't come around often, and leaves you wanting more. Sure the combat is difficult and is going to kill you, but it's far from impossible. Once you've gained a feel for what works best, stick with it and don't change it. Eventually you will succeed. This game places a task in front of you that is seemingly impossible and gives you hardly any direction of where to proceed. A strong scence of risk and reward is put into play that truely tests your abilities and gives a sinscere feeling of accomplishment.   The story and lore are as vague as possible. Any information you gather is mostly derived from item descriptions and a little character dialogue as you progress. The different characters met through the journey each have their own tale of how they ended up where they are. The true story as to what happens is ultimatly left up to the player. It is easy to miss large bits and pieces of whats happening. Replaying the game a second time, whether it be on a new character, or an old one that continued on, allows you to pick up on what you had missed, as well as allow for multiple different playstles.   Experimentation with how you play is encouraged. Some may prefer to take a lot of hits, while others prefer to be dealing them, or even staying back and shooting arrows or casting magic. This game gives the player many choices as to what kind of character they want. A warrior, rogue, or mage? Even those categories arent enough. The stats and leveling system grants a great deal of variety.  The versatility of playstyles is only matched by the wide variety of weapons. Almost any weapon that could be imaged can be found somewhere in some form. Weapon upgrading is also very helpful. Upgrades dont change the appearance of the weapon in most cases, but does allow alternatives forms of damage, and different uses for different situations. Almost all weapon choice is situational and made by the player in regards to what is best.   Armor, like weapons, is also based on player choice. Different types of armor are better in different areas depending upon their defensiveness, resistances, and their weight. A large variety of armor can also be found.  Multiplayer is available, but should be looked at by new players with caution. Both cooperative and player versus player is allowed. The many covenants that the player may join influence the online interactions someone has with the others who are playing the game.   Overall this game is nothing short of challenging. It's difficult, and at times bleak. It may scare some, and amaze others. It allows for ultimate player choice. It will keep you engrossed in its environment for hours on end. This game will simply leave you wanting more.  For those of you who read this and feel that this game is worth a shot; I have one thing to say:  Wecome to Dark Souls.

The best TES title to date if you can withstand the combat and animations. The game world is rich with a ton of things to do and has a intresting main storyline, in where exploration is key with a capital K. The factions in this game feel like beliveable organizations with their own agendas seperated from the main story. Also features a rank advancement system that to advance up in rank in a faction certain skills and attributes need to be at a certain level. So no more pure fightrers being the leader of the mages guild without using or knowing magic like in Oblivion and Skyrim. The only letdowns besides the combat and animations are the game is known to crash often so download some mods that fix this and SAVE OFTEN. Also the the questing system can be convoluted if you put a good number of hours in the game, you would take some time cycling through your quest journal to find a quest to do if you've forgetten about it or want to know how to reach a place for a quest. Overall a excellent RPG and TES game that is still being played and modded to this day.  Pros: + Rich and beliveable world + Intresting main quest + Belivable faction system + Great loot + Unleveld enemies and loot + Tribunal expansion + Bloodmoon expansion + FREE DLC on the Elder Scrolls website to download + Music + Art + Environmental sounds + Water textures + Travel systems  Neutral: / OP magic system / No quest markers / No essential npcs  Cons: - Combat - Stealth mechanics - Animations - Quest system - Frequent crashes - Save system  8.8/10

A beautiful 2D puzzel game. The artstyle might be a bit discouraging at first but you get to appreciate it after getting used to the scenery. The point of the game is to make your way to the end point while also collecting the collectibles in the level. This can be quite challenging, since the movement of your charachter, Quozzle is not intuitive and it takes some time to get used to. In a weird way, this game made me empathize to some extent with the people with physical disablities, who have to make a lot of efforts for doing even the simplest tasks. I don't know if this has been intentional or not, but in any case, it is an interesting experience.  What's interesting about the puzzles, is that unlike many puzzle/platformer games, when you can't get something right, smashing the keyboard and doing that part over and over, usually doesn't get you anywhere. You will have to be patient, and know what you are doing. In that sense, the game has a meditative aspect which is quite refreshing.   All in all, it is a unique game which I would strongly recommend for anyone interested in puzzle games.

*As a note before you read this, there may be some spoilers to the first game. If you are reading this with no prior knowledge to the game, please read a review for the first game. you have been warned  Ah, The Walking Dead. A game which, although I haven't played in a while, I can still remember every detail of.  The Walking Dead: Season 2 is a sequel from the game The Walking Dead (quite self explanatory really). You asume the role of Clementine, a young teenager (aging up from the previous game) who was left with a babysitter while her parents went to Savannah for a vacation. Little did they know that there vacation was to be cut short. As the name suggests, the dead become the undead and begin walking around and grunting in the usual zombie way, leaving Clementine in a world of death and torment. She leaves the safety of her home to travel with a man called Lee who does everything in his power to protect her. You are faced with many different characters and personalities, much like real life and are forced to make decisions based on the situation. The best part about it, you're in control of those choices.  As I've given the story, I may as well review that element first.  The story is the main element of this game and makes it into what it is. The choices that you make affect the story heavily and drive to connect with the characters that you have been presented with, be it that you feel like being a complete badass throughout the whole of the game, threatening to kill someone for trying to tickle you or being the friendly and conserved type. Lets be honest though. who plays nice in these games?  The emotional connection that you get with some of the characters is absolutely phenomenal. When you start playing this, chances are you will probably end up caring for some people more than you care about some of your so-clled 'friends' that you swear that you have but always seem to be busy when you want to do something with them. Lets be real though. Who needs real-life friends when you've got all the people you could ever want to talk to here?  Next up is the gameplay.  The Walking Dead has a rather different style of play to most games. It's a break from the old FPS and MOBA genres which now appear to steal the show. However, the actual gameplay is very limited. Remember I said that the story is the main element? Yep, that's what this game is. The Walking dead has been from the first game 3 different modes of gameplay. A mode where everyone is calm and has no problem going on at that particular time and so you have all the time in the world. A mode where the previous mode requires you to make a fast decision and finally a mode where you mash buttons. The first and second mode play out like a game of detective. You move the mouse until you find what it is you're looking for to progress with the story. I guess you could say there are technically 4 if you include parts where there are 4 diffferent dialogue options for you to choose from. This is also usually timed but in some cases is not. In Season 2, there are quick-time events which require you to do things such as using the arrows to move left and right or pressing a specific key in order to do certain actions.  As you can currently see, not much in terms of technicality. But that's the beauty of it. The simplicity really does make this game better as it focuses on the finer details of the game as opposed to games that rely on their complex mechanics to which the game-devs scratch their heads in confusion. The Walking Dead focuses in on the story, using the timer and the atmosphere to put the player at an emotional disadvantage and make them reall consider their choices in-game.  The best part about this game is the way in which it is meant to be played over many times to get the different outcomes and to play in different ways to which the player gets to choose. Also, thank TellTale Games for placing 3 save slots so that you get to do a playthrough of to get the different endings, all ready for the next game to come out. If it does that is.  The art style of this game is also very creative, using hand-drawn textures which suit the games style and mood. This also makes it much easier to run for those who don't have good PC's meaning that the game's experiences are to be shared with everyone.  These are the overall Scores:-  Story:- 9/10 - Very memorable and really hits the player hard. Draws emotional attachment out of the player. My only criticism is the way in which the majority of the choices have no bearing on what happens in the end  Gameplay:- 7/10 - I had a lot of fun playing through this game and even cried towards the end, but sequences eventually become easy and there is no difficulty in the completion. It is very rare you will fail. Also, trying to find what you need to do to progress can become annoying.  Textures/Art-style:- 9/10 - A beautiful game with stunning scenes that make an impact on its own. The scenes in this game really show off the atmophere and feeling.Can't ask for much more than that.  Replayability:- 10/10 - This is where this game really excels. The multiple choice element to this game makes you want to go back and redo the whole game again, leaving your mind with the 'What-if?' thought constantly after you make a decision.  Overall:- 9/10 - Overall, this game is a must buy. It is one of the biggest emotional rollrcoasters you are likely to go on. Hell, I think I would have cried less if you had made me chop onions. If you locked me in a room with this game, I'd cry every time you put me through having to watch the closing scenes.  I hope this gave you some insight as to whether you would want to buy this game (please do) and I hope it helps :)

Got it on the last day of the EA, and I do not regret it one bit. There is way too much that this game does well and only 2 things that it does poorly. But first, I just have to do this:  - Was entirely unlucky, got failures at almost everything, including the treasure chest - but for the evil carnival, I got 2 huge successes there - Got down to 2 health and got the close shave achievement - Went down to 10 HP with no food, got ambushed and restocked - Went down to 5 HP and got a shop - Entered the Scales boss battle with only 35 HP. - Then killed the stompy stompy tail lizard and his band, all of this one the same level.  10/10 will try my luck again - and I will go play the lottery too now cause this was like 1/100. BRB.  Back. Now what does this game does well and what poorly.  (EDIT: I think I could have been less excited and more informative about the game. I am sorry, I was way too hyped. So here goes...) Foreword: this is a card collection game, unlike any other. Here you do not use cards to fight - the cards represent actions or encounters. In essense, the cards fight you - or help you. In the beginning of the level, a maze of cards is laid out in front of you, which you must traverse to the end, which results in entering another area (different card layout) or a boss battle. And of course, to make your life even more miserable, you have food, gold, equipment and life points. Food needs to be replenished at a regular basis, or you will start losing life points and die of starvation. Gold is used in shops and random events, equipment and life points are used in battles.   To make the long read short short you have: - Random events - ranging from quests to finding a treasure chest or running a gauntlet to it - Fights - there is a number of encounters you need to fight through, including a boss at the end of the level who play in a 3D Batman/Assassin's Creed style - Shops and helpful NPCs who will give you buffs or food  Good: 1. No 2 games are the same, even if you play the campaign. Decks determine playthroughs, not scenarios. 2. Winning a whole encounter and beating up the boss of a level with a starter rusty axe has never been more satisfying. 3. Building a deck matters, molding it to your liking even moreso. 4. Some people complain about the slowmo attacks, but it is exactly these that give you a good view of the battlefield. 5. Mashing buttons can both win or lose you the game. Cool. Makes gamers learn something.  Bad: 1. Bind the camera to buttons, people. You have both tabs unused, make it like Dynasty Warriors 8 and you will be golden. While I agree that you can't see behind your back and it adds a realistic combat, rogue-like games are punishing as they are by default. 2. Boss battles become a bit repetitive once you learn how the bosses work and what is their attack sequence.  Semi-bad: this game is best played with a controller. I hope you have one. My RAZR is doing a magnificent job with it.  Buy the game on this last day of the early access and enjoy it. You don't have much time left, and the free first DLC is just an added bonus.

app_name: Heroine's Quest: The Herald of Ragnarok
review_text: A love letter to the old 'adventure games' (the nice ones..when the term wasn't used to indicate QTE based stuff like Uncharted, The order 1886 ecc) It has, thankfully, just enough modern touches to correct what was the worse part of titles like King's quest, Monkey island, Day of the tentacle ecc, meaning the parts where you didn't know where to go or, even worse, when you got stuck every 3 steps because you needed to do something almost impossible to figure out if not by trial and error (the classic 'combine object A with B, if it doesn't work try A with C, then B with C' ecc ecc...) This is a VERY nice title, with a lot of clever humour ,and it is a very nice way to know a little about norse/heathen mythology without giving too many informations or going too much in depth..which is a big thumb up for me, since it's what i study, but this setting will probably be hit or miss for a lot of people. The fact that there are some RPG elements, which are implemented pretty cleverly, also adds a lot of replayablity since many situations can be solved differently based on your class (Warrior, Sorceress or Rogue..classic!) I personally played as a Sorceress with very low health and with the difficulty slider at half the bar and i've seen the 'game over' screen just the right amount of times. The only part where the game struggles a bit is the combat which appears as an afterthought, but in the end it works for what it is supposed to do. If it was priced at 10-15€ (which it DEFINITELY deserves) i would probably give it a 7, but since it is free, and with so much value, i simply can't deny it an 8 out of 10. Try it! It's free! : )

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: I am a long time MGS fan, my first M rated game was MGS2 and playing that game for the first time was one of the most nostalgic moments I ever had. I have played through these games to near completion (or exhaustion) and I have never got tired of replaying them.  I put over 300 hours in the PSP version of Peace Walker and developed everything plus another 250 from scrach and redeveloped everything in the PS3 version. I spent several days playing through and watching all the cutscenes of MGS4 and trying to figure out what the ♥♥♥♥ was going on there (I love that game)  I almost cried during Sniper Wolf's death scene and burst into tears watching the ending of MGS3 for the first time.  I have a long history with this series and I always wondered what could possibly set Big Boss over the edge and become the Villian of Metal Gear for the MSX and I waited for this game to answer my question, and it did.... in its own way.   After spending 185.4 hours (at the time of this review) I spent most of that time doing side ops, S ranking every mission, doing side ops, hunting wild animals, doing side ops, rasing my bond with my Buddies like Quiet and DD to the max, doing side ops, disarming nukes (34 as of now), doing more side ops, processing that mind♥♥♥♥ of a ending while doing side ops, Rescueing Kojima again even though I did that twice already in Peace Walker and Ground Zeroes and that happened to be a side ops, I took over nearly every outpost using Quiet's 'Cover Me' command and watched in awe, and then did more side ops.   That is a small SMALL sample of everything I did in Metal Gear Solid V the Phantom Pain.  The gameplay is the best this series has seen and is so fluid (playing with a controller FYI) and responcive that I have no gripes with it at all.  The open world isn't designed to be as interesting as GTAV (even though thats not interesting at all) but instead designed for you to tackle any mission in any way you want to and serve as a cooldown to process that cluster♥♥♥♥ ending,  The Mother Base meta game is addictive as hell and almost a requirement (who am I kidding it is) to complete the game but thats not a bad thing as attaching balloons to guards to build the units is fun so building Mother Base is never boring even if it is a grind.  The story is pretty lackluster when compared to the other games in the series (especially 3 and 4) and that is mostly cause of Konami screwing with development and probably forcing Kojipro to release a year early (I WOULD HAVE WAITED DAMNIT). The actualy story is pretty good if you think of the end of chapter 1 as the true ending and the rest of the story being extra goodies. Chapter 2 is where things go downhill because its mostly rehashed missions with a unique twist (like subsistance missions where you are forced to go into the mission unarmed) and this should have been a toggle for every mission rather then take its own space in the mission list. The actual story you get in chapter 2 feels rushed and unfinished. There is one unfinished plotline called 'Kingdom of the Flies' which was started in chapter 2 but never finished. And the actual end which I wont spoil is... Interesting, yeah thats the word.  The Ending itself isn't bad but it felt like it should have been saved or hinted at more in the game (there are hints to the twist but just not enough) and it does connect nicely to the original Metal Gear for the MSX.  One problem is that they replaced most of the cutscenes that MGS is infamous for (I love the cutscenes T_T) with cassette tapes where you Listen in on dialogue between characters that most of the time would have been more interesting to watch.   Another small gripe I have is that David Hayter the longtime voice of Snake was replaced with Kiefer Sutherland and I'm okay with that. Sutherlands preformance isn't bad as Snake (infact his lines are some of the best in the series) but the reasoning for using Sutherland was because he could use that facial capture and physically act with his face. The problem with that is that Snake almost never talks and always has this blank/depressed expression on his face which makes you wonder why they hired Sutherland in the first place.  Overall I would rate this game a 10/10 if it wasn't for the cut content and underdeveloped plot twist (it needed a LOT more build up for it to work because as it is now it just comes out of nowhere) so instead I will rate this game a 8/10 because the gameplay we have is amazing and the story we do have while sparce is also pretty good (not perfect though). Some people will tell you this game is god aweful because of the ending but it has MGS2 syndrome going on for it where people will hate it at launch and learn to love it as it gets older.

The protagonist's movements felt smooth with him reacting to walls, slopes, hiding behind things and balancing all of them looked very natural. The art of the game was very beautiful and simplistic. The only things about INSIDE that I didn't like were some of the underwater parts with the girl felt too trial and error and the ending was really confusing (and slightly grotesque)  All in all this game was great and I must congratulate the Playdead team. This is not really a review. It is more like a personal opinion than anything else. I'm a fan of the old school Mortal Kombat series and I must say, since the first Mortal Kombat in 3D I have lost faith in the series, and I must confess this is the first game in the series I've played more extensively than any other, besides the endless hours/days/months I spent playing the vanilla ones.   Anyway, I am playing on a laptop and even though I've got a good machine (I can play witcher 3 in medium/high settings for instance), the game kept running very poorly, and I couldn't understand why. I found out the game was forcing windows to use my on-board graphics board (intel), instead of my geforce. I was close to quitting because the game looked like it was made by a blind, sexless monkey, but as soon as I managed to find the solution on the web (for those with a geforce graphics card on a laptop, just go to nvidia settings and make sure you force its usage by default, and not the generic Intel card), the game ran almost flawlessly, regarding framerate (some screen tearing on cutscenes but nothing incapacitating).   The story mode is kind of cool, but stupidly superfluous. Still, it's probably the only way to make a story mode in a game like Mortal Kombat. I found it quite an interesting experience. It's quite easy but a bit challenging until you start evolving your fighting skills, but you'll reach the end in a couple of hours. I did it for the koins, for shinnok and all the other stuff.  The faction thingie in the game is, once again, very different from what I was used to, but an interesting game mechanic never the less. I haven't played online yet and I'm not planning on doing it. I prefer Mortal Kombat as a single player experience.  Now, after completing the story, the game is far from over. You got a lot of rewards to win, leveling up, items and even hidden fatalities, and alternate costumes for your fighters. You gain koins, you get inside the Krypt (kind of a marketplace for hidden stuff - beware of the monsters you find here. Monsters you fight using quick time events), and you start spending your hard earned koins on goodies.   What I like the most about Mortal Kombat X is the fighting itself. Challenging even on easy, you really feel you're playing a fighting game. It's absolutely horrendous when it comes to blood spilling and I must say, I love it. The fatalities are very well done and the graphics go alongside with the horror show. It's exactly the feeling I used to have when I was a kid, playing Mortal Kombat on my commodore amiga 600.   Awesome fighting game. I highly recommend it to someone who has a good PC, with a bit of patience to solve some unresolved technical issues so the game can shine in all its glory  Give Mortal Kombat X a chance. It's a good game and should be in every gamer's library who likes fighting games.  Go go Mortal Kombat.   (Please bring back Shao Kahn and his hammer)

bit Dungeon II is a tongue-in-cheek mash-up of the The Guardian Legend and Dark Souls, with a Rouge-like(-like-like-like) perma-death consequence system + Diablo-style 'loot treadmill' that propels you (at hilarious speed) through its semi-procedurally generated dungeons.  If it wasn't clear from the game's title (or the way-over-the-top festival of jargon and videogame argot that was my first sentence), this game, like most Rogue-likes, is very self-aware ('self-conscious' might be a better word, with its connotations of over-wrought affect) and the overall game design is extremely sparse/generic (in the literal sense of that word).  All of which is to say that if you're looking for an immersive adventure experience where the mechanics exist as footnotes to the story (and not the other way around), this is probably not the game for you.  Here are three things that I really liked about bit Dungeon II:   - It takes about five minutes to figure out how to play. If you're familiar at all with min/maxing for DPS and high-drain, the learning curve will be practically non-existent.  - You can do a complete run-through in about an hour, if you're about your business and the RNG gods smile upon you (i.e. you get the drops you need when you need them).  - The simple scenario design and the settings are excellent: just really, really good art design and direction throughout. I enjoyed every environment in this game, which...I mean, that's saying a lot.  Here are three things that I didn't like:   - The sound design is garbage: from the idiotic, try-hard EBM chip-tunes to the arbitrary compression and processing on the blips and bloops (seriously: if you worked sound design on this game, you are definitely doing it wrong: play a Jun Fukuda game and try to learn something), I'd say the whole thing is pretty much a tear-down. There are no redeeming aspects to this game's sound design. I hope someone reads that and it hurts their feelings.  - A little more variety in the main character's gear would have gone a long way: the hats are cool, but all other armor looks the same (all boots, for example, are just color-swaps with all other boots).  - I played the game on my Xbone gamepad. Generally, the experience was flawless, except for the bits where I had to navigate menus, e.g. the start and pause menus. I kept doing stuff I didn't want to do because of bad layout or unclear button functions and that could probably use a little clean-up.  tl;dr Definitely play this game: you will have an awesome time.

Addictive. REALLY addictive.  To compare this to any kind of tank battle game is somewhat an injustice. I mean, tanks are tanks, and these mechs are 100 tons of ♥♥♥♥ YOU. This game requires several times more thinking power than any other FPS I've played.   Excellent team communication, strategy and execution are all part of the formula that makes a match a victory.   You can easily spend HOURS tinkering with the settings to optimize your mech, and there's SO MANY mechs to play with.  With such a vast variety of weapons and ways to set up your mech, there is a plethura of tactics and playstyles to learn and utilize (i.e. Light and fast to flank the enemy team, and sunder their defenses while your team rolls over them like the extremely overweight freight-trains-on-legs that they are, or Heavy and kitted with the firepower to tear asunder any unfortunate soul to turn the corner to greet you).  Oh, good lord.. the combat. While sometimes it can be frustrating, it's quite apparent that PGI has worked to balance everything carefully so that each mech, each weapon and each player has its place on the battlefield. And, you have to be careful how you go about things. Being careless could give the enemy team the opportunity to destroy one of your components. When I started, I carelessly peeked around a corner and moved out into the open without looking around. Within a second, I had my right torso -- which held the ammo for my ballistic weapons, along with my right arm (where half of my weapons were set) -- destroyed. It's things like that which can turn the tide of battle, when a mech has its firepower crippled or completely neutralized.  While I'm sure there are some negative aspects to the game, I simply cannot see them from my perspective. Keep in mind, I wasn't around pre steam release, but since I picked it up less than a week ago, I've already logged 43 hours (since the time of this post) of constant gameplay. If a game is good enough that my family will probably need to schedule an intervention to make me stop, then I'm not sure what else needs to be said.   10/10, would have both of my arms get blown off and make jokes about being 'disarmed' for the whole match again.

Pros: Excellent graphical detail even on low graphics settings Excellent storyline  Excellent use of interactive features to tell the story Virtually no cut scenes Unique combat system Lots of customization Game is currently unfinished, the devs have already promised and planned a number of massive updates to be released over the next year Cons: Endgame is essentially one big grind (Devs have stated that this should change in future updates) Dark Zone (end game area) requires 2+ players to be farmed optimally and solo players will be unable to survive past the first three sections of the dark zone regardless of their gear. PVP system is unrefined Due to stats scaling with gear higher geared players will have a massive advantage over new players Due to the complete lack of anti cheat the Dark Zone is currently overrun with players using mods to max out stats, teleport, and essentially break the game. However I seriously doubt this will last as I am sure ubisoft is aware of the problem. Decent amount of server lag on release Pro/Con (depending on play style and point of view): Difficult and time consuming to master.  Tom Clancy's The Division is a truly unique game, the devs really went out on a limb when designing it and many of the features will seem alien to traditional FPS players however after getting used to the changes it is an amazing and enjoyable game. If you have a group of friends looking for a game to hang out and play together this is it, while the game can be played solo you will have the most enjoyment with a team. I have put about 40 hours into the game in the past 6 days 12 of which with friends and the game has yet to get old, the massive amount of customization options tend to make you forget that you are spending all of your time grinding NPCs and the game devs expertly used world events and alternating skill levels and weapon types on the NPCs to make each encounter seem different. Even if you dont plan to grind out gear and just want to play casually the initial story mode/leveling process is truly amazing and certainly more fun than the story modes of games like COD. While the game is great asis the devs have planned updates and I am sure the game will only improve from here. I would recommend this game to both hardcore and casual gamers

Anyways, defintely worth the price, however the gold edition stuff is not redeemable right now for a lot of people, including me. I would like to give it a neutral review, as the it has both many pro and many cons, and I can see why people would both like and dislike this game. But ultimately the craftsmanship and care is what makes me give the game a positive review.  On the pro side; The pixel art is superb, easily the best I've seen in an RPGmaker game, it has a lot of detail in everything, and it makes the unique and surreal designs comes to life. The atmospheric soundtrack makes it even better. The story was well done. The first act was great, it had both solid puzzles, suspense/horror elements and introduce the player to the world and its surreal nature pretty effectively.  Now on to the downsides. This game pretty short, clocking in around 2 hours long. Another big issue is that while the first act is great, the second and third act are drug down by some tedious puzzles that are on the unintitutive side and require a bit too much trial and error or repetition to complete and ultimately detract from the experience. The horror and suspense elements are also gone after the first act as well. Lastly, aside from the introduction sequence, the first act was released for free as a pre-release teaser/demo, so the best part of the game is free and was played by many people (such as myself) months before the game was finished.  That being said, the skills of the developers certainly have some well honed talent, I sincerely hope they take the criticisms to heart and make more games with that in mind. I look forward to what they will make next.

What do I think of  The fall of gods ? Well, I'm not sure what the best words to describe it are. This is an action-adventure game, reminiscent of  The Legend of Zelda  and its many clones. You're the young man destined to save the land from evil, and so on and so forth. This makes for a story that's deeply rooted in tradition. But hey, tradition is good. As for the game itself... eh... I guess I would describe it as 'in-offensive' and 'oddly compelling'. That doesn't exactly sound like a thrilling or even confident description for this game, but it's the best that I can muster.   As expected of an action-adventure, you'll wander the world and seek enormously-powerful objects, while fighting monsters and solving puzzles. How The fall of gods goes about this is pretty interesting. The world of Ergia isn't massive, but it is very dense. There are a lot of houses and caves to explore, each serving some sort of purpose. As you progress, the tools you obtain will open up previously unavailable areas, which lead to optional yet welcome rewards. This satisfying game-play loop is bolstered by a thoroughly pleasant soundtrack.   The dangers you'll face are almost entirely ineffective. As you explore, you'll earn experience, which can be used to improve your stats. Even early on, you'll find yourself grossly overpowered, and monsters never pose a threat. Keeping your HP and MP up is simple, due to the large number of restoration items, you'll recieve from fresh monster corpses. Most of the bosses are dealt with by figuring out their weakness. It's an extension of the puzzle-solving you employ to get through the dungeons, and it works well enough. Altogether, the combat mechanics are really poor, but you learn to ignore them after awhile.   The fall of gods  has a ton of spells, too many in my opinion. Most of these spells are only used a few times over the course of the entire game. There are even a couple that are so useless; you were probably better off never finding them in the first place. The same could be said for the weapons and items. Your inventory will become bloated with quite a bit of junk. The density of this game works against itself, especially towards the end. When you find a spell and it only serves its purpose in one or two instances, then it might as well have been a key with fancy special effects.   The likelihood you'll persevere is exactly why I use words like 'oddly-compelling' and 'in-offensive' to describe this game. Its combat isn't particularly great, and the constant barrage of collectibles erodes its charming qualities. Still, I found myself entranced by this adventure. The lack of difficulty works in the game's favor, since I could put it down and revisit it whenever I had a few minutes to spare. Sometimes good game design is knowing your limitations. I know I would have dropped this game much earlier; if monsters were capable of killing the hero in a few hits, falling in a hole resulted in instant death, or that progress couldn't be saved at almost any time.  Do I recommend  The fall of gods ? Honestly, I wish that there was something in-between the thumbs-up and the thumbs-down. I stuck with this game to see it to the end, and I didn't hate it, but is that really enough for a recommendation? On the other hand, while the game suffers from a number of issues, the adventure elements are pretty solid. Rather than torment myself further, I'm going to break it down. if you're looking for a game that is as good as Zelda, let alone better, then this isn't it, not by a long-shot. If your expectations are low, and you don't mind dealing with some jank, then you could be in for a pleasant surprise.   No guarantees though. I've been a devout Mount&Blade fan for years, going back to the early versions of the original. One great thing about this game is how it sticks so closely to it's roots. The core is combat. There is something very satisfying about the combat.   Many melee based combat games detach you from the experience, where you click a button and your character performs a physically precise set of movements that constitute an attack. Mount & Blade connects you to the combat like no other game, by factoring in the direction of your swing, height, timing, etc to make it feel so good when a hit connects. Defense is also simple but effective, and balancing your attacks with your defense makes each battle a little chess match.  The rest of the game is fairly straightforward basic RPG, stats enable items, favor enables ownership of land, you eventually stockpile enough money and get a big enough army that it all just comes back to combat.  If you haven't tried this game and you like the idea of a sandbox army mangement and combat game, give it a shot.

TL/DR: In its current state, I believe Planet Coaster does not deserve a recommendation. It is a FANTASTIC game, but it has touted itself to be a revolutionary game in its genre when it is sorely lacking in basic areas that even Roller Coaster Tycoon 1-3 covered pretty well. While there was no major cons, the minor ones added up to very, very slightly outweigh the pros, just nudging Planet Coaster from 'recommended' to 'not recommended'.   Long version:  First off, I would like to say I was an Early Bird backer. I really looked forward to this game and in general I like it. But its not without its many small flaws.  First, let's start with the negatives, because I feel it is important to get those out of the way first for the betterment of the game/developers and for future consumers.  Cons:     Not enough themes. Where are all of the Spooky/dark theming, and the dark rides? Hopefully that will be DLC at some point, but its a shame it wasn't included from the get-go. I understand funding may have been an issue as game development is expensive, but it was still disappointing.      Too much annoying micromanagement in terms of shops. For example, if I wanted to charge more for drinks I have to manually change the prices by clicking on each individual vendor. I remember in Roller Coaster Tycoon, there was a 'keep price the same across the entire park' option...it would be nice to have that back.      Not enough pre-fabs of roller coasters and (themed) shops. You shouldn't force players to go to the Workshop to find themed content if they do not wish to create it themselves. At least have 1 pre-fab per shop type (food, etc) per theme.      Deleting paths is a tedious chore. You have to delete them individually, piece by piece, and it is incredibly annoying.      The management/tycoon aspect of the game doesn't seem deep enough, almost to the point it always feels like a sandbox even when you are not in sandbox mode. The career mode leaves a lot to be desired. I have yet to try the challenge mode, but I expect more of the same. Not enough challenge in the management portion, which is a shame since it is a tycoon game.      Not enough variation and choice in flat rides, missing a few of the classics to boot (go karts, bumper cars, something like an observation deck, the classic ferris wheel instead of the more intense one, etc). Also it is fairly obvious that most of the flat-rides suit the Sci-Fi and/or generic theme. While I have nothing against this theme, something like The Cube ride looks incredibly out of place in a fantasy theme park. There are also no western-specific flat rides to speak of.       It seems like some themes are favoured over others in terms of quantity of objects you can choose to place down. Want to make an entire Sci-Fi theme park? Have at it, because there seems to be a vastly greater quantity of 'stuff' to place down in comparison to other themes.       Not enough animatronics for all of the themes. While the current animatronics are very nice, and very polished, I would like to see more in future updates. Maybe more monsters would be a good start?  Adding on to this point, I would like to see more 'serious' versions of the dragon animatronics. The current ones look dorky and childish which don't really suit any theme, IMO, not even fantasy that it is based in.      The scenery rating along queues and the rating for in the ride itself can be manipulated to all heck. Sure, you could spend hours decorating everything so it looks beautiful and perfect, or you could just place mist special effects underneath the queue to reach that perfect 100% rating.   Even if you do spend hours legitmately making the queue look perfect and right for its theme, the game might have other ideas and give you very low scenery ratings simply because you didn't put down enough 'stuff'. So it seems the scenery rating is based on the amount of things you put down in the queue, and not the quality of their placement.   I understand that would be the easiest way to program/calculate the scenery rating as everybody's creative tastes are subjective, but I feel it is still a problem that should be addressed for balance's sake.      To my knowledge, there is no way to save blueprints of the things you create using the terrain editor. So you just created a nice (for example) dragon head using the terrain editor, and want to place it in a different park? Tough luck, because you can't!      There really are no consequences of bad roller coaster design or ride breakdowns. Nobody dies if you intentionally make it crash, and it doesn't seem to affect the people's opinion of the ride at all. There are also no coaster accidients or 'disasters' - like in Roller Coaster Tycoon 1 the station breaks might fail which might cause people on the ride to die. In Planet Coaster, your ride just breaks down, and....that's it. Everybody is still safe.       No weather effects, so guests can ride roller coasters as long as they want because there is no risk in getting wet.      Why are there tutorial videos instead of tutorial missions? Also, this makes for a very steep learning curve.      Some annoying bugs, but I'm sure those will be fixed in time.     Now for the positives!     Beautiful graphics, no matter the quality setting you have it on. Simply a feast for the eyes. Everything from the quality of the textures (and how they blend together), to the lighting is superb.     The most advanced (and fun) terrain editor I have encountered. You can create practically anything if you are patient and skilled enough.     Building roller coasters in general is pretty fun, though it can be a little tedious at times fine tweaking everything.     Fantastic music     Roller coasters can have triggered sounds and lights, enabling you to create complex scenes and tones for your rides!     Game is Steam Workshop compatible, so when I don't feel like creating a building, I can just pop on to the workshop and download somebody else's work. But, like I said in the Cons above, using the workshop should be a choice...not a requirement (due to not enough pre-fabs included)    I will update this review (maybe even change it to 'recommended') as (or if) things improve. Otherwise, in its current state, I believe Planet Coaster does not deserve a recommendation. It is a FANTASTIC game, but it has touted itself to be a revolutionary game in its genre when it is sorely lacking in basic areas that even Roller Coaster Tycoon 1-3 covered pretty well in themselves. While there were no major cons, the minor ones added up to very, very slightly outweigh the pros, just nudging Planet Coaster from 'recommended' to 'not recommended'.

Single player review: As a really big fan of RTS games I was very disappointed with this expansion. It's ridden with bugs and glitches, complete with syntax errors in unit descriptions, I've actually played betas very much more playable than this. While the original AoW Direct Action has some issues it is a fantastic game - this expansion looks as if it was not made by the same team. Due to either glitches or horrible mission scripting you often end up having to load save games every minute which isn't my idea of fun. Especially when, due to a bug, what is usually very long loading time sometimes grows up to 5 minutes per load (!) on a very high end machine. I'm 6 hours into this game, mostly reloading and restarting missions. I have just as low opinion on story execution, voice acting and missions design, while they made me chuckle a couple of times (due to absurdly low quality of it) they're completely devoid of any tactical challenge. I've seen unfinished Indie games better executed than this.

The greatest mil sim, possibly FPS of all time  I've been playing ArmA since Operation: Flashpoint (Renamed Armed Assault [ArmA]: Cold War Conflict]. I've always loved the concept of a sandbox game. Do whatever you want, your game play is literally limited to your imagination. We all know BIS has this aspect of the ArmA series in spades, but the military realism and breath of immersion with A3 is unreal.  You step in the game and you actually FEEL yourself move within the environment. Thanks to the PysX engine, when you drive a vehicle the tactile feeling of traversing the terrain is like no other game out there. Weapons don't just aim themselves, if you miss your target down range (which you will!) it's your own fault. Working in squads and managing situation awareness is crucial to your survival, and you'll find plenty of players who want that next level realsim. The lush graphics and visuals are by far the most advanced of all current FPSs. Finally the best improvement, IMO, is the modified stance system. No longer do you have to clumsily stick to a wall to have cover, or just choose between getting shot or going prone and not being able to shoot. With this new mechanic, you can adjust your position in very specific ways to have sights down range over any variety of cover.   By and large this is the greatest experience I've had in video games in a long time. ArmA 3 is only still in beta right now and It's still the most compelling, and in depth video game I've ever played.  10.00/10.00

The next time you go to church and the preacher tells you that video games are evil, you can look him in the eye and tell him.......er....uh......Well, since this game's release, there's really no good counter argument. LOL Yep, in this game you play as the son of the devil. It is heavily influnced by the 1976 classic 'Omen,' and that honestly is the only thing that initially attracted me to this game. It's a third person mystery/adventure game where you explore a huge mansion and find creative ways to kill off the help. You do not play a hero. If this game has any heros at all, it's the detective and your clueless parents. The chapters of the game are divided into each person you kill, and with each murder, your satanic powers are increased. You get such skills as mind control, combustion, and memory erase. You can move things with your mind and set people on fire, but you can't be seen because you don't want to draw suspicion. The story is progressed through very good cutscenes told between chapters and the game has a lot of atmosphere. The story reaches it's climax during a very cool exorcism scene, but man I've never played a game where I wanted the character I play as to loose so bad. It's a very interesting twist that honestly, I wasn't totally comfortable with. But it was original, unique, and very interesting, with great controls and a neat open world mansion to explore. So if you don't mind taking the role of a devil child and knocking off helpless people for Satan, this might be your cup of tea. BWAHAHAHAHAHAAA!!

Fallout New Vegas. Where to begin, where to begin.. As you can see by my playtime, I love this game. A whole lot. Yep, I know.   For the lazy people: TL;DR: You play a delivery boy with a special special package, you gotta get that back in a wasteland where nearly everything is out to kill you. Lore is amazing. Have fun!  New Vegas takes place in what used to be the Mojave Desert in Nevada, near Las Vegas. In 2077, China dropped many nuclear bombs on the United States in an act of pure terror and vengeance after losing the last oil supply in the world to the Americans. Nuclear bombs landed all over the US and many thought this was the end of Humanity, but it was just another bloody chapter in human history.  The story of New Vegas begins 200 ish years on, in 2281. You are a Courier for the Mojave Express delivery company who is given a special, oh so special package to deliver. This package is sought after by a certain well, not to be rude, but certain d*ckhead in a checkered suit. Not going to spoil the story any further.  The good thing about Fallout New Vegas is the ability to do whatever you want, and build whatever kind of character you want. You have the freedom to travel anywhere in the wastes. As you level up, and get more experienced, you will likely find yourself in love with the game, much like myself. But you might get to a point where you are wanting more. More guns, more quests, more companions, more armour, more more more. That is what the NexusMods community is for. Fallout is known widely for its modding community and how it can change up the game so much, its crazy. There is a mod that lets you drive cars!. CARS IN FALLOUT.  All in all, I greatly recommend this game to anyone who likes first person shooters, third person shooters, survival games and RPGs. A great game to get into.  10/10 from me.

The magic of Warframe is not how beautiful the game is in terms of graphic, even though it's Free-to-play.    Not how fluid the combat is.   Not the MR system, which requires you to actually know how to play the game in order to rank up.   Not the various amounts of warframes and how each of them have their unique set of skills.   Not the trading system, where anyone can get platinum as long as they work for it.   Not the free-to-play system, where one doesn't require to pay in order to get ahead (more like pay-to-get-stuff-slighty faster)   Not how there's an epic lore behind all this grinding (Second Dream. I crie evritiem) (Ordan Karris anyone?)   Not the ludicrious amount of weapons that can be crafted.   Not how you can build your frames and weapons anyway you want? (More firerate? Go for it. Want to achieve critting a crit? Hell yeah you do. More damage per bullet? ♥♥♥♥ yeah)   Not the helpful companions which varies from cats to dogs to tiny floating robots.   Its beauty lies in how much the developers care for the players, how they work sleepless nights in order for us to get the best updates.  Its beauty lies in how this game even existed, even though publishers they talked to had thought that this game would fail terribly. They risked everything they had in order to self-publish this game.   It's beauty lies in how DE meticulously created, drawn, and perfected each and every single aspect of the game  (except RNG cus ♥♥♥♥ dat ♥♥♥♥).  Truly, this game wholly deserves my 1000 hours. I've played on and off since late 2013, and it's a giant shocker to what I came back to, to be honest. A refined diamond standing in a sea of wrinkly ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.
