This game. It's rage inducing, it's time consuming, but most of all, it's fun and it's addicting. Ok maybe not quite time consuming, it mostly depends on how long you stay alive, which can range from seconds to hours. You play a knight, the decendant of a long line of knights, all pursuing great riches deep in the bowels of the castle. Each time your character is killed, you are brought to a screen showing your family tree, all of the predecessors that you chose will be shown, along with their name and title, to a rather comedic effect. You have 3 choices with each generation, and each person in that generation is somewhat unique, in the sense that characters in the game can have some form of mental or physical disorder; i.e. characters can have Irritable Bowel Syndrome, which results in them periodically farting.. So as you can imagine, most of the ailments have a comedic effect, while others are seriously helpful.  The game is hilariously fun, and with each death it drives you on to reach further and further. The castles are randomly generated, (though an upgrade can be used to 'lock' a certain generation of castle down for one turn so you can re-explore), and within each run-through you will be able to find gold, runes, armour and journals, the former (gold runes and armour) providing you with upgrades and bonuses, the latter providing some background, and a story of sorts.  Recommended pickup, it's bloody good fun, and a great pass time, who can say no to chicken legs dropping from broken chandeliers?

Episode Breakdown (95%, 90%, 70%, 80%):  A point-and-click adventure that was surprisingly fun to play. I've played similar games on iOS before this (Pendulo Studios' Hollywood Monsters/The Next Big Thing & Yesterday and the Special Enquiry Detail series) but the postcognition was definitely a good addition to the usual point-and-click. Tedious and brain-racking at times, but still fun.  Pros: Excellent voice acting (especially Raleigh Holmes as Erica Reed) Great music Decent graphics Interesting storyline and puzzles  Cons: Animation glitches (my Erica model glitched in Kelsey's house, Ep 2, where she could walk through an invisible wall, and in the lakehouse, Ep 4, where basically her head was in the middle of her torso) Lack of keyboard input (forced to play the entire game with a mouse even though the settings launcher has default keyboard bindings) Looks like a straight port of the iOS version with the Unity engine; no remaster of graphics or sound for PC version. Could've had shooting and fight sequences (like The Wolf Among Us), hell, you're playing an FBI agent of all things and they kick down doors and shoot people all the time!  All in all, I'm glad all the Kickstarters that funded the game managed to see it developed and completed. Bring on Season 2!

After 113 hours, i've decided....This game has a certain charm to it. It's an extremely slow paced Realtime strategy. The best feature has to be the multiple resources and the length it takes to produce soldiers and food.  Another interesting feature of the game is that you have to consistenly feed your military units. It is difficult to slowly produce a large army to just steam roll because of the feeding mechanic.   The overall game has a nice balance of managing resources that is missing in other rts games.  All of this said, the game has exploitations due to it being almost 20 years old. On top of that, frequent glitches (even with fan patches) can cause the game to crash and even make a stage unwinable (due to a crash everytime something is destroyed).  The game cost so little money it could be worth it, but it's glitches and slow pacing can be draining. But if you've got a buck to spend and some time to kill, go for it.

review_text: (THIS REVIEW IS FROM SOMEONE THAT PLAYED THE FREE WEEKEND MULTIPLAYER ONLY VERSION, THEREFORE, I WILL BE REVIEWING THE MULTIPLAYER ONLY. IF I GET THE FULL VERSION, I WILL REVIEW THE SINGLE PLAYER CAMPAING AND ZOMBIES?  It´s fun and I´ve always said that I love the CoD shooting mechanics, the new Specialist thing is great, and the parkour/Jetpack element is super fun, but I have one simple question... BUT WHY DO YOU DIE SO ♥♥♥♥ING FAST?  This has been going on since CoD 4, and it´s ♥♥♥♥ing annoying. Seriously, there´s no learning from your mistakes or what you did wrong, you always die so fast. And the game´s unforgiving if you don´t pick the pace, your dead, and that´s not really a good thing. (Also, saying 'git gud scrub' doesn´t change the fact that it´s annoying as Hell, and it really isn´t a valid argument.)  Did I mention 1 hit kill weapons, and a gun that 'simulates' wallhacks? Oh goody.  I´m glad to see CoD evolving and everything, but the fact that I can´t enjoy it 100% because I die so fast really makes me not want to play .And of course I want to get good at it, but the fact that I shoot a dude like 3 times and didn´t die, but he shoots me 1 time with a standard rifle in the chest and I die is frustrating.  And even then, if you´re not good, you´re not gonna be able to use Killstreaks or Scorestreaks, wich makes you playing the game even worse when a guy uses the drones and other stuff.  Maybe if they gave you armor and if you didn´t die with 3 shots to the chest I wouldn´t be so pessimistic, but then again, that would destroy the core game.  Anyways, it´s really well optimized. I can play it on 30 FPS on my laptop and it feels 60 FPS-ish, wich is great.  I honestly want to give it a good review because Treyarch did a fantastic job, but it´s so unbalanced and unfair that I just don´t really want to play the game.  As of right now, I can´t recommmend it to people that want a fair and balanced FPS, other than that, it´s really good and fun for a CoD game.  7/10

Once upon a time, the harbingers of divine provenance known as 'Kickstarter' and 'Greenlight' descended to grace our earth. As the light of their infinite potential shone upon us all, a hundred nerds realized they could make a game exactly like that unique SNES RPG they always loved, EarthBound, and people would pay them for it! (Others thought the same thing about Secret of Mana, but that's off topic.) They did so. And people paid them for it. And all was well in the land of RPG nostalgia.  Except that off to the side, another faction of jaded RPG fans wished those developers been a little more creative, since the very source of EarthBound's brilliance was that it set itself apart from every other RPG at the time with its modern-day setting and overtly humorous tone. For a game to slavishly copy EarthBound is a willful rejection of this. A declaration that indulging in nostalgia, and nothing but, is of greater worth than forging one's own path. (Much the same could be said of all the 'lo-fi surreal horror' RPG/adventure games trailing in the shadow of Yume Nikki, but that's also off topic.)  Thanks to this dismal outlook of mine, I was genuinely taken aback when I gave LISA a chance, and it proved itself different from anything that's come before. This makes it a more worthy successor to EarthBound than any other latter-day game, but there's no need to even call it a successor. LISA stands on its own.  LISA does not conceal its sources of inspiration. But instead of illustrating the same idyllic and urban landscapes as EarthBound or the same endless surreal realms as Yume Nikki, it fuses elements of both into a setting equally unexplored by RPGs, a post-apocalyptic wasteland colored by its observer's insanity and reminiscent of Borderlands or Mad Max. The unhinged individuals you meet in LISA are not (merely) sources of momentary amusement, but hints that something is horribly wrong with the world, driving some to the brink of sanity and others far past it. The mutated horrors you do battle with are not fleeting intruders from the creator's twisted psyche, but creatures dreadfully at home within the setting and the story. And yet that story is not about horror. It's a story about trying to do the right thing.  (As an aside, LISA is a sequel of sorts to a 2012 RPG Maker game by the same creator, retitled as 'LISA: The First' to distinguish it from this one. Though it hews far closer to Yume Nikki than its successor, considering that it does have a message and it was made and released for free, I bear no grudge against its existence. It's worth playing if you like that kind of game, but by no means is it essential to enjoy or understand this one.)  Add to that an expansive world which rewards careful exploration and a battle system that also takes after EarthBound, but which has a surprising amount of tactical depth exceeding that of its source of inspiration (and honestly most SNES RPGs) and you've got yourself an RPG well worth playing despite its modest length (befitting of its equally modest price.)  The game has its flaws, many as a result of being (originally) made in RPG Maker. Most settings besides the controls, such as window size, can only be configured via command-line launch options. There are a few areas where the game becomes distractingly choppy, in particular all of the zones containing football players (probably a coincidence.) It's easy to accidentally walk off a cliff when the game is only displaying at a few FPS, resulting in an instant game over, and there could stand to be less 'easy' accidental deaths in consideration of performance issues like these. Or in consideration of 'pain mode,' which can force you to go a long time without saving.  Also, I seem to be in the minority in this, but I wasn't impressed with the music. It makes heavy use of a leitmotif which, to be blunt, failed to make me think anything besides 'Oh, it's that melody again... and it still sounds like something I myself would noodle out on the piano while not really trying.' Other tunes are forgettable, pure ambiance, or annoying due to their use of obnoxious samples; there's probably only two or three tracks I genuinely enjoyed. Still, I can't say any of it's unfitting for the atmosphere of the game, and it's a small blemish on an otherwise solid and cohesive aesthetic.  And that's all there is to it. Play this game.

Rarely do I review a product on Steam but I wanted to leave a recommendation to purchase this game, especially now that it's on sale.  The main reason I want everyone to play it because I don't own a PS4 or Xbox One and I REALLY want to see more PC ports for the Tales game series.  I know I am only 20 hours into the game but here are the pros and cons so far as I see it...  Pros: 1. A general enjoyable plot. (Yeah sure I don't nitpick but I really just want it to feel like a fun engaging story) 2. Entertaining and enjoyable characters. 3. I ALWAYS love Tales graphics. It NEVER gets old. 4. Cool Music all throughout the game even when it's atmosphere based! 5. NO bugs that I have noticed and it works RIGHT away with my Xbox 360 wireless controller adapter. Great Job!  Cons: 1. I actually poke my head around the corner a bit to make sure games don't have a CRAPPY ending. Like a real stinker waiting for me. I heard this has MULTIPLE endings. Now that's ok and all. BUT I am paranoid that if i mess up, something will suck later and nothing pisses me off more than a sad ending. This is a con for me because.... wait I can't give away spoilers. Sorry. 2. The learning curve is a little more than the other tales (to me at least) BUT. BUT. I have to justify and say that I LOVE THE improvements so far to the entire fighting system and grade integration. Wait. this is a pro in a con area?  There must be more out there but I am only 20 hours in, and I am REALLY not perceptive. Can't you see I just wanted to get a semi helpful review out there and just promote that I enjoy the game? Yes I recommend it. Go play it if you enjoy Tales games. If this is your first, it's a LITTLE complicated on the gameplay side but enjoyable!

The Mean Greens is a simple shooter with some incredibly fun and unique maps.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0s1oLUZMBE0  The fact that you play as toys in this game allows for some really imaginitve objectives and maps. For example, a kitchen map has you zipline in by a string attached to plastic cups. You battle in between gigantic doughnuts, bubbles from a sink, and your mission is to knock over the enemy cup so that they can't zipline into the level anymore. There are so many creative objectives placed on colourful and alive maps. It's a nice breath of fresh air in the shooter genre.  The gameplay itself is quite simple. You sprint, jump, shoot and roll. The combat at the moment isn't very in-depth. There are no weapon pick-ups, you just have all 5 on you at once. No killstreak rewards, or class systems. Personally, I like it. It's very simple and easy to pick up and have fun with. My only gripe at the moment is the game's cap is 5 vs 5 which leaves some of the bigger maps feeling a tad bit empty sometimes, although the devs have said they plan to raise that, as well as add more content in the future, which is great to hear!  That's really all there is to say. Overall, simple gameplay but the fun variety of objectives and maps keeps the game fresh!

This is no 'Pillars of Eternity' thats for sure.  I was disappointed overall. These guys (devs) just came off of P.O.E. (With a ton of Success) and honestly I was expecting at least something comparable and maybe even better?  The story is slow to catch on and then once it really gets going you start expecting this game to be a nice ride to the end. Then it just chops up Act II and Act III is pretty much non-existent. By the time you reach for a Dorito and Coke the games over.  Even at 1/2 price I would not have bought this game.  @ 75% off i think the time:value ratio gets better. The Load screens get to be a bit much after Act II for some reason. I was on Trial of Iron so it has nothing to do with the number of save files you have.  The Game Graphics are great.  The Map is ghetto.  The Voice Acting is good.  It has good combat balance as well.  The Spell/Staff system is gimped though. By the time you learn the spell you need, your already almost done with the game. They dont have trainers for Spells so you need to actually find the spell to learn it. I liked the spell craft system, it just needs to be tweaked for earlier access and training because Magic is not heavily weighted like the 2H in this game. They gimp your magic and then increase your recovery times = weak mages.  This game has 0 replayability for me. And that is sad because I really do want to play again just to see my other choices with character development. But, I refuse to sludge through this game again.  Unless its on sale, I just cant recommend it. It feels rushed to market with less and less content-per-minute of game play after Act I.  Hope this helps someone.

app_name: Contradiction - the all-video murder mystery adventure
review_text: I was very impressed with this game. If you like mystery TV shows or movies, you will absolutely love this game.  I usually have problems with adventure point-and-click games, I want to like them, but after an hour I usually just stop caring about the story, I end up skipping over all the dialog, I get frustrated with the pointless inventory puzzles, and next thing you know I give up and delete the game. Contradiction didn't have any of these problems and is one of the only adventure games I have ever completely finished. The story was captivating. By using real actors (only two of which had talent, but the rest were decent enough) and very high-quality video, the game made the story feel real and compelling. Inventory puzzles are downplayed (almost non-existent) and are replaced instead by dialog puzzles. Basically, to win the game, you need to interview the towns people, carefully listen to and understand everything that everyone is saying. By cross-interwiewing multiple people, contradictions in their stories emerge and by identifying these contradictions, you unlock new clues, new suspects, and new bits of the story. Despite the fact that that this game feels a lot like a British mystery TV mini-series, the interactivity is still quite high as it is up to you to figure things out. The puzzles are all logical but often not immediatley obvious, making this a satisfying and well-balanced game. I highly recommend this game and I hope that there will be a sequel!

Here's how much I love this game: I originally bought a used copy. After playing it I felt so guilty for buying a game I enjoyed so much in a way that didn't give the creators any money that I've proceeded to buy it on Steam, on Xbox Live, in Humble Bundles that contained it even though I already had it here, though Amazon digital even though I already have it here, on the Playstation Network, as gifts for people... Essentially any time I'm given a chance to throw more money at Double Fine for this game I will do so, because I don't yet feel like I've paid them back enough for how much enjoyment it's brought me.  It's not a perfect game, mind you. The platforming can be clunky, there's a few jumps it'll make you spit teeth trying to get at, there are a few too many collectables quests (and whoever decided to put figments along the length of Milla's race, where they blend in so much that you can miss seeing them when your almost on top of them, should be throttled). But it has an entertaining plot, lots of humor, one of my favorite casts of characters in all fiction, and the development team loaded it with so many little details that even after multiple playthroughs you can still find things you never noticed (make sure to check the camp bulletin board after every area you complete!) It also rewards you for trying out different powers in different places; my first time through, for instance, I don't think I ever used invisibility except for the three times you're required to (and one of those is optional) but my next time around I tried it out more often and found places where it's a great enough tool that I've pulled it out in them ever since.

Half Borderlands styled dungeon crawl with raid bosses and half Super Monday Night Combat type MOBA shooter.  This game is nothing like Overwatch, but seemingly that doesn't stop people from comparing the two. The only key similarity between the two is the fact that they're both cartoon styled shooters with multiple playable characters. Surprisingly, even though everyone has been slamming this game since release, i gotta say, this game really isn't half bad! The best part of Battleborn is easily the story mode campaign, in which you set out to clear dungeons of monsters and robots and, well, whatever really. You can play this mode with a maximum of 5 other people and it really is plenty of fun, if you've ever played Borderlands you're right at home here. You unlock new gear after every mission which you can then equip for your characters and make them even more powerful.  Multiplayer in this game is closer to a MOBA than it is to Team Fortress 2, you level up, you kill minions, build turrets and try to kill other players using a plethora of different characters. Fairly basic stuff with a couple of twists, if you've ever played a MOBA this will also be fairly familiar to you.  All in all, the game really is good fun. It's a very strange mix of different genres put into a single package but the value proposition isn't really that bad to be honest. You've got a very replayable single player/coop campaign and the MOBA style multiplayer mode on top of it. There are also bots in case you're wondering if playing with other people might prove an issue because of the small playercount.

So I like this game. Like, a lot. I think I'm gonna be playing this for a while. I did have an issue where the game crashed once, but otherwise it worked perfectly.     My pros:  --------------------  1) The framerate stayed constantly smooth at 60 fps.  2) It looks pretty.  3) The loot system isn't super insane so you don't end up with like fifty things of the same chestplate right at the beginning of the game.  4) The combat is really fluid.  5) Hits feel impactful so it feels like you're actually doing something.  6) They know there are people who like WASD versus mouse clicking so they included both movement types. This isn't exactly innovative but it's nice they did it.   7) It's pretty easy to start a party, add a friend, and play together.  8) The classes aren't ridiculous like other fantasy MMORPGs.      My cons:  --------------------  1) It can be intimidating at first, there's a lot of information that pops up suddenly when you're in town.  2) The enemies seem more like training dummies than enemies (at least in the lower levels).  3) The training area pretty much overpowers your character for the first town so it feels less like you're being involved in the game's progression and more like you're being taken for a ride.  4) The game can crash without real warning. I think I hit J when it crashed the one time it happened to me. I haven't lost any progress or items after crashing but some people have claimed to so be careful.  5) There's only four classes and each is gender/race locked. So if you want to be a ranged character either you're a female mage or a loli sharpshooter. That doesn't detract from the overall experience as much, but this can be a turn-off for some people.      The verdict:   --------------------  If you're not used to Diablo-esque games this can be intimidating, at first. But if you give it a chance and take it all in it can be quite fun, especially with friends. If you have a decent enough PC the game can look really good as well as run at a solid 60fps. The actual combat, however fluid, can be kind of boring. But it's always satisfying as a mage to send out a fireball and kill five crabs at once and see their bodies fly away in all directions. If you're a fan of Diablo 3, this game should feel similar. And for those of you who like Diablo but can't afford Diablo 3, this is a good free alternative.

This is the best in the series, IMO and one of my favorite games. Was made by the talented developers at GSC Game World, which later became the 4A games that made the fantastic Metro series. This game's great and even better with mods( which you can find at moddb). This is a MUST have for anyone interested in post-apoc FPS games.  For new players: - Takes a while to figure out and get into, but you won't put it down when you do, so be patient. -During emmisions(storms), there will be a yellow arrow or maker on the map indicating a safe place for you to hide until it passes. -Some get confused about what you should be doing, but it's to check helicopter crash sites and see what went wrong. You can get more information about how to get to them, by asking other NPCs and the locations are marked on your GPS. - Starting weapons feel inaccurate and underpowered to me, so I suggest youtubing 'STALKER Call of Pripyat secrets' to find where the hidden stashes are for a decent early weapon with a scope. -Start on vetern difficulty. You can change it later in-game if you think it's too hard. -For mods; I'd suggest atmosfear, absolute nature and absolute structures, which all make the game look gorgeous, but keep a pretty vanilla experience. Check out Misery after you beat it, because it's much harder and a major overhaul changing most of the game. -If you play without mods, try increasing the contrast in settings to the max, because it makes the game look much better.  10/10 - We need a redux version of the S.T.A.L.K.E.R. games.

This review will be done like I do with my Life is Strange review, since it is in episodes. I'll be talking about what I think about the game after playing the episode and you may see an evolution in my opinion as longer the review gets.  Episode 1: I didn't realise until way later that this game was going to be divided in episodes, if I knew this earlier I would probably buy the full game once it was released, but I couldn't resist buying episode 1 because of a sale. The first episode has given me loads of good impressions. I knew from before that the game was going to be in black and white but it didn't bother me at all. It gave a special sort of scenery that fit with the story very well. The graphics look hand-drawn and it gives an extra sort of feeling to it, not negatively of course. The story is very confusing at first but at the end of the episode it was slowly evolving into the full story, not as catchy as some stories are but I kept playing anyway. Maybe it was because of the good execution of the game. It has good interactions, they really considered that you should be able to interact with every object. The voice of the bear is amazing, I do not know how to describe it but I hope he gets a lot of lines next episodes. His voice is not the typical cutie bear voice but a very deep voice, reminded me of the stereotypical voice of a war veteran of some sort. The cutscenes are a nice addition, it gives you a sort of visual what you have accomplished the past 'chapter', but not a necessary one. The game category is not really clear to me, it has good humour but it tries to be as scary as possible. Honestly both of these think worked out. The humour is rare to find in this game but with effort you'll get a good laugh. The scary part is also well executed, when you play this for the first time you really don't expect these random scary moments.

This game improved upon the original Borderlands in every way, including the one way that the original way lacking-a story. I really liked the story of Borderlands 2 and how it attempted to give the original a story too by referencing key events from Borderlands 1. This game also added more and widely varied classes as well as a wealth of DLC content and more guns and more and new vehicles. This version of Borderlands is like the perfect one. It just did everything right. Tons of enemies-more and more and mroe of everything I loved about the first Borderlands. I wish I could play it and the first one at the same time somehow. That's a game I would lose weeks to. Honestly, the only downside I saw to Borderlands 2 was that there was no proper tank class. Brick from Borderlands 1 was my favorite class and I wish more than anything that I could play as him throughout Borderlands 1 & 2. If you can find a bundle with both Borderlands 1 & 2 plus their DLCs-snag it. You won't regret it.
If you love first-person shooters, RPG lootfests, Co-op, and tons of fun, I feel sorry that you don't already own this game.

TL;DR = wander around a dark house spamming F on everything and waiting for something to happen. Several bland jump scares. 40 minutes of time you could spend doing something else.  The remainder of this review contains spoilers.  Frequent power outages and some guy has invaded your apartment. He likes to play hide and seek. I called him Jeff. Spamming F to re-light your lighter that only lasts 12 seconds so you can see where you're going. You could use the bright light switches that remain visible in complete darkness. The notes can also be read when the lights are off, so the player can only activate their night vision when reading things.  After spamming F to activate all the objects in the apartment, you'll be chased by Jeff in a poorly-designed maze. Who put the computers down there? How did my character know there were 3 terminals to activate? Fortunately you can't permanently die in this game, so when spoopy Jeff finds you and slaps you upside the head you can just respawn and find the remaining computers. I recommend playing the Benny Hill theme while you find the computers.  Then the game turns into a platformer. That's it.  Oh, and the boltcutters you might instinctively want to pick up early in the game can't be picked up until it's convenient. You could have discovered the hidden room much earlier if the game wasn't so painfully linear. The boltcutters sit there for most of the game. And the wooden boards can't be broken until the story allows you to.  BTW, the notes throughout the game are blurred on lower resolutions so I have no idea what the second one said. Still completed the game so it's not needed. Maybe it has the safe code.  Fortunately, this is free. Although I would like my 40 minutes back. Reviews usually come with ratings, so I'll give this a 1/10 because it reminded me of the Benny Hill theme.

If you've ever played games like Madden or the old NFL 2K and found yourself more interested in managing your team than playing the games, then this might be the game for you. Once you get past the admittedly outdated UI, you will be hard pressed to put this game down as it can have the 'one more turn' effect that Civ has as you push through multiple seasons attempting to compete for the Super Bowl.   You'll watch with delight as your 1st Rd QB develops into a superstar, then struggle to hold the team together as contract negotations take place in the offseason. Do you give into his demands and potentially lose great players at other positions or drop him and hope to draft a new franchise QB so that you can keep other top caliber players as well?  Decisions like these can make or break your team for years, which is what keeps people like me coming back for more.

I would say maybe, just maybe the game would have some redeeming factors if the music was any good, but it's pretty bland. Maybe I'm just really bad, but the game overall feels highly unrewarding. This is one of the best train games I have seen.  The track building is easy, with simple train conducting and making. Although the scenarios of railroads are near impossible (as i have found with most train games), the sandbox is extremely good, and also gives you a sense of achievement, as you still need to upgrade cities and supply areas.  Suggestions for the sandbox: Build lots of track and connect all cities before making trains; consider what I do, and set up districts of the map and connect them all with 2-4 fast-lane tracks; set the configurations to hard - though it's annoying when you have 5 trains fighting over 1 track and nowhere to build more, it's the most realistic and doesn't seem right when they all suddenly travel through each other.  Probably the biggest downsides are the lag when you build more tracks if you have a lot of tracksand the glitch which lands trains into an endless loop. At this point, delete it and remake the train...it won't fix itself.  Great game. =D

Hmm... I might only have about 60 hours logged on steam, but since KSP is moddable, i might have gone past a 1000 hours due to different installs in different locations...  KSP is pretty simple, build rockets, fly somewhere. Now, exactly where is 'somewhere'? Well, during the first many hours of this game, 'somewhere' is going to be straight back into the ground. But at some point, you might get a glimpse of space, and reaching orbit for your first time is really epic. I recall my first orbit, i almost didn't dare to send my poor kerbal Jebediah Kerman down again. What if he burned up in the atmosphere? What if i didn't control the ship? After a long time of no decisions and only worry, i decided to point my rocket backwards... I took a long deep breath. I lit the engines. The engines roared with power, and after a couple of seconds, i turned them off. I checked the map. Hmm, trajectory looks fine, going down to 30 kilometers, I guess this is enough?  When the flames started to engulf the craft, I held my breath. It looked quite cool though. Now Jeb had reached the lower atmosphere, about 10 kilometers above the surface. I felt relieved, what could possibly go wrong now? The great sense of achievement struck my body, spreading joy. It was is this moment, I realized, I had no parachute.  At this point i watched Jeb, falling towards the surface. This courageous Kerbal had gone where no Kerbal had gone so far. This Kerbal, had not only gone to space, but also returned. This Kerbal, had not only returned, but also almost landed. *poof* Jeb died, I cried, RIP Jebediah Kerman, first Kerbal to ever reach space.   On a serious note, KSP is not about building rockets, its about building stuff that doesn't make sense, but still takes you to the moon (mun) This is awesome, you can strap 10 rockets on a small command pod and see your Kerbal accellerate like a rocket (pun intended) And then you can go to the moon. Construct orbital stations. Go to other planets. Go to their moons. Return from all these places, but still forget your parachutes. Brilliant. Just brilliant.
The first game to inspire you to learn and think about space. All the time.

Why does everyone like this game?  First off, the gameplay, its bad. Quick time events with intermittent bursts of unsatisfying shooting, and tutorial that seems to go on forever. The QTEs in particular feel like they were designed by someone who's never even seen a keyboard.  Second off, this game was just ported over from consoles, and it definitely shows. The menus are minimal and clunky and were clearly designed to be navigated with a controller, the graphics are subpar by console standards even on max settings.  On the plus side the game runs very smoothly.  And even though this was clearly designed to be played with a controller, the controller support is abysmal. The game recognizes my controller, and then supplies me with an unplayable control scheme, which it won't let me change, sure there's a part of the menu that supposedly lets you change the controller layout, but it does nothing, selecting anything to change just locks up the menus and I have to unplug my controller to get them working again.  Also, there is no 21:9 support without modding. Is this really such a difficult thing to offer?

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: This is a fun game with some great puzzles and story. It can be beaten quickly, and it doesn't have very much replayability, but I strongly reccomend it for people who like to do playthroughs on youtube or like story-driven games. The games story is easy to understand, but I won't spoil it. Due to the fact that the people in the game speak a made up language, you have to really just guess what it is. The conrols are easy to learn, but hard master. Basically how it works is the left side of the controller controls the older brother, and the right side of the controller controls the younger brother. This does make it hard at times when they start to move to seperate sides of the screen, which makes the controls cofusing, and you will probably die. Finally, the graphics and music are both BEAUTIFUL, but you need a really good computer to handle them without too much lag. Brothers: a Tale of Two Sons gets a 9/10

Just to be sure that someone wanting to buy the game is reading this, I will include no spoilers whatsoever.  The summary of the game states: 'Enter the pages of the hand painted world of Journal. A journey through the life of a young and troubled girl as she tries to face up to the choices and responsibilities that come with childhood. An experience that questions the reliability of how we choose to remember events and explores the truths hidden within our dreams.  Journal is a narrative driven adventure game by Richard Perrin, the creator of Kairo and the white chamber, with art and writing by Melissa Royall.'  Well, it certainly is everything stated right there. This game is something that hits hard with reality. It doesn't put anything unrealistic or stupid in it, but rather truthful and depressing subjects that hit hard with many who play it.  The story, the ending and the characters are all brilliant! The only thing holding this game back (Which lots of people have mentioned already) are the slippery controls. You know every ice level EVER on a platform game? Where you're sliding all over the place and the controls are so sensitive? That's this game for the entire thing. But after about an hour into the game, I stopped paying attention to the slippery controls and fell in love with the story. Another con would also be that the games too short for a $10 game on Steam. I got this on sale for $1.39 and it was a bloody bargain.  If you, the reader, aren't aware of my rating system it goes like this.  BURN IT! (1-2/10) Refund it politely then wonder why you ever played this game (3-4/10) Buy it if it's on sale (5-6/10) Buy it anytime and complete it (7-8) BUY IT THEN REPLAY IT OVER AND OVER AND OVER AGAIN! (9-10/10)  So, this game is totally worth buying and it only take 3 hours to complete, but I think you should probably get it on a sale. This game also has a secret ending so, there's more for you.  I rate this game 6.5 depressed children out of 10.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: DISCLAIMER: Your experience may vary from mine, however please take this into consideration before purchasing this product.  Before you read this review: I played Call of Duty 4 quite a lot back in the day and switched to battlefield a little over two years ago and have not bought a Call of Duty game since. The review is looking at the game from a technological point of view and a general game point of view. I did play the beta for the game and and have played many other FPS games throughout my gaming carreer.  Short version: Do not get this game unless you are a major Call of Duty fan with a hefty PC (I'm talking Titans as graphics cards or better), and if you have massively low expectations for the game in the first place. My experience consisted of technical issues that should not be occuring in such a hugely hyped game. The campaign seems unrefined on the technical side and match making has issues in terms of finding the best servers.  Longer Version: So I bought this game with relatively high hopes of what it was. I thought I'd really enjoy it as I had heard a lot about it and it's cool new features. I downloaded the game only to realize how big it was (Aprox 49 GB). This, for one, surprised me since Call of Duty 4 was only 7-9 GB from my memory and the new game is 7 times bigger than it. I instantly boosted my expectations for maps, textures, and general campaign because I knew that this game was nearly (only 10 GB off) the size of the great game Grand Theft Auto V.   Once the game was downloaded I launched it. I had previously read online about the mouse sensitivity somehow being linked to the FPS and that it was recommended to cap it at a steady peak. I chose 60 FPS because my computer can run any game at 60 FPS quite easily. I launched the campaign not knowing what to expect, but still having high expectations for what it should be. No, I have not finished the campaign and I never will. My first thoughts were quite good, however as I continued to play, the missions got overly repetitive and boring. Each time I reached a cutscene my face lit up because I thought I had finally reached a good part of the story, only to be completely disapointed by a continuation of the mission. On top of this, my game was not holding a steady 60 FPS either. It got lag spikes randomly, even when standing still. This is quite excusable as it might be my system, however my system is relatively high end and I do not know what the experience must be like for other players with graphics cards, CPU's, and maybe less RAM than I have. I stopped the campaign to try some multiplayer, but first, another complaint about the campaign.  Why the hell do I have a ping of 20 as the host of my own campaign? Why is it that every single time I pause the game for a break and unpause it, all animations cease to exist and you go around in the most derped stance ever. Not even the animations in the cutscenes work. They released a game and did not even check for the basics in issues people might have with the game. From a technological point of view, this is just horrible development of the game. For the first time in my entire life have I had connection issues to my own game, WHILST IN CAMPAIGN MODE!!!  Anyways, moving on. I went to try out multiplayer, because CoD has never really had much of a good singleplayer anyways in my opinion, but the multiplayer has always been a redeeming factor. Nope. Just Nope. I played many different types of games and none of them have failed so massively at selecting the correct server location for it's players. I ran into Australians on the American servers, American's on the Japanese servers, and Australians on the European servers too. I played so many games where each and every player in the server had a ping of over 200. What kind of server locating algorithm are they using. And why was I not always connected to the Japanese server in the first place? What was I doing all the way in an American server when my geographical location is in South-East Asia?!?!? On top of the horrible latency issues everybody was having, all of the matches were very repetitive. Same guns, same routes, different people. Everybody has figured out the best guns and continues to use them. Sadly, you cannot unlock those straight away and it comes to be quite difficult to adapt to the game for new players. Everybody uses the same paths and nobody chooses any variation of sorts, unless they jump a few extra times.   As a bit of a side note, the FPS lag was also pretty intense when you first joined a multiplayer map and also when you walked through a new area of the map.  The guns did not sound very satisfying in my opinion, and I feel like they could have done them better. I want to feel powerful when I fire a weapon and not feel like I'm swatting mosquitos.   Some positives about it, as not everything in this world can be negative.  The graphics are quite good in a lot of maps. Water is extremely well done and the reflections are also very nice. Only issue with graphics are the random frame drops that the game keeps on getting.  The game stays true to the franchise. It feels and plays like a Call of Duty game, however with better graphics, and more in game mechanics to fiddle with. I love the wall running, jetpacks, and awesome powerful punches.   The games Graphical User Interface (GUI) is amazingly smooth. In terms of looks and functionality they really did a good job.   As somebody that played the beta, I must say that not much has changed. It is far to evident that they only beta tested the multiplayer as a community and privately, as the campaign has so many issues. The game is evidently based off of the mutliplayer aspects and heavily relies on them to be a 'good' game. Even in the beta there were issues regarding FPS drops and they were fixed ever so slightly, but not too much.   I personally am going to refund the game and would not recommend anybody buy it until they fix the stupid issues with the optimization of the game and the server issues in campaign mode.   I do not believe my system is at fault for many of the issues within the game as my specs are moderately high as shown below. My graphics card can run massive games such as Battlefield 4 and Star Wars: Battlefront at a minimum of 100 frames constantly in multiplayer and singleplayer.   My system is also not overclocked.  My Specs are as follows: CPU: i7-4790 3.6 GHz GPU: GTX 980ti  RAM: 16 GB OS: Windows 10 Home (64 bit) Storage: 1 TB HDD and a 2 TB HDD Power: 1200 Watts  Thank you for reading my review, I hope I helped you with your decision on whether to buy this Call of Duty or not.

I had the pleasure of meeting the minds behind this game at PAX South 2016, sharing a cigarette with one of their marketing people who told me about the game.  He didn't grab my attention with the details about the game, until the -last- detail.  He tells me :   Isometric Rogue-like Procedurally Generated Bullet Hell  ...and then...  'You can flip tables for quick cover.'  It was at this point that he had my attention... and after watching a few YouTube videos, I took the plunge.  This game is challenging... brutally so at some points, but never makes you feel like a '♥♥♥♥♥♥ Gamer', even when you make obvious mistakes. The soundtrack is well put together, memorable, and the enemies feel unique and easy to read against the backdrop, as well as beside other types of enemies. You will ALWAYS know what you're fighting, and what they're capable of.  ---  You walk in to a room, and have to quickly make a plan of attack, how to move about the room, what weapons to use. You have to look for floor traps, pits, explosives, as well as walls, tables, and anything that can be used to block enemy shots. Be quick on your feet, quick with your mind, and quick on the trigger, while un-lifing adorably rendered enemies.  I'm giving this a solid 10/10...  If you want a quote?  'Adorably Brutal.' - J. Wolf

Absolutely great game! Don't let the negative reviews put you off - everyone is entitled to an opinion, but I feel from the negative reviews I've read, people dislike the game for it not being what they wanted it to be rather than reviewing it for what it is...  On the surface, the game itself is visually impressive. Each area looks distanict and feels as though thought has been put into it, each region has it's own unique feel so travelling doesn't get boring. Tyranny's art style is a little different.. although some if it may feel 'cartoony' at first (especially the character models and art panels) does fit the Bronze age theme and has a Agean Pottery / 'Disney-Hercules' feel to it.   The Story and the writing is supurb. Was quickly enchanted the story and the paths it takes you. I really got stuck into the Lore and was eating up all the dialogue. There was great interactions between characters and companion development within the story was good despire there being no specific companion related quests - two of the companions made the game for me!  It's true that in comparrison to Pillars, Tyranny felt a little short but I think thats missing the point. Comparing the two would be like comparing Conan the Barbarian to Lord of the Rings. Tyranny like Conan is an in your face fantasy story - unapologetic, gritty and visceral. Pillars like Lord of the Rings feels like a saga- philosophical, poetic and romantic. All in all, Tyranny's story wraps itself up well and gives you multiple endings to choose from.  The only proper complaints I have is the UI wasn't the most user friendly and made accessing skills/spells a little awkward. I was also a little dissapointed that the maximum party size was 4 but I guess that means I just have to go back and replay it to get to know some of the characters I underused in my first run :)  Overall I'd give this game a 4.5 / 5 stars. I hope this helps! I can't wait for a sequel or DLC expansion :D

Her Story is mystery game where you play the role of a detective reviewing old interrogation video clips and trying to uncover her story. The clips are stored in a computer and the only way to access it is to type one word or more and the computer will provide you with the first 5 clips those words were mentioned in. Using this computer you will gradually uncover her story.  The game was short. For me it took around 4 hours to get the whole picture. The concept was interesting and I really liked the story. It was a very unique experience that worth a try.

As a whole, Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex - First Assault Online is a value first person shooter that provides significant entertainment for its free price tag. Possessing a moderate variety of weapons and an interesting blend of class-based cybernetic abilities, Gits enters the same market as other shooters such as CoD, Overwatch and TF2. The gun play is above average, though not entirely great, and the graphics are polished in this highly optimized, well-performing shooter.   The game has a variety of modes including Team Deathmatch, Demolition and Conquest and you can utilize a myriad of equipment options ranging from simple grenades all the way to tachicoma tanks. The true centerpiece of the show however are the excellently themed and organized maps that present unique, contested flows with an even balance of long/close-range combat. Character customizeability is limited to purchaseable content but almost all game-impacting upgrades are relegated to strictly in-game currency only. This mitigates pay-to-win dynamics tremendously.  The game therefore, is a fun, polished, reasonably well-executed class-based shooter that leverages great maps and a low price tag to keep its audience happy. Gits isn't devoid of weaknesses, however. The game has few customizeability options and those few that exist are locked behind an exorbitantly priced pay wall. Additionally, the game fluctuates between fun gaming environment and hacker-infested shark tank. Many VAC-banned afflicted players have gravitated to this game due to its low price tag and you'll often see evidence of wallhacks or aim botting. Grinding can be a chore to level and obtaining in-game funds can be difficult. Ability-improving chips are practically ineffectual unless leveraged in massive quantities and daily/missions and objectives do little to provide varied gameplay opportunities.  Couple those weaknesses with a lack of singleplayer and the game can on occasion become significantly frustrating. Still, despite its shortcomings, Gits offers those individuals on a tight budget an inexpensive, attractive and polished alternative to CoD, Overwatch or TF2 for nothing but a song. The game is a worthy pickup and I think most players will find themselves more engrossed by Gits than they ever expected.  Good value during a slow gaming season.  7/10.  Recommended.

I'm writing a review with only 11 minutes of playtime. I don't have much to say about the gameplay itself. It started off super boring, I'm sure it gets better over time. The issue I had is that the tutorial is impossibly bad. This game was stone unplayable without a list of controls on the second monitor.  While playing in 1080p the tutorial messages were so small on my screen that I didn't even notice them at first. Once I did notice them, I was incredibly disappointed by them. They did not tell you what buttons did what. The first prompt was 'Press the action button to travel between your personal house and the back of your shop.' What it didn't tell me is what the keybind for the action button WAS, or that I had to press that button while selecting a trapdoor on the ground.  After I figured that one out I walked into my shop and it said 'Place a display in build mode before you open your shop.' I promptly had to google how to enter build mode. The next prompt it gave me was to open my shop. I understood this one, but immediately went 'Waaaait, don't I need to put ITEMS on these displays first?' Fortunately, my earlier random key smashing had brought up an order form, and I recalled that the keybind for that was Q. I ordered some health potions and placed them on my tables! Hooray! Success!  'Time to open my shop!' I said. Then I hovered over the potions to realize with horror that I was selling them at cost! My extensive time in terrible retail jobs told me that was a bad idea. I promptly returned to my second-screen wiki control page and found the controls for increasing and decreasing the prices. When I pushed the button to increase the price, nothing seemed to happen. I was confused. But wait! Something is happening! It's increasing the price of my health potion. . 1% at a time.  Now, you might be thinking that there's nothing wrong with that. . But when your health potion costs 10 gold, having to press the button to increase its price 10 times in order to raise the price by 1 gold is pretty damn annoying. It was a death by 1000 cuts. Those 11 minutes did it for me. I'm out. I wanted to like this game. I have failed.

Excellent game! Solid 8.5/10. This game hits home where you want it to, the battle system is much like most other mmorpgs yet has it's own style, such as for using melee weapons certain skills will actually do more damage if you're behind them. It makes kiting useful in an mmorpg where other games have failed at this, such as guild wars 2. The class system is amazing, any single character can play any class just by changing their equipment, which makes you want to customize your characters appearance to the maximum when first making a character. I drop it half a point for the map, it was very confusing to figure out where everything was in the first city because there were so many different entrances to the same spot so I would find myself running around in circles constantly checking my map while saying 'WHERE THE @!*$ is this quest?!' The graphics are awesome, the storyline so far is what you would expect from a Final Fantasy game. The damages and health values are very low and very balanced, so it's easy to end up biting off more than you can chew when trying to farm. Overall, one of my favorite games and very much reccommended.

This is probably the only game that is truly free to play.  Pros: -Free to play, no purchase in the game affects gameplay (other than effects) -Capable of endless replayability -So many possibilities that issues of repetitveness is rather low -Huge community, great Twitch streams to learn about the game -Legacy; game as a whole is relatively old.  Cons: -High skill ceiling -Difficult mechanics -Community members can seriously toxic (use report feature) -Time sink, games can be as short as twenty minutes to as long as an hour -Drop rate for cosmetics has lowered, meaning if you want to look cool you need to spend money.  As a free to play game, 10/10; probably the only true free to play game that provides good replayability and enjoyment for those that enjoy the genre.  As a game, 8/10; The game is memorable and greatly enjoyable but does have faults. Requires a stable internet connection to get the most out of the game and demands both lots of time playing and outside commitment to be good at the game. The report feature is limited to prevent abuse and rewards successful reports of actual toxicity. Gameplay can be repetitive, though each character is different.

I feel very cheated by this game. I was promised an experience, not any experience, but the ability to experience what is known as 'The Longest Journey'.  I go back to their proficiency (or lack thereof!) in the name, because it's key to what makes this so special-- if not successful. What is shown by the game is far less engrossing than how they amplify that, sometimes excruciatingly, until the pointing, clicking, and adventuring is raised to a gale force bellow. You're pulled so close to the guts of the mouse that by the end you'll think you could build one yourself.  The first half of 'The Longest Journey' simulates how it must feel to be eaten alive-- at least until the second half, when player one attempts to eschew the paradigm, drowning in it. But in the back half of the game, the storm ends and it = switches to a hush. 'The Longest Journey' is ambient and gentle, almost absent-mindedly turning upbeat at the end, and 'The Longest Journey' grows so static that it's not even a dirge: It's lovely to play but bereft of tension.  Which is why I'm so curious about whether it's actually 'good', if 'good' even has a meaning at all (it doesn't). Like early Pink Floyd, it, (both player and game as a one-self 'playergame'^[1]^[2]) engaged in an exploration that was unrehearsed and maybe even naïve. The game tried to become something other than the sum of its parts-- and by emerging from free and unrehearsed improvisation, the game resisted containers; the wails and ballads emerged organically, and their apparent goal-- of creating spontaneous, non-idiomatic, and exhilarating adventure-- was reached again and again, if only by proxy through existence.  It feels like the game wings itself in front of us-- thrilling us with new discoveries one minute, and the next, batting around ideas that go nowhere. Is the organ lineup here to stay? (Probably not) Is the pervasive melancholy a stylistic choice, a side effect of playing without truth, or an emotional reaction? Does 'The Longest Journey' survive, evolve, or die?^[3] Whatever the future holds, this is some of the riskiest improv ever recorded: In every moment of the game, you just wish you actually went on the journey which was in fact, in the longest, but you just ♥♥♥♥ing don't.  Footnotes: 1: As put fourth by Charles Montagu Doughty in The Titans.  2. Also referenced by name in a song^[1] by the Beatles.  3. People die in real life as well. (It's true^[2])  Sub-Footnotes: 1. By another person's name: Charles Hawtrey  2. Truth is subjective^[1]  Sub-sub-footnotes: 1. Subjective is what someone calls music when they are told they have bad taste.

A fun, chaotic, and at times downright insane MOBA/Third-Person Shooter hybrid. Super MNC certainly has a couple flaws in it's balance, but otherwise a solid game full of well designed maps, gamemodes, characters, and amazing atmosphere. With a duo of sports commentators broadcasting your triumphs (and failures) across the map in a booming, energized voice, while obese mexican wrestlers are laching themselves towards you, while disturbing obsessed Monday Night Combat fanatics do the same, and you simply blink away with your super hero powers, Super MNC truly makes you feel like a champion.

You start the game and be like 'What am i even doing?' so you dig and build and explore like it was Mincraft or something like that. Then, after you find a huge pile of junk and some hearts, you'll get a Message 'You feel an evil presence watching you...,' and your Pants might feel warm because you already know that Terraria loves to kill you. Boss spawns and you might die. Or you're able to kill him! After that you'll look at the Wiki and realize 'Holy mother of Slimes this game has content!' And THAT is where your jorney truly begins! Gear up! Fight Bosses! Gear up again! Fight more Bosses! Get killed! Try again! Build stuff! Survive Events! Die to random traps! Fight MOAR BOSSES! Dig to Hell and back! Get OP! Build on! Slay random stuff for your amusement! Farm stuff you don't even need anymore! Watch all your fellow NPCs get slain by the next Boss! Catch Fish! Catch a bigger Fish! Get killed by that bigger fish! And so on!  9/10 Because it doesn't have infinit content. D:

Max Payne 3  Well, finally got around to playing this game after having played both of the originals in my youth. Suffice to say, Max Payne 3 is probably the best recreation of the first two games. The story is very well developed and I felt connection to all of the characters. (Turning off subtitles really immerses you with Max if you don't speak Spanish) In this game, you play as a disgruntled, almost philosophical Max as he seeks out a new career as a body gaurd in Brazil after the events in the first two games.  The gameplay is as it should be; hard.  The gunplay and slow motion effects feel refined and refreshing. You don't get to use slow motion all the time, and I think that's nice, because you have to treat every second you fight in as precious. One mistake and you'll be diving for painkillers. Don't be afraid to use the last painkiller when you need it, because they are scattered in the game like it's nothing.  The graphics are well done, as you would expect from Rockstar, and the music choice is very fitting for the setting. The track played on the last level really made me pumped to slaughter my enemies. (It's called Tears in the OST)  Pros: Everything  Cons: My character dying more than he should  Definitely get this game if you like Max Payne and/or enjoy a challenge.

A wonderful little gem inspired by the classic brick breaking game, Breakout. This game brings a number of fresh concepts to the simple ball and paddle genre for an all new experience. The difficulty curve is fairly balanced, casual players should be able to trudge through but experienced players can find a satisfying challenge. The visuals are flashy and overall enjoyable to watch as many things happen all at once. Although rather unimportant in such a game, the story is cute and creative. The highest point however is the game's amazing soundtrack. Very chilling and cool, I could listen to the album for hours on end. This all adds up to an extremely cozy experience that I can't reccomend enough.

This was my first Arkham game. Purchased on a whim, and wow, was not disappointed.   Graphics and gameplay are overall amazing. The extended cut scenes mid-game are fun (especially when they are interactive!) -- boss fights are fun but, argh, can be infuriating. Free flow combat is tricky to master but once you get the hang of it, you feel untouchable!  Regarding comments I have seen about bugs, most of them are fairly harmless. The worst of which (so far, for me) have beening falling through the bottom of the map, getting stuck, and having to restart from the last saved checkpoint. Once I fell through the bottom of the world map, the other time I fell through the training arena floor. More amusing bug was knocking an NPC over with a glue grenade against a barrier, and them getting wedged into the floor.  I will definitely be buying and playing the previous two Arkham games, based purely on the fact that this game is supposed to be the 'worst' of the series so far!

My suggestion(s): - find 3 friends who want to play - buy the 4 pack on sale  - play the tutorial/practice arenas for a couple hours - have fun  Pick up groups can be hit and miss in this game. You really need to know the boat you are flying and the guns you are shooting (as well as the ammo and tools you load out with) and so does everyone else on your team if you want to win matches. That'd be that learning curve people mention. The community online is pretty friendly. Don't be afraid to ask questions.  All that said, getting blown out of the sky is fun too. So don't worry about losing matches too.

I consider myself a rather new player to the game and think my insights on many matters are still off the point, thus I'll not waste much of your time talking about the game too in-depth.  What I will do is tell you how much fun I find this game to be! Not only is the battle itself a thrilling experience and a true bout of tactics, wits and skill, the fight begins already in the ship and crew loadouts. Simple can be effective, but several harder to aim guns provide their own take on combat which is something I personally have a bias towards. Bringing the correct type of ammo may also save you from death or net you those kills you thought you'd miss. Maybe the Burst rounds in the Hwacha barely managed to disable those crucial few guns thanks to the added AoE?  The teamwork of the four person crew plays it's own part and then there is the teamwork of the captains who keep the assault of the whole team intact. And the people I've met this game have been so nice! Of course there are exceptions, but thus far nobody has been complaining about my skills (or lack of them). When everybody knows their place and function, you truly perform your best, and that is a rewarding feeling.  All in all, I wholeheartedly recommend the experience! See you in the skies!

Stealth done right. It gives a low iteration time when you make a mistake, thus allowing you to try again quickly. It has a nice upgrade system, which while allowing for more methods to kill and incapacitate your enemies don't actually make the game easier. If anything trying to use the new skills make the game become a bit more tricky... Well up to a point. There are rewards for performing tasks within a level, some that will tax your mind as you try and work out how exactly your going to incapacitate three scattered enemies within a few seconds. These rewards are usually new suits that can vastly change the style of play by removing distraction items or weapons. The in game art style is beautiful and detailed but not so much as to distract. The animated movie cut scenes look like they were lifted straight from a cartoon show.  There is dlc, but I can't comment on it as I haven't played them myself.  Either would highly recommend this game to anyone that enjoys a stealthy game and a bit of a challenge.

Insurgency is a hardcore first person shooter game. It's inspired by the previous Iraq War where you shoot at middle eastern terrorists. You can also play as the terrorists, or insurgents. While playing multiplayer, I've noticed that many players do not play together as a team. This is probably because of the lack of voice chatting. But there are times where you will be teamed with a group of voice chatters, and have a fun time communicating.  The game also has co-op mode. Where you play as an english speaking soldier with 5 other players. You must push your way through the insurgents and complete all objectives. Unlike multiplayer, co-op will force everyone in the team to play together. This makes the expirience very nice. You actually feel like you are part of an military squad.   The main two features that's very appealing is the realism and realistic sounds. If you're comfortable playing cod or battlefield, where you do not have to worry about running around, then you're going to hate this game. You absolutely must take cover while being suppressed. Whenever you're in a situation where you and an enemy are shooting at each other at the same time, you must back off and take cover. Trying to survive your way like that will not work. And finally, the sound effects are really interesting. I have no knowledge about how guns should actually sound, but if I were watching a military movie, this is how it would sound like. You can hear the ricochets while hitting an object. The snap sounds are pretty good.   My complaints are that there is no search & destroy gamemode. There is a similar gamemode, but it's not the same. Dead players are respawned whenever a teammate captures an objective, which I think can be very annoying.  Flashlight attachments are useless. Some players are playing with the attitude of cod where they run&gun.   Overall: Good fps game. If you're looking for a hardcore game where tactics, voice chatting, and realistic sound effects, you should pick this game up. I do not regret buying. Okay, Battleblock Theatre ROCKS! This game is a fantastic platformer, with a good sense of humor, great levels, and in game faces and weapons for you to buy for your character with golden balls of yarn and gems found throughout the levels.  Gameplay: The gameplay in this game if great. It's a simple side scrolling platformer, where you must explore through each level to find as many gems and secret goodies as you can, and make it to the exit alive. There's a multitude of things in your way, such as cats dressed as cops, other characters who you must attempt to kill to steal their gem. laser beams, spikes, and MUCH MORE. I love platformers, and if you do, you'd love this game.  Controls: Now, assuming you have a wired controller, this games controls are as smooth as buttah! Moving your character is very tight, and is a joy to move him through the game. The different buttons to a multitude of things, such as shoving people and punching them, throwing a projectile, and killing yourself when you're trapped in a hard spot so you can respawn back at the last checkpoint. Now, if you're going to use a keyboard, I personally don't recommend it. Using WASD controls won't be as smooth, and the different actions you can use will be difficult to remember, even though you can create your own button layout in the options menu. So, the controls are great, assuming you're using a controller.  Story: Basically, you and a bunch of your friends are on a boat, with a charming young gent named Hatty. Everyone has a great time, until you suddenly get trapped in a huge storm, and the boat crashes. You explore a nearby building on the island you crash at, and find that Hatty has turned evil. You get sent to jail, and the levels then begin. Now, some could argue that the story may be too basic, but I love games that have a simple and very understanding story, so I love it!  So, in conclusion, this games is fantastic! The game has a bunch of funny jokes said by the all mighty Stamper, who you may or may not know in some of OneyNG's videos. and the game has a bunch of creative levels. BUT, I do recommend waiting for the $15 price to drop form a sale, as that seems a little steep. I got it during the Holiday Sale, and got it for around $4. So, wait a little bit, you won't regret it.

3/10  The description for this game makes it sound like populous meets an old civ game or something... It plays more like an old version of civ made for people who like to hotseat to play against themselves AND use cheats. Over half of the 'god-like' powers you have do nothing but add resources. This might be a 5 or even 6/10 for the rigth person..... but not me.... Might be more of a 4/10 than 3 considering my expectations of the game were SO wrong. lol. Still, despite the good coding/lack of bugs, (making me want to give some good score/recommend) I just couldn't get any fun out of this. :\\ and can't recommend.  Pros: - No Bugs (not that I came across) - Has working options/settings - Has proper tooltips - Limited Tutorial  Cons: - No Campaign - No real-time elements - No Editor - Different races have same buildings to make, so that the whole point of 'balancing' in the game FOR MOST OF THE GAME becomes not so much a case of balancing between rival factions as balancing between 2 of the same faction. You can 'deliberately' do things bad/different and not play to your best, but it just makes it even more like a boring simulation/replay than any kind of actual game.  Points of Interest: (Could be pros or cons really) - Unique Menu Interface (IMO looks messy and don't like it) - Graphics are 'Dated'

Running With Rifles is like regular war only in order to gain the ability to call artillery strikes and get better equipment, you have to salvage horny magazines and booze.  RWR is an RPG-style top-down shooter. Your mission is to take over enemy territory until you are the only faction with territory left on the map. Pretty straight-forward, but it's easier said than done. If you're playing with vanilla settings (the way it should be), you'll find that going rambo like in all those other games won't work. At all. Working with all the other soldiers in the army is absolutely necessary if you want to succeed. You'll start as a grunt; not much more than cannon fodder. But as you learn the mechanics of the game and start killing off more than 2 troops a life, you'll gain ranks and soon, you can command your own squadron. When you get here, you won't need to follow the commander's orders that much; instead you think you could do more damage by leading a strike squad to that isolated base over there. From an expendable troop to a valuable leader - that's where the beauty of this game lies. Another important piece of this game lies in the RP (Resource Points) system. Using resource points, you can change your equipment as well as call down support from HQ in the form of artillery, paratroopers, etc. Getting RP requires you to gather information on enemy vehicles, capture territory, and safely delivering that chewing gum you found back to base. But the most important thing to remember about RWR is that it's all about tactics. If you and the enemy are locked in a stalemate, maybe you should try flanking them while they're preoccupied with your allies. If you've got a silenced weapon, put that to use by sneaking into the enemy base unaware. For an kinda silly game, RWR is perhaps one of the best war simulations to have ever been created.

tldr; If you enjoyed the demo and feel like you could do that for 10 more hours, buy it. Otherwise, stay away.  I honestly I shelled out 7 bucks for this product simply because this one actually featured a full demo, and seeing that in a game was worth my money. Now that I have completed the main campaign, clocking in at around 8 hours, I have to say.... sure, this was worth my 7 dollars.... but not much more than that.   I enjoyed the automotive themed puzzles, I enjoyed learning (*cough*) more about the underpinnings of cars than I neccessarilly knew before, and generally had a pleasent time with the game. However, there were several aspects of the product that caused it to suffer not only as a game, but as a 'simulator'.   First and foremost: the game is shallow. At the risk of it no longer being offered: after playing through the demo, there really isn't that much more to the game. You basically do what you do in the demo... 73 more times. Sometimes you'll receive a fairly realistic customer complaint 'It's making noise when I do this, fix it' and have to tear apart the car to find the solution, but more often then not the game boils down to 'Run the diagnostics, testdrive or simulate a test drive, replace the red bits, repeat.' There honestly is very, very little more to the game beyond what you see in the demo.   The developers seem to have spent both far too much effort and not nearly enough in developing their little driving simulation. It looks great, I'll give it that. However, as far as I can tell, there was absolutely no feedback you received while test driving the vehicle. You never hear any noises that the customer complained about, despite having 'completely wrecked' suspension a car can handle just fine, etc. Crashing into a wall, peeling out, revving the engine unneccessarilly, or just about any stress at all performed during the test drive seems to have no impact at all once you are back in the garage. Completing the test drive requires you to pass through certain highlighted green areas, and stop in certain red ones. You can completely blow the 'course', as long as you meet those two criteria, the 'test drive' is a success..... which automatically displays the condition of some of the parts for you. Hurrah. It would GREATLY benefit from greater response/interactivity while driving, some added realism, or even just getting a REPORT at the end of it saying what the test driver found... rather than automatically highlighting specific parts green/orange/red.  As previously mentioned, if you've played the demo, you've seen nearly all the game has to offer. Repairing a part never gets more exciting than taking it to a workbench, clicking 'repair', then putting it back into the car. Despite having RPG-esc features involving 'training' your in-game avatar, none of them revolve around any manner of increased skill, ability to repair parts, diagnose problems, or any such thing. You can arbitrarilly get more money, get new parts for cheaper, auto-magickally know the quality of random parts on a new car, and turn bolts faster (that last one being the only actually handy one).   Speaking of turning bolts (Which is all we do), that enterprise itself is surprisingly shallow. From the first time you take off a wheel in the demo, your only interaction with a tool is to click on a bolt and hold down the mouse button until the bolt comes off. The immersiveness of the game could be SIGNIFICANTLY improved by just giving us some darn wrench or ratchet action. Why not make an 'upgrade' being going from a wrench, to a ratchet, to a powertool? You know, get some actual hand movements in there, cranking on a rusty bolt on some of these clunkers. Instead, we get the magical mouse pointer of destiny. The pointer looks like a little wrench icon though, so, that's something.   At several points throughout the experience, I began to feel that I, having watched/supported my uncle do some engine work on my car, had more automotive knowledge than the developers... or they are choosing to be overly simplistic. They make you pull a little oil catcher over to catch used oil or they fine you every time, but not once do they ask you to grab a torquewrench and tighten some sparkplugs to specifications, actually referance some OBD codes from their magical color-painting hand device, worry about the brake fluid line as you yank off the calipers, or ever once worry about putting a gasket back on the crankcase.   Rather than put more effort into the mechanic part of their simulator, the developers seem to have put too much effort into the CAR part of their simulator.... the test-drive, the high-resolution interiors that you get to see when you're waiting for the OBD wand to finish, and the schnazzy custom car you get after completing the campaign!!.... whose parts you can't modify or even inspect... and you can't even sit inside.  Now, onto the GAME part...   You are prohibited from finishing a job unless you caught every broken part and replaced the oil if it was bad. You never lose cash, get reprimanded, or suffer any form of punishment other than being required to find the missing link before moving on.   You might be fooled into thinking you'd be able to haggle prices, make estimates, charge customers for parts and/or manhours.. you can't. The game's claimed 'interaction with customers' consist of you being told what you will receive for the service, regardless of how much it costs you to perform said service.   Despite a customer telling you they want the car to be 'driveable', attempting to purchase and mount a 'used' part and then driving the vehicle around the test area does not count as a successful repair. Only after purchasing and mounting brand spanking new 100% quality parts for almost the entirety of your budget will you be allowed to move on.    This game has a lot of potential and fills a little gaming niche that I haven't seen approached before, but is lacking some serious development and thought put into how to make it a more engrossing and interesting experience. Despite the 'add on' having been published last april, a cursory look at the forums shows the devs crowd sourcing ideas for a new product (Simulator 2015!) rather than improving on this one.   ALL of this being said, I paid 7 bucks and was entertained for 8 hours. Despite all the little annouyances that really bugged me, there's a certain satisfaction to reading off a random customer complaint and now knowing to check X, Y, and Z.
Just ... Awsome :)

An unusual 'Conquer everyone' sort of game, where you are the last survivor of your civilization, all by yourself - and thus unable to conquer anyone! Instead, you are trying to convince every other civilization to form together into one Federation. Unfortunately, not everyone can get along by themselves, so it is up to you to manipulate things behind the scenes.  Beg, bribe, or threaten races to act more friendly to each other.  Rival empires forming a hostile political pact? A little sabotage, some planted false evidence, and a couple of false flag operations can drive a wedge between enemies, leaving them open to manipulation by you.  Finally, for those utter recalcitrant races, you can just arrange to have them killed off.  The game is simple in concept (make everyone friendly with each other) but deep in possibility, as you have many options to accomplish your goals. The learning curve is somewhat steep, but there is a lively and friendly community able to help. Overall, an unusual game with an enjoyable premise and interesting mechanics. Do not expect a Paradox-style Grand Strategy game, and you'll be fine.

Pros:     Japanese voice over (makes for a fun 2nd playthrough with slightly different characters personalities)    Engaging combat system    Great graphics (close-up textures and geometry are very good!) Cons:    Very linear (I recommend skipping a lot of encounters until you get to Gran Pulse)    Unstable performance(expect frame drops during combat)    Some questionable plot holes (i.e. why are there so few guards in this important area? etc)   Overall I'd give it a solid 8/10.
