LiEat is just lovely from beginning to end.  Though it's labeled as RPG, it plays more like a visual novel, laying heavy focus on the story and containing only rudimentary elements of traditional JRPG. It's not hard to find the few hidden items and it's harder to lose the battles than to win them. That being said, the game is still quite enjoyable since it's really relaxing but still gives you the feeling you actually have to do something to progess instead of just clicking and sometimes making a choice like most visual novels.  The story is divided into three arcs each considered their own game, making it a trilogy. While it certainly doesn't deliver the most depth, the lovely characters manage to pull you into their world and make you want to unweave the mysteries and see the solving. There are multiple endings to each arc based on your decisions and the key parts of the story are beautifully illustrated/animated.   Another part about the game that really stuck out to me was the music. Every arc has it's own set of 10 themes and most of them became stuck in my head at first listen. Not only is the soundtrack diverse and catchy, it's also always perfectly fitting the current mood, enhancing the atmosphere in every situation.  Each arc of the LiEat trilogy takes about 1-2 hours depending on how fast you read and how meticulous you search for hidden stuff. While I do wish there was a little more focus on the RPG elements, I enjoyed the game a lot and can only recommend it. 3€ is 1€ per arc, which I think is more than fair.

There is no multiplayer, which could be seen as a plus, as they put all their work into creating the best single-player campaign they could possibly do.    Overall I am mega impressed by this game. If you were a fan of the older Wolfenstein games, this is a some-what-continuation of Return to Castle Wolfenstein, and Wolfenstein(2009) and I highly suggest you play The New Order! For newer fans who never experienced Wolfenstein, The New Order is still a great standalone title. You may not understand some of the smaller intricacies and minor character reunions but the game does well enough on it's own to explain that so you won't be left in the dark. This game is one of my all time favourites, I love the personal scale of it and the stories that are created. I expected a high-class strategy game and got that, but also to my delight a game that revolves around story-telling, not in a verbal way of course but you get to play through and influence important decisions all through the life of generations. This is what is most memorable to me in the end, and I´d like to share one such story.   It is a story of a ill-fated marriage, playing as the king of Sweden one of my vassals and a distant relative, the duke of Norrland, got children late in life but was blessed with twins that both turned out to be good leader material. The brother was kind and a natural leader of men, the sister was charismatic and intelligent though a little too good at lying for her own good and far more interested in women than men. As their father died and the brother inherited I felt content that I could look forward to a long life as king with my loyal young vassal supporting me from the north. To further influence him I helped him secure a marriage with a good norwegian girl in my court, thinking that she was also kind and seemed generally nice and they would be happy together. I settled back feeling like I had made a good move, but a couple of weeks later I got a message from my spymaster warning me that he had caught wind of a plot against the duke. I investigated and found out that it was his twin sister that was plotting, that the dukes norwegian bride had become the sisters lover and joined in the plot, and everything seemed utterly messed up. I had little time to think about what to do about it for the duke was brutally murdered in his sleeps shortly thereafter. This made me pretty upset, and while the murderous wife escaped to her relatives I ordered the arrest of the newly crowned duchess of Norrland. I felt that seducing you brothers wife and getting her to murder him in his sleep is just too foul a thing to accept, and I felt obliged to execute her for her crimes. Justice had been done, bitter as it was, but then I noticed that the rest of my vassals were furious with me. Turns out it was a really unpopular thing to do, they thought me a tyrant for imprisoning and executing a duchess who also happened to be a relative without what they considered proven justification. This chain of events turned my long reign from a peaceful stable time to one of turmoil and constant rebellions from vassals, getting thousand of people killed in useless battles with malcontents.  Things didnt turn out well, but along the way I hade choices that could have influenced events. I could have been quicker to threaten/bribe the sister to perhaps prevent the murder, I could have accepted the murder after the fact and pretended I didn´t know the dark truth, or I could have settled for just imprisoning the duchess and not executing her, and so on. This is part of the greatness of this game, the way your choices really makes a difference.  I would also like to write about Berserker-Bjorn, the faroese chiefs only son who was named in order to invoke favour from the gods, and who later in life did indeed become a berserker and warrior of such epic ferocity that he personally killed upwards of a dozen french noble characters on the battlefield, and continued leading the charge at the age of 65 despite being maimed in battle in his thirties, and still killing young men in single combat with ease. His tale is too long for now though so I leave you with that.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: I played through I think what would be the first half of this game with the keyboard and mouse. I could never fix all the problems associated with this incredibly lazy PC port, and eventually being unable to target anything killed it.   Anytime I accidently hit the target button, my target would rapidly jump between any mob on the screen.. making combat impossible if I was fighting more than one enemy. So I had to do the whole thing without autotargeting, and aiming.   I think I had the truest Dark Souls experience with my broken controls jacking the difficulty of even the tutorial level to a point where I could be killed, and still making it through every boss up to the 2 golden knights in Andor Lundo or whatever the heck that first pretty place you visit is called. A boss fight that basically requires targeting to keep your eye on abilities you absolutely have to dodge. At that point the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ PC port won.

A classic-style strategy game with a massive number of classes to choose from. Each of the unique classes have their own strengths and weaknesses that provide a high level of entertainment. The economy system is extremely innovative as different classes (armies) gather different types of resources from the same terrain. For example, a Baron (the knightly themed army) gathers an increased amount of gold from his villages and towns but finds little of value on forest squares, while a Druid gathers plants from the wild to produce his special units.  A very unique, yet nostalgic feel to this game. I highly recommend it.

Transistor lives up to the expectations set by Bastion. Super Giant Games are proving themselves to be a top notch indie studio even though they've only released two games.   The set up stands that you are Red, a popular controversial singer in the Utopian Cloudbank city where literally everyone has a voice. Heck, they vote on what color their sky is. You awake to find the Transistor a sudo cybernetic buster sword that provides company to Red on her journey across the vast city as they try to unveil mysterious disappearances and the invasion of hive mind bots called The Process.   The game is gorgeous that it's sensual art style will leave you entranced accompanied by the games masterful soundtrack. Gameplay wise, things can be turn-based by pausing at intervals to plan your strategy of attack or you can go out in real time combat, why not do both? The leveling system depends on RPG elements to combine and upgrade your attacks by stacking abilities to preform on certain play styles or enemy types. Let me say that the amount of customization for your abilities is staggering that you'll be experimenting through out your playthrough.   The story I don't want to spoil since there are true moments of good story telling elements, that also combine with gameplay. It's a bit like Bastion, in fact if you look close how the game was designed you wouldn't be mistaken for thinking Transistor was Bastion's spiritual successor. That's just a big plus cause your in for about 12 hours of gameplay depending if you do challenges and turn on difficulties enhancements.   In all honesty the game is phenomenal for its presentation and story that you'll be sure to visit again and have the soundtrack playing your head for a experience that triple A games can seem to always muster. WARNING! THIS IS THE MOST WARY RECOMMENDATION I HAVE MADE! YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED! WARNING!  So, if you are reading this, then please understand that this is a game you should try at least. The reason why is because I both love and hate this game. For almost every positive aspect, there is a negative one. The reason why is cause of the devs poor quality for customer service and polish of the game. Lets go over the pro's and con's.  Pros:  1. Characters are unique. There are various characters in the game, each with their own traits. Some may be better than others but each has their ups and downs. This changes the game flow for each character you play. One may be a soldier with amazing shooting skills but is an absolute jerk and a coward who is very good at cooking. The vary in character traits makes each playthrough a little different.  2. Breakdown DLC. This DLC is crazy fun. Its a DLC to test how long you can last before you meet your end. You also have to choose which character to bring with you to the next level because the goal in it is to find and fix and RV to move up the level. Each level you progress increases the difficult. The higher the level, the more zeds you will find and trust me, fighting them is hard. This game is unforgiving since characters can die permenantly. You can retrieve the dead characters gear but it will be a huge impact on your survivability. You also start out with a randomly generated character and by completing challenges, you unlock Hero survivors that have crazy bonuses. Once you unlock those Heroes, they become playable themselves in your next playthough, giving you an advantage on each playthrough, though some Heroes are painfully better than others. This DLC itself has issues but we will get on that in the cons.  3. The mechanics. In this game, you need to make sure your group of survivors can live on by gathering food, medicine, weapons, ammo, etc. As you continue to fortify your base, more survivors will want to join you. Of course, you are given the option to take them in or not but it is a hard choice. More survivors mean better defenses against hordes but also a cost of more resources. You also need to build special buildings in your base. Although some are far more important than another, the essential buildings are medical tents and a workshop. This game makes you think as well has play carefully. This isnt like L4D where you can clear 100's of zeds in 10 seconds with a auto shotgun and pills, this is harsh. Fighting 1 zed is easy but when there are 3 or more, the difficulty in fighting increases drastically, making you the prey and them the hunters. There are also special infected like the feral who is agile and can take your survivor down if you are alone. So always be prepared even when scavenging.  4. Lifeline DLC. Now this one may be the most challenging of them all. This DLC puts you as the military during a zed outbreak as you try to control the situation. Almost everything is bleak with hordes everywhere, many soldiers lost to the zeds, and survivors in need of help. This paired with the fact that the number of playable characters you get is very small makes every soldier count. Losing one soldier is much more impactful in Lifeline than in Breakdown since there are barely any other surviving soldiers out there. In exchange, you get a ♥♥♥♥ ton of ammo and guns to defend your outpost along with support from the military that you have to capture yourself. This puts people at the edge of their seats wondering if they should go scavenge for supplies or save another citizen in need. The choices are yours to make.  Cons:  1. Bugs. Bugs everywhere. I am not joking. This game is so buggy that it even puts Assassins Creed: Unity to shame. There are questionable bugs like cars thrown into the air for no reason, small taps of the car on an object can explode or throw the car 30 ft away, survivor AI is mostly buggy and braindead, floating survivors, falling through the map, etc. The devs have not fixed many since its release and it really sucks. This game is fun but you gotta go through a lot of bugs.  2. Breakdown DLC. Why is this in the cons when I put it in the pros? Cause this DLC also has issues. The map never changes when you advance in level, making it repetitive and dull after going through 2 levels of it. Certain Heroes can make the game far too easy like the church guy who can give you max stamina when tired along with the radio operator who can do the same thing, making you go on with 1 guy for the rest of the playthrough. Another is a soldier Hero who can call in artillery, has leadership skill, amazing gun skill, and has good stats overall. Not only that, once you play the map enough times, you know where to go to get supplies, where to get weapons, what bases are the best. It gets very repetitive fairly quickly. Oh and did I mention this is on a real time mechanic? That's right. After you quit the game, the day can continue on, making you lose resources depending on how long you left it alone and items can reset. For example, I had 400 rifle ammo with 300 shotgun ammo, 10 of each med, 500 snacks, and 3 cars outside. After leaving it for 2 hours and hoping back in, I am down to 100 rifle ammo, 50 shotgun ammo, 5 meds, 30 snacks, and all the cars outside are now different than what I once had. This game gives you items when you leave based on the resources you have. So if you have 500 ammo for a pistol but a resource of 10 ammo, you will get ammo equivelant to the 10 ammo of resources instead of the 500 ammo you got, making things frustrating as hell to get it all back.  3. Story. This story sucks. The story is very boring and most of the characters I shrugged at or didnt care. I only cared for characters that had good stats die and even then, the character are never fleshed out well. Oh and did you know the ending of this game is a cliffhanger? That's right. The devs were planning on making a sequel without even making us understand most of what the ♥♥♥♥ is even happening. Since then, the devs have been silent on the sequel since. I dont know if they are even working on it or not but this is absurd for the devs to give us this ♥♥♥♥ of a story. The Lifeline DLC is a little better but I would have much rather play a COD or BF story than that.  4. Maps. There are literally only 2 maps, the original and Lifelines. That is all they give us and these maps arent even huge. The original is okay but its not very far. Lifeline is small and closed off more than the original. This can make playthroughs dull.  Overall, if I was to rate this game, it would be a 6/10 or a 6.5/10. This game is average with some interesting mechanics. It is worth a shot but dont get it for full price. I got mines with all DLC half off and that felt just about right for me. The devs really need to polish this game and if they are making a sequel, they better have more maps and better quality cause I dont like it when I am playing a game then the frames suddenly chug from 30 to 10 just from 10 zeds on screen.

I'm a newcomer to this game, never played it back in the day. I'm a David Bowie fan so that was my main motivation for the purchase. The soundtrack is awesome, especially the intro movie track. I'm also a Cyberpunk fan and Omikron has that kind of vibe in spades.  Omikron gets some flack for it's dated visuals and controls. The visuals are on par with the time, so don't listen to those that say the game is 'ugly'. It wasn't beautiful in it's day, but on par for certain. Deus Ex is ugly by today's standards too. The controls are hard to get used to but they too were par for the course in 1999. The contols remind me of the original Resident Evil.  As for the game itself, I'm not very far in, but it's holding my interest. For some reason it gives me vibes of three specific movies, none of which are related to this story: those movies are Blade Runner, Demoition Man, and Terminator.  I ran into several hiccups after launching Omikron on my Windows 10 PC. I've worked past all those and have created a guide in an effort to help others as well. Hope it helps someone out there...enjoy!   http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=829517818

Just to start i must stat that im a huge fan of the homeworld series (owning all 3 big box editions of the original games in all their glory) when i found out about this i preorderd the special edition. To start the game is beautiful with new stunning skins, great new sound effects, cleaned up audio for the voice acting and wonderful draw cutscenes, not to mention the easier to use UI of homeworld 2 that makes homeworld 1 singleplayer that much easier and fun to play.  These games hold their own on gameplay alone (dont believe me?, try the classic editions bundled into the collection!) but the remaster really does make the places you play in that much more special. The multiplayer (against bots for the moment) is also fun, being able to use any of the 4 major races across both games is really intreasting and gives alot more replayability. Its not all good news tho as this games does have bugs, so far iv noticed certain ships clipping through each other, ships not dissappearing after hyperdrive, the overview map not working at times, and crashes to name a few. However i still recommend this game as it is, with gearbox supporting modding for this game and a huge modding community already around, as well as possibility of (decent!) DLC and bug fixes, its a bright intergalactic future for Homeworld.

Risk of Rain pulls on the right strings for those who would love a fast-paced roguelike platformer.  The things I love about this game are the procedural generation, the wide variety of items, the nostalgic yet fresh pixel art, an atmospheric soundtrack, and the 10 unique classes. All of that packaged together makes for some FUUUUN TIMES. Risk of Rain manages to feel new and exciting through multiple playthroughs and with each of those playthroughs being fairly short, it doesn't necessitate a person to dedicate a huge chunk of time finishing the game.  There are things that warrant polish however. Some of the classes are much better than others, I think a few tweaks in the numbers department would balance the issue. The Multiplayer could use some additions, like being able to kick someone from your server, having a server save list, and being able to see a selected class's abilities during the selection screen are a few I can think of.  Overall, Risk of Rain is a nostalgic, quick blast of fun well worth it when... Steam has it on sale. 7.5/10

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: This game reminds me a lot of Never Alone, the game about an Inupiat girl. It is similar in a couple of ways: The gameplay is light puzzle solving, but the main impact of the game comes from the story and the educational aspects. The gameplay in Never Alone is a bit more physics-based with light platforming, whereas Valiant Hearts is mostly puzzle solving with some timing-based driving and running sequences for variety. Valiant Hearts is never particularly challenging, though you will probably have to replay a few sequences until you get the timing just right. The checkpointing is reasonable, though, and I never found the game frustrating.   The primary similarity between the games is the educational aspect. Never Alone breaks up the gameplay with videos describing Inupiat culture and history. These are presented as interesting interviews with actual Inupiat, and add a lot to ground the game in the culture. Valiant Hearts includes a lot of information about World War I, including archival photographs. Although I found the information interesting, I think they could have done a better job of presenting the history of the war. For instance, there are many historical objects used as collectibles, each with a short description. The objects range from military items like helmets to everyday civilian items like ration cards. However, the list of items could have been more varied. There must be half a dozen different kinds of dog tags, for instance, the differences between which would only be interesting to WWI military historians (and maybe not even to them!). The writing of the descriptions is also not the best, though I think part of the problem is that they were obviously translated from the original French.   Special mention should be given to the art and music. The game is built using the UbiArt engine, the same engine used for Child of Light and the recent Rayman games. It gives games a hand-drawn aesthetic that looks like an animated graphic novel. It is used to particularly good effect in Valiant Hearts, which is primarily a 2-D game but with multiple levels of background that move in parallax with the character's movements. This gives the gamespace a unique feeling of depth, even though the action all takes place in two dimensions. There are also doors and pathways that sometimes take you into the background layers, linking multiple 2-D levels and further enhancing the feeling of physical depth. The music is softly beautiful, sounding period-appropriate but not dated. It adds a lot to the overall melancholy feeling of the game.  The gameplay of Valiant Hearts is simple but engaging, the art and music are beautiful and dynamic, and the story is touching. This is a game with a lot more heart than we're used to seeing, and I think it's well worth your time.

I can see where they've tried to go with this game. The developers obviously wanted to make a Dark Souls esque game, but they fell flat on their face not exelling in ANY of the areas Dark Souls is even decent at. Lets start with combat first, they have tried to make combat challenging, but it ends up being challenging for the wrong reasons. The combat is absolutely atrocious... Enemies seem to rubber band to hit you when they are nowhere near, hitboxes are wonky on both vertical and horizontal attacks and for some reason even the dodge button has been reversed to work on the button release. I was sooo hoping for another DS like solid combat experience... That was all the expectations the game should've filled and I would've been happy.  Characters so far have been absolutely bland from voice acting to personalities, but enemy designs have been somewhat decent, but DS series was a lot better while not going for the obvious tropes. The beginning of the game seems rushed as hell and so far the game has been very poorly paced.  When you add the release day performance issues to boot (not applicable in my case), you have a game that should have not been released. I can finally add one more company to the existing list whose games people shouldn't buy in addition to EA and Ubisoft. When you try to copy a genre defining title, you damn better get it right or get in the line to be shot.

Sooo.. I saw a Let's Play of this game and decided I needed it in my life. The Kitty Powers character - while I don't personally like her - is very reminiscent of the Lily Savage style character, a fun but fabulous drag queen who rocks it.   The gameplay itself has you setting up clients with potential dates and trying to make the right matches for them based on personality type and more. It gets pretty nuanced which is pretty cool.   But that's not all, you're also a fly on the wall in each date, the client has an earpiece through which you tell them what to say. You have to work out what the date wants to eat from clues like 'I want something cold with no meat. And no wheat.' You play a slot machine for conversation topics. You'll have to choose whether to lie or tell the truth on some of your answers, whether you think the date will be pleased or not. If you lie, you spin the 'Wheel of Misfortune' to see if you got away with it or not, with the chance of winning bonus coins. The coins you use in the shop to unlock add-ons to the salon where you can do your client up before their date, unlock more pages of potential dates, and unlock more retaurants. Each restaurant unlock adds in a new 'thing' in to a date. For example, one restaurant unlock throws in the chance for your client's ex to randomly show up and you have to decide whether to be honest about who it was or lie. The date will either care greatly, or not. If you lie, you get the Wheel of Misfortune and if they don't believe you, as before, it's a strike. You get 3 strikes in a date. If you get 3, they walk out. Oh and if your client gets dumped they get a broken heart. If they are dumped 3 times, they storm out and you lose a bunch of reputation.  TL;DR - It's a very pink, camp game with a really fun centre and I'm bloody addicted. But prepare for a British culture slap in the face.. I wouldn't be surprised if they decided to 'go for a cheeky Nandos for a bit of bants.'

This was well worth what I paid for it. A fresh tactical combat system, a reasonable story, excellent voice acting and moderate game mechanics tying it all together. The combats are quick, so if you have to 'try again' because you realized you didn't quite see the mission puzzle, you don't lose much time. The variety of playstyles makes some fights easier, some harder, but it seems there are many feasible ways to skin this cat... thus I call ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t on many of the negative reviews I've read about the difficulty curve. Just be imaginitve.  Do the choices change huge things? No. But that's what makes them good ethical dilemas. You're not min-maxing for an item/skill or possibly even an ending, you're making very personal choices that get a nod from within the game and you get the opportunity to reflect on why you (or Cassia and company) made such choices. To that end, good job there.  There are a few minor bugs. I had a game breaking one very early on that never repeated, but everything else was cosmetic. In the sea of buggy/lame crap I've been playing, this turned out quite good.   If you like tactical combat with reasonable RPG elements and a decent fantasy story, this is really worth your while.

This game combines best parts of Castlevania III with innovations of newer Metroidvanias. You're traveling between different types of locations (like in Castlevania III),  but there are lots of places to unlock and explore, and upgrades to find within locations themselves  (like Metroidvanias). You'll be going back and forth, but locations have lots of variety,  so everything works out really really well.  This is a REAL classic game, just with typical technical limitations of the era removed. No pseudo-retro pixelated BS that you see these days.  It also adds something old Castlevanias hadn't: - Superb and very elaborate cut-scenes. It will blow your mind like Ninja Gaiden's cut-scenes did back in the day. - You can always expect some reward for your exploration (unlike Castlevania II),  also shortcuts, secret passages inside secret passages, teleports and other stuff make it all the more fun. - You are not knocked back to the nearest danger all the time (unlike all Castlevanias),   and sometimes you'll wish you were actually knocked back, believe it or not. - You keep all your stuff and level progress after Game Over. - You can buy or find more continues. - Very stylish visuals, resembling NES Castlevanias style (via reduced color palette) - Pleasure to see a classic game at 60 fps and no sprite flickering / slowdowns common for the era. - You can play it classic style with 2-button controller or with the modern 4-button one. - You'll find all cool classic gameplay solutions in one game.   It also has some challenges old games couldn't even think of. - As it was a standard earlier, the final boss will have all your skills tested.   Multiple phases, lots of damage, trick attacks, changing patterns. GIT GUD or die trying.  But new players shouldn't fear, the game will give you a brief introduction to what a classic game feels like via top levels, while vast bottom levels and their bosses show you 'the real deal'.  Did you want a classic Castlevania? Or maybe at least a good clone?  But who makes them these days? Joymasher does. But if sales figures will be as bad as they are now, I fear they will stop.  Currently sales of Odallus are marginal (below 4k copies), according to SteamSpy. If you have been waiting for Konami to make a 'more classic' Castlevania all these years,  stop waiting fruitlessly and play Odallus instead. Hope they'll do a sequel.  P.S. For more awesome hidden gems, follow the steam  CRIMINALLY Low Sales  curator.

I started playing this game a few weeks ago and began liking it. The game is enjoyable in single player or multiplayer and the progression of items and levelling provides incentives for progression. The difficulty is a strong draw for me (though other players may not like intense difficulty).   However, the numerous string of bugs that have gone on unfixed (after reading well before I started playing) is frustrating. Of note the recurring issues I experienced were: inventory items not saving, level progression not being saved, frequent game crashes/false starts. More frustrating however is the reaction the devs have to constructive feed back - no responses/acknowledgements nor plans for fixes. The patches target minor glitches that rarely negatively impact the player base -or- increase content. Content increases aren't a bad thing at all, but when there are no published plans or discussions for fixing major game issues it's a problem because it indicates that fixing major issues is not a priority.   Further, it appears that efforts to discuss these issues are censored from their discussion forums as numnerous posts I have made (and others read) were subsequently deleted within a day of writing. The posts were professional and constructive in nature and contained no derrogatory language.  Note: The discussion forums are moderated to not allow negative discussion of game facets (even in a professional and constructive manner).

I really want to like this game but, in the current state, I can't. As many others have said, this game is all about grinding. A lot. And some more. I wouldn't mind to grind for experience and money if there were many different ways to do so but at the moment there are just a few options and after a week doing that, it just a chore, not a pleasure to play this game. There is a dire need of more content.  Add that the AI and controls are not good... pilot would auto-hunt to their demise, there's no threshold setting to play around to ensure they flee back to base on time. Even more, once engaged in combat, they keep fighting no matter what, even after you order them to fly back to base (they completely ignore the order). So yeah... I'm going to uninstall it and hope that a year or two from now it will be better.  Still, my respect and kudos for making a SteamOS executable. That makes me happy to have paid for this game despite it not being something good yet at this stage of development.

Overture is a rogue-like game in which you kill hordes of enemies by just walking around, holding your mousebutton and aim in the direction of where the enemies are coming from.  Basically a game doesn't get any more simplified except if you count 'clicker games' but when playing this game you are not far off from that level.   I guess this game could best be described as a combination between 'Hero Siege' and 'Real Of The Mad God', which are not my favourite kind of games but at least they gave me some kind of enjoyment. Overture is unsatisfying to play and feels like a chore.  Apparently it seems this kind of game is considered 'fun' nowadays if you look at the Very Positive rating but I doubt anyone who has played games with more substance would enjoy this one. It surprises me the developer even dares to say this is inspired by games like Diablo, Zelda and The Binding Of Isaac because I don't really see the connection there.  If you get enjoyment out of grinding for hours and hours this might be a game for you. If not, I would stay away from this one.

app_name: GUILTY GEAR XX ACCENT CORE PLUS R
review_text: Finally, Arc System Works ports one of their incredible fighting games themselves, bringing the Accent Core series to the PC for the first time. Compared to Accent Core, Plus R brings story mode, team battles and gameplay balance changes and, of course, Kliff Undersn and Justice.  It is a good port, too, offering customization, online mode with Steamworks compatibility. It works fine between players in the same country, I can't speak for international.  And on top of it, the frankly amazing soundtrack is freely included in the game files.  In short, Guilty Gear Accent Core is the best fighting game in the market, and ArcSys has ported it well, at a really good price, too. If the studio follows up with its promise of further games on Steam, I have every reason to be optimistic.

FINALLY an American football game on Steam! The gameplay is fun, the game is simple (a good thing in my book), and the games are quick enough that you can pop in and play for 15 minutes at a time. I enjoy the season mode a lot as it is not too complicated. I also enjoy seeing your combined margin of victory along with your W/L record. It's an interesting way to sort out 'bubble' teams at the end of a season.  My biggest dislike is that all the teams are NOT equal. If you want to play as your favourite NFL team (erm, city?), you're out of luck if you don't like Green Bay, Pittsburgh, or a select few others. Only about 1/4 of the teams are actually playable - the rest are just too difficult to bother with. I imagine this can be changed a little by modding the rosters, but it's a little silly to be forced to edit your team just to make it a viable option.   Overall it's an enjoyable football game with decent AI and fun gameplay. Yes it's short on features, but it's priced accordingly. For an arcade football game, you won't be disappointed!

'Assembler programming game' is an extremely small sub-niche in an already tiny corner of the puzzle game-space that is 'puzzle games that you have to think really hard about'.   And Human Resource Machine is a good assembler programming game. The presentation is adorable, the UI is functional (although liable to drive anyone with real-world coding experience to distraction), the puzzles are smart, and the difficulty progression is measured.   So why aren’t I more enthused with it? I think that the problem lies with me. I’ve dabbled with code over the years, I took an Assembler 101 course in college (which literally involved solving many of the exact puzzles provided in this game), and I’ve played other games that did the same job better. And by that, I mean that I’m a fan of Zachtronics’ games. Between SpaceChem, TIS-100 and Infinifactory, I’ve solved much harder and more complex coding problems. By contrast, Human Resource Machine is a bit of a snoozefest.   And therein lies the problem. Human Resource Machine is an excellent coding game FOR PEOPLE THAT AREN’T EXPERIENCED CODERS. For folk with any degree of serious software chops, this isn’t going to stretch them. Of course, there’s some scope for chasing the optimization dragon, with in-game goals for lines of code and cycles to complete, but given the extremely simple nature of the puzzles, often those goals don’t even provide much of a stretch.

(Anyone who is looking for a break down of Planetary Annihilation's as a game, I recommend this review.  http://steamcommunity.com/id/voodoochilli/recommended/233250/  by Voodoochilli. The thoughts expressed below are as much about the game of Titans and Uber's poor handling of its community)   Where to begin really. I am a huge fan of Total Annihilation, a massive fan of Supreme Commander 1 and Planetary Annihilation was a dream come true when I heard Uber was making it. However, what we got at the end of the road was not quite what we were expecting. The Original Planetary Annihilation is a solid game, though with some pretty heavy problems that are still unresolved.   So what is Planetary Annihilation: Titans? Its the content you should of received at the 1.0 release of the original game. This is a horrid cash grab and to make matters worse the launch is plagued with bugs. Do you want to restart the game after every match you play? Well that is what some of us are dealing with every time you try to go back to the main menu. So not only is this a shameless grab for your credit card, but it is a buggy mess as well. Nothing in Titans is extremely new beyond units and it is only 21 units. The AI is still broken and nothing is much different. This is not worth the money if you already own the original planetary annihilation, which is fun at times.  I am sorry Uber that you guys really screwed the pooch and PA did not become the success you hoped it would. But that is not our fault and trying to rob us blind because we want to support you is how you sour your fans. Honestly, I think you guys need to close the doors if this is how your treating us now. Your customers will bite you back if you try to abuse us and there goes any hope of you guys being a developer.  Save your money for a better game.  Edit: Now some people have been taking the stance of 'they put a lot of hard work in to this content' and so forth. How much hard work? 21 units and some game modes. How is that a lot of hard work? If you owned Planetary Annihilation, you got a 66% Discount on the game which brings it down to 13 dollars or so. 13 dollars for 21 units that feel like they should of been in the main game. I say should of been because all of this feels tacked on and not the good kind. I mean the fact that its all unpolished and rough around the edges. This is why I say its deplorable. This is not a bug free, polished experience your paying for out the gate and really the fact this is all running on the same engine leaves little excuse as to why they broke their own game.

My actual playtime is around 1000 hours. So don't look at the playtime here ;)   I'm one of the old alpha player and I have to say that they turned a really nice game into a damn little piece of luck based MOBA ♥♥♥♥. First of all gameforge and robot couldn't care less about player feedback in the last 2 years.  Someone suggested on the forum consumables for skulls because you can't do something with your skulls once you hit max level and have all the cards. So they come up with consumables for real money. (also for crafting) But this greed is insane. Even if it's just usable in the PvE part.  I suggested a lot of things during the alpha and mostly BETA game and they ignored most of that stuff. 1 year later they said 'WE have a awesome new patch for you. WE thought this is cool.' And I read 95% of the ideas they rejected before... Well they also turned this into crap.  This is just to give you a idea how they developed 'without' the community.   Back to the game and what it is now. I think that it is 'pay 2 win' because money gives you a huge advantage since you can craft a lot of traps and other cards if you buy enough chests for gold (real money)  With my Master Founder Pack I have 40k ingame Gold and I bought like 10 chests (600 each) so far. They allowed me to craft a lot of stuff and I also got a lot of good cards from it. This gives you a huge advantage in early pvp matches. Pay 2 win = confirmed.  (Also gameforge is more expansive than robot entertainment over on the NA Servers. Again a good sign for Greedforge)   Game Pros: Survival Part is in a 'okay' place.  Cons: Gameforge, Failforge, crapforge, shitforge, dumbforge, CANCERFORGE  I actually like crafting in survival games but it does not belong to a MOBA. Because this turned it even more into gambling and farming game. And the mats you 'can' find are random as well. I can't see a real 'crafting' system anyway. Clicking a button called 'crafting' does not make it to a crafting system.   Queue times are ridiculous long. Sometimes we are in the queue for 15-30 minutes (with around 5000 player online)   A lot of problems with unbalanced stuff  Still no map editor to come up with new maps. It took them over 2 years to make 3 PvP maps. 1 isn't even in the game anymore. And 6 PvE maps (4 of them are just parts of the PvP map)   Bugs like: Game ends, you can't quite and have to ALT+F4 and you get leaving penalty <<< worth it! And this is known for far over a year now.  A few words of what they removed because of people who complained that the game is way to complex: Physical gates to close and open whenever you want to (Quite some funny tactics)  Leadership system to for minions and warcamps. That was a awesome thing with a lot of tactics and deep gameplay in it. They also removed that because new player were to dumb to spend this leadership at the gate. They removed different weak and strong points on minions because it was to complex and replaced it with a weird kind of 'tag' system. We used to have fire, lightning, Arcane and frost armor. Now we have just 'magic armor' because it was to complex.  I think they did most of that because of the console version that's coming up... Console noobs are not used to complex PC games ^^  I keep adding negativ things... don't worry... there are enough ;)

I highly recommend that you use a controller like I mentioned in my WotS4 review as the game is a console port and a controller is more suited to use for this game. In my WotS4 review I stated that if enough people bought WotS4 we might get a port of WotS3 and low and behold we got WotS3 ported to pc which is another great addition to the samurai sim like games. Unlike WotS4 this game takes its story a little more serious and keeps a lot of core features from the original games such as Weapons denote the skills you can use not the style chosen from the menu, so finding rare swords rewards the player with special styles that are relative to those weapons. The game is set to the backdrop of the Sengoku Jidai period, the period that games like Nobunaga's Ambition and Samurai Warriors are also famous for. You take on the role of a former samurai who is now a ronin and must choose how you wish to proceed. You could help the peasants who face brutal taxes at the hands of a new lord, serve the new lord and raise up the ranks for power and wealth, serve the remnants of the old clan who are now resorting to banditry to harass the new clan or serve the attacking army of Oda Nobunaga on his conquest to unite Japan under his rule. The game totes 22 different endings so there is a lot of replay value to the game and in comparison to the console version the games framerate is better, the game looks way sharper, the draw distance isnt 10 feet infront of the character and finally the load times are also far more tolerable and dont take as long as they did on console. If you are a fan of the WotS series or want to play a game in which the theme of playing as a samurai/ronin appeals to you then you should pick it up.

If you're looking for a MechWarrior game with Atlas, Hunchback, and Spider type mechs, you'll be disappointed.  This game uses mechs as a reference, but it's it own thing. It's about conserving armor and putting your mech in the right hex and facing to use the weapon you want (Think of solving a template puzzle over aiming and shooting a weapon and you'll do fine). You can customize loadouts in terms of favoring shorter or longer ranged weapons. You can chose whether to put your armor on the right-left-or body facing. You gain implants as you level up. It's a leveling game where you get better through unlocks rather than a mech building game where you're factoring size, armor, speed, and loadout.   I admit...at first...I was disappointed that this wasn't a rebirth of a MechWarrior Tactics type game. But I got over that and the game is what it is. The PVP is nice and better balanced because of the game's simplicity.  It's not a bad game. It's a bit phoney (Phone app like :-) ) and shallow for a PC Mech game, but there was enough to keep me entertained. Your milage may differ.
Now this is a huge improvement, the game looks so much better with Steamworks rather than Games for Windows. I give it two thumbs up.

There are only a handful of points to be made about Two Worlds II.  The good points are as follows:  -It's not Two Worlds 1.  Now for the bad stuff-  Two Worlds II offers an interesting world and the promise of a quick enjoyable fantasy experience, but they become null points under the weight of the poor gameplay.  The combat is clunky and barely functions. Your swings have no weight to them, and it feels more like an MMO than an action RPG. The reason I only have 5 hours in the game is because I couldn't progress any further. The combat was so awful that I couldn't progress through the damn story.  Beyond an interesting world and the RPG pleasures of getting new equipment to make your character look EVEN MORE badass, Two Worlds II gives you no reason to play it.  Only get this piece of trash if it's on sale for like...3 bucks. Unless there's a better game for 3 bucks.

I think this is one of the greatest science fiction point-and-click adventure games you could find on steam. It's got old school pixel graphics and just great voice acting all-around. The puzzle elements are also a lot of fun. The story behind Gemini Rue may seem derivative. It's set in a universe like the TV show Firefly and it's movie counterpart Serenity, it has the tone and feel of Blade Runner and has the plot of Total Recall. But it never made it bad. It's just taking the good stuff on what I like about science fiction.  You play as Azriel Odin, an ex-assassin that is looking for his brother on the planet of Barrucus, which is part of a Gemini Star System, a system that won it's independence after a war and is now run by a crime syndicate known as the Boryokudun. The other character you will get to play is Delta-Six whom is a patient of a mysterious facility. This is all I can say about the story without going into spoilers.  Throughout the game, you talk to characters, solve puzzles, do detective-like work to learn about things and occasionally go on shoot-outs. There's an interesting shooting mechanic in the game that you will learn how to use.   All of the characters in Gemini Rue are well written and feel real. The philosophical themes of identity and memories are also explored in this game and is pretty much what the game wants to explore and talk about. There are a couple downsides of this game. Some of the puzzles are little too difficult and the fictional universe that is offered in Gemini Rue remains mostly unexplored with the exception of a single city on one planet and a facility somewhere else.  But I recommend this game to Science fiction, cyberpunk and story adventure fans. It's definitely a must-buy!

Ken Levine and the staff of Bioshock Infinite are geniuses. I promise you the storyline of this game is one of the best. If you don't own this game you are missing out on playing one of the all time greatest games ever developed. The graphics are fantastic. The music is amazing. You get connected to lead characters early in the game and there are no slow sections or parts that are left open or confusing. When I played this game I thought it was going to be short. I kept thinking the end was near but the plot kept opening more doors and kept becoming more complex. Interactive media does not get better than this. This is a new standard for gaming. Yes there are a couple of small issues with the difficulty balance I played on hard and felt that should have been medium but I also felt this was a game that people who didnt like fps games could jump right in and enjoy so maybe thats a pro more than a con. I encontered 2 bugs the entire game one of which was a targeting issue (crow commanding) the other was a boss section (boss caught behind unopenable door) and thats it! I hope other developers can start creating games this polished because lately it seems like every AAA title is broken on delivery and its insulting. Bravo to the staff you nailed this one. I also believe there is some replayability on this title but maybe after a 6 month break when you've kind of forgotten the story a little bit similar to halflife2 ( I always go back to it). There are plenty of ways to defend and there are different paths you can take with your perks and weapons preferences. This game took me 16 hours to complete. **One last note I was getting motion sickness from 'Bioshock 2' and could not complete it 'Bioshock Infinite' did not effect me at all and I played for 4-8 hour intervals** 10/10

Saints Row IV brings to player long time of fun... well not regular fun, but damn twisted crazy fun. And believe it or not, this madness has a story. You are actually a president but unfortunately some aliens appear destroying your house (no surprise, ♥♥♥♥ happens). Then their boss, bigass alien moves you to simulation of the world... which is actually same world as in Saints Row 3. But this time, you gain superpower. Cars are only used before you learn to super-speed-running and that will be just few minutes into the game (after intro missions). You are getting more superpowers, as you progress, like powerful stomping or telekinesis. To upgrade these powers, you have to fly across the city and collect power lighting things... and you will need to do that A LOT !   The main story missions usually bring crazy situations and... different games, that creators came up with during some friday night`s weed party - in good way of course. You may find yourself in 2D world of 90`s action shooter or in a... damnit just try that out and be surprised - thats what this game do the best.  Optional quests are not that great. They give you few tasks to do in simulation, like finish a Blazin Activity, climb a tower, kill a toilet... and for reward you get some drunk costume you wont ever use. However, it is still somehow fun :D If you want to finish all optional missions and activities, you will spend most of gameplay in simulated city flying everywhere on map, which can get pretty booring after some time. If you want to complete challenges... well have fun, see you in a month.  Oh, this review was made from my Single Player experience, but i guess co-op can be even more fun !  + MADNESS ! + Superpowers ! + Co-oP option !  - Flying isnt as much fun, as driving, while cars are actually useless in this game. - Map activities are way too easy  While SR3 had 9/10 from me, i liked it a bit more, so...  8/10 !

PlanetSide 2 is a great free to play first person shooter that pulls you in from the start of the tutorial. What you first will notice are the excellent graphics. Sony entertainment online has definetly spent a lot of time making this game. The game consists of one extremely large and long battle across a whole world. There are 3 teams, and each one tries to gain complete control over the entire land by pushing down and capturing enemy bases, for your own team. You can spawn anywhere in the world as long as you are spawning in your own captured base, or you can deploy a 'Sunderer' for use as a moving spawn, where your whole team can spawn on it no matter where it is, but don't let it get get destroyed. There are a massive selection of different weaponis to suit your play style. There are quite a few vehicles to choose from that are both fun to drive and very balanced. Getting a fighter jet really doesn't give you an advantage, as a ground unit's rocket launcher can easily take you out if you are not wise. The creators have done an extremly good job on designing the world, as there are a large number of small details done to the terrain to make the fight more interesting. There are 5 main classes, with one sixth where you have to spend points that regenerate to buy it. The gameplay is very fun and addicting, and the community is very helpful and not just a bunch of little kids like on Call of uty. Overall I give this game a 8/10 and is one of the best Free to Play first person shooters I have ever played. In fact, it is better than most of the paid first person shooters I have ever played! I reccomend this game to all my friends, and it's free so what do you have to lose?!

An insanely fun puzzler that has you building towers, bridges, and more to reach your goal. You build not with tools and machines, but with sticky little living goo balls that link with others. The goal is to get a set amount of goo balls into the vacuum at the end of the stage, but once they're applied to your structure they can't be vacuumed up, only the extras can. This requires you to only building the most precise and direct structures possible. The game has a very quirky look, a catchy soundtrack, and every level is original and keeps you guessing. As the game progresses you're introduced to different species of goo and hazards that can damage structures. The game is great if you're into challenging games. One of the best indie games you can get.

Marvel Heroes 2015 is a fun game to play. It mixes the game style of Diablo III, with the characters, story, and environments being that of the Marvel universe. There are plenty of notorious characters to choose from, such as Wolverine, Spiderman, and Deadpool. Overall, the missions are pretty standard, but the story tied to the missions are what make it worthwhile. This game is also a true free-to-play game, as you can acquire all the items that you can pay for, but it will take time. For example, you can buy the Spiderman pack for $X amount, or play the game for roughly 20 hours (play time varies, on how lucky you are with the Fortune Cards, the amount of crystals you receive during the city challenges, and the Hero Boxes/Tokens you can get). With new content always being added (I've played the game for roughly 2 weeks at the time of this review, and there have already been 3 updates with content for the game), there is plenty of playability.

Never played before, but know a bit about Second Edition D&D. Friend insisted I experience it, so I played.  Made a character. Fighter/Mage Male Half-Elf Multiclass. 3d6 in order, no 'adjusting' stats because that isn't how rolling works. 78 total stat points, 09 strength, 14 intellect. Core Rules difficulty. No reloading after failing to scribe a spell.   The plot can be summed up as Everybody Wants You Dead: The Game. You live in a monastery and your wizard foster-father just starts freaking out one day and telling you to pack some stuff because we're GTFO-ing. Somehow a couple of extremely incompetent goons made it into the monastery despite the entrance fee being 'you must donate a book worth A @#%&load of money,' in order to kill you, for... a 200 gold bounty? Maybe lower? Whatever the case, they aren't getting a good return on their investment, mostly because their nonmagical daggers are flimsy.  So you leave, then Big Armored Guy shows up and kills Wizard Dad. Your little sister shows up to help you because it's boring in the monastery, but apparently Wizard Dad just doesn't care about her because he fully intended to leave her behind. Then you meet your replacement parents and learn that all the iron in the region is messed up somehow and it's probably your fault, because of... reasons? Oh, and more 'assassins' try to kill you while the bounty notices they carry show a steady increase in price. None of these fights can be avoided by not telling the people trying to hunt you down what your name is. The option to wear a cowl and avoid people should exist, but whatever. The game must be close to twenty years old at this point, yeah? I'll cut 'em a little slack for that. Game / programming limitations and all that.  Throughout my journey in 'The Sword Coast,' I've encountered a female chicken that gets turned back into a male human, an old male hermit extolling 'the joys of being a woman that one summer' when a wild surge hit him, a group of 'amazons' who are really just female adventurers trying to murder you for the reward money, more chances to belittle female adventurers for looking so gosh-darned cute in their chainmail bikinis, a bard who tells women they belong in the kitchen making cupcakes, and a radfem who treats men as unnecessarily horribly as the men in the setting treat women. You also have the opportunity to tell one of the new characters 'lololol make me a sandwich' in response to her saying she's having the time of her life adventuring.   The writing and setting are completely sexist, and the cast of companions is BORING. Except Minsc. Minsc is kind of hilarious, but once you get tired of the same few lines he gives out, he goes from 'entertaining' to 'we get it, you have a pet space hamster and that's half of the entire basis of your personality.'  The only time most of the main cast ever speaks up is to react to reputation changes or comment on your surroundings. There's generally a line for certain characters referencing the fact that you're in a city, or you're in the woods, or you're in a dungeon. And sometimes two companions will interrupt a traveling session to fight to the death. Seriously. The only party members that even bother speaking about something else are the ones that were added in the Enhanced Edition, and the sexism from the old game is still visible in dialogues with Neera. And normally I'd probably say something about Neera being completely self-centered, manipulative and unlikeable, but 1) that also applies to a lot of other characters, and 2) she's a wild-mage. Wild mages are supposed to be chaotic, catty, and fun, and Neera is all of these things.  Dorn is single-minded in his obsession with vengeance against those who betrayed him, and if you take him on as a party member you don't really get the option not to ask about his powers and not get an answer when he forces dialogue, but... the writing is lazy elsewhere too. Also, what would you expect from a Blackguard? They're 'evil but not chaotic paladins,' so Dorn makes a lot of sense as a character.  The game just oozes magical items though. You get a magic belt almost as soon as you leave the monastery, the new characters come with their own magic weapons (with Neera's staff being a bit hazardous for her to wield in combat because it might be her that gets burned), some encounters just drop amazing weapons for such a low level party, but it looks like longswords and zweihanders are the favored weapon in this game, just like so many other RPGs. You can even grab a set of elven chainmail (read: armor that wizards can cast spells in without penalty) and magic rings that increase the amount of spells you can cast/prepare per day by a large amount. And if you've got a wild mage in the party, casting through Nahal's Reckless Dweomer with such a ring turns that character into a completely overpowered powerhouse.  Baldur's Gate is such an easy game once you know how the game interprets the 2nd Edition (Best Edition) ruleset that I wonder why there are easier difficulty settings than the one I chose. 'Normal,' which is apparently the default setting at character creation, penalizes the damage enemies do. It should be called 'Easy,' but there is also an 'Easy' mode where enemies are penalized even further and player characters get a +6 bonus to all their rolls. And *then* there's 'Story Mode,' where the player's party literally cannot die. Ever. It reminds me of the DOOM difficulty setting known as 'I Am A Wimp.'  And yet, with all this rampant sexism and magical weirdness, so many reviews on here are complaining about the game being full of Social Justice because the expansion has a minor non-evil transgender cleric that can be encountered. Or there are complaints concerning 'They Changed It, Now It Suck.' Or that multiplayer doesn't work. But who plays a computer RPG for the multiplayer? Why is that even an option in the game? I thought multiplayer was what tabletop D&D and MMOs and Shooters were for. But whatever, obviously multiplayer Baldurs Gate appeals to somebody, or it wouldn't be in the game.  But, the interface is easy to make sense of. Not giving commands to the party when enemies appear isn't a suicidal tactical option because the AI generally knows how to handle itself. People with bows engage enemy units without input from the player long before said enemies can close in for melee. Melee characters move to engage nearby foes, and move on to the next foe when the one they're attacking gets hacked into chunks. Sometimes they get hung up on pathfinding, but telling them to attack the next target generally fixes that issue. And, for all my issues with the writing and the boring plot that was probably a lot more interesting in the late nineties (when RPGs weren't flooded with the 'special snowflake protagonist who's descended from a god or a dragon or is Destined For Greatness that the player must customize from the ground up' archetype yet), it's a pretty solid game.   It's certainly a lot more entertaining than Neverwinter Nights, even if you do run into Mary Sues like Elminster, The Doctor, and Drizz't Dro'Urden. At least here you get the option to murder such characters in cold blood! And for what it's worth, the game also serves as sort of a history lesson in gaming culture.  This is the sort of video game that led to the birth of the kinds of people who threaten female video game developers in today's age, but it's also a sign of evolution in the industry regarding the treatment of female characters in RPGs. But, it's also evidence of stagnation in video game storytelling, because in all the time that's passed since the release of Baldur's Gate, the majority of RPG protagonists are so, so very bland because they follow the same cookie-cutter design process.  Baldur's Gate isn't really my kind of girl, but I've taken worse ones to bed.

I've played every Hitman - I remember buying the original, and I remember how insanely difficult I found it.. No saves, easily spotted.. Very few 'set piece' kind of kills - it was you only, and you had to be smart. It wasn't great, but its was exciting. The series quality varied over the years, but never found the sweet spot between map size, difficulty, and story. I looked forward to this new installment, as it promised a game in which you were just a hitman once again.. No nonsense story, just a simple series of contracts, and some intrigue behind it..  So what's it like? Essentially, despite the advertising, it's the same as the old one. The graphics are nice, and the big wide open spaces filled with hundreds of people are impressive, but Absolution looked nice, and had the same impressive quantity of people.. The interface is fine (although I really miss the old map), and the controls are fluent and simple.   The problem is the gameplay; it's deteriorated (over many years) into a series of mini puzzles. You're given quite big maps, but the objectives are simple, and the targets just do their little 5 minute loop routines. How do I kill this guy? Well actually killing him isn't difficult - killing him while getting a perfect score requires a little more work, but it doesn't require any further skill. And the ways in which you kill people are so scripted now... And usually these ways are annouced within a minute of beginning a level, by 'overhearing' someone talking.   It just feels so limited, and hasn't moved the game on at all. I want an mini open world, and I want to be able to really get into the character.. Even the first game used to give you a few blocks around your target! You could even ambush the target enroute if you were quick about it.. Where's the urgency? Why can't we have limited opportunities? A narrow window? Give us a game where we have to switch on and really play, rather than one where I can just press 'blend in' and go for lunch.  But hey, I guess we're in a world where nobody wants anything new - same old ♥♥♥♥ please.   Massively disappointed. 5/10.

Path of Exile is Diablo-esque action RPG that fails to live up to its contemporaries at almost every possible point. Ugly, boring, and bland, it's a joyless, soulless experience that I'm baffled by the positive reception of. I've tried three times to get into it with no success. The tiny inventory is a huge, frustrating problem, and the easiest thing that I can point to as being wrong with the game. But my issues with PoE go so far beyond that. In a genre that is entirely about finding a groove, I never found one here. It's a pale shadow of the addictive dungeon crawler that it should have been.  The game just doesn't feel good, it never grabbed me, there's no hook to it. It is blandness incarnate. For all of its major flaws, Diablo 3's basic flow at least actually functioned and it had some nice art assets, neither of which are the case here. I can't even really find words for what about PoE didn't click with me so much, it just doesn't feel good. It doesn't feel engrossing in the way that I get from other games in this style. Every time I attempt to give it some play time, I end up wishing that I was doing something else. Obviously you've got nothing to lose from trying this, it's completely free and to the game's credit not at all pay to win, but I can't recommend Path of Exile. Not a single aspect of it worked for me. Do yourself a favor and pick up a copy of Torchlight II, or for that matter the original Torchlight, they are similar but infinitely better games.

review_text: If you have enjoyed games like Lunar, Chrono Trigger, Lufia, and FFVI then I think you will enjoy this game. This release is a reworked version of the original PC release which includes the additions from the PSP version (though at the time of writing the monster book is still being implemented).   The game itself really excels in the story and the attention to detail in the characters themselves is really what has sucked me in. If you are a fan of JRPGs and RPGs (especially the old school sprite kind) and enjoy a detailed story then I feel you will enjoy this game. The game can currently run in high resolutions though it may seem a bit blurry compared to our high definition standards. Personally, for the best look I run the game in a smaller windowed mode which is easily changable in an easy to use config at launch. :)

I'll say up front, I'm pretty torn about this one. On the one hand, it's an interesting experiment in storytelling through non-traditional means, and it's no secret that I love stuff like that. On the other hand, it's not a terribly fun one for reasons I will get into. In the end, I think it being free and short really work to its advantage and put it in a place where I can recommend people trying it for themselves. But now I'm getting to the end before I even begin, so let's back up a little.  Serena is the story of a man alone in a cabin, musing about his marriage. The cabin is just two rooms of static, rendered scenes to navigate, with hotspots to examine for fully-voiced narration. This is the entirety of gameplay, and to explain at all about what he finds would be to spoil most of Serena's appeal. Don't come looking for puzzles or investigations or scares, because what you're getting is a low-key journey into one man's psyche. The issue here is that you need to examine everything in the cabin until something changes, and then examine everything AGAIN until it changes, and so on. You might cotton on to the specific hotspots to trigger the next change but they're not really hinted at, and checking everything over and over can really stretch this journey out.  The saving grace here is that the journey is a pretty interesting one. As you examine the cabin, you'll hear plenty of monologues that reveal glimpses of the man's personality and relationship with Serena. They're not exactly subtle, but for a game this short they help hustle the story along with a welcome briskness. It's clear a lot of consideration went into the relationship on display, especially once your examinations start yielding new information. And it all winds to a bit of a twist ending that's worth seeing once for how well it's set up.  This is one of the rare games where the asking price really factors into my assessment of it. While I appreciate the story woven into the quality dialogue and detailed locale, it's just not that fun to get through. You're going to have to go through several cycles of examining the cabin's contents in the 30 minutes or so it takes to see Serena finished, and yes, that means clicking on the same cabinet or magazine quite a few times. It's limited enough in scope that I wouldn't be comfortable paying money for it, but as a free experience, there's really no reason not to see if it works for you.

**Note: this is a copypasta of my review from DESURA**  So if you're looking for a simple Visual Novel, this is your ticket.  If you are not the sort who prefers clicking things in the same predictable pattern for 30mins or so, hoping to get enough RNG mojo to get the (usually 1-2min) ending you would prefer just to see them, it may definitely not be your ticket, even if you agreed to the first.  The art is quite well done.  Music is limited in quantity, but actually pretty decently done.  The story and characters are somewhat interesting, though stereotypical, and also sadly fall flat in terms of the sheer limited number scenes/length of scenes available that aren't the click-spam that composes the rest of the game.  The click-spam between story bits is what you do to try to gain relationship with the various men, and then there's special endings unlocked if you also get one of the 4 'stats' associated with them to max. Only thing is, you 'succeed' only sometimes. Sometimes you don't. The 'difficulty' seems only to affect how often the fails occur, which doesn't necessarily mean much either, since you can buy items using the ingame money you earn from your part-time job to improve your chances anyway. Well, we hope they do, since any actual ability to understand those %s are locked somewhere in the game engine.  Don't get me wrong, it's a pretty game. And if you're looking to burn some time with just something simple, it may be worth a couple bucks just to relax with. Though please don't expect something amazing out of it. :)

This is a great game if you are into building/management. Unfortunately it should still be in Beta as the AI is terrible when you get to around 100 population. Im not sure if they are going to patch this or let this awesome unfinished game go now that it's been released.   Tips for players on getting around the bad AI (if they dont patch it)  - Do not create a 'Loop' base where it stretches all the way around a large mountain  - Try to keep all airlock/external structures on 1 side of your internal structure chains, do NOT build on both sides no matter how much land you are working with  If Devs are reading this review, this is one of the games problem :  - The AI are set to go to the closest airlock to get to an External location - so when you build an airlock on 1 side of your chained internal structures and you've built something on the other side, they will go through the closest airlock and sit there and die even if there is an airlock 5 meters away which has perfect access on the other side.  - The other problem is getting resources to your starport/airfield. I haven't worked out the cause of this bug yet, but when you get to a large sized base, the colonists liturally go for a random walk out into the middle of no where and pace back and forth until they die... and when they die, they drop their packages, and then more colonists go out and get stuck trying to pick up the packages.  Really hope you guys can get the issues sorted as i really do love the game

Great game, strongly recommend.

If you get this game in a bundle or for <$2, it's just about worth the 30-45 minutes it takes to play it. There are 12 scenarios, each of which have 5 mostly static scenes that you can rotate around in 3D and then put in sequential order. When you do, it shows them to you again in order and you go to the next. If you get it wrong (once I switched two scenes), it shows you which ones were right so you can fix the rest. That's it, you're done, back to the menu.  Someone went to a lot of trouble to make these cute 3D scenes in the Unreal engine. They are charming, and a few illustrate interesting potential stories that you can imagine going on, but two or three seem totally throw-away, such as one with harvesting on a farm and one with a goal in a soccer game.  It's kind of clever, but not really worth paying money for, and it's very easy and short with no replay value at all. Sorry.

The pacing of this game is one of its downfalls, you're pretty much always on high alert, which is a shame because so much more could have been explored in the world outside fighting. This game doesn't have the feel of a linear game, so it's a shame that's the way it plays out.  This game was a throughly enjoyable experience and while it doesn't charter into the unknown, outside of not being ambitious enough, it's has little to apologize for. Well worth the money, especially on a 75% off sale, when it's under $5.

I've only just started playing this game, but it seems pretty epic despite the fact that I'm trying to run it on an old i3 and a brand new Geforce 630. The graphics are beautiful, the charecters are wonderful, and the gameplay is simply a thing of art. It's sort of a mix of the classic cover system type duck and cover type and a traditional FPS' free-roaming shoot em' up action. It rewards you for taking out enemies with a M3 'take-down' silent knife kill, thus making you think more tactically instead of bumrushing with a dual Assualt Rifle (though that is really fun :D). The one complaint I have with this game so far is the scarcity of certain ammo types and the common-as-dirt others. My favorite weapon is the Pistol, but I haven't been able to main it due to the fact that light ammo is extremely rare this early on. On the other hand, I seem to be always maxed out on Assault Rifle ammo due to my compulsive looting of dead Nazis. I really, reallly, really, dislike using the Rifle, but I have to use it all the time just to remain ammo-solvent. I hope this gets better later on.

Shall we gather for whiskey and cigars tonight?  One of the best games ever made. Dishonored's unique world is built on top of a wonderful stealth/action hybrid that encourages you to experiment with the many different items/powers that you can acquire throughout the course of the game. You can go through the entire game without killing a soul, not even primary targets; or you could be a relentless killer and leave *literally* no witnesses; or you could find somewhere in between. This system gives Dishonored some of the highest replay value of any game ever made.   Play this game before Dishonored 2, not only for the story, but also because you'll be missing out on this amazing experience of you don't end up playing it.   TL;DR: Masterpiece.

I just finished the game, and I don't really know how to describe it. As a 'game' strictly speaking, it's minimal. As a story, though? It surpasses all other interactive stories I've ever read or played. The way the main characters, Johnny and River, are written is absolute perfection.  Emotionally, there are highs and lows, but that's the way life it. Even with it's RPG-esque graphics, this game to me is the perfect algorithm of real life. There isn't anything I didn't like about this game. Some of the mechanics need work, but that's minor, and it didn't affect my intake of the message at all. If you're thinking that you don't want to drop ten bucks on this, let me tell you: it's well worth it- and more. No game has ever hit me so emotionally. And I'm SO glad I purchased this game.

This is a very solid game, and the graphics are still very good for a 2007 game. The story is extremely engrossing, and morality is handled in an organic way, there is no 'good/evil points' system. The characters are all believable and interesting, and the game world feels alive with NPCs moving about. The game tells an engrossing story without dragging on for too long. My initial playthrough took around 40 hours, and I did a fair amount of side quests on my way through the game.   The parts of this game that some people might struggle with is the combat system and inventory management. Combat consists of selecting your sword (silver or steel) and combat style (heavy, fast, or group) and then clicking at the proper time to combo attacks on your enemies. I'm sure that some people will find this boring. (There are combat overhaul mods, but I did not use them, so I cannot vouch for how good/bad they are).  Inventory management is a chore in this game, but luckily there are not too many things that are vital to carry around. Also, quest items get their own inventory with unlimited space, and alchemical ingredients also get their own section. This helps alleviate the problem, but it can still be annoying.  Minor gripes aside, this is a very good game, and if you aren't put off by the combat system it is certainly worth playing.
