This game has a few issues. It doesn't start off on a good footing by making you sign up for some account that serves only to advertise another game before you start. The game itself is beautiful with a very dark, sinister atmosphere. The inventory system is not very good and the way of moving objects is useless. The puzzles are nigh on incompressible. The built in help system does nothing except either tell you to do things that you have already done or tell you things there are no help at all. I am not the best puzzle gamer, I will generally reach for a walkthrough more often than necessary but here quite early on I just gave up any attempt at solving the puzzles and followed a walkthrough in its entirety. There is a health meter but the game is really not that clear on what injures you so losing health just feels arbitrary. Through the game you collect intuitions and offers no explanation as to what they are but you need to work this out to progress to the final endings. All these things do not mean I did not enjoy this game, the mystery and the story kept me wanting to carry on. The multiple endings add a bit of replayability This game also comes with the original Anna so you can play that and realise that the terrible inventory, controls and incomprehensible puzzles are a significant improvement on the original. Completing certain endings on the original is dependent upon random events you have no control over happening which is annoying. So overall I am giving this a thumbs up, it is flawed but pull up a walkthrough and enjoy the atmosphere and story.

As you'll see I have quite a few hours in-game (many have far, far more). Quite simply, while there are a few flaws with this game, the raw scale and complexity far outweigh any negatives.   Planetside 2 effectively encompasses several different games in one massive combo. The largest (and biggest selling point) is that ridiculously large scale battles can be waged. You'll be on the ground, ducking behind trees as infantry trying to make it close enough to a base without getting sniped, when a giant tank will come rolling directly at you and instantly blow up from hitting a mine. At the same instant, one of the jet fighter aircraft (ESF in planetside lingo) will blow up right over your head. Basically, it's the best battle simulator I've ever run across.  That I feel is the main pull, but as I said it's a ton of different games in one. 1. Close quarters infantry wars similar to what most people are used to in FPS shooters. 2. A very robust air vs. air game. Fair warning to someone picking up the game, learning to dogfight in the ESF's can be very frustrating at first since you go up against people with sometimes thousands of hours in air. However, if you keep it up, it's very rewarding and fun. There are also troop carriers that allow you to fly in over a base for a quick drop to even the odds. 3. A very strong stealth game if that is your style. The infiltrator class has essentially a predator cloak that allows you to get behind enemy lines and wreak havoc. You can also use this class for sniping if that is your cup of tea. 4. Heavy armor wars, tank vs. tank. Every factions' tanks are just a little different, which helps you formulate your own strategy.  I could go on and on, but the main point is that there are so many different things going on in the game that it is hard to get bored. If you find one aspect unappealing, you just switch it up. Don't like getting mowed down as infantry? Get in a tank. Etc. Etc.  The one warning I will extend is that one of my favorite aspects will likely turn some people away. That aspect is the depth and time it takes to level your character and vehicles up. For this reason, it is a very difficult game to get into. There is much to learn, and even after hundreds of hours of play, you'll still be leveling up and unlocking items. Essentially, there will always be someone better than you. However, it is that depth that keeps a game fresh for years and years. That being said, as it's free to play, there isn't much reason not to give it a shot ;-).  I would just say, if it doesn't seem like your bag, stop playing it, come back in 2 days and give it another shot.   It's quite rewarding. *11/26/2016* edit Still on point!

8/10 Overview: Shoot Em Up  The Good:  + Simple, clean art style that fits the style of the game  + There is only one ship that you can customize between 3 body parts- Weapons, body and engine. There are 5-6 parts in each category depending on how many you unlock, giving you well over 100 different combinations. Each body part is unique and provides its own sets of strengths and weaknesses depending on your play style.   + Every body part you choose has 5 bonus objectives for you to complete giving you something to play for other than a high score. For example certain objectives may include 'kill 5 planes in one play session', 'destroy a battleship', 'kill a ship at max combo etc'.   + The game has the 'one more time' feeling. The game can be very addicting as you try to get new high scores or complete objectives  +Two difficulty modes to test your skills, normal and hard  +Solid Soundtrack  The Average:  The UI leaves something to be desired, it could have been a lot easier to navigate menu screens. Given the context of the game there really is no reason to have a UI that isn't simple and easy to navigate.   The game can be very short if you don't care about achievements or high score, getting some of the bonus objectives can be time consuming and not worth the hassle.   Conclusion:  I am not the biggest fan of Shoot 'em ups but this game is the perfect pick up and play arcade game to kill some time. Its highly addicting and trying out all the different ship parts to find the right combination that fits your play style is part of the fun.

I've played all of the Assassin's Creed Games. Well.. not all, I did miss out the handheld releases, but I've played through the entire main release and the entire storyline so far. I was very excited for Black Flag - because I assumed that what Ubisoft had done was to keep that core Assassins Creed gameplay.. and add Pirate ships. As it turns out, I was exactly right.   Assassin's Creed 4 Black Flag is a brilliant game. Not quite perfect maybe - but most certainly worth full price. Very interesting 'out of animus' storyline - great locations, extremely colourful, massively open world with some great characters, a very interesting main character with his own motivations. Combat is great, and although it does take some getting used to, and it is somewhat easy to die if you don’t know what you’re doing – it’s a fluid system that makes the game just as good – if not a little better as every Assassin’s Creed so far!  There's a great deal to love about this game. There are a few issues - for example free running isn't perfect. I've made Edward run in directions I certainly didn't mean him to - which thankfully don't happen too often. There are also some fairly frustrating missions - but are all perfectly completable, with a little effort.   In conclusion - the PC edition of Black Flag is fantastic. It looks beautiful, plays almost perfectly and has a huge amount of great content.

The good: The feel of the racing is great, not too arcadey or too simulation, just right for a casual racer like me. And the fact that AI now make mistakes is a very good feature. For ages I've wanted a game where the player isn't the only driver doing any overtaking or causing drama, because in real life every driver can make mistakes and Grid has finally managed to show that. The drivers actually pressure you in this game, where as in some games you feel like the AI dont challenge you enough. Lets be honest, there is not a lot of good touring car games out there, and this is probably the best of the touring car games.  The bad: It's WAY to predictable. In Grid 1, the ravenwest team was exactly the same, dominating every race and winning everything, and when Grid Autosport was announced, codemasters was happy to announce that ravenwest was back. The only thing i was thinking when they announced the return of ravenwest is 'Not good'. What makes racing exciting (for instance the BTCC) is competition. Anyone in the BTCC, ranging from matt neal, jason plato, andrew jordan, giovanardi, colin turking, and a lot more have the chance of winning races and being a potential champion. Grid Autosport however... has completely got that all wrong. I am soley a touring car driver, so i dont know About the other events, but in the touring discipline, you can pretty much gaurentee Nathan Mckane and his ravenwest teammate will come in 1st and 2nd, with the team kicker guys coming in 3rd and 4th. EVERY SINGLE RACE, you can pretty much predict where everyone is going to finish. The occasional switiching of positions, but thats rare to say the least. You feel like theres no point there being any other team, since ravenwest will ALWAYS... and i mean ALWAYS win the races. This bad feature alone has made me dislike this game A LOT. If the game was less predictable and ravenwest wasn't the only team capable of winning a race, this game would be much MUCH MUCH better. I also don't like the career mode. I never have in Code master games. They have improved it since Grid 1 since now i can specialise in touring cars, but theres no true progression in the game. Each season the teams are exactly the same. In games such as FIFA, each season is different as players transfer to different teams, new younger players are introduced, and old players retire. Each season is different. But with Grid Autosport (and likewise for games such as F1), the teams are always the same every season, and if you change to a different team, the other driver that was in that team just randomly disappears.. you'd think if i took one of the ravenwest guys positions, because they are 'So great' they'd change to a different team, but no...  Sidenote: I also think they should of added team creation again. I wanted to be competing in the touring cars championship with my own team, and my own selected team mate, like in Grid 1.  Despite not recommending this game yet, if they fix what I think is wrong with this game, I would recommend this game to ALL. I like that code masters have gone back to touring cars and by no means hate the disciplines in the game, just the predictablity.

What a great game, I loved it! The art style and the music are outstanding and combine to create a fantastic atmosphere. In more than 25 years of gaming, I think I have encountered few games with this much style.   The gameplay as such is good, but probably nothing you haven't seen before. What I like about the combat is that hitting monsters feels very 'meaty'. All in all, the gameplay is fairly minimalistic, but still very satisfying. The visuals are clean and relatively easy to read and input always felt responsive. Overall, I think I prefer HLD's combat to the more complicated ones of games like Bastion or Transistor, for instance.  I played the game on mouse and keyboard and that was fine. Dodging took some getting used to (gamepad is probably much better for that). On the other hand, aiming with mouse is probably superior.  It took me 10 hours to finish one walkthrough of the game (first half before the invincibility patch, second half after). The second half felt much easier to me, probably due to the patch. Either way, I think the level design is fair and the game isn't too frustrating. I definitely support the implementation of multiple difficulty levels, though.  Point of criticism: I didn't really get the ending, even though I was trying to pay attention. I generally like how the game explains very little, but the ending should get fleshed out a bit more.  Now on to unlocking the remaining weapons and outfits (however that is done, not sure yet)! ;-)

I should mention first that RTS games are the games I dislike the most. I really don't like the whole idea behind going down a memorized checklist for the best builds as quickly as possible. I just don't have the time or patience for that. That said, this game isn't bad so far. I've only played offline because of the above reasons and I don't think this game will change my mind on RTS as a genre. But, there is something about this game that is keeping me hooked where other major titles haven't (Starcraft, Warcraft, CoH, etc). Maybe it's the complete lack of combat or the unique focus on economics. Or that the skirmishes themselves are very short.   It probably helps to have some background knowledge of economics going in, but it's not required. The tutorials do a good job of explaining the basics. And, while I do find myself pausing a lot (and kinda wish there was a turn-based mode as a result), I haven't felt overwhelmed yet. And I appreciate the developers giving options for turning off the black market. If it does end up turning into the typical RTS “build order checklist”, that will be disappointing and I'll update my review accordingly. But, for now, I'd say it's worth the $13.59 price tag. The AI has been competent and the individual games, and approaches as a player, have been fairly unique.

As much as I'm enjoying the game, I wouldn't recommend this to my friends, especially with a $60 price tag. No Man's Sky is an interstellar safari adventure, nothing more.  Planets - the good: If you just enjoy exploring like me, you're going to have a good time at least for your first dozen or so planets. You'll learn new Gek words, blueprints that will help you adapt to harsher environments better, and maybe you'll find a few areas that look so cool, you just have to stop and take a screenshot. That is what this game does best, and if you are considering purchase, keep this in mind. That and the brilliant soundtrack to go along with your travels.  Planets - the bad: Although planets are often huge and proceduraly generated, most assets stay the same, therefore there is no point to exploring an entire planet. I spent about six hours just on my starter planet hoping to explore the whole thing, but the truth is there is no point. Once you discover the unique animals, rocks, and flora of that planet, it's usually safe to move on to the next one unless you want to mine or explore reused asset locations in hopes of finding a recipe you didn't already know. There is no reward or satisfaction in mapping an entire planet, and you aren't going to find anything unique like natural wonders. There are ruins, but even that is underwhelming due to how small they are.  World Building - On your journey, you'll learn more about the Gek race, sentinels, etc. What you won't learn much of is the history of the planets you come across. This is the negative to procedural generation. Nothing has any real history, and structures more or less have the same design and architecture. You'd think ideas would have been different planet to planet, but you'd be wrong. Even if the game had colossal city ruins (which ~20 hours played, I haven't found any yet), there wouldn't be any journals or records to show who the inhabitants were, their culture, religion, etc. Sadly, world building just doesn't exist in this game other than allowing you to rename the creatures, flora, planets, and systems that you discover. You don't create anything, you just upgrade and move on.  Despite all the negatives provided, I need to make it clear that I am enjoying No Man's Sky. Despite the terrible PC launch and broken promises, this is a solid game, especially considering it was developed by 11 people. Is it worth buying if you have that special personality type like me? Only if you have money to blow. You're most likely better off waiting for the price to drop to $40, or lower.

Probably the most immersive space simulation game to-date. Stellaris can be thought of as grand space opera RPG, and your civilization is the main character. The Pros and Cons of this game I'm sure have been covered in other reviews. Here are a list of specific nuances that make Stellaris unique compared to other 4x and enhance my own experience with the game:  - Each population is treated as a discrete unit, rather than a number. Each unit has their own set of morals and values, wants and needs. They can immigrate and emigrate based on socio-ecological and environmental preferences (yes, a mini Schelling simulation). Their morals and values can deviate over time. When they reproduce, they inherit the genetic traits of the parent population. This causes them to react differently to your decisions. Unhappiness isn't just a number-- they can actually form their own sub factions within your empire, have their own rebel leader, demand independence, etc. You can even mix in alien pops into your worlds (if they love the conditions) or send your pops to alien worlds.  - Alliances actually mean something. Your computer allies will rally their fleet and fly in formation with you, instead of blindingly toss their units at the opposing side. There are inter-alliance politics as well as intra-alliance. When you declare war against an enemy with allies at your side you can negotiate surrender terms and who gets what piece of the pie.  - Your leaders grow old and die. If you have a democract government. they're elected from the pool.  - Primitive civilizations-- Such a nice touch! There are worlds which contain civilizations at various stages of development. And you can choose whether to take the Star Trek(TM) prime directive approach to studying them, abduct them aggressively, or help them into the space age. All of which have consequences and profoundly impacts the development of the civilization. There are also proto-aliens that are on the brink of sentience, and you can uplift them with the right technology.  - Ship design process and UI are both super accessible, and it feels like you're designing a ship EVE Online style. Slapping on rail guns, balancing out energy grids, putting special systems on board. Shall I tank with shields or armor. Decisions decisions.  - All three types of FTL paradigms in a game. No more being confined to a network of starlanes like most space games these days. You can warp around the universe like the MOO2 days of yesteryear, or you can jump to any system in range of your wormhole generator (my favorite, because it feels like a BSG jump drive)  - An actual endgame that can change the narrative of your grand space saga. Extra dimensional beings can invade your universe. A civilization that treats and oppresses its robot people can spark a robot AI sentience rebellion that can infect all robots in the galaxy, and igniting a huge organics vs synthetic war that will turn old enemies into allies. There are also Fallen Empires which are insanely powerful empires that you can choose to appease or have an ultimate plan of conquest over.  - In other games, science is merely a number... its sole purpose is to get your technologies and becomes useless after you maxed out the tree. In this game, science drives the spirit of advancement, allowing you to advance the missions/story quests. It has a symbiotic relationship with the spirit of exploration, interacting with advanced alien lifeforms, even salvaging the aftermath of a huge space battle.  This game actually feels like a space-empire-simulation... rather than a space-empire-board-game. A good direction for the genre. Highly recommended.

The story is very well written and absolutely compelling, if you like oldschool horror/mistery writers there's a 100% chance you'll love this game. The gameplay is simple, but it totally works in its context. I have to admit I didn't expect The Last Door to be THIS good, it is hands down the biggest surprise of my 2014. This is a simple but very enjoyable tactical action(ish) game. Taking the role of an officer of a standard unit, you decide the best way to arrange and utilize your forces in a series of skirmishes. Added to this plain setup is your own skill in determining limited placements in real time unit movement. This is something that would liven up any sort of strategy game rather than being rather cleverly disguised hex movements.  The art and design of the game is very pleasing, even to non-anime fans. There are anime tropes involved, but being a Japanese production of late that can't be helped. Even so, it's story is rather well thought out even with the more mystical elements in this rather subdued tale of kinda/sorta Dieselpunk action.  I reccomend everyone try this one, and hop that they can port the sequels easily... even though they are on the PSP.

Gemcraft is one of the most successful tower defense series for a reason. This game is all about options, because with a little experience you can earn mutators to be set pre mission to increase the difficulty and the XP earned. Since you only gain XP over what you did previously on repeat runs, grinding isn't really grinding, it's raising the bar for a second run. You can also pour your power (through gems,) into the oncoming monster waves, to toughen them and earn you more XP.  You also can find drops of talismans for more upgrades, and these can be improved.   If you like tower defense games, this is a no brainer, up there with Defense Grid and Defender's Quest.
Great balance of creativity/new ideas and the core elements of tower defense.

MUH DRILLZ!  I'll get straight to the point: this game will definitely drill its way into your heart. *pause for laughter or groans*  *ahem* now that I have you either groaning from the pun or laughing your ♥♥♥ off, I'll start the real review.  This game comes to steam years later from the initial release in japan back in 2004, which got ported to PSP in June of 2006, made a north american appearance in 2007, and got an original PC release worldwide on steam. From my point of view, everything about this game is great, with just a few minor complaints I have, which I will get to in this review.  Graphics: 9/10 - The graphics remind me of early playstation games such as Brave Fencer Musashi. They get the job done, vibrant, and great use of color. You can also tell it makes great use of cell-shading, but the characters do tend to look a little too simplistic. Overall, however, this game looks great.  Gameplay: 9.5/10 - The game plays very well, and that's as much as I can say about it right now as I've only just started playing this game. I will definitely update when the time comes, but one minor complaint: so far, I haven't really run into any boss fights. EDIT: Just ran into my first boss fight, and lemmie tell ya, they can really drill it to ya -pause again for laughs or groans-. They're challenging, but fair. It at least gives you time to use healing items and not have them clobber you as you do so. Overall, the boss fights are great and vibrant.  Controls: 8/10 - Here's where I have to make a few complaints about controls here. Some of the moves aren't very clear on what to do, especially if you're using a gamepad (or xbox 360/XBOne controller) for control. The instructions are strictly PC oriented with no mention of what to do with the gamepad. I would complain about it more, if it weren't for the fact that you learn pretty quickly what buttons do what move, so it's no big issue and the controls can be customized*.  However, the controls do the job well, either way. The jump controls are interesting, but can get a little frustrating, especially having to make platform jumps. Fortunately, the shadow effects will help you, and you can angle the camera any way you please (right stick for xbox 360 controllers). The inclusion of timed hits to the beat of the music with the beat bar displayed on screen is a wonderful addition. If you've mastered Crypt of the Necrodancer, you'll definitely find that getting critical hits via the beat of the music to be very easy and fun.  Sound: 9/10 - The music, even though you can tell it's synthesizer, is VERY well done. The english voice acting is a bit cheesy, but works anyways. The music is upbeat and well composed, in my opinion. You'll need to get that tempo down if you want to do critical hit damage, but the timing of each beat is precise and works very well.  Overall: 9.25/10 - This game is definitely a title for platform gamers to really dig into -pause for groans or laughter-. Hopefully, we'll see some patches released to fix some of the issues, but overall, I recommend this game for anyone who loves a good platform game.   *At least the options to do that are up, but I've seen some forum posts about the game not running because options got changed. Hopefully, this gets patched soon, as I did suffer the same thing when I tried to change around a few options. Should you find your game not starting up, just hit the restore defaults button on the windows menu screen that comes up and it'll start normally again. I have successfully changed a few options after having do to this, but kept it to just force feedback and maybe one other thing.

Incredibly addictive. An immense waste of time. But what can you do? This game has all the properties of the classic browser game Cookie Clicker, but with a more financially influenced theme.   The game features a unique method of invigorating the gameplay by resetting parts of the progression for more investors. This keeps the game going for much longer than you could imagine, even though the premise of the game stays the same.  It's a game you can play over many days, weeks, months and years, even, as it continues to run when the game isn't actually running on your computer. That allows you to return to the game after a few days or weeks, to find an immense wealth accumulated within the game. It makes it interesting to return to after being away for a while.  Overall: an enjoyable waste of time and clicks.

Should you buy this game? ABSOLUTELY.  TLDR: Great weapon upgrades, great enemies / variety of enemies, RNG is well balanced, key bindings / HUD / Menu are fantastic and clean, replayability is incredibly nice, graphics are fantastic, game runs 100% fine even on crappy laptops, 0 space consumption on the download, NO frustrating elements to the game WHATSOEVER (not even on death), music is wonderful, perfect pick up and everything you'd expect you'd be buying and more for these genres!   ______________________  This game is phenomenal! EVERYONE should have this if they're a fan of these types of bullet hell space games. The content is great, the replayability is great, the graphics are fantastic, the characters / ships aren't annoying or overly done, the weapons are very unique (Huge jaw that can bite enemies? SWEET!), and much more. As someone who is rather picky with my purchaces and tests games out on an old crappy laptop BEFORE using them on my good one, I can say I'm very satisfied with this game. It runs great on any old laptop and especially the newer ones. The only thing that's a slight, slight pain is that bosses and certain enemies can have REALLY fast attacks that are incredibly hard to dodge without prior knowledge of what they do and how their patterns work, so it can be tough to play through on your first couple runs. Although, that's to be expected. That being said, it's NOT overly difficult to learn, and there's DEFINITELY not a super, super steep / frustrating learning curve. It's done just right.   It's everything you'd expect you'd be getting and more. What's especially nice is the crazy support for key bindings (mouse friendly) and controllers! More game companies need to get in this habbit. It's so refreshing not to find myself frustrated with annoying controls or lack of controller usage.

What can I say about Wurm Unlimited? I`ve only played 179 hours (at the time of writing this so I`m not that far in the game jet, but wow, this game got some serious depth to it. The graphics look good, but both the crafting system and the UI is something to get used to for sure, but once you do get used to it there is nothing to complain about. And whatever you do, whatever project you start it will feel slow as heck in the beginning but it WILL get faster and better as you level up your skills, and when you finally finish the project, big or small you will feel rewarded for all that hard labour.  My plus & minuses:  + Nice graphics. + Deep crafting and skill system. + Great community (best I`ve ever experienced) (server). + thousands of things to craft and even more to do. + And so much more!  - Animations are getting old. - Can be a bit repetitive. - UI looks to be from the 80`s. I played SpaceChem in the past and enjoyed it thoroughly. For that reason alone I was willing to buy TIS-100 sight unseen. I have to say that once again I am not disappointed by a Zachtronics game.  No programming skill is needed to play, but you will need to reference the manual quite a bit for the first few puzzles. Like most of the reviewers here, I would highly recommend printing out the manual rather than referencing the pdf constantly. You will find yourself learning the commands by the second or third puzzle anyway. From then on solving the puzzles really boils down to how much time you are willing to commit to making the 'wrong output' beeps go away.  TIS-100 is not always easy, but if you are willing to commit a little time and brainpower it is very rewarding to watch your program complete its cycles perfectly. For anyone enough on the fence to be reading this review in its entirety, buy this game. You are the kind of person who would enjoy it.

If you've ever played Journey on PS3/PS4, you'll know what to expect going into this game. It's short, it's stunningly beautiful, and the gameplay is simple and meditative.  Exploring these underwater seascapes never becomes frustrating or boring thanks to subtle environmental cues, which highlight points of interest and various objectives in creative ways; like using contrasting color pallettes to guide you in the right direction, or showing schools of fish travelling towards a mysterious cave, or swarming around a magical pool that leads you to your next destination. It's a natural and organic feeling of progression, and I wouldn't be surprised if ABZU was used to demonstrate these videogame design methods in an actual classroom.  Did I mention how ♥♥♥♥ing beautiful this game looks?   Playing this game becomes a near-religious experience thanks to gorgeous, fantastic, stunning, not-enough-synonyms-to-describe visuals, and you'll be tempted to screencap everything in sight. It's eye candy on a massive scale, and I won't soon forget how awe-struck I felt while taking it all in. You'll definitely want to show this game to friends and family who can appreciate just how jaw-droppingly beautiful it is, not only as a videogame, but as a true work of art.  The game's length is probably its weakest asset, clocking in at just under a couple of hours for your standard playthrough.   Twenty dollars might be somewhat steep, but I would argue that this game's value shouldn't be judged on length or replay incentives, but on the solid foundation of it's stellar art design, setpieces, environmental design, and pacing. It's not often that a game manages to nail every one of these aspects, and further padding the game with meaningless objectives would've only lessened it's overall impact.  ABZU won't satisfy players who are looking for more involved or detail-oriented gameplay, but almost anyone will be able appreciate the game for what it delivers artistically (in spades) through it's focused, direct approach to creating an unforgettable journey with a minimal degree of interaction. This is an absolute must-play for PC gamers, and I can't recommend it highly enough.

All in all, I would say get this game when it's on a discount and I don't think you'd be disappointed.

First of all, I haven't played this game very much on Steam, but I've played for hours and hours on my iPod and Xbox, so I know what I'm talking about. This is spectacular game. As the dark lord of insanity, you must save the world so that you can destroy it. It's a hilarious premise, as the bad guy must become the good guy so he can get his powers back to destroy the world. Everything about this game is a parody, right down to the enemies' descriptions and the assorted dialogues. If you exect this game to take itself seriously at any point ever, you've completely missed the point.   Some reviewers have talked about this game being boring or too easy. I've found that this is not the case. The characters and story are funny and I always want to keep playing to find out what will happen next. I'm playing on normal difficulty, and the battles are somewhat challenging but not overly so, which is exactly what I wanted since I play a lot of hard games (Doom, Prototype, etc etc) and needed a game to just relax with, but it also has two higher difficulty levels if you need a more challenging game like the old-school RPGs.   Basically, if you're looking at the screenshots and description and it sounds like something you might like, you should definitely try it. It's a great game, especially if you can get it on sale (like right now).   --------------------------------------------------------------------  If you enjoy my reviews, why not follow my curation page? I always liked the gameplay of roguelikes but was never really into the typical fantasy setting they were framed in. This, though, this is neat. A space roguelike. Over half a dozen ship types all suited to different playstyles, randomized galaxies so no two playthroughs are ever the same, and a difficulty very much in-tune with the genre it comes from -- it's quite easy to get killed misjudging a situation or taking too big a risk. Travel from encounter to encounter is turn-based (the enemy fleet pursuing you only advances when you choose to warp to the next system), but combat and ship operations are real-time. To avoid being overwhelmed by the concerns of surviving a fight in space, the pause feature (Spacebar) allows you to queue commands to execute when you unpause. It's a fairly small game which won't offer you 8-hour playthroughs or awesome graphics, but what content it does have is well worth the price tag.

You're going to want to get this one for the story. The cyberpunk/fantasy mix has some pretty cool elelments to bring to the gameplay, but it's difficult to do anything really new to the concept of a tactical RPG. The combat sequences do work perfectly well and are every bit as fun as say, X-com. Still, the story for the initial campaign is AMAZING. It combines so many classic concepts in a such an interesting way that it becomes something which feels unique. The open ended skill tree is also great for playstyle customization. Perhaps one of the most brilliant moves is the utter lack of voice acting. Given the extensive dialogue this game could have been utterly ruined by just a little bit of bad voice acting. Loading screens aren't a problem as they take the opportunity to advance the plot via text while they load the next map, you won't even notice you were loading at all. It even provides a decent challenge if you aren't careful in battle or don't have a very combat capable team with you. I can't think of any reason why you wouldn't want to play this game. Buy it!

An incredibly clever twist on the rougelite and rhythm genres, mixed in a really unique way that's very satisfying to learn and master. The soundtrack is incredible thanks to Danny B being at the top of his usual game let alone the excellent bonus remixed soundtracks that are also playable. There's lots of little unlockables and secrets to keep you coming back and feeling consistently rewarded and there's even an active modding scene adding more to the game every day. It's a great all-around package.  It's not perfect though. The game is hard. VERY hard. Bordering on frustrating due to how quickly you can die and how unfair it can seem a lot of the time. This is a game that requires practice, dedication, and constant repetition to win even as the default character, let alone any of the optional extra characters.  There's also a serious lack of consistency in tone. The game works off this really fun and playful concept and this crazy high energy soundtrack but between major areas you're given one of the least self-aware and least fun stories I've seen in a game. It's not even a particularly interesting story and it really feels out of place. It's a minor thing but it definitely drags down what is otherwise a great experience.  I like the game a lot, but it's definitely not for everyone. Casual players are going to get burned by it hard.

I am reviewing this as someone who has never played a game in the Wargame series before. And well I can tell you, buying this franchise now, when it has so much content is a fantastic experience, as everything is new to me.  At first the game seems a little overwhelming, and you wonder how you are going to be able to concentrate on so many elements, and make tacticaly pushes at the same time. But after playing a few rounds of the game, and getting a couple of hours under your belt it becomes an addictive fever of how you can improve your army choice and their tactics of use. In this game you can create completely custom armys, with a choice of over 750 units per side, so thats over 1500 units in total. And you can put a combination of any of those 750 in to your army you want, or you can use a specific nation only, and if you want a specific role, airbourne, marine, mechanized, armoured, just some examples, and with these you will get bonuses, allowing you to field more units, not less, in your specialisations!  I have only really played the British and USSR in any detail yet, although I have also created North Korean and Anzac (Australia & New Zeland) armys also. And well I can tell you, the choice of units you get, first seems overwhelming, but don't worry, pick something, try it out, after a game or two you will go, hang on, nope i prefer a longer range artillery type, or possibly you feel your vehicle anti air vehicles are to vunerable to being spotted and prefer to field infantry anti air in future. And this progression and refining is continual.  I find 4v4 games enjoyable, but I find the 10 vs 10 games exciting, and thrilling, as ten people try to push and defend a line, and support one another, it feels a lot more unpredictable, and evolving and in the end realistic then any other RTS I have played. If you watched back many of the 10 vs 10 battles on video you would struggle sometimes to realise whos units are whos, and that it wasn't a real historical battle, the way lines are formed, holes found and pushed in the enemy lines, and weak spots reinforced in your own line.  The only problem I have found with the game is when your team starts loosing, a lot of people will be quick to drop out of the game, which is rediculous as many times I have found you can fight back from a loosing position if you work together. I think there should be some form of punishing the statistics of those who quit out early. Okay the players resource points is shared out between players, but sometimes a team is only just slightly loosing, because they lost their harbour, and half of their team will quit, meaning micro management just got a lot harder! And even with half the team quit you got to give the guys sticking to it credit though, they fight on to the last fire base, and hold every inch of ground.  I vow to be someone who never quits out of a game early because I am loosing. Its all about having good fun, gameplay, and in the finish, last stands can be great fun. You can hold them off to the end of the timer, it will be a draw often.  Do not be put off by the lack of VOIP (Voice chat) in game, you will find the system, where you can type short messages and ping it, smoke tag a spot of ground, with the writing much more useful. You can quickly type 'Enemy AA' or 'Enemy Arty' and tag it on the enemy position. Or tag where you will capture, or what troops you are pushing on to a spot very quickly. And this is better then struggling to describe something on these huge battlefields some times.  So in short, a great Real Time Stratergy game, if you haven't tryed this series of games yet and enjoy RTS games, I would say it is a must buy. A great experience, and a new level of large scale RTS. What are you waiting for, buy it!

Hard to give a bad review, though it's hard to give a good one too.  Game Guru is not for pro nor total amateur. There're scripts and if you don't use them, you'll do nothing. And if you don't modify them, you'll do the same as the other GG users.   Modify a sentence, modify a sound, or rewrite the whole script or a part of it, or BUY scripts on the store. That's what I did. Plus the ones you find for free in the forums. you'll also need entities (3d model). Again: your own, GG store or the same everyone owns.   Pros:  - Easy game maker (you don't have to know how to write a script) - If you're creative, you'll have possibilities. - If you know scripting, you'll have possibilities. - If you love simple things at the moment, that's what you need.   Cons: - For an easy game maker, it's not that easy at first because tutorials only shows the basics for now. - It's not that easy when you don't know what a script is. - Lots of bugs. Unstable (the map is huge, but if you want to make a street alive, your CPU will suffer. - Not worth the price I paid (not the steam 20$, but a 100$ when it was called FPS creator reloaded)  - Careful what you wish for  BUT: - As an early access, there will be updates. Improvements. New scripts, new this and that. - Let's hope for more tutorials. - I don't know scripting, I learned just a micro-bit, but I have creativity and I'm trying to concentrate on the story and the music/ambiance/voices.  An advice: use the forums. Don't stay alone in front of your computer, Without the forums I wouldn't have done much.  Watch videos, try other's games, make yourself an idea of the product with the forums (Steam and GG) then you'll know what to do.   My review will make you think, potential buyer! Bother me if you want to know more.

I've been looking for this game for years. I played it about 10 years ago when JoWood first released it. It also came with an editor in which you could build a multitude of different 'unique' buildings requiring different materials. However this isn't included with the Steam Version.   The Campaign missions for the US and Europe are the same as the original game. I haven't played the Australian missions before this release, so that's all new to me, however i know they were also released as an add-on a couple of years after the original game came out (I bought the add-ons but my crappy computer at the time wouldn't even recognise the discs).  Pros for the game is it's diversity of starting points. If you start in 1850, it's with horse and cart, simple ships, and a 'horse train' options. As you start in later years, more updated modes of transportation become available. Road quality is just a gravel road in the 1850's, but can improve to Autobahn standard later in the game. An endless number of maps can be created depending on what type of game you want to play, from a mountainous continent to a small island archipeligo and everything in between.   I love the view of the game. There are plenty of in-game options eg if trees confuse the view, or the names listed are in the way, then you can simply remove from your view by clicking on the appropriate button on the bottom of the screen. Discrete but effective. I always remove the trees, they dont really serve a purpose in the game except to clutter the screen. The mini-map is movable or can be removed from view is you require. I personally leave it in the upper right corner as it becomes a quick guide to locations.  The only part of the game that bugs me is the train connection system. If you start a line, then future additional railway lines additions MUST come from the lines already placed. There is no other way to create a new railway line. If you want to create a line on an separate part of the map, then it must join with an existing line before it can be built. Many a time I have wanted to create a railway line far away form my original line, but have had to use other forms of transport, or spend a lot of money on getting a virually useless line to my new setup.  I haven't had any screen issues as what may potentially happen as per the game page. The game seems to run as it did many years ago.
Overall a pretty good game.

I've tested Alpha and Beta builds of games and this is far more developed than your average 'Early Access' title. It needs some refinement and adjustments for balance and such... but overall, a title worth picking up for those who like Deep Strategy/Campaign style games of galactic conquest. And the soundtrack... worth the extra $10! :steamhappy:  Does it need work? Yes. Will it take 10+ years to polish out those updates for a full-fledged gaming experience? No. Expect some DLC in the near-future to add more content and depth to the game.

The game is very good, I find it fun and enjoyable without it being overly difficult, I like that Iam given a choice with the difficulty lvl, adventurer the normal difficulty is perfect for me, though it gives the option of a harder difficulty or easy for those who just want to explore and not have to fight anything. There's a few things that could be added or improved, such as even with a roof on your building it rains inside your house. It should not rain inside buildings, that is just strange. I see buildings that are in the game, built by the military or scientists have fences around them, but there is no option to be able to build fences yourself. This is the first time I've ever requested a refund for a game I've purchased. Ever. I just received an eMail confirming that my refund will be honored, and I realized that for the first time since dealing with this game I felt satisifed about it.  I made my purchase after watch videos and listening to the developers talk about the game, it sounded promising and I went into it with the understanding that this would be a game with D&D elements but that it was very much *not* a digital adaptation of the pen and paper game itself.  So, even with all my research and knowledge ahead of time, it was even more disheartening when I jumped into my first headstart weekend and felt buyers remorse. I gave the game several chances, about 8 hours or so worth during headstart weekends and then gave up.  When the game went live I decided it deserved another shot, so I loaded it up and tried it out - continued disappointment.   * The polish simply isn't there. Many aspects of this game make it feel like an incredibly early, Alpha version of the product; from controls to graphics this doesn't feel like a released game. * Pacing feels extremely clunky. This is an important aspect that a game like Pillars of Eternity really nailed. Sword Coast Legends is all over the place with its pacing, going from fast paced to full stop in seconds. * UI/UX feels really clunky. Everything from the nested menu selections to the options themselves feels very incomplete and not at all intuitive. * Dungeon/World Builder feels underdelivered. I was expecting something more robust and clean, what I received was a pale imitation of a world builder that could provide only the most basic of levels for my players. * Connectivity issues abound. Playing with friends was a huge chore, getting into a game was very frustrating and people were dropped from the game all the time. We tried as many kinds of hosting methods as we could and nothing seemed to work; from what I read a lot of folks had this issue.   There are a plethora of general design decisions I strongly disagree with on a personal level, but don't need to go into all that. One thing I feel had real promise was the random dungeon system where you could get a group of friends (in theory) and run through a randomly generated dungeon; cool idea but again, poor execution.  I've never gotten a refund for a game in my 15 years of gaming, I'm truly sad that this is the game that finally made me do it.

Calling this a point and click adventure is stretching it a little bit. It is more like a choose your own adventure book. Theres very little interaction, your character is just drawn as part of the background. The writing is fan-fiction quality, maybe its better in german. The 'gameplay' consists of clicking on 3-5 items in each room, which are drawn as part of the background, and choosing whether to look at them (which gives a 1 line description of the object), pick them up (or move to another room), or use an item. Theres no options menu, which is annoying because the game is insanely loud and only runs in fullscreen borderless, and there are no resolution options either.  The only well thought out feature is the 'cutscenes', the way theyre presented in comic book style is kind of neat. Too bad theres no game around those.  I wish i could return this, because I dont support the idea of interactive stories being sold as games. It would have taken bare minimum effort to turn this into an actual point and click adventure with the drag-and-drop game making tools available today.

DEADBOLT  is a stealth-action, 2D, third-person shooter that invigorates the player through fast-paced, deadly firefights, near-missing bullets, strategy, guile, and Super Meatboy-esque trial and error. The gameplay is just addicting. Definitely worth your money.     BENEFITS   DEADBOLT is a very solid game, having the simple gameplay mechanics of  kill him before he kills you , with the exception of putting you in a room of 10-15 enemies. Expect to die multiple times in one level, as the degree of difficulty can range from  incredibly easy  to  incredibly insane . That's what makes it so attractive. The feeling of victory after accomplishing a level that you've poured nearly 30 minutes into is next to gold. The controls themselves are also very simple, with the usual, 'WASD to move,' 'SPACE to jump,' and 'MOUSE to shoot/aim.' The gameplay is product of easily-to-learn mechanics, allowing the player to spend more time playing the game rather than trying to figure it out.    DRAWBACKS   The major flaw holding this game back is the level design. As you progress through the game, you are given the same features every level: doors to shoot through, vents to hide in, weapons to pickup, and guys to kill. This blandness caused me to quit out several times, as the repetitivitity (combined with the difficulty) was just too much to bear. Granted, I did come back, but I feel as if they developers included broader, longer levels, then DEADBOLT would be a lot better.    In conclusion, DEADBOLT is still a very cool, fun way to satisfy your gaming itch for the day.

Complex, fun, strategic game. This is a game that all RTS gamers MUST have in their library.  EDIT (7/22/2014)  My comment (above) still stands. This game, even two years of playing, is still fun. The community is friendly, large, and very active along with the developers constantly at work to fix bugs and add in much more content.  The game to most new players may seem extremely difficult at first glance and start and is not your occasional 'Train, Drag, Click, Murder'. AIs will commit invasions every 5 minutes and early games at times while you only have such a handful of ships. However, once the user begins to learn, it'll begin to the challenge the mind in attempts to strategize and organize the fleet used for offense and defense. Players can choose to micromanage every single ship in their fleet or macromanage. Both work and don't work depending on the situation at hand.  Eventually some will get bored of a strategy game as the AIs become more predictable but no, not AI War. There are more than a dozen AI types, each in which provides a new challenge and way of thinking to face against the ever unpredictable AIs.  Now this game does have DLCs. Most may think that it's expensive and not worth it. Wrong, every DLC throws in a large amount of content and provides much more !!FUN!! to the game, such as ships, new factions, and new AIs. These ships can be used against you or the other way around. So yes, I bought all the DLCs, but one, and all of them are completely worth it. Oh, and DLCs cost no more than $5 USD.  In conclusion, the game is difficult to start at first with the ruthless AIs, but however, what makes strategy games fun is the difficulty and the thinking it takes to succeed and win.  If you love RTS games along with sci-fi, this is the game.

[ Fun: 6 | Plot: 6 | CTRL: 6 | GFX: 6 | SFX: 7 | Length : 9h ] [ Total: 6 ]  + Decent 'Point and Click' mechanics + Fun linking cut-scenes and presentation + Good attempt at a compelling plot and characters + How many games have their own original songs!  ~ Basic talk and hunt & combine puzzles ~ Odd tone with comical characters, murder, possession, dismemberment anmd drag queens ~ Some disjointed plot points that make little sense ~ Some animations are oddly disconcerting  - Lots of puzzles bottleneck, require repeat actions - Awkwardly hidden object hunts - Last two acts are heavily padded  A solid first entry in this trilogy that never quiet reaches the greatness of the golden Point n Click era.

Note: Play the games in this order  Bioshock 1 > Bioshock 2 > Bioshock Infinite   The year is 1912 and you play as Booker DeWitt and you've been tasked with saving a girl, Elizabeth , in the city of Columbia.   The game is an Action FPS that is true to its genre. The game offers a fairly fast paced action that might sometimes feel a bit tedious. Bioshock Infinite contains a multitude of weapons that are era specific and are lovely to use.  You also have access to powers, known as vigors, which you can use to dispose of enemies or to just help you make your way through the game. The vigors can also be combined with each other making it an intriguing system to play around with.  In addition to Weapons and Vigors you have other tools, like the skyhook, which lets you jump around from hooks attached to the side off certain buildings.  The stealth system within the game is good. The detection is based around both sound and vision so walking on a carpet can suppress your footsteps while walking on puddles of water or broken glass will make them louder.  The games scenery and enviroments are absolutely outstanding. When I got the first glimpse of Columbia I was speechless.   The voice acting in the game is great as well with excellent dialogue. I'm quite serious now, I can't describe how PERFECT some parts were with how the characters acted and what they said. This game has brought forward some amazing quotes. In addition to that, all of the characters are well made and very sympathetic.   I like games that have a good story, and Bioshock Infinite has just that. The story is incredibly rich when it comes to content and will leave you with memorable parts that will make you smile when you think back.  Bioshock Infinite is a game that you will not regret playing. It's fantastic.  edit - removed Chrono order to please people.

Quoting from my blog post review on 3/3/2007 (which, FWIW, was probably before the 'remastered' edition was released):  Not bad at all. First of all: It's a traditional adventure game (albeit with a 3D, 'Gabriel Knight 3'-esque interface, rather than 2D point-and-click) featuring Sherlock Holmes investigating a global conspiracy of Cthulhu cultists. How could I _not_ play this game? Better yet: Despite being essentially a _Cthulhu by Gaslight_ adventure, it also remains true to the authentic Holmes stories by _not_ including anything that is unequivocally supernatural. The story ultimately leaves it ambiguous whether the cultists' activities would actually have awakened Cthulhu and destroyed the world, or if they were merely insane. Furthermore, this game is blessedly free of anything like the obnoxious timed stealth puzzle that intruded into the previous game in the series. The graphics are at times breathtaking, with lovely animated reflective water, bump-mapped surfaces, lovingly modeled gory, dismembered victims... Voice acting is consistently above-average for computer game voice work. I didn't encounter any game-crashing glitches. All in all, a solid game for adventure fans, Holmes fans, and Cthulhu fans.  However, I will say, 'The Awakened' is sort of an uninspired title. I sort of prefer the original French title, _La Nuit des Sacrifiés_. Also, in a peculiar affectation, many of the characters in the game appear to be named after various personalities from the early history of pen-and-paper RPGs: There's a Dr. Gygax, a man named Arneson, and a couple of books authored by E. Otus and D. Niles, although they seem to have left out the obvious Petersen.

DoW 40,000 RTS gameplay has been refined to a much more appealing formula in DoW II. You don't build a base anymore; the focus is on controlling your heroes, your squads, and capturing important points on the map while killing your opponents. The sounds are especially good; the graphics are great (but not the best compared to today's games). It has the most popular races - Space Marines, Orks, Eldar and the Tyranids. Each race has a unique strategy to it. The Campaign is a blast, replayable, also because you can play it in co-op with a friend as well! Relic is wisely not letting go of this title, there are always some fixes and patches, new maps, and also The Last Stand, a fun multiplayer co-op mode has become a part of it. Multiplayer is great and nicely balanced. If you like RTS games, you need to try this.

Its not often a rare gem like this comes to my attension. Thanks to a good friend who unfortunatly doesn't want to experince every bit of this game like I do due to his perefence in difficulty. Call of Juarez is a Western FPS, Single player and come with Campain (Story mode), Arcade and Quickdraw Duels  Arcade: In this mode you try to go for the highest score while choosing a certain class of weapon. (Gunslinger) Double Quickshooters. Ranger (1 Revolver and a Rifle) and Trapper (Shotgun, Revolver). Your goal is to be a combo master-god and to (or at least attempt) to keep a combo going for as long as possible (perferably start to end of the level for the bigest score multiplyier) All the while trying your best to survive the onlast of of enimies your running into to keep you combo up (Unless in a situation where they are comming to you).  Each class in this mode also have a set of skills that you unlock over time ascoiated with them that will help you imensivly as time goes on. The only problem with the system is the trapper class due the sole reason 'I CANNOT FIGURE OUT HOW TO KEEP A COMBO WITH A 2 SHOT SLOW REALOADING SHOTGUN' but all that aside with shotgun being my most look down upon weapon in the game.  The other 2 clases are built really well in comparison. Gunslinger is really good at high populated areas where you need to take them out as fast at possible without dieing. and the Ranger is good for when the range of the enimies is to great to take on with a revolver without risking losing your combo taking time to go in closer. Oh noteworthy tip to note, Rifle headshots are worth twice in points compare to revolver/quickshooter headshots.  Duels: This mode was desing to forcus the boss battles in the game is a western like fashion. In other words, its a quickdraw tourniment where the slowest lose. It takes awhile to master since you need to do 3 main things. Line up your target with a very (on purpose) wibbly wobby crosshair that gets smaller more your forcus in. Make sure your hand speed is at the highest by having a balencing minigame est with your hand slowly moving towards your gun to quicken your pull out speed. and lastly if you want to play morally and get achivements like the cools cats do. YOU NEED TO BE THE 2ND ONE TO PULL OUT OF HIS HOLSTER. Unlike the arcade mode, the duels are done all in one sitting giving you 6~ lives to get trough all ## bosses (I'm leaving the number a mystery on purpose). If you go for the honrable kills on all the bosses in the game, out drawing them on every turn and also surving the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t they throw at you (you get to find out as it comes). For me at least, it was one of the most statisfying feeling I had in video games in a long time, To survive and beat all the bosses while all the being honorabled killed. PS: The Duels can be a ♥♥♥♥♥ at 1st but you'll get used to it eventauly  Story/Campain This part of the game is the ture meat of it, the game is a mix of both Arcade and Duels but in a more casual fashion (where you don't need to grind out and just have fun). The vidoe game events is being narated by a old bounty hunter in a pub telling the floaks inside about his life-story. Due to this the game is very linear but you do get moments where (Wait, it din't happen like that) where you go trough the events in a differnt way giving you a slight amount of amusment of how much things chould have been all changed around/paused, filler). Other than the levels being linear/1 track (Unless something changes in the story). The levels are great, they are all in a cell shaded style that can resemble Borderlands greatly but with its own color pallet.  Gunplay: 1 serious thing to note, the crosshair and actauly place where the bullet is going to hit is a little lower than other game I play, to compinsate I just got used to aimming up a higher a bit than what I'm used to.  Revolvers: Longer realoads (Realoding each bullet instead of a clip) but has a longer range than Quickshooters, good but might get annoying in situations where you don't have time to shove bullets into you gun Quickshooters: Shorter clip reaload but with less range t Trade-offs, Trade-offs. Ya-da Ya-da Ya-da  Rifles: Greatest acuracy weapon in the game but has the slowest reload time, Great for getting the guy on the hill 200ft away. Not so good when your in a saloon with 10~ guys.  Shotguns: Maybe its because I don't know how to use it. But it pretty much one shots people where-ever you hit them and has moderate reaload. Now if your a guy like me who is poping people's head off left and right, this doesn't have much use for something like this.  Last notes about the game. Story mode you can carry 2 Holster weapons of the same type (You can 1 or 2 armed to fire at the same time) and 1 Special weapon which can be a Rifle/Shotgun. Your bullets are limited but the games make sure you shound't run out unless your missing or using 1 weapon at little 2 much. And on a Final note you need to play this game twice in story mode to get a true feel for it all. You see there is a difficulty unlock upon playing the game called 'True-west' mode. Where you basically don't have any hud elements except for the letter box around your screen. This means you cannot check you special ablities or see your crosshairs. Now you can check how many bullets you have via weapon switch wheel but other than that, that is the only break you get. In Duel bosses in story mode, you don't get to see your focus % or your quickdraw % but you do get your crosshairs and you do get to see how close your hand is your Revolver so you can find the sweet spot visualy.  I need to learn to type a proper review that itsn't just full with 'ands' and incompletly sentences or thoughts. But I said what I think neede to be said, I hope you all pick up this game eventauly in your life and I guess Happy Kwanzaa?

Remember back when FPS games weren't all about how many kills you got in a round? HL2 is probably one of the best examples of an old gem that is far from forgotten. I mean, a few areas of the game were pretty nerve-wracking. (cough Ravenholm cough) Another thing HL2 is notable for is the fact that the scripted events allow the player to react however they please during them, as opposed to cutscenes having you react the way the game wants. What really made this game compelling for me was the overall atmosphere. The aesthetic components of the game feels fairly original in all aspects. Textures, models and music are all appealing and blend together nicely to compose a feeling of mystery and loneliness.   The game mechanics are fairly simple and the puzzles that you are tasked to solve range from easy to semi-hard in difficulty. I am not a great puzzler and still managed to finish the game without to many hickups. Basically it's all about using a special swapper gun to create clones and transfer your mind between these clones to reach spheres that unlock new parts of the ship as well as the story.  A story that is excellently written and executed at that. It all starts out as a great mystery and then you unravel it bit by bit by interacting with consoles and other objects on the ship. A bit of reading and thinking might be required to get to grips with what has been going on on the ship but it's definitely worth the effort in the end.

This installment of Borderlands is for the people who been with the franchise since the first one. It has many, many refences to the other games. Just the main characters alone are from the other games.   Lets start with the stroy, its very interesting, to people who care about the stroy of borderlands and such. If your just the person who got this and its your first borderlands, you wont know what the hell is going on, ALIENS? VAULTS? JACK? WHAT ARE THESE? Anyway it follows jack's progression to evil and clears up some questions like 'whats under jack's mask?' 'why does jack hate vault hunters?' 'Why theres no jump pads in the other games?'.   Next would be graphics but its the same as the first two games, shellshaded. Nothing much else to say about that, like its suppose to look cartoony.   The characters, they are different from what we have seen (I havent seen the new jack character yet).  The quick run down of the classes are Wilheilm the enforcer, for the person who wants to play by themselves, with two bots that fly around him that heal and kill things for him hes perfect for those who want a complete single player experience.  Next Nisha the gunslinger, or as i like to call it the 'I have never played a FPS before please, halp' class. Her ability is to auto-lock onto enemies heads and shoot them dead on.  Next is Athena the gladiator, i dont know what to call it, captain america? i guess. Her ability is to block incomeing damage with a sheild, which can be upgraded to be thrown.  Last but not least CLAPTRAP, the Frag trap, the poster boy, or the randomize class. Claptraps ability is random, the game looks at your situation and gives you the best ability for that moment. It can be upgraded to be more random and to add a priate ship ability (yes, you read right, a Frick fracking pirate ship ability).   Now that the classes are out of the way its time for the hours. Someone looking for one run as fast as possible is 6-8 hours, people doing one run but doing everything 10-14 hours. Then theres the borderlands fans, who run the game with every class on vault hunter mode and such, yada yada yada, you know who you are. They will take about (with the current state with no story dlc, just the one extra character) 30-45 hours or even more, hell I have multiple characters in BL2 and i have almost 250 hours.   This game is Highly recommended to a borderlands fan, if you not a fan i would suggest playing one of the two before this (a.k.a play both the games, they're both really good!). 9.5/10 (because im a BL fan)

The high asking price of this title always drove me away, but I should have picked it up sooner - it's expertly crafted and intensely engaging.    The Flame in the Flood  is an incredibly polished survival game that can nicely be compared to a linear  Don't Starve .  Don't get me wrong though, this is its own thing - while the gathering/crafting mechanics are similiar, the flavor and feeling of the game is very much original. I use the word 'linear', because you are constantly moving down a river, instead of setting up a series of bases throughout the world.  The Flame in the Flood  is a bluegrassy struggle for survival in a world not dissimiliar to our own, while  Don't Starve  is more of a quirky steampunk/goth/emo survival in what could be an alternate universe (both are must-own titles).   The music really steals the show here, and helps create such a feeling of end-of-civilization isolation that I've yet to find in another title.  It deeply reminds me of my stepfather, who is a blues musician. He would appreciate this music and the overall tone of the story. The feeling of being alone and hints of despair (yet perhaps hopeful?) feel very real listening to the music while cruising down the river at sunset.    Essentially, you gather ingrediants and craft resources a la  Don't Starve , to help you get further down the river - and ideally to your destination.  One really neat aspect is managing your raft/boat, and upgrading it. There are several strategies you can take for success. Like  Don't Starve , you  will  juggle inventory, but thats a big part of the strategy, I suppose. Things that will end your run can include, but is not limited to - wild animals, dangerous insects, hypothermia from cold/wet exposure, your raft breaking down, poisonous foods, and much more. You get the idea.   Pros:  +Wonderful wonderful 'experience' and setting; it almost made me emotional at times +Learning curve is not steep; simple to get into, yet difficult to master +Different modes with varying difficulties +Excellent controller support +One of the best, most fitting soundtracks I've heard in recent times   Cons:  -Asking price is high, but you will get a good bit of time out of this if you intend to be successful -Inventory juggling can feel tedious, but I think thats part of the strategy   If you enjoyed similar titles, you owe it to yourself to give this one a go.  In hindsight, this is worth full price, IMO.

Star Wars: Dark Forces was one of the first video game entries to incorporate elements of the expanded universe. Built on an enhanced version of the DOOM engine, Dark Forces reset the bar for first person shooters. It introduces the lasting character of Kyle Katarn into the universe and proved that you don't have to be a Jedi or a starfighter pilot to make a successful Star Wars game. An epic storyline, combined with memorable characters and impressive level design makes this game a must have for any Star Wars fan, or those looking for a sense of nostalga about how far gaming has come.   The graphics were top notch at the time of release (1995) with smooth animation and distinct sprites and textures. Digital voice work is crisp and helps to give the characters a sense of personality. From the filtered 'halt!' of stormtroopers to the anamalistic groan of the Diagnosa as you wade through raw sewage, you will always know exactly what is hunting you.  Gameplay is standard fare for shooters of the time. you have a wide variety of weapons - pistols, blaster rifles, thermal detonators, along with more exotic weapons such as the fusion cutter or the assault plasma cannon. Health is non-regenerating and must be restored from medkits, along with shields.   Sound direction is excellent. The music features remixes of well known Star Wars tunes along with new music. Each tune fits the level it is attached to. Weapon sounds are unique, giving every gun its own feel.   Overall, this is a geat game for retro lovers. Solid gameplay, a memorable story that has influenced many other games in the franchise, and characters with actual personality makes this a good choice.   On the technical side, this game comes bundled with its own auto-loading version of DOSBox, meaning that it will work on modern systems. Confirmed functional on all systems up to Windows 7 64-bit, with reports of it working properly on Windows 8 systems.   9/10.

Frankly, I wasn’t expecting to buy this game. I’d never heard of it before, but boy howdy am I an absolute sucker for pixel games, point-and-clicks, and colorful palette choices. And I have to say if this game doesn’t deserve to be on someone’s GOTY list, then it at the very least deserves an extravagant honorable mention. It’s a really good game!  This is definitely a story where I know the people involved know their stuff about technology and politics. Its cute art really doesn’t prepare you for its rather darker (but remarkably fleshed out) contents. I won’t get into details, but when ♥♥♥♥ hits the fan you will probably be as surprised as I was. Every time. Multiple times. Where it really builds atmosphere is in the soundtrack. It has amazing chiptune and other electronic music featured in the game.  It also features a varied cast of LGBT characters, and while some Steam reviews might complain, it frankly feels natural considering the setting. (Which, honestly, if you think this game is somehow 'overrepresented' then maybe this game isn't for you.) The game asks you not what your gender is, but merely your pronouns (some are available as presents, but you can manually enter whatever you want.) It also has a few pop culture references that anyone can appreciate.  As far as gameplay goes, it’s really relaxing, honestly. There’s a few puzzles to solve (that are all easy, even for someone like me who could and has got lost in a Freddi Fish game), 2 mini-games (which are essentially the same), and ONE single combat section which is pretty generous with time (you can actually practice it using an arcade game in the nightclub early on). The game itself is vastly story-focused so honestly its a comfy game that anyone could enjoy. The pixel font might be tough to read for anyone with dyslexia, though.  All in all I would rate this game a 7.5/10.

zero spoilers ahead  Pros  + Level design: if there's one thing Mankind Dividied not only excels but also beats the original at, it's level design. Whether it's the huge Prague hub itself or any individual missions or thematic places, there are always many, MANY various way to solve problem, get to a point of interest, etc. Actually it's so good that on my first playthrough, I kept getting lost while (like always) trying to explore every corner of the world, always bumping into new points of entry and getting sidetracked + Graphics: the game is absolutely gorgeous, with amount of detail no other game can even compare with. As far as urban and interior environments go of course. + Updated augmentation, stealth and gunplay mechanics: the game not only looks like a perfect representative of this generation's games, it plays like one too. + Story and setting: while the ending of Human Revolution felt somewhat anticlimatic (not as much for WHAT's happend as HOW the game handled it due to budget and time issues), it left the world of Deus Ex in perfect state perfectly summed up by trailer's 'The promise of golden age is over.' HR rubbed my sci-fi nerdy parts right in the way how it presented the wonders of technological progression as well as beginnings of worldwide discord and systematic political oppression. MD now picks up after a major worldwide disaster showing the world at its worst. Posers say how it fails to be political, nitpicking on details, ignoring the fact the dystopian and technologically advanced future of Deus Ex can't be less related to real world problems. Thus, in it's own universe, Mankind Divided fits into original lore of far fetched conspiracies and enslaving technologies, builds upon it and expands it. And what's best, the game being kind of removed from reality, it still feels relatable. That being said though, the final fight is anticlimatic again, a bit of a let down after intense final levels. + Side quests and exploration: as opposed to the main story, the side quests of MD get better and better throughout the game. With tons of content to explore, any sneaky peeper will once again rejoice in reading other people's emails and PDAs and listening to their conversations. + Game longevity: my first playthrough took me about 32 hours, ten less than I spent with HR the first time around, but old and new features such as New Game+, Hardcore difficulty (one life) and Breach mode, as well as more ingame 'collectibles', they all offer more gameplay than I had hoped for. + Prague: 'nuff said.  Neutral points +/- Voice acting: professional as expected. American, British and Russian English is great. Czech is brilliant and probably most pleasant surprise about the game. It's where Czechs speak English the immersion got kinda broken for me. It's nitpicking but what can I do, I really wish they hired Czech voice actors for Czech as well as for Czech English +/- Story: As previously stated, the ending is cut-off really brutally. The entire story arc makes sense and is closed with just enough questions and a cliffhanger to make you look forward to planned sequels, but that doesn't excuse how weak the epilogue, until now one of the strongest suits of all Deus Ex games, feels.  Cons - Voice acting: the constant eastern accent can get pretty annoying, especially in few cases of the real bad ones. - Square Enix: microtransaction store I can live with. It's not great but is (would have been) a feature easily ignored, despite its reinforcement of an increasing trend. But when half of the Season Pass and Preorder bonuses are single use only with the sole intention of boosting sales in aforementioned ingame store, that's where I draw the line.   There. The last point is the only reason I have to give my GOTY a negative review. Don't buy it. Get it from 'alternative sources' (bay of yarr-sayers), enjoy it, and praise how great it is everywhere you can, because it sure is. Maybe, just maybe, in time Square Enix will its head out of its collective ♥♥♥♥ and start practicing fair marketing. Until then...

From the outset, it's clear that this is a very deep and interesting game. But 30 minutes in and I'm just sick of the awkwardness of the controls & dialog system. I've missed too much dialog due to either pressing the wrong key (there's 2 different keys for different types of basic interaction), pressing the Advance Dialog key hoping that it would show the text faster (it's really slow, and only shows you like 5 words at a time), or walking off screen just as dialog starts, causing the whole conversation to be skipped. Also, your flashlight turns off at every scene transition (this happens a lot - the game is mostly set in the dark), your character can't sprint for very long, you have very little time for exploration each night. Lots of annoyances add up quickly.  I wouldn't recommend this game because of the controls, dialog issues and other annoyances, but I'd recommend watching this developer because it's clear they can make great things.
