Rabi-Ribi, a game in which you play as a bunny but you're a humanised bunny girl now. Smash stuff with a hammer, use magic which acts widely different from eachother, find abilities to use and so on. I'm never really a fan of pixel style games but the pixel art in this is absolutely on point, couple that with some rather lovely VN style images and you have a very nice looking game with great art. I've enjoyed every track in the soundtrack thus far they are all very noticable and fit the feeling of where you are.  Now is it perfect? No. Does that matter? Nope not at all. It is a fun game, the combat is very solid with challenging bosses. I will say that 2D platformer bullet hells are some of the most infuriating games seeing as gravity really wants to work against you. The challenge of the bosses can range from easy to utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t, some projectiles and abilities literally cover most of the screen if it wasn't for the small hitbox I'd say it is impossible to dodge them.  I hopped into this game playing on hard because why not have a challenge. I started out just kinda running around figuring out where to go, seeing as I'm not the type to read tutorials or look at maps for details it became very fun. You quickly find a lot of off limits areas and places much too high level for you. Within the world I've found a few small areas that you can farm for cash to be able to outright buy a few upgrades if you struggle so really difficulty doesn't mean as much as you'd think unless you are going for a speedrun.  When I'd found my first boss I quickly realised that they hurt, you don't have much life to get by with and getting hit a few times means you're out. It's fun dodging about and charging magic to shoot off while edging closer to smack them in the fact. With the magic you can get a few types; such as the default blue which the charge shot is just a straight shot that deals decent damage, green which the charge send out 4 homing shots that actually heal for every 2 that hit, or even yellow which while charging deals fast but low damage in a line and the charged shot is a heavy damage shotgun. You basically have a magic or upgrade for every job you'd need  What do I think? It's a great game, if you're a fan of metroidvanias I'd suggest to buy it right away. The price isn't that high and for the quality of the game you'd expect to pay a hell of a lot more. It's a solid 9/10 game it does so much right and I really can't fault it on anything except the occasional ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t ability of a boss, when you get unlucky and the AI decides that it wants to spam the strongest ability because it has had enough of you winning. The characters are also enjoyable especially Ribbon the fairy and a lot of the boss/side characters.  TL;DR -You like metroidvanias? Buy this It is inexpensive and a quality game I'd go as far to say it would be one of my top picks.

Update:  Trion Worlds has pushed an XP bonus live for affected players. 'Affected players' being people with no gear and unable to use skills. Trion claims to have sent compensation for characters who lost gear. I have received nothing. They do not yet claim to have fixed the problem with skills being disabled. Perhaps they could explain how affected players are intended to benefit from an XP bonus in an ARPG where they have no gear and no usable skills?     I registered an account on Friday using Chrome, since their website apparently dislikes Microsoft Edge. Today I logged into the game for the second time. My character had no gear at all and I get an error message when attempting to use skills. So their website didn't work properly and their game didn't work properly.  I can't properly judge their Free2Play model yet. The default two character slots feels a little stingy, but not wholly unreasonable. At the time of writing there is DLC available for €4.99, €19.99, €49.99 and €99.99. I won't be spending money on any of these until the problems are dealt with and Devilian has been stable for some time.  Overall I thought the combat was fun, the game felt responsive and it was a pleasant enough looking game. It's designed for Eastern audiences and I'm not personally fond of anime, e.g. the Canoneer class (each class is locked into a single race & gender, however there's customization options for hair style & colour, several faces, etc). The game did seem overly booby at times - rotating the Evoker results in some very noticeable jiggling.   tl;dr:  Trion strikes again? The publisher doesn't have the best track record, and early problems combined with asking for €99.99 for the 'Immortal Pack' makes me wonder whether this is any more than a cashgrab. The ARPG / MMO hybrid has potential, but for now I'm sticking with Path of Exile & Diablo III.

Overall, this game delivers a constantly fun experience throughout the whole game, which is more than what many FPS games can claim. It feels as if the designers thought about what's fun first and let the rest of the game fall into place around that concept.  I consistently enjoyed the core shooting gameplay. It rewards precise aiming in that headshots do lots more damage. However, the enemies can feel like bullet sponges at times. I overcame this by using the strongest ammunition I could find. I ended up playing most of the game using the starting pistol with Fat Mamma and the shotgun with Pop Rockets. A headshot from a fat mamma or a direct hit with a Pop Rocket is an instant kill on normal enemies. So, if you start to feel frustrated by enemies seeming to have too much health, try that out. Even then, the frustration factor is mitigated by a revive mechanic AND a quick save and quick load mechanism.  There's also racing. The racing controls on a keyboard kinda suck. The wheels turn before the camera, so you'll often end up turning to sharply. I didn't try playing with a controller because I didnt' know how it would handle switching between schemes, but I bet that would be better. That issue aside, it's actually a really fun addition to the shooting gameplay and a nice way to break it up and make the environment seem bigger and more lived in. In particular, I liked how they would put challenges that would reward bonus money when you were driving to an objective. It made it feel less repetitive even when you were driving back and forth. between the same areas.  It's not without issues. They re-use several areas a few times. I didn't find this to be much of a problem because you're honestly just more focused on the shooting and what the enemies are doing, and they change up the enemies when you re-play through those areas. Also, if you play on a low sensitivity like me, the menus are very unpleasant and difficult to use effectively. It's the fact that they use mouse acceleration combined with a low sensitivity setting that just makes it possible the worst menu experience I've ever seen. Thankfully, you don't need to spend too much time in menus, but it would've definitely enhanced the experience.  If you want a fun, precise FPS, definitely get this game.

Assassin's Creed III Review:  I have never been a particular fan of the Assassins Creed series, so when I bought this game I was pretty excited. HOWEVER, due to technical faults (which I could not solve) I couldn't play the game without Uplay crashing and burning it. Ubisoft customer support were very quick about replying however, but we still didn't solve the issue. Eventually, I had to do a COMPUTER RESET BACK TO FACTORY SETTINGS just to get it to work. Once this was dealt with, the game worked perfectly.  So the game. You start the game off as Haytham Kenway. A suity kinda guy who you would definitely NOT expect to be climbing CInema walls. And yet this is what he does. He climbs cinema walls just to kill a dude with a necklace. There is then set a wild goose chase, as we try and see if we can find some kinda temple or other.  You then play as Connor ( or Rahetuhaskjdbnjksbd apologies for bad spelling :D) who is a kid. You then play hide and seek with other kids and then get caught. Connor grows up really quickly to the point of being a tree hugger and climbing through trees. You meet an old guy on a hill. He turns out to be aan assassin and all that crap.  Overall, I think this game is amazing. The graphics in-game are a stunning HD, and with my Nvidia GeForce Graphics chip, the game runs pretty smoothly. The fighting system works well, as you can easily block enemy attacks. Combo moves are a bit tricky, but once you get the hang of it becomes great. The map is extremely big, covering New York, Boston, Davenport Homestead, and the jungle wilds. There are a few side quests, but if you get bored it may be worth getting the DLC addon. I would give this game a 9.5/10. Great job Ubisoft!

Another successful addition to the Arkham franchise. With this winning formula, no developer could go wrong. A great example of one of those cases where more of the same is a good thing.  In this installment the trilogy takes a darker turn, with a story more centered around death and its consequences than before; it becomes more like The Dark Knight trilogy than Arkham. Thankfully the new voice actors fill their roles perfectly, in some cases even topping their predecessors, and the boss fights have been greatly improved, offering a much more cinematic feel this time around and do a lot to drive the story.   Sure the multiplayer isn't a whole lot of fun, but this is still a very good game.

Many gamers ask how Alien Isloation actually is, good or bad? The answer isnt easy, if you tell it is very good the expectations are too high,  and the gamers review tends to be bad. The game itself sticks very close to the Alien Universe,  no mix with Predators or other circus attractions. Genuine actors, like Ripley, found all their way into this game.  Creative Assembly has recreated the whole atmosphere very carefully and accurately,  in order to make this game a worthwhile experience. For a video game Alien Isolation is absolutely awesome, it took me 30 hours to complete the main campaign on hard.  And still, for a true Alien fan it is a two edged sword.  Gameplay is sometimes very tense, the AI works in most cases as expected,  but...there is always something to improve. I had the impression there have been missing about 10 people in every staff,  animation, meshers, texturing, effects, etc. to make this game look like perfect,  through the whole missions, from the beginning to the end.  But still the animation doesnt allow those movements you'd like to expect from the Alien. Indeed, the animation technique used in this game is very good.  But yet it still cant maintain the impression of a 'real, AI driven' Alien. Why? For instance, It is not possible to burn parts of the Alien, it doesnt jump over tables, all those quirky things. After a while you have seen all of its animations/behavior, they repeat.  Nothing new, nothing surprising, nothing that reminds of a 'real' breed.  You start to sense the automation, and then, in most cases,  the Alien becomes the well known computer enemy dude.  I lost some of the fascination I had at the very beginning.  And not because I was annoyed by hiding in a locker too often. But, to be honest, I do not know any technique in animation yet,  which would allow a much better, physics based animation giving the Alien  much more diverse and surprising movements/behavior. We might have to wait awile for this to happen in computer games.  All in all, I would rate this game a 9.5 out of 10.

I don't remember who convinced me to buy Hotline Miami, but they have my thanks. It would have been a shame to miss out on what became one of my favorite games ever.  It's a twin-stick shooter in which you receive calls every few days, telling you to 'visit' a certain place. Basically, murder everyone while donning an animal mask.  Everything kills and dies in one hit, including yourself, so every level has you running from enemy to enemy trying to hit them before they hit you. Because the worst that will happen if you die is you lose the last five to thirty seconds of gameplay, you always go for the absurd to try and maximize your score, which makes the whole rush all the more satisfying.  All of this is compounded by what has to be one of the better assembled licensed soundtracks ever, a collection of fast-paced electro music that matches the pace of your killing spree all too well. It's really a fantastic game that I'd recommend to just about anyone into action games.  PS: If you read reviews, especially on Metacritic, from when the game launched, a lot of them will mention that gamepads don't work: That has been fixed, and actually I'd recommend using one. Also, the game now crashes a lot less than the launch version.

A cute, relaxing little game. There's not much to the storyline: This is a simple text-based Choose Your Own Adventure that allows you to roleplay through a limited number of story endings. The actions are light-hearted, and each choice can be easily 'fixed' or negated if you're unhappy with its result. It's good for a laugh.   The calming music is honestly the best part. This may be a good game for people with anxiety who need a quick distraction at night. It doesn't take much attention of commitment to play, so it's easy to spend a few minutes clicking through the story and letting the music do its work.  $9.99 is a rather steep price tag for such a simple game, though. I'm glad that I bought this on sale and only shelled out $2.50. It wouldn't have been worthwhile to spend any more money than that.

app_name: Castlevania: Lords of Shadow - Ultimate Edition
review_text: This is an amazing game that really should get some more honors or awards for its outstanding qualities. Every aspect of the game is just done so well.  ENEMIES This game has one of the most interesting and challenging set of enemies that you'll ever encounter. The monsters know how to fight and have an arsenal of moves that will keep you on your toes. They are beautifully animated, and there are a ton of different creatures and monsters to encounter.  COMBAT Combat is a total blast. There are many combos to learn, with a huge variety of cool moves that lead to fun combat, which also requires good combo skills to master. There are no pushover fights, and from the very start you will have some challenging and epic battles. You can also dodge, block, and use special powers.  MAPS There are alot of maps to go through, and they are exquisityly designed and detailed. Everything is beautifully rendered, and a pleasure to look at. The settings are unique and interesting, and you will go through many different environments as you go through the story.  STORY The story is interesting, and keeps you motivated throughout. The story is narrated by some great voice acting.  PLATFORMING In certain situations, your character will need to jump, run, dodge, wade, climb, grapple, and smash his way through various areas. You can even capture beasts and have them smash doors or climb up walls with you riding them! It just adds to the fun!  PUZZLES You will encounter puzzles now and again, and they offer a good challenge and will test your wits.  CONCLUSION This is how games should be made. Fun, challenging, great combat, tough enemies, epic encounters, beautiful visuals, interesting mechanics. I can go on and on. BUY IT TODAY!!

A very likeable story rich adventure game. I recommend first playing the first part of the series, 'The Longest Journey' (if you can bear it) and even more so the second part 'Dreamfall: The Longest Journey' which provides a lot of background and introduces the main characters.  The combat system of the previous game of the series (Dreamfall:TLJ) has fortunately been removed and Chapters has been made a choice-based game. The choices obviously dont affect the grand story arc but are still interesting and meaningful. You mainly play as Zoe and Kian as in the previous game, and now and then the new character Saga at various ages (starting at toddler age and later at age 4 which is very cute).  If you come from Dreamfall:TLJ, note that the voice actors for both Zoe and Kian unfortunately changed. At first this is very noticeable especially for Zoe but Charlotte Ritchie does a very good job and it's easily forgotten after a short while.  Some adventure games these days have poorly constructed/underbudget/strange/too short/outright sad endings but Dreamfall isn't one of them. The ending is well proportioned and bearable for soft hearts like me.

Good:  (1) Fantastic aesthetics, looks beautiful throughout. Great use of music. Very immersive, enough so that I played the game straight through without pausing.  (2) The relationship between the main character and the dude in the sword is very well communicated. Plus, they don't neatly fit the usual guy / girl lead character tropes, so the story isn't as predictable as you'd expect. Yay! (3) Some cool gameplay mechanics, especially the combat planning mode. Lots of ways to combine skills, especially when you go for new game plus and keep leveling your character. The last boss was completely unlike any other enemy in the game and actually required some deep thought to figure out a reliable solution to.   Some observations:  (1) It's obvious how OP Cull combined with the Void and Crash debuffs are. If you use the hiding skill beforehand, you can use Cull to one-shot each of the last boss's forms... (2) You're practically forced to use combat planning attacks at every opportunity if you don't want to die over and over. It reminds me too much of how much of a crutch VATS was in the Fallout games...  (3) Unlocking parts of the story by having to use each ability in a different way didn't feel quite right, either. They could've tied some other non-story element to that mechanic, like bonus artwork or something...  Bad:  (1) Way too short, even for a game I bought on sale. Took me five hours. A good fifteen or so minutes of that was spent messing around with different skill combinations on the last boss. (2) Losing your combat abilities when you 'died' felt like an arbitrary punishment. If that's going to be a thing, then at least let us have our abilities back when the battle ends, and not two access terminals later. Going into the next fight without your best ability is basically death, anyways. (3) There are a bunch of side characters in this game that don't get fleshed out enough.... and by that, I mean everyone that isn't Red or the dude in the sword.  Even worse:  The ending was a let down. She has the power of creation in her hands, and she does THAT with it? You've gotta be kidding me. If you can use the sword to command the robots to rebuild the city, you can command them to do other... favors... I won't say anything else due to spoilers but that was a really unsatisfying finish.  I'll recommend Transistor, but just barely: Only pull out your wallet if this game is on sale. 7.5 / 10

Assassins Creed: Shadow of Mordor with Lucas Arts Mercenaries expansion content. If that doesn't make total sense, let me clarify. Plays a LOT like Assassins Creed, I mean a lot, to the point of you climb up towers to reveal sections of the map. It's filled with side missions to upgrade gear and get XP you 'level up' by getting better runes to add to your weapons. You do still level up more traditionally, just like AC, you gain new and improved skills as you gain points to do so through taking out bad guys. Where does the mercenaries come in? Through the AWESOME and I do mean AWESOME bounty like system, the goal it to get to a 'general' or sorts, who has almost two dozen Captains under him. Just like in the LOTR stories though they are in an almost constant state of internal conflict as they challenge, betray and assassinate each other in pursuit of greater power among their own. Enemies will kill each other, making the survivng orc stronger and more difficult to kill. There is a ton of development around this sytem, with awesome features like enemies you defeat will sometimes return, mangled and disfigured from your previous fight with the intent of revenge on YOU for what you did to them. Enemies also come with an assortment of weakness and strength, such as a fear or hatred of fire. Things which they fear either do extra damage or cause them to run in fear giving the player the ability to instant kill them, things they hate do the opposite, increasing their damage, defense and in many cases causes them to instantly regenerate to full health (Only one full regen PER thing they hate can be triggered though). The big let down is the DLC. I got this on console and enjoyed it, but wanted something backwards compatible so got this on PC with all DLC, the DLC added virtually nothing for me, some side missions which add little replayability to me and a handful of alternate costumes, none of which I liked more than the default game out fit. Without the DLC, I'd say it's still worth $30, with the DLC still $30. For me the DLC adds zero value.

[Taken from my online blog] Well, its a Wednesday today which may be news to you but if it isn't and by some miracle you've actually returned to this site, you'll know that this isn't my usual release date. Something went awry with my schedule however. I was all set to review some game that some right old knob has been asking me to review for a while when I saw that there was a Steam sale. 'Blimey', I thought,' I wonder if any recomended games would be worth buying'. You see, I take punts on games like these. More obscure games that have some sort of gimmick bolted onto the end of it. So I bought the Betrayer. Now I love atmospheric games and having recently weaned myself off Alien Isolation with a bit of Outlast and Betrayer, I have to say I've been craving more. The Betrayer IS an atmospheric game for damn sure. Made by Blackpowder games, this black and white tale takes place in New England and if you're from New England then allow me to tell you that New England actually means America. You arrive stranded on a beach, waking in a anamnesia state with your vision blurred and no other backstory about how you actually got there, so basically just my average Sunday. You can see a few ships off the coast, however you never seem to try to make contact with them. Odd enough when you consider that you are supposed to be trapped there. From there you take a linear path up to your first location serving as the only form of communication between player and game, which is pretty much how the game starts to go on. You are not lead through the game thats for sure. Once you find the first location, Fort Henry, you're pretty much on a free reign to discover at your own pace all the while being blue-balled by a literal red riding hood.  The game's gothic influence is a strong aspect I have to say. There's no disputing the fact that the eerie sound design, black and white (with a hint of red) visuals and the spooky monsters you fight all fit the bill of old gothic literature. Having said that though, it reminded me more of those old horror films set in time periods similar to this one (17th century) like Witchfinder General and other masterpieces that have been cut from the same cloth. It's definitely not Five Night's at Freddies where things jump out at you for a few minutes before passing out face-first into the dirt, but Betrayer is asking for more than three pound fifty, so it knows it needs to grip the viewer preferably by the gonads as you're lead through dark forests and solve elaborate and chilling mysteries of the land's previous inhabitants. The lack of direction may be a middle finger to some players who are used to the half arsed games like Skyrim where you can click a location to immediately and inexplicably warp there. In Betrayer you can only use this trick if you're in the specific location and only to travel to the predetermined 'safe points'. I gotta admit, I quite like this system. There will no doubt be a literal horde of angry GTA fans, still wounded from last week, that will claim that the game doesn't direct you enough, but that's just it. The beautiful thing about this system is that you are lost, destined to wander the world in search of the next enlightening piece of the story pie. It feels atmospheric and gothic with only a slight whiff of the pretentious artsy-ness about it which only becomes obvious when the black and white mode is on (which you have the option to switch off).   If I were a gambling man, I'd be ready to say that you may be worried for my health at this point viewer. After all, I am the kind of guy who's idea of a wild night is to sit in his room with an oil burner silently getting drunk and slinking into the arms of the weeping abyss of self loathing. But even I like things, and the Betrayer is one of the things I like. It oozes atmosphere all over the place which is pretty difficult to explain when people come to visit. Guest scaring aside however, I like the Betrayer because it's something a bit new. I could play through the thousands of modern warfare games in a week and walk away like I've just been given a frontal wedgie by Hulk Hogan, or I could sit down to play some original games. Sure, modern warfare games are good for spectacle if nothing else but that's not what a game is there for. If someone said you were going to watch a good film and hands you a book that's just a lie and it's the same principle when a game developer gives you what amounts to a film with a few button prompts. Luckily the Betrayer doesn't betray the gaming audience and instead gives us an atmospheric and mysterious little gem that's got a few cracks in it, but ultimately would make Indiana Jones a very happy man.  Having said that, there is a point in this game where the atmosphere receives a little hairline fracture. If you've played the game you'll know what I'm talking about because I don't want to spoil it for anyone, but I want you to let me know if you had the same experience. It's a moment in the game where straight-up combat takes sway and you're forced to fight wave after wave of evil dudes, kind of like World War 2 without the fast pace of a heavy machine gun. For the combat here is effective yet slow. As to be expected with a company that names itself after a slow-firing weapon, the combat has some pacing issues in that fighting large numbers of lads can result rather quickly in your brutal demise, which is fine... For a while. However when you're only way to stop the armoured target running towards you is to shoot him with a horribly inaccurate blackpowder weapon that has a 10% chance of deflecting off of the target and there's five of the buggers now so hurry you ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, I can't help but wonder if there's a bit of a design flaw. I would say that it's intended though. As I mentioned, when you're fighting a reasonable amount of guys with rusty, inaccurate and slow-firing weapons, the encounters with the fast brutal enemies can become all that more intense. After all, man hath no fear of hell, if handed a high-grade assault rifle.  8/10- Yes! I've done it! A good review! Well, as good as I can get before downing the rest of the bottle and passing out in a road side ditch.  Please be sure to stop by my site to egg me on in my content and help support me and what I do. To me Castlevania: Symphony of the Night rates as #2 greatest game of all time, bested only by Unreal Tournament 2004. It started the entire metroidvania subgenre and made a rather large impact on many people, including me. This game is made by someone who no doubt shares great love of SotN and metroidvania in general. If you are one of those fans you don't need to read any further, this is as good as it gets, the finest metroidvania I played since SotN.   For those not familiar, this game is set in a large two dimensional world where to progress to new areas you need to acquire keys and abilities (metroid style), but at the same time you procure new equipment, level up and specialize. The biggest strength of this game lies in it's combat system. This is a perfect mix of min max stat gameplay with skill based reactive 2d brawler. What this means is that you will have to take great care of how you invest your stat points, skill points what you upgrade, what you use and most importantly how well you understand and play the game. The combat system is simple at overlook: light attack, heavy attack, block, cancel dash and magic, but undrneeth there is a whole deep level that will make fans of stylish action games rejoice. There are lifters, slams, blocks, parries, force blocks, secret techniques, stealth, backstabs, criticals, magic oriented attacks, stuns,assists, crazy combos etc etc. In all honesty this game is rather overwhelming how much there is to the actual combat. To someone who invested time it's second nature but the sheer amount taken into consideration when creating a build for your character far exceeds what I ever thought a game like this would offer.  Currently there are two characters (there will be two more with a forthcoming free update). Each character has their own three skill trees to spend points on. Each character has different weapons to use and completely different playstyles. There are four main stats (strength, agility, inteligence and luck), you get to put a point in this as well as picking a skill when leveling up. It opens up a tremendous amount of specialization and replaybility for each character. Upgrading gear needs to be done wisely because the materials needed are finite, and for certain item types you will only be able to upgrade one item to maximum (weapon for example). Choose wisely which weapon, armor or spell you upgrade. Speaking of gear, everything is a sidegrade. There are no upgrades in this game, each piece of armor, accessory or even weapon is suited for a specific build or playstyle. Each armor has special properties and unique benefit. This alone expands the amount of possible play styles and builds significantly.  The game's combat challange is done correctly. The more I play the better I am. I started and i would get thrashed by every boss I'd encounter, barely able to finish it. Now I'll get an S rank on every boss encounter(tip, better rank on bosses gives permenant stat increases). It feels like Devil May Cry or Ninja Gaiden, where the game teaches you the hard way to play well. On the other hand the platforming portion of the game is not so ideal. If I had any setbacks for this game it's that platforming does not work as well as combat does. Sure after some time it becomes trivial because you get used to it, but at first the platforming will be a chore, mostly because the way movement works. It's difficult to pinpoint your character, don't expect perfect pixel platformer controls like in Mega Man. Though after time it goes away and you learn it like it happened with the combat. A very minor setback to overall fantastic experience otherwise.  So yes, I absolutely endorse this title. For the amount of replaybility and content this game offers, along with free future updates to come this game is an absolute steal even for it's full price. For you metroidvania fans, I swear you will get that feeling that SotN had you as you explored, grinded and fought in Dracula's castle. Just an absolute blast of a game.

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: When was the last time you played an emotionally engaging game about war? If you have recently played Spec Ops: The line please disregard the previous question. Valiant Hearts is a game set in the first world war, which is actually very rare for video games. It was created by Ubisoft so, yes you do need U-Play. This is a puzzle/Adventure game when you'll mostly be picking up items and using them on certain things to preogress the story. The story is about 4 people who are forced to participate in the first world war and they're all connected to one another. The thing thats really interesting is that this game doesnt have any main villain or antagonist of any sort. The villain/antagonist in this game is more or less the war itself. You play as characters from both sides who would really prefer not illing each other and just going home to their families, which is very intriguing. The ending will also bring a tear to your eye unless you are a robot. I highly suggest you buy this game, even if its not on sale It's very much worth it.

Carmageddon is an absolute classic and one of the best driving games you will ever play. The Story and goal of each level is basically: Run over pedestrians, destroy your opponents, win races :P One of the main features that set Carmageddon apart from other racing games is the variety of ways you can win each race as well as the wide open maps.  The easiest way to win each race is to just destroy your opponents and while your opponents get stronger the further you progress, the Power. Armour and Speed upgrades you can put on to your car will help immensely. The other 2 ways are the bog standard checkpoint route and running over every single pedestrian which will take a LOT of time as there are hundreds of them every race and hidden all over the track. There are also plenty of powerups strewn throughout the races both fun and useful such as an electro ray and frozen opponents, or disadvantageous to you such as jelly suspension and turbo cops. Speaking of cops, you will occasionaly encounter them as you first play but towards the end there will be cops around almost every corner and are very hard to destroy making the later courses fairly difficult.  The graphics are still very playable to this day with nice car and damage modelling, funny pedestrian sprites and a decent variety of track layouts to explore. The audio is equally decent with a kickass soundtrack from Fear Factory and the crashes and clangs of each car not to mention the funny quips from your pratcam driver. With over 30 cars to drive when completed and over 30 tracks to complete there is plenty of value to be had, not to mention the splat pack expansion which adds an additional 20 tracks and cars each.  Overall Carmageddon is a timeless classic that all oldskool pc gamers and racing fans will enjoy,

Cinders is a visually appealing modern take on the Cinderella fairytale. It did a great job of including occasional social commentary without detracting from the overall flow of the narrative. I enjoy anime but hadn't delved into visual novels, until Cinders. The slight changes in movement and expressions of the character portraits, music, and the overall storyline kept me engaged, without feeling like I was trudging through a glorified illustrated book. The decisions that you make actually change the storyline and the interactions between characters. Choices in the game also come up often enough that you’re not reading for extended periods of time and sitting as a passive observer, but rather as an active participant. Although the option to “fast forward” through text made completing alternate storylines a relatively quick process, I would have preferred the option to “skip” text and scenes entirely or the ability to move directly to bookmarked decision points.   I consider this a fun and endearing introduction to visual novels because of the familiar, yet engaging theme. It also created a spark of interest in other visual novels. The inclusion and portrayal of characters derived from African/Afro-Caribbean traditions and beliefs was a very pleasant suprise.

So, I have a few things to say about this game.  I have to give credit in a couple areas, but unfortunately it just doesn't save the game for me. The music is pretty good, the art is really cute and aethetically pleasing. I've only played for a couple hours, but to be honest I couldn't really stomach playing it for longer.  Why?  The movement feels clunky. Despite watching videos and other people stream the game, I still wasn't sure in the end if I was even playing the game 'right.' It felt uncomfortable and was not easy to slide in to, as it should be. The writing is done in such a way that it almost treats the audience as if they are dumb, and cannot infer information based on decently written dialogue. The mini map was not helpful or well 'mapped out' at all. I had no idea what the game was trying to indicate to me with its' little green squares for everything they were trying to mark.   Further, a little bit of a pet peeve about this game: you have to pay money if you damage stuff. That sounds logical maybe, but listen -- most of us have been conditioned to break things if we see we are damaging them. We want to see what goodies are inside! (I broke a stump had to pay money. You should be paying me; I removed that nuisance from your yard.) And due to this mechanic, I basically started the game bankrupt. Furthermore, what I was doing felt empty. Boring. Pointless. I didn't feel engaged. Make it matter to me, because right now, it just doesn't.   I did have some interaction with the devs and I have to say that for the most part, they are some pretty nice people. Unfortunately, that doesn't make for what I consider to be a 'good game', all due respect.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: o... I've spend about 135 hours in this game and feel it is time to write a little something about it.  Negative point: The first thing I have to bring up is that I am not happy AT ALL with the fact that you cannot purchase DLC packages seperately but that you have to get the Season Pass to get access to DLC... What the heck is up with that? I had Black Ops 2 (Blops 2) on PS3 and now on PC and loved the fact that I could opt in or out of getting a certain DLC offering based on my likes and/or dislikes of that specific package. I want to get one of two of the Black Ops 3 (Blops 3) DLC's and will probably NEVER own any of the DLC's just because the Season Pass costs as much as a new game... Not cool at all.  Now wit the negative out of the way, :-)  Positive: I am extremely happy with this game and I feel, next to Blops 2, that this Blops is one of my all time favorites. I enjoy the movement mechanics loads and the fast paced action is really satisfying. Yes, there are times where you die unfairly (camera issues persist as this engine will always have the issue of you thinking you have turned the corner and are out of harms way and then BAM, head-shot by a guy that you ran away from) but that said, I love the sniping in this one. I am a sniper and love aggressive sniping (run/gun and at times quick-scoping when my old man reflexes allows for it) and think that sniping in this game is much better than in Blops 2. Graphics are great, although, you do need a beefy GPU to take full advantage of the prettiness of it all, but the game runs at a smooth 60fps on high with a few settings turned down. Zombies... have not really jumped into it in the 135 hours that I've played the SP and MP sections but will probably give it a solid go soon as I feel it is time to tackle the complexity of the zombies game (objectives and Easter egg hunts). Single layer was an interesting one with some mind bending themes and set pieces that at times left me a bit baffled on what the story is actually about, but it was entertaining and I enjoyed it more than any of the previous games'SP campaigns including MW 2.  All in all I feel that Black Ops 3 offers the best COD experience if you just opt to buy the base package and will absolutely recommend it to anyone that want to have a great MP/SP shooter with some Zombies action. This game will live on for a while still as the modding tools have been released and there are already some amazing zombies maps that have been done by modders. I am excited to see what the peeps come up with as I enjoyed the mods on WOW's zombies.  Go get it if you need to decided between this and Infinite Warfare... there is no comparison. :-)

I know these maps take a lot of time to create, but 40 dollars is quite a lot for a new playable route map. I wouldn't mind paying $10 or maybe even $15, but $40? Other than that if you have been a trainz fan for a long time I think you will get used to this edition, however if you are just starting out in Trainz, I still think that Trainz 2012 is probably the better choice.

so life is a wierd thing and actual reviews are a waste of time so i got something better   enjoy this chicken stir fry recpie   - Serves 4 - 10 mins to prepare and 10 mins to cook - 325 calories / serving - Healthy  INGREDIENTS:  - 1 orange, zested and juiced - 2tbsp soy sauce - 2tbsp sesame oil - 250g (8oz) tender stem broccoli - 5cm (2in) piece root ginger - 1tbsp sunflower oil - 1 garlic clove, finely sliced - 1 red pepper, deseeded and thinly sliced - 150g Straight To Wok Noodles - 400g (13oz) chicken fillet, cut into thin slices  METHOD  Step 1 - Mix together the orange zest and juice, soy sauce and sesame oil and set aside.  Step 2 - Blanch the broccoli in boiling water for 2 minutes, then drain well and set aside.  Step 3 - Finely shred the ginger. I'll start with the positive: the battles look amazing. It's fun to watch the different units tear into each other, if you are willing to wait until you actually encounter another race (which takes a while as the empire or vampire counts.) The negative, for me, is the departure from total war. The economy in this game is completely awful. I agree with another reviewer who said they just added in background income to try to fix it. There is no point in building economic buildings, since they give so little income. This means you can't field multiple armies, just one or two. When the Chaos warriors arrive, you basically get to set there and defend, in battle after battle, with no hope of expansion. You are never able to autoresolve. The vampire counts v humans & greenskins v dwarves combined with quest battles take out a lot of the create-your own stratgey, snadbox feel and gives the game a linear progession.  If you never played total war, then hey, you might enjoy this. I have bought every total war title since the orginal rome, but I will not be buying the next total war warhammer. I wish I could return this one...

app_name: Ori and the Blind Forest: Definitive Edition
review_text: I have only cleared 52% of the first area but I love this game so far. I will update the review as I complete more.  The game is beautiful. It introduces new mechanics very smoothly. The leveling seems to be a bit of an illusion as I usually can only level one thing due to prerequisites. Perhaps those who don't search every crevice might actually have to choose perks. I have already experience one interesting mechanic where platforms appear or don't appear depending upon whether they are in the radius of a glowing ball that you can carry. While I die a lot, I never feels stuck. The game seems generous with allowing me to set save points and repeated deaths are usually just an issue of timing when jumping across small platforms with projectiles flying by.

For me the first night playing this game has been a flawless launch-n-play no non-sense just drive experience.  My Xbox 360 controller got detected automatically with the normal button mapping. The graphic settings got selected at Medium for my system and I had a smooth 100fps on a GTX 680, i7-2600K @ 3.4GHz with 16GB RAM. I went straight into matchmaking and it found a match swiftly. The online experience was smooth even at 22 players from all over the world. North America, Europe, Asia and South America all in the same session. Lag was not an issue as the cars become ghosts when they lag or crash.  Overall a super fun frustration-free first online experience. The controls are great with a gamepad; The car feels heavy and grounded. And it looks great at Medium settings.  UPDATE: Game is 500% better after disabling all voice acting.

====================================WARNING========================================== If you hate rage quitting, just don't get this game!!! Don't say that I didn't warn you! ('Cause I did) ===================================================================================== Anyway, Super Meat Boy is an extreme platformer where you play as (you guessed it) Meat Boy). Your job is to rescue your soulmate bandage girl from the clutches of evil mastermind, Dr.Fetus. Awkward names aside, this is a great game! Like with most indie games, the art style is what makes it shine. It has a cartoony yet sick and twisted art style. The game itself has little replayability sadly, but is extremely hard... dragging it on... and on... and on... and while that might sound bad it's actually what kept me playing. I had a desire to win and complete it (which I still haven't). The game itself pays homage to a lot of old side scrolling platformer games. It's cheeky references and nostalgic gameplay makes this a must buy for retro game fans.

I would not reccomend this game. It's not very buggy as I know, but the textures aren't very smooth, and gameplay is slow, timewise. It takes forever to progress, and the fact that you have to unlock certain animals is kind of deterring.  PROS:   VERY good idea. It has potential to be a very fun game, both for the purpose of messing up an ecosystem or legit trying to make one.    Nice GUI and overall appeal was good   It was a good effort. I would probably play this game if it were better.    CONS:   SLOW GAMEPLAY. Although you can fast-forward, you still have to wait a long time before anything can reproduce and move on gameplay. This is mainly what pushed me away.   Small area in which to work. So, you get the coin thingys at the end of each week, and that takes about 0.4 hours, cuz I only spent 13 in-game days on it, so... Anyways, it costs 50 coins to unlock area 2, and there are 5 areas. You get 10 coins a week or so. THAT WOULD TAKE A LONG TIME FOR GRINDING A MEDIOCRE GAME. It's not really worth the time and effort.   You have to wait to unlock certain animals, plants, and decomposers. They cost coins, so basically for a while you only get the bobcat, jackrabbit, shrooms, that one plant, and a moth. BORING. So yeah, needs more animals to be able to use.    OVERALL: 4/10. I wouldn't reccomend spending money on this game, as it isn't worth it. Rather than buy this, buy Spore by MAXIS and EA. It's the same, exept you can create a character, make a tribe, create an empire with sick military jets, tanks, and boats, then create a spaceship and conquer the universe. And the first two stages+ space stage is centered around creating/stabilizing/surviving in an ecostystem. 10x better, and worth the money. I'll refund this game and buy something else.

So this is not a polished, deeply involved game. The dialogue in the tutorial is rough. The resolution isn't good. (Though the Alt-Enter trick to make it full screen helps considerably). The graphics are serviceable.  It's a fun little survival roguelike, however. You pick three characters to start and pick three goals from among a list of easy and difficult tasks that comprise your winning condition. You then give your characters tasks each day to build up your base, gather resources, sleep, explore, etc. Win or lose, you get some points that you can use to unlock new characters to choose from in your next play through.  There is quite a lot of variety in characters, skill upgrades, and equipment. Combat is straightforward and brutally fair. There's a real risk of dying all of the time, which keeps you engaged.  Would I have wanted to pay $25 for this? No. Was it worth my five bucks? Definitely.

Time isn't the only thing that stutters in Quantum Break. The game itself does, literally. While the characters fight the end of time in the plot, the player fights performance stutters behind a controller.  This isn't the good old low framerate, this is unlike anything you have seen. The visual freezes, slows down, and fast-forwards on screen, erratically. You hear really really cool sound effects from the headphones. You wonder what is happening, but there is little choice but to let your mind fill in the blanks. It comes without a hint. Things were fine a second ago.  Most of the times, you imagine what that cutscene would look like on a really expensive rig. Once in a while, it's long enough for you to sing the prayer so the game would not crash. And when the inevitable comes, you get shot or fall to your death.  Welcome to Quantum Break. Fight the Stutters(tm).  In a less relevant note: the photorealistic graphic, the sounds and gunplay are great; while the fact that you may not touch the controller for more than half an hour is worth a head scratch.  In an even less relevant note: I wrote this while singing that prayer towards the end of the game, which did not work (read: the game crashed).

What can I say, probably THE best and most versatile combat flight simulator by a longshot. It will satisfy any dogfight enthusiast, from the arcade player to the most hardcore realism junkie.   The physics are very well recreated (but one can choose not to activate them for a more casual experience), and players have the options to choose how to equip there plane and wether they want limited ammo/fuel or not. Plane models are near perfect reconstitutions of real WW2 crafts, either built or imagined. The '1946' jets and heliplanes are a sight to see.  Finally, experience missions like a real ww2 pilot : learn the map and fly on sight, as there is no minimap.  I truly recommend this to anyone (it's way better than BF jets, more visceral)

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Although there's not much of a compelling story, the gameplay itself is the real treat. After hearing about how difficult the game is, I gave it a shot, and damn it did not dissapoint. There will be times when you *will* get frustrated, but once you clear the difficulty, the feeling of victory is very satisfying; it's not something you get to feel with many games now.  On the worst days, this game will be completely sadistic and make you hate everything. But when those days pass and victory is achieved.... you just need to feel that sensation....  Note: I played this exclusively on my Nvidia Shield and had 0 problems. I highly recommend you follow the store notice and obtain a 360 compatibile controller. Also, as others have noted, for the PC version, you'll need the DSFix mod so you can play at high resolutions.

You could also bang your wrench on a wall and hurry to a differant place, and the alien will investigate the spot you ran from, ive never done that. I reccomend this game to just about anyone that is not too sensitive. My ex thought skyrim was a scary game, so she would not make it past the first five minutes of this game. app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons isn’t much of a game (at least in the traditional sense), but it’s a great experience. It’s a quick, simple tale of two brothers and their Grand Adventure™ to find the antidote that will save their father. What it lacks in challenge, it makes up for in charm, charisma, and childlike wonder. You simultaneously control each brother individually with your controller’s sticks (or, WASD and arrow keys if you’re feeling masochistic.) Sometimes, you need to work them together, or sometimes you need to split them apart. The game constantly finds new ways to keep things fresh along the journey, though most of it is simply spent trekking through the picturesque landscapes. Brothers is eager to play on your emotions, sometimes more overtly than others. Even though the brothers’ words are unintelligible, the feelings conveyed simply through their inflections develops chemistry between them and fleshes them out to be more than avatars for the player. Brothers is worth playing if you want to sit back, relax, and spend a few hours in its lush, fairy tale world. It’s a breath of fresh air compared to most games today, and a great example of gaming’s breadth of potential.

Receiver is a game that was created by Wolfire Games in a 7 day FPS challenge. The plot of the game is you are what is known as a 'receiver' and you exsist in what people call 'Reality B.' Receiver is known as a rouge game, meaning everytime you die, you take a new body, the rooms are connected in a different order, to top it all off, you start off with a new gun in a different state, maybe even a flashlight. The guns you could receive (no pun intended) in the game are either a Colt M1911A1, a Smith and Wesson Model 10, or a Glock 17 with a full auto mode. It tries to potray a 'realistic gun' in a way (seeing how the graphics are pretty basic); you can manipulate every part of the gun, from safety to pulling the slide half way to check for a chambered bullet. The enemies are what the game calls Killdrones: An flying one with a stun gun on it, and a basic turret. What sucks about the game is how one hit means death. (Hex, Remember to write a transition between death and goal, -Hex.) The objective of the game is to 'absorb' 11 tapes holding information about the world around you. Overall, it's a fun game and I think you should check it out. 8.5/10

Oh god the nostalgia...the nostalgia man.  Myst was one of the first games I ever played as a child. It helped shape the future of adventure games, and became the definitive PC game of the early to mid '90s, only being outsold by the Sims as late as 2002. Myst is a timeless tale of a mysterious island filled with books that transport you to new and fantastic locales. Ever played a game where you point and click through different scenes? Myst probably inspired it.  It wasn't until the last few years or so before I realized just how much Myst really affected me as a gamer. I believe that everyone's gamer identity is in part defined by the games you are first exposed to. So it makes sense to me why I have such a love of atmosphere and immersion, because Myst had that in spades. There's nothing quite like standing on the shores of the island, listening to the wind and the waves.  The game is primarily puzzle based, and that's where most people take issue with Myst. The puzzles are often complicated and convuluted. This remake edition solves some of it, but there's still a lot of head scratching. Myst operates by its own set of rules that you have to figure out as you go along. The game doesn't have a tutorial, or a compass, or anything telling you where to go or what to do (although this edition comes with a hint guide in case you're truly lost). It is really a game about exploration and discovery. And believe me, solving a puzzle on your own is a fantastic sensation.  realMyst: Masterpiece Edition may be a little pricey at nearly twenty bucks, but it all depends on your viewpoint. For me, it was worth it. For some just coming into the series, it might seem a little like price gouging. But if you're interested, I highly recommend checking out this standout title from gaming's youth. This new edition enhances the graphics and includes the free form movement from the original realMyst, making it (in my opinion) the definitive edition of Myst. So whether you're just an old fan looking for a trip down nostalgia lane, or a new arrival, Myst welcomes you to an adventure of mystery. Because, after all, perhaps the ending has not yet been written.  EDIT: I neglected to mention in my original review that this version of the game is sometimes janky. It's not optimized very well at times, which might just be due to the Unity engine itself, but I can't be sure. In any case, it's something you should be aware of if you are thinking of buying it. I never had slowdown so bad it made the game unplayable, but it did come close in the Selenitic age. I still consider it the definitive modern edition of the game, but it is certainly not without its faults.

Bought this game by accident while drunk after getting home from a party. Played it for an hour and found it fun enough to keep, but not fun enough to play a lot. Recently I've had some sort of a Medal of Honor itch. I really wanted to relive some of those great memories I had with the earlier games. I tried the first game on an emulator, found it charming but dated. Frontline looks ghastly these days. Allied Assault, which was by far my favorite back in the day, has not aged that well either. It was groundbreaking at the time, but it has not aged the way other classics such as Half Life have. Then I came to this game. The main difference between this game and the others is it seems to depart from the setpieces and story focus of the other games and is almost entirely gameplay focused. There are no pre-rendered cutscenes, everything happens in game. Each mission you choose your weapons, jump out of a plane, control your parachute and decide where to land, and start completeing objectives. You can coplete them in whatever order you want. You can also choose your combat style, etiher you can take point and advance with an SMG, or lay back, hit the rooftops and provide covering sniper fire for your squad as they advance. The health system is also a nice combination of regeneration and old school depletion which was done even better in the new wolfenstein games. The graphics aren't great but they aren't terrible either. The weapons feel nice, especially the Kar98 and the Thompson, and you recieve upgrades the more you use them. The more you play this game it can kind of feel like you are playing a multiplayer Call of Duty with bots and can get old for sure, but as far as the Medal of Hono games go, it's currently hte most fun to play.

Lichdom is a pretty boring game, that makes you feel like you're throwing ice cubes and flaming potatoes at enemies with axes. It boasts very damage-spongey enemies and it doesn't feel at all like you're the powerful mage you're made out to be. You have slightly creative control over what kind of spells you can create: you can pick three 'elements' to equip, which essentially function as your guns in any other FPS. Each 'gun' has a normal fire mode - which can be set to a ray, cluster-grenade or bolt, an alt fire mode - which is an AOE grenade, trip-mine or lasting effect like a gas grenade, and a shield - which when timed correctly causes splash damage in front of you. It's a neat idea, and quickly switching between the three elements you chose can help stack damage or cause interesting effects. Unfortunately, even the weakest enemies really soak up tons of damage and you mostly find yourself repeating the same cycle of attacks over and over, once you've found a few spells that feel the least flimsy.  You walk a linear route and every 50 meters you enter another arena where you instantly become locked-in with force fields and enemies spawn out of thin air until the arena is considered completed. There's no way to tell how much damage you've done, which enemy is almost dead, or how many enemies you have left until that little section is complete. Then the force fields disappear and you can continue walking down the linear path for 50 meters until the next set of force fields pop up and enemies start spawing in front of your face again. Out of boredom I eventually snapped up a trainer, and even with unlimited health, quickly leveling spells and instantly powered-up charges (hold the mouse button down for a second and your spell becomes 'more powerful'), you STILL feel weak and even the weakest enemies take 5 to ten spells to die. A properly crafted fireball with all your best 'epic' runes won't even make a little wobbly skelleton flinch. I'd really rather take a pistol and pop a hole in it's skull than muck around with flamboyant attempts to trap, freeze and then roast every reanimated skelleton that teleports in front of me every few seconds. There's some very lazy game design going on here and I'd steer clear of it. Lichdom is a pretty but poor execution of a good idea.  If you DO enjoy this game, even remotely, you'll probably have a lot more fun in God Mode. It's similar in that it has arenas to progress through, but it doesn't pretend to have a boring story and it doesn't dress up regular gun-play as something it's not. God Mode is focused on arena combat with guns, where aiming for a head-shot actually makes a difference. And the most fun thing about it? You can play it with friends. Lichdom's only claim to fame is the focus on magic, but if you play it for a few minutes, you'll poke right through that and realize you feel more like a guy weilding very unhandy, weak, flaming-potato slingshots than a wizard.

Overall rating is 6/10.  This is a somewhat fun, addictive and non-traditional soccer (or football, if you will) game. Instead of controlling any of the characters themselves, you instead get access to a wide range of powerups that you can use to influence the outcome of the game. Initially, you begin with just mines that you can place to blow up the enemy players (or even your own, if you're so inclined) but as you progress in the game, you get access to other powerups (some of which include cows that run people over, rockets, UFOs that pick players up, rabid fans that help your team kick the ball, etc).  Each match in the single player mode has an objective, which may range from 'win the match within the time limit' to 'get the score to exactly 5:4'. Therefore, there are times where you'll have to use your weapons on your own team mates to manipulate the score.  The gameplay is relatively fun, whacky, hilarious and addictive, although the game suffers immensely from imbalance. Some items, such as the Red card (that sends a player off the field) are incredibly overpowered and ruin all the challenge in the game. I would actually recommend trying to avoid some overpowered items if possible, as they completely eliminate any difficulty in the game, which further removes any real incentive to use different tactics to complete the objectives.  The graphics and sound are pretty mediocre, although none of this is particularly bothersome for a game of this nature. Apparently this game has multiplayer too, although I haven't tried it out yet. I imagine it'd be relatively fun, particularly if you were able to restrict the use of some items (i.e. the aforementioned red card).   Overall, I would recommend you try out the game, as it did hold my attention enough to actually beat it.

This game was clearly inspired by Dark Souls and Demon Souls, however it falls short on trying to replicate the gameplay in many ways. In many ways it takes on from Dark Souls 2 rather than Dark Souls 1, which in my opinion is a bad thing.  Pros -  * Great graphics. * Game flows decently if kind of slowly. * Voice acting is spot on. * Weapons are pretty neat, if you can actually use them.  Cons - * All weapons are slow. Save perhaps for daggers and smaller weapons, anything bigger than a stick takes a good second to swing regardless of whether it's a normal version or a great (large) version. * It's rather unfair at times. Took me probably an hour just to beat the first boss because he simply turtled the entire time and I was stuck with a one-handed hammer that took a second to swing each time I tried to attack. Keep in mind I JUST STARTED. * The game offers little to no information on anything without you having to dig through menues. I get that I should look at more information on weapons if I want to really minmax or something, but I was using a weapon I couldn't actually weild without even realizing I didn't have the stat requirement. It's extremely confusing and trying to look at things with more information is a pain in the ♥♥♥.  Overall the game seems like it had potential, but with the combination of it trying to be Dark/Demon souls with it's 'difficulty' it just doesn't work. If you like Dark Souls or are looking for a tough game to kick your ♥♥♥ for your mistakes, look elsewhere.  Edit: This game can be summed up as Fan-Made-Dark/Demon-Souls. They wanted to make a game like it, but didn't know what made it so great or at least how to replicate it.

I'll start off with this, most people both over hyped the game and then let the devil lose with rage at this game. I really enjoy this game, my wife and I both play it, she plays on the ps4 and I play on the PC.  I have not experienced any performance issues beyond lower than expected frame rates, but honestly? I don't particularly care, they are working on a fix. Neither my wife or I have experiences any of the issues 90℅ of people are complaining about.  The game is purely exploratory / grinding. You grind to explore and explore to grind. Think of it as a less creative space Minecraft but you take your stuff with you while sacrificing creative freedom. You play this game for a few reasons, you love the grind, you love the unknown, or you love the possibility of experiencing something unexpected.  Yes there are repetitive actions and goals but let's be realistic. If your going to complain that all you do is mine to build then in an FPS all you do is spawn to die. Or shoot to hit. There is no game without repetition, it isn't possible as we can see that even in a randomly generated world there are multiple similarities across systems.  Some people will complain about price, I would argue that CoD games should be 20$ for the amount of experience they offer. If you want to complain about broken games at launch that are trash, hop over to the Division thread. At least Hello Games went above and beyond pulling an all nighter launch day to try and fix their issues. AAA games wait what, a month after launch and don't actually fix anything?  It was a niche game to begin with and all in all they delivered on what they set out to do barring all performance issues, whether people's expectations went wild and they ended up disappointing themselves is their own fault.  I love the game for what it is, exploring and grinding. No stress and just in it for the next experience or screen capture. Sure there are things I wish were different, clearance restriction on planets is a killer but not a deal breaker, the game was obviously made for a controller which I am fine with but that will be a turn off to some.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: Honestly, this was my GOTY for 2015. Yeah, I put more hours into Fallout 4, and yeah, I hate the fact that I have to support Konami to support this game, but MGSV is just so well put together on a technical level that I'm willing to overlook both of those other facts. The controls finally bridge the gap between the super soldier we've been told that Big Boss was and the actual movements we've been able to do as him, and the gameplay has an innate replayability that I haven't really found in many other AAA titles. If only Ubisoft had further refined this tried and tested formula for their next gen game rather than released Unity 3/4 done, who knows what would of happened. Anyways, Rogue is a further refined version of Black Flag, with a fun, gorgeous, and expanded naval section and the same Creed land based stealth we've grown to love (or hate). For me, as one who loves the classic AC experience and adored what AC3 and BF did with the Naval parts, this game is the true next-gen sequel in place of Unity. While Rogue visuals aren't quite as detailed on land as Unity, the sea and ships are stunning. And while Rogue doesn't let you crank up the visuals like Black Flag did to show off its lush jungles, in exchange, you get a vastly smoother experience. Black Flag was very difficult to keep running at 30-45fps without the aid of 3rd party drivers and while I can't speak for Unity's PC counterpart, I doubt it eclipsed 30fps on console or PC in its first few months. Rogue, on the other hand has not dropped below 59fps in my 20+ hours so far (i5 4690k, gtx970) and the few visual options it does offer do make look very pretty indeed. In short, if you're a fan of Black Flag and are looking for it's true successor, then look no further..

I can easily say that it's the best free 3D modelling software out there, it has everything you would like to have in a 3d modelling programs:  -3D painting -Sculpting. -Retopoly tools(Though, if i remember it's an addon you can install, i am not really a sculptor, but sometimes it's useful) -Baking, for anything, really, You can bake normal maps, just baking for realtime preveiw, or for game developement that doesn't really have the best shadow engine. -Contains automatic UV unwrapping for newbies, or just for those who do 3D painting. -It's fully customizable, colors, addons, controls, everything, and really easy to get into. -Has a big and great community, and side addons, they are hardly compared to 3DS max ones, but most of them are even better, than most of paid programs do have. -Also contains one of the most flexible Render engine ever created, it's called 'Cycles', With it you can easily setup many scenes and shaders working together. -And many more! Short free text adventure.  Yes, it's free, but is it worth your time? The plot consists of a handful of short scenes that lack a point. You lose a telescope and go retrieve another. You go exploring but don't find anything. You see danger approach but nothing happens. You cauterize a wound and pour whiskey over it (waste of good whiskey), yet stay sick. And in between all this, diary pages that flash you back and make time and equipment skips: now you have candles, now you don't; remove shoes and jacket, but removing pants is 'a flawed plan', even with a leg wound. Wet your hanky in the ocean and use it to combat sunstroke? Unthought of.  Technically, the game works, but storywise, you're going to be pushed along the rails the author intended, never in charge of your destiny, towards an ending that is in the dark and doesn't motivate you to get there. It just about might be readable as a short story (but if you want to read about being stranded on an unknown island, get  Lord of the Flies ), but as a game or interactive fiction, it seems a failure to me. I have played much better.    If you are attracted to the theme of sadly being stranded on an island with people that died of disease and diary pages of reminiscences, try  Montague's Mount : not everybody likes it, but at least there's an actual island you can explore in 3d.

Review copy provided by developer  Described by the creators as a point and survival game Skyhill sees your character survive a nuclear apocalypse and after eventually running out of food and supplies having to venture out from his protected penthouse on the 100th floor with the intention of reaching the ground.  The main mechanics of the game are managing both your health and hunger as you travel down each stairwell with the option of investigating both rooms on each floor. Every move you make uses up one point of hunger so sensibly navigating the games rooms is essential. Another key mechanic is the games combat. As you meet an enemy you must select your weapon and then choose which body part you are going to attack. Different body parts have a higher percentage chance of missing but equally harder to hit parts do significantly more damage. This turns the combat into a logic puzzle as you get further down the building and the enemies get harder and harder.  In order to help manage your hunger and health there is a well fleshed out crafting system to allow you to craft food, resources and weapons as well as a level up system with a simple set of stats you can add to.  As you descend you unlock access to the elevator that allows you to travel back up to your penthouse and improve food and weapons. It also becomes important to use you Penthouse to sleep and regain life especially on the higher difficulties as enemies become harder and first aid kits become scarcer. This works by increasing your health but reducing your hunger bar meaning you are generally starving when you awake.  There are a few issues I have with the game to do with balance. I will say that with each play through there is an active and passive ability to help you that you can choose at the start. Some are more helpful than others with a particular overpowered favorite of mine being Bulimia which reduces your hunger loss by 50% but makes it possible to take damage when you eat. Equally there are a few weapons that are easier to gain than others that are very powerful the clock work axe being a good example of this.  Now I am not usually a fan of roguelikes but there really is something about Skyhill that grabbed me and would not let me go. As you progress you are drip fed storied through articles you find as well as some key bits of information about side plots that only become apparent through multiple playthroughs and alternate endings.  Having completed the game relatively easily on easy and just about on normal the hard is proving difficult and the currently locked nightmare mode is about as far from seeming possible to me right now as it could be.  All in all Skyhill is a very original roguelike game that is different from anything I have played before and is well worth your time. Games take a reasonable length and it never outstays its welcome with subsequent playthroughs drip feeding you more and more of the games story.  Cannot recommend it enough though, fantastic game.  For our video review check out the video below   http://www.thegametutor.co.uk/videos/game-tutor-grades/150929_game-tutor-grades-skyhill

Refunct  isn't good or bad, it's simply another option among the multitude of things to do. I recommend doing one of those other things instead, but if this sounds like your idea of a good time you probably won't be disappointed.  After my first several minutes with  Refunct  it struck me as a cross between  Mirror's Edge  and  Q*bert . As I bounced from platform to platform in the first person to change its color I was discouraged by the distinct lack of parkour mechanics. However the evolving landscape guided me toward discovering the parkour mechanics just beneath the surface of the gameplay and not only was I pleased with this extra layer of depth, but I was also impressed with how the game design revealed the game mechanics.  From then on, interacting with various features of the environment to reach the next chunk of the level passed without event. I was apprehensive about missing a jump and landing in the water until again the evolving environment dumped me in the water and I realized that my character could swim. That was a relief. Then the sky began to darken and I was filled with a sense of dread in anticipation of the monsters that invariably come out at night, but night passed without incident. I came to enjoy sundown in  Refunct  because I liked how the shadows lengthened and moved across the world.  After 32 minutes I had 99%ed the game.  Refunct  is everything it is advertised to be: it is short, it is colorful, and it is peaceful. Peaceful however is not necessarily soothing or relaxing. The colors of the world do not make for draw dropping visuals. The short length of the experience isn't a strike against it, either. It's a short, low-pressure romp through a colorful environment, and that is it.  Refunct  isn't good or bad, it's simply another option among the multitude of things to do. I recommend doing one of those other things instead, but if this sounds like your idea of a good time you probably won't go wrong.

What happens when you combine an Isometric Action RPG with an MMO? A game that almost works, but not quite. That is because one significant design choice reallt hurts the gameplay: Attacks Always Hit. There are skill shots in the game, both friendly and hostile, and they are the attacks that hit like a truck, but alongside these skillshots are attacks that follow the target, and automatically hit, If anybody swings a sword, it doesn't matter if the target leaves melee distance, that melee attack hits. If a witch attacks you with a ranged attack, no amount of dodging will stop her homing missle of free damage. This is a sacrifice made, I am assuming, to combat latency, allowing for the experience of an Isometric Action RPG with many people all over the world. But in my opinion this sacrifice was too high of a cost. I can ignore a bad story: not saying this has a bad story. I can look past an artistic direction I do not care for: not saying it looks bad. But I do not want to slug through unsatisfying gameplay, and to me, the introduction of auto-hit attacks makes the game unsatisfying.   As a Hardcore (Perma-death) Diablo player, this made the gameplay unsatisfying. The rest of the game was enjoyable, but due to my dissatisfaction, I cannot give this game my recommendation.

Okay so there's a few disclaimers here.  1. I've been wanting to play this game since before it was released, so needless to say I was kind of excited to get it on sale. If you don't have that deep desire to play this 'game' it may not turn out so well for you, like most of other reviewers would tell you.  2. I wouldn't necessarily call this a 'game' so much as an 'interactive story'. No shooting. No zombies. No real puzzles. Just click, observe, read, find combination, read some more, and feels...ohh the feels.  The bad: - Incredibly short. Could have been developed on further, but would have proven detrimental to the story. This isn't about the gameplay, it's about the story, and any added puzzles or interactive nonsense would have taken away from that. - For some, the lack of 'gameplay' is irritating and disqualify this as a 'game'. I say pish posh, different strokes for different folks.  - Price. Luckily, I picked this up for $5, but I don't see how anyone could justify paying the full $20 for this game.   The Good: - Story story story story story. The story, albeit a slight bit predictable and not really surprising, is still amazing and heartfelt. Trying to unravel this yarn while dealing with the creepy dark house on a stormy night with this overwhelming sense that something terrible has happened to your family and then finally finding out what's happened is a sensation that my words can't describe. Just know that there are feels of many shapes, sizes, and colors, and there are oh so many feels. So many. gah. - Very interactive. You can spend 10 minutes just looking at stuff in a living room. Belive me, I did.  - Immersive experience. Throughout the game, I could imagine being in this situation and how it would feel coming home to an abandoned house and nothing seems right. That sense of urgency when looking for a light switch in a pitch black room knowing good and well there shouldn't be anyting in there but can't bring yourself to believe it until the light comes on. That sense of relief when you find out that what you think has happened is just you jumping to outrageous conclusions. An experience like that is something I have seldom felt in all my years of gaming.  - Have I mentioned the story is awesome?  All in all, I loved it. I hoped my expectations would live up to reality and was pleasntly surprised they did. This is a great interactive story that you won't forget if you're willing to take the chance.

i truly loved this experience. It was such an elegant little story with such clever exposition. i'm hesitant to call it a 'game' per say, because it's more of an interactive novella, but very engaging and refreshing nonetheless.
