app_name: Sword of the Stars: The Pit
review_text: Sword of the Stars: The Pit is a great roguelike title with plenty of depth and only some minor drawbacks. The Pit features so much randomization and replay value that it becomes quite addictive to continue playing once you're say... 15 minutes through a game. Being a roguelike, there is of course permanent death in this game. You die and it's all over, your score is added to a high-scores board and then you have to start all over again with one of three playable characters. But chances are you won't really care all that much, as beginning a new game doesn't really feel as daunting as you would imagine it to be. This video game is also a very user-friendly title for anyone that may be new to the roguelike genre. If you have ever played and enjoyed Dungeons of Dredmor and fancy a change of setting, consider this to be the sci-fi DoD.

Here are two lines from a recent update:  *Max number of commanders increased 9990 -> 20000 *Max number of units increased 160000 -> 250000   The graphics may be less than appealing, but this game has serious heart and heritage. Quality updates continue to trickle in, and I'm more than glad some of my money went to help the devs, and the future of Dominions.  For a 'real' review, I'll talk about how the game plays. For each turn, you: 1. create new units or commanders and assign troops to those commanders 2. decide if you want to build any structures (temple, science-research, and castle/fortress/fortifications, basically) 3. assign new magic-school research, if needed 4. issue move orders, if any 5. End Turn. I mention this because I know, at first glance, Dominions 4 seems like it might be one of those 'Cool concept, but too complicated to play!' kind of games, when it is not..  The game is nowhere near shallow, despite the above statement. Each faction (spanning through three different eras of time) has many tricks and strategies, some obvious and some downright devious, and 'learning' a faction is quite challenging.. Adapting to your opponents strategy is also serious business..  There is no 'diplomacy' here.. If Pangaea has a massive army of elite Shark Tribe warriors supported with massive underwater behemoths, well.. There is no 'playing nice' :) Your goal is to conquer, one way or another..  Anyway, I'm sure there are far better reviews than mine, so I'll quit rambling. Just wanted to show my support and toss another vertically-inclined thumb into the mix ;)

Difficult, brutal side scrolling dungeon crawler.   Your characters will die.  Random rolls will make you furious.  You will rage quit.  You'll love every minute... Dark Souls was tough but fun and I am having the same type of fun with this. the soundtrack by Stuart Chatwood (of Canadian band the tea Party) is moody and perfect.

First and most importantly I find this game fun. The game has a shallow learning curve and the FBTHF (From Boot To Have Fun) ratio is about as low as any game since Doom & Doom2.  Now, one thing you will immediately notice is that your heroes have flaws. Yes, thats right, not the super hero movie kind of flaws that are 'cool to have flaws' ('I'm not a hero, I kill people' or 'I'm too perfect for my own good'); no your heroes have real flaws like being a cheat, petty thief, or having a 'holier than thou' attitude. These quirks manifest themselves in game by preventing you from easily managing stress levels and lowering combat attributes among other things. This is part of the game. Its what makes the game fun. If you are looking for perfect heroes who just need a healing potion and then off to the next adventure you probably don't want this game.  In my opinion the game is well crafted with bonuses like cool narration and a well done Gothic art style to boot.   Also, the game makes me believe that Red Hook, the organization behind this game, realizes that people are going to be imperfect and yet they can still offer great value. Perhaps this game should be used by management students and/or in onboarding processes to prepare management for the complexities of real world personalities -- or maybe they can just play it as a stress relief activity :-D . In any case I would bet that most would find the game fun. I sure do!

Come on, €/$10?  In conclusion, gets boring really fast and is really repetitive. Actually, what would you expect from a crash test game?...

Edit: If you want to see the game in action, I strongly suggest you check out the  League of Geeks Twitch channel  [www.twitch.tv]  Sundays at 6:00pm Pacific time for their weekly Devs vs. Community game. It's a good time and a great place to ask any questions you might have and actually get them answered.  Since I bought it back in November, Armello has become one of my all-time favorite multiplayer games (still narrowly beat out by Timesplitters, but what can you do?).  Each match is a 4-player free-for-all power grab to rule the land. There are multiple ways to accomplish this:   Combat : Break into the Palace and kill the King in single combat.   Prestige : Having the most renown when the King dies naturally (he loses 1 health every other turn) or if another players kills him, but dies themselves in the attampt.   Spirit Stones  Collecting 4 spirit stones that may be found in dungeons, rewarded from quests, or randomly spawned on the map then breaking into the Palace and use them to banish the corruption from the king (which, conveniently, also kills him).   Rot : The corrupting force in the game that has infected the king and spurred the heroes into action against him. Though it very difficult to pull off, a player can achieve a higher Rot value than the King and then Break into the Palace and kill the King in combat. This is very similar to a Combat Victory, but when 2 opponents both have Rot, the one with the higher count gains the lower's count as bonus dice. Against the King (who is for the most part a stronger combatant than any of the heroes), this is both a great advantage and hinderance (You roll more dice but for every die you roll that doesn't get matched to attack or defence goes to the King to roll against you). Basically, pulling off a Rot Victory is mostly for bragging rights.  My favorite part of this game has to be that, even if the whole game is going against you, the other players attack you, banes attack you, your card draw is terrible, etc. etc. etc, it is still possible to sometimes go for the last-ditch-effort break into the palace and attack the king. At worst, you'll die killing him and give the game to the Prestige leader (or just die, granting the king 1 more health and extending the game for 2 more turns), but there's always the chance that you can kill him and win, turning around a game that had been going against you the whole time, and snatching victory for yourself. These are the wins that are always the most memorable because steamrolling your opponents is only fun so many times. The best victories are a  struggle .  After every (online/non-private) match, you are given a chance at either a key or chest that, once you have one of both, you can use to unlock new dice skins. Winning has no apparent effect on the drop rates, though keys do seems to be somewhat less common than chests (despite which one you get being represented by a coin flip). The keys, chests, and skins can all be traded/sold on the Steam marketplace.  The biggest (non bug related) complaint I have is that, since the map is randomized for every match, sometimes you just get completely screwed on the placements of settlements, dungeons, mountains and swamps (either for too much or not enough of any of them near your spawn grounds).  The biggest bug related problem is that sometimes, when a player disconnects, the whole match will feeze and everyone will be forced to quit out, negating the end-of-the-match reward. This seems to be more likely if it happens while someone is in combat, and is definitely the biggest bug/glitch problem the game has.  One other point of irritation is, since you can sell the rewards from the end of a match, I occassionally run into bots/scummers that pass every turn (some contend that it is almost literally every match, but I think they're just especially bitter people in general). The especially awful ones spam the emotes constantly. Having one less player usually makes the game significantly less interesting and fun. Furtunately, you can report these wastes of air through the in-game bug report feature.  At first it might seem like everything's random so you're success or failure is based solely on RNG, but as a long time player of XCOM (new and original), your success or failure is based more on how you operate with the options you're given, rather than the options themselves.  TL;DR: If you think games that rely on RNG don't take any skill, go ahead and skip this one. You'll only wind up bitter.

Wonderful game! Its fine as vanilla, and even better with mods. It's like having your own living model railroad, without spending a fortune. As the game goes on, the towns grow toward your stations, buildings, cars, and even the pedestrian's clothing styles change. You can spend hours just getting your lines set perfectly; the learning curve is not too steep, yet the gameplay is pretty complex, with each individual citizen having their own schedule, home, and workplace.  All that complexity has a bit of a price though; I don't recommend playing a map size larger than 'small' if you have a 32 bit system. Even with 64 bits, the game will start to lag toward endgame (there are now mods to help with that though.)  The graphics are a bit cartoonish, which I think was the point, its like a model railroad, not intended for hardcore realism. The sounds are fine, but the music soundtrack leaves a bit to be desired IMO, it doesn't fit the mood of the game at all.  Overall, I HIGHLY recommend this game if you are interested in trains, model railroads, or transit systems at all.

If you belong to movie fans Mission to Mars, The Martian, or Interstellar, then this game will for you.  Before play first act, we got simple introduction. Earth is on the verge of ecological destruction. Desperate humanity begins search for new worlds to colonize, and we play as a member of one those responsible for this mission teams of explorers. The aim of the research expedition was reach to the planet, made establish base there, exploration planet to the research and preparation for the next expansion of the settlers. After twenty years of travel stellar, research expedition of The Solus 3 arrive at the place, but the landing on the planet ends in an accident, of which the result killed the entire crew! (of course except of character player) Worse, the damage is most of the hardware, including communications equipment. Thus, we are completely alone, and we can count only on themselves! Just you and mystery planet!  The Solus Project is based on survival. The game included simplified mechanics of crafting with logical elements as also the discovery of secrets, hidden by the world and equipment recovered in the course of the story. Player need sleep, must maintain proper body temperature or the level of food and hydration. Must avoid injuries resulting from the fall or confrontation with hostile flora and fauna. You can die by starvation, hypothermia, overheating, drowning, hurricane, lightning storm or even meteor shower!  Visually, we get unreal engine 4 with excellent sound effect and music, by what the game encourages you to explore all parts of the world, filled with the many secrets waiting to be discovered. Each sector is filled with monumental ancients structures, which can visit on your own or as a result of the progress of the story. Realy nice to see. Pleasant atmosphere with a touch of fantasy.   I had good fun and I recommend try it!   9/10 - requires patience and determination to finish each act. There is a lot of walking and long distance from point to point and sometimes after long walking (special in caves) it becomes plunge.

review_text: ahhhhhhh the memories :)

Going to be a longer review - so be aware. (TL;DR - It's a MUST!)  I'll be honest here, I pirated the game for the first time. I didn't know what to expect, just wanted to give it a shot. After my first playthrough I was shocked - in a good way. After 2 weeks have passed I bought the Limited Edition box set and played it again from the beginning. This here is one of THE best games out there in existence. And it deserves this title for several reasons.  If someone doesn't believe that videogames are art, show this game to them! If someone is going through a harsh phase in life, show this game to them! If someone is in front of a major decision (regarding anything) and doesn't know what to do, show this game to them! If someone says 'I don't have a soul!', show this game to them! If someone wants a compelling and truly thoughtful story, show this game to them! And the list goes on and on...  The music, the atmosphere, it's all there and very well executed. It pulls you even deeper into the story - my recommendation at this point is to play it with a high-end headset.  This game was a life changer for me, and I think I'm going to play it several times a year. Although the story is fictional you can relate events, persons, locations from your own life to every one of them in the game.  Only two other games made me so impressed in my entire life so far: One of them is the classic 'Flight of the Amazon Queen' - For me it's THE best point-and-click adventure game (yes, even better than Monkey Island), with a humorous story, exotic and diverse locations and great voice acting!  The other one is called 'The Cat Lady'. Now this is something different. I don't recommend this game to everyone (it focuses on themes, like death, suicide, depression stc.) BUT if you play it through, you won't sleep for a week because your thoughts will be swirling like a whirlpool. It's the deepest game out there - hands down.  So don't hesitate and buy this game (digital or not) you won't regret it and won't be disappointed. Cheers!

A few weeks in and I have trouble coming back to FO4. I have a few peers who feel the same, and we've discussed it at length what might be the core issue that makes us hesitate before clicking 'play'. ... To date, we've yet to narrow it down to a single cause. Be it new mechanics like base-building that turned us off, or a wasteland however varied, too-similar to fallout 3 and New Vegas, or even the characters/factions with all their charms - falling just shy of memorable; there was just not a terribly compelling reason to come back to the wastes of Boston after an initial 15-20 hour romp.   Now, I've never been to Boston, so I have no real connection to the area besides what I've read about. So if you have any real knowledge of the region, by all means indulge in this for what it is: a heartfelt homage to the area in one version of post-apocalypta. Otherwise, be prepared to follow my footsteps and utilize console commands to prolong the fun this title has stored.

Note:   This review is mostly of the multiplayer components , you know the things you're supposed to spend most of your time on after you beat the story once or twice.  Despite being born after the original Doom was released, It's just not Doom. I've had played enough of it and various mods including Brutal Doom, which this game seemingly takes a lot of notes from. The gore is there, but shooting feels very spongy most of the time and in other instances feels way too OP, the balancing almost feels like it was decided a month or two after the game was set to release. At the end of the day, I can only see myself playing it further with friends in the map editor and completing the story. The multiplayer has no redeeming qualities that really make me want to sink my teeth into it, I got enough of it in beta and even at that I was bored after a few hours due to how linear it is, and even with the multiple game modes, everything feels like a TDM.      Multiplayer Review:    The multiplayer is pretty generic, feels VERY console oriented, feels like CoD in a lot of ways and by that I mean tasteless and bland. The maps are tiny maps, poor respawn spots and a guaranteed spawn kill awaits every respawn. But, once you get the hang of the respawning it gets pretty easy to get some kills, once you get used to getting kills the newness of the game wears off and then you realize there's people who are level 30+ and it makes you wonder when the fun starts. And no, it didn't change much at all from the beta. The 'hook' or 'gotcha' to keep you playing is to upgrade your character, and the only thing you get are skins which thankfully offer no stats since there's MANY signs pointing to skin packs being a thing, I.E micro-transactions.      Map Editor Review:    Initially the controls are very bad, they still are honestly, but thankfully they're rebindable. The tiles are rather boring, despite lots of blocks and tiles being available, it's hard to tell what they are till you place them, which involves switching modes and flying around for every block. All of the blocks require players to be funneled into a tiny little door way, so the thought of making a large open box map becomes impossible due to this design, so essentially there's a choke point at every turn and corridor, which isn't terrible considering the games design so it's not terribly out of place, but it's not exactly great and partially hinders creation. However, that being said, the map editor does have a slight level of complexity that allows for some neat scripted events and scenarios to play out, which can be seen by the developer made showcase maps.      Story Review:    Will update review for story once completed.

If you're looking for a new MMO made within the last few years, this is easily one of the best. Unlike most MMO's on the market, FF14 is actually story driven. While the writing isn't always the most amazing, and the voice acting can be hit or miss on some characters, the overall end product is quite well presented. And the story itself is overall quite good, and does a great job at making you feel heroic and important at appropriate times. There are innumerable non-main storyline quests available if you care to do them. Every class has their own unique story to follow. And some side quests that are hidden away for you to find are true gems. There are hundreds upon hundreds, if not thousands of hours, of content to do in quests alone, depending on your pace and weather you're the kind to read everything offered. (Which I suggest, particularly in regards to speaking with nearby characters during main story quests to get an abundance of bonus dialogue that can often be amusing, insightful, and interesting.)   Many of the areas within the game are quite beautiful, making exploration quite enjoyable for new players, and older players who have long since seen everything will find many 'favorite spots' dotted about the game that they can always return to if they feel like it. Some scenes are truely a sight to behold when seen at just the right time.  As for the gameplay, for anyone who has played Square's previous MMO, FF11, you might find the grind to be somewhat familiar. Or, some might find it to be conciderably easier. Your milage may very based on personal perspective and experience. In terms of leveling, it's far from as mindnumbing as many other games, as Square included several systems to lessen the time sink involved in leveling, in addition to offering items that can increase exp gain. Casual play could see you through to cap with your first class, mostly by following the main quest, within a few weeks provided you read and watch everything. Rushing hard and skipping everything you could cap within a week, maybe two depending on your free time. Combat, contrary to many shallow complaints by people who likely never saw a job past 30, is not extremely slow. A global CD of 2 seconds is not the end of the world, and becomes largely irrelevant rather quickly as classes accumulate off gdc abilities you can use between them. Without even reaching the original~ level cap, you will find most every class has something you should be doing at all times provided you aren't being deliberately lazy and coasting through combat on one or two buttons whie your party does the work. That's a player problem, not a game problem.   While there is mountains more that could be said about any number of things, if anything should be mentioned, it's the flaws within the game. It's not perfect. The game's dungeons and raids can range in difficulty from childishly easy, to eye gougingly, aggrevating, difficult. Especially if you have poor party members. Square's idea to lock some content behind a pre-made party wall makes playing all the end game content difficult without a dedicated group to play with. And depending on what server you join, there may not be much, or any, end game playerbase, while others might consist of nothing but end gamers standing around waiting for queues. As amazing as the game looks, many areas are devoid of relavant content past certain levels. As a result, many places will appear entirely dead because there's no reason for anyone to return after a certain point outside of leveling or rare events like hunt mob spawns. Again, this is largely server dependant, but is~ a problem none-the-less. It causes players to concentrate in select few places over time and leaves the world feeling empty, even when a server is completely full. Playing with Free Company members, or joining newbies that you see and taking the opportunity to level something new can do wonders to avoid this problem. Or be a loner that doesn't care about other players being around. That also works, but is kinda boring to me. Perferences. Once you've spent an inordinant amount of time in the game, you might find that the selection of dungeons, while large, grows increasingly repetative after you've leveled multiple classes and have seen the same boss for the 200th time. It's annoying. While not a problem for new players, or even for most players within the first several months or more of playing. For long, long time players. It's aggrevating. The game needs more dungeons, especially of the lower end leveling kind. The biggest flaw in the game, and the quickest to grow tiresome, are the FATES. Randomly spawning overworld combat events. Square overuses FATES for literally everything they can shoehorn them into. Seasonal/special events? Go do FATES. Grinding end game relic gear? Go do FATES. Grinding levels? FATES. FATES get old, and they don't age well. In a few, rare instances they're done well. While it might occationally be lackluster now, with low player turnouts due to the new expansions and higher lvl cap making the fight easier, coupled with fewer people actually needing the rewards. Keep your eyes open for Odin in the black shroud if you do play. That~ is a fate that was done right, and is still exciting to stumble upon even to this day, where I have long since passed any need to participate in it. And if it's on a good day, you might experience something rather impressive to behold, as others in the past have when finding him for the first time.   In summery, the game isn't perfect. It's got problems. At least a few more than are listed here. And some common complaints are often blown far out of proportion by people who haven't, or refused, to get invested in the game or give it a fair chance. But it's got an abundance of good points as well. A great story. Great presentation. Solid gameplay. Ongoing support which will someday amount to an even larger game than it already is. Possibly far larger (hopefully). A growing class/job list that provides plenty of variety, all at your fingertips on a single character, and much more.   Of all the recent MMOs that I've played, and I've played more than a fair few. FF14 is head and shoulders above the rest, with a potentially strong future to boot. I'd easily give it 9/10. The flaws it has aren't enough to hold it back from being great, and only really~ impacted me after thousands~ of hours of playtime, and I'll keep playing whenever the opportunities arrise in the future.

Everybody's Gone to the Rapture is a walking simulator. Full stop. If you don't like walking around some pretty envrironments SOLELY for the story, this probably isn't for you. It is first and formost a story, and not much of a 'game' in a traditional sense. However, the story it tells is compelling and extremely well acted, as all of the characters have a depth that comes out due to the amazing performances of the voice actors/actresses. Key moments of this acting are then swollen to incredible crescendo's thanks to the phenomenal soundtrack as well, and if you're curious at all I highly recommend giving yourself a taste of the music before you purchase the game. It is easily Jessica Curry's best work, even above the beauty of Dear Esther and A Machine for Pigs. The game itself is almost an evolution of Dear Esther in many other ways aside from sound as well; feeding the observer a story in a much more organic way than a voice over as you walk the shores and caves of an island. The world and characters The Chinese Room creates in Rapture is almost tangible, the detail and creation of the town so close to the source material I could imagine being in Britain at the time, despite never having actually been there.  There are, however, a few bumps in the long road from Yaughton. While most of the story and characters are actually very nicely cleaned up, the ending of the game (while not as terrible as some say) does leave a few more questions than answers. Occasionally, while the game does a fair job of guiding you to everything, you can miss a few small scenes and getting back to them after you've finished a section can be a bit slow and tedious. This is really the only time the slower walk speed becomes an issue, as I found for the usual wandering the speed was absolutely fine - there's so much detail to look at that the slower walk speed allows for a greater sense of place. Personally, if I was exploring an abandoned town, I wouldn't be sprinting for five hours solid between houses. But, to each their own. The game in general is geared towards a patient audience, and doesn't compromise on it's vision of a story for the sake of appealing to a broader gaming crowd.  If you enjoy a quiet, yet powerful story and want to spend a few hours in a town seperate from your own time and place, I absolutely recommend Rapture. Really though, if you're not a fan of the 'walking simulator' style of story telling, that's all this is. Even if you're on the fence, wait for a sale and see if you like it. I quite enjoyed it though, and it shows how Chinese Room still knows their game with interactive story telling.

TL;DR: The good: The game is stupid funny. Graphics are nice. Time between waves is made just perfect. So the player is engaged into playing all the time without going somewhere. There is very good balance between fortress and troll. It is challenging at the right pace.   The bad: There is no 'FAIL!' state. As in, at all. You just lose a wave, but all it does is that you can't collect the loot dropped from enemies and your troll starts with minimum HP. Interface is somewhat clunky, so you'll have to get used to it. Finished whole game in 1 day on easy... And don't want to play it again on medium / hard.  The neutral: Somewhat bad pathfinding for troll for RAM attack.  _______________ The good: 'Stupid funny' as in, if you like throwing things around in games, you'll like this one. It takes little time to get used to the game and then you have hours and hours of fun. Like, a whole day. But that's just it, it will last just a day. HOWEVER! The day will be full of lulz, laughing and, if you'll get the moment, funny screenshots. And the 'Ram' attack can be brought into good because even if it leads to you failing a wave, it's a no problem. You can't fail here. Like, at all. So all the more reason for laughing how troll 'dances' around the enemies that he tries to ram. While trampling everyone around those enemies.  I actually laughed while typing this when remembered how this worked well. And failed horribly a few times.  Playing this game 'smart' or 'serious' way will take away the fun. For me, playing games by reading guides and calculating my moves is the standard. Here, I didn't want to do that. I didn't NEED to do that to have fun. It was just 'bumble around, wreck havoc'. 'You're a big troll, just run over your enemies! SMASH!'  Graphics. World of Warcraft style graphics. Just enough, but no more. You won't find super shaders, flares or whatever. It's just enough. But if you like things 'fancy', then get a DLC with suits. Dress up your Troll. Why I don't say it's a bad thing? Well, because for the most time it's useful to be zoomed out maximum to have control over battlefield.  Game pace. Okay. You get something done, you look at your fortress, you look at troll upgrades, OH NEW WAVE!!! SMASHING TIME! Now let's see, something to upgrade, maybe this, maybe that? New wave! BASHING TIME! The pace goes as you read this text in a slow pace. Pause is a useful button, but don't use it at first or second fortress at early stages. There isn't that much you can do, so don't try to brainstorm it. Smash, bash and trample stuff.   Balance of Fortress vs Troll. Your fortress IS a fortress. Don't forget that. Your troll is NOT invincible. Don't forget that. Keep both in mind, never lose. Got to arrogant and got surrounded? Ouch. Forgot about fortress and didn't trample those who were bashing it? Restart the wave. Ran around like an idiot through ranks of enemies till they all were on their backs while your fortress was shoting them? That might just work. Pss... Abilities rock.    Challenge. At the easy level it's possible just to look at how the last wave went and figure out what needs upgrading first: fortress or Troll? Both are important. If one is lacking, the other will pick up SOME slack, but not all of it.   FAILing. You can not fail. At all. This was, somewhat, frustrating when I got to Survival. Eventually got all achievements at easy and... Um... Now what? I can go and play on medium, but I don't expect to see anything new. There are no achievements for beating game on Medium or Hard. Or some special achievements for beating bosses on those difficulties.   Paying again? Might go for Medium or Hard some day, but not sure.

Left 4 Dead 2. These words bring memories of spending hours into the morning, killing zombies with friends. Oh, how I miss those days. Anyways, Left 4 Dead 2 is an incredible game from the Valve corporation. You play as one of four playable characters - with DLC, eight - Nick, Coach, Rochelle, or Ellis. After you choose your character, you will be taken through one of six campaigns. It's amazing! This concept works perfectly. It gets even better with co-op. That's right, co-op. Now, you can kill millions of zombies... with friends! Even after you finish the single-player mode, there's multiple multiplayer modes, as well. I won't get into details, but, all of these game modes continue to bring me back, day after day. I give Left 4 Dead 2 a 10/10 - not because I think it's the best game in the world, but, for what it has, it does it wonderfully well. Well done, Valve, you did it again.

Before March I didn't know much about Dota 2, I heard about it and that was all. And then that day came when in March I launched Dota 2 for the very first time. I was excited, didn't know much what to expect and was kinda looking forward to finally play it. Now at this point I must also say that a had a friend who usually plays MOBA and RPG games and was trying to persuade me to start playing Dota 2 with him and was always saying him maybe next time. Well fast forward 2-3 months I got an Invite to Dota 2 Beta. I said lets at least try it and we will see. Together with me was that friend( on skype) and then I accepted my first match.   I was hyped, the screen showed simple but kinda cool logo of Dota2 and then the hero screen came, I didn't know much and hero list was big so I clicked on Random hero and I got Timber. Even though I had my friend with me I sucked bad... hehe I sucked really bad and feed for like 7 games straight. My friend who was my coach those first games really helped me and with the help of him and tutorial videos on youtube I started to familiarize myself to Dota and for those first games even though I was really bad I was starting to get to know the basics of the game. And yea even though I sucked those first game I was enjoying myself :D  It didn't bother me if someone called me a noob as long I was enjoying myself. Games went I was starting to play more like the game wanted me. I played a lot of game since then and i'm still enjoying myself when I have with me people who actually knows we need a support and we can't always have 4 carries :D  It's true game is a little bit hard and you need to have someone with you who knows dota or you need to at least watch those tutorials on youtube. but when you learn this things game really opens up and you're set to play :D I also really like cosmetic items and the way the dota allows people to create some great looking items and hopefully put them into the game. Pro scene is also great, I watched TI 2013 and I saw my favorite team win :D Games sometimes turn really crazy and that's the charm of dota.   This game is really great when you get to know things. So new players don't lose hope, watch some tutorials, play safe and you should be set.  I rate this game 9/10.

WTF is it? - Orcs Must Die! is a third-person trap laying orc slaying monster delaying tower defense game. It is the second game made by Robot Entertainment whose studio is made up of people from the now defunct Ensemble Studios. Why is that significant? Well at one point in my life I was a console gamer, I won't go into specifics because those were dark times filled with low framerates, but I digress. The game that I played the most on xbox was Halo Wars which was developed by Ensemble. Being the amazing devs they are, they have yet again impressed me with Orcs Must Die! The objective of the game is to stop orcs from reaching the rift(s) on each level by placing traps and using weapons. Each level has multiple waves of orcs that increase in difficulty over the rounds and if enough orcs enter the rift(s) the rift score drops to zero and you lose. The plot of the game is surprisingly interesting aswell since you play as a War Mage who is the last member of an order dedicated to defending the rifts which are sources of magic for the two worlds. I think they could have gone more in depth with the story even though this is a somewhat silly game.  Verdict - Well worth the time and money.  BTW - This is better than that Rift mmo.

This game is a masterpiece. I think this game set a STANDERD for most of the 2D sandbox rpg games I see popping up all the time on steam. Terraria is highly confused to be like Minecraft, but no it is way different. Minecraft kinda lacks in the rpg survival elements and it pretty much ends with you building random stuff, which I can get bored of easily. Terraria has soooo many weapons, armours, classes, to choose from and bosses and enemies to fight. Even if you put mods onto it, it still can't compare to that. Minecraft has crappy villagers, terraria has actually npcs! So that is out of the way, and I hope I had changed your mind about the game if you thought it was too much like minecraft. I was like that once, but a really good games is what i found behind it.   This game is so smooth, it runs very well, even on a Windows 8 which is what I currenlty have. I have found very few bugs in this action packed game. The 2D makes it seem pretty deciving but it actually works pretty well. Not only is this game a mining and building sandbox game but it is an RPG too. I love the elements like npcs, weapons, and armours. They even started a sidequest thingy with the angler. Maybe more npcs will be added with sidequests. Pretty much any real (Not focused on bug fixing) update in terraria is packed with new things! If you like RPGs and MMOs, If you like building and mining, and if you like to enjoy a fun, 2D, and longlasting game, I recommend this to you.  I hope this had helped.

I know I might get flak for this, but if I could give this game a middle-of-the-road 'Eh' rating instead of a recommendation, I'd do that. I think the part that people will most find a problem with is that Wolfenstein: The New Order is exactly what it advertises itself to be. It's a shooter... where you kill Nazis... in pretty environments... like an action hero. It does that part fantastically. I just don't want to recommend a game because it did what it set out to do well. I rate it by how much fun I found it. The answer to that? A resounding... Eh.  To the game's credit, it takes the elements it sets out to perfect and does them to a tee. Combat flows seemlessly between a variety of creative environments, each new area feels rich and original, and the story has such an interesting vibrancy to it that there were times where I felt like the plot was the element keeping me going, rather than gameplay. The characters are interesting enough, and there are a handful of moments that tug at your heartstrings, which completely threw me off guard when I expected the main character to be another short-haired meat head white male who grunts out action lines. I mean, he -is- that, but it's still done well.  Unfortunately, that's where my praise ends. The stealth segments in this game are pretty laughable, with AI that makes Skyrim NPC behavior look revolutionary. The weapons feel like mild to major rehashes of traditional weapons (shotgun, rifle, pistol, AK47), and the few spins they do take on the weapon design feel unnecessary or slow. Even the enemy design gets a bit lazy. Big guy with gun shoot gun at you. Other big thing with gun also shoot. You shoot rocket at big thing. Big thing go boom. Repeat. The game is incredibly good at scratching the itch for a shooter, but once that itch is scratched, it almost starts to get irritating. Okay, I scratched the itch. Okay, starting to hurt now. OKAY YOU CAN STOP NOW.  Look, if you're a fan of a good shooter with a decent story, Wolfenstein: The New Order is good. Hell, it's flat out great. I, however, am starting to get bored sick of the same rehashed formula of the modern shooter, and there just isn't enough here for me to give a glowing review. Buy it on sale, if you're in the mood for a well done shooter --otherwise, a quick watch of a Let's Play will pretty much give you all you need to know.

app_name: LEGO® The Lord of the Rings™
review_text: LEGO The Lord of the Rings is a fun and simple game which does not require a lot of skill. You take charge of a party of protagonists and work your way through the Lord of the Rings plotline (in the style of Peter Jackson's movie trilogy). Along the way you collect lego bits as a currency, smash enemies and objects that get in your way, and solve an occasional puzzle allow you to progress ahead. There are a bunch of optional side quests which allow players to collect/craft various items and have some fun outside of the storyline.  As one might expect from LEGO-branded games, this one seems fit for young or new video gamers who don't want too stressful a challenge. The only penalty for dying is losing some of the bits you picked up on your current level.   The story follows Peter Jackson's depiction of Lord of the Rings, however it does take a little bit of creative liberty. The end of Boromir, for example, is more comedy than tragedy, in the LEGO telling of the tale. This is almost certainly a necessity when making an E-rated game out of a PG-13 rated movie. The musical score, character voice clips, and scenery right out of the film trilogy really make you feel like you are playing through the films, and not some alternate rendition of Middle Earth. Except, of course, when creative liberty was taken here and there to cause LEGO-appropriate silliness.

app_name: Fallout 3 - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: This game is pretty fun (if you can get it to run that is), but it is B-U-G-G-Y! If you're not able and willing to sink a lot of time into jury-rigging this game to work on your OS (unless you have a windows XP computer lying around), buy New Vegas. If you ARE willing to try it, you should download Games For Windows Live first and run it, that should at least get it running. Been playing this game since closed beta and it has had a ton of improvements since. Unlike it's release there is now quite a bit to do to keep yourself occupied and it doesn't feel like a chore/grind like other ARPG's. The micro-transactions are cosmetics, heroes, or little boosts like XP or rarity bonus. Both boosts and heroes are easily obtained without spending a penny and you don't have to grind at all to get them, you'll end up picking up a new hero now and again as you play the game.   There is a store item that you'll feel obliged to buy which is probably to core of Gazillions income seeing as how easy it is to earn new heroes for free, and thats bank space.  You only get 1 page of bank space for free but this will suffice for many hours of gaming. Later on after you've beaten the story and levelled a hero to a respectable level your going to start to pick up more interesting loot and that's the moment when you decide whether or not to put money into the game. Bank space costs under $10 per page (cant remember exact price) and is still very reasonable, don't worry you wont need 20 pages, I only need about 6 pages and I've played over 2k hrs (out of steam).  Tons of replayability especially with the recent patches that've come out and the ones that are coming soon (Playable villans :D), very F2P (have a few friends that have clocked 2k+ hrs on this and spent $0 - $10), PVP was released recently, and new story content. Raids are coming soon (which im looking forward to seeing).  All in all if your a Marvel fan playing this game is a no-brainer, the lore, character models, dialogue and costumes are amazing and you'll love it. If your not a Marvel fan but enjoyed Diablo II or another ARPG then give this a shot, it's great fun and the devs are very active with balancing and rolling out patches and have great forum boards on their website.  If this was back in June I wouldn't have written a review because I would only have been able to recommend this to Marvel fans, but now I can fully recommend it thanks to the recent patches :)

So since everyone is talking about the greatness that is D:OS I'd like to talk about a few of its faults. Don't get me wrong, it's a great game but even great games have problems.  The slow start:  Divinity has this problem where, after a lengthy but necessary tutorial section, the game opens up into a city with a rather tedious and laborious amount of dialogue and an underwhelming investigation sequence. Not that the dialogue isn't well written, but the pacing of the game goes from a very fast tempo to a complete and utter grinding halt. If the game had started slower this might never have been an issue, but when you begin your game with a hectic amount of combat and then suddenly stop two hours in and force the player through another two to three hours of exposition dump rather than exploring the narrative more naturally you can ease in a sense of boredom. It also hampers the replability viability. Often I've said: wow I really want to play through this game again and see what I've missed! But those first six hours are just dreadful.  The lack of direction:  An open ended story is amazing. Divinity has this ability to allow you to create your own stories by not holding your hand and directing you from point A to B. You can bypass entire story points by arriving at certain areas out of order and create your own narrative. However, this is not entirely to Divinity's credit. Due to a sincere lack of direction you can often stumble into higher level baddies than you can possibly handle that will completely murder your party, and even if you do succeed there's just more of them lying in wait around the corner. Often times quest givers will give a very vague sense of direction and this results in a very vague sense of 'well I know it's around this approximate area somewhere OH GOD DEATH KNIGHTS!'  The Lack of Interesting Mechanics introduced later on:  Divinity's use of enviroments enabling combonations of certain spells is fantastic, in the early game when it still matters. By about level twelve or so it's pretty safe to ignore it due to the complete lack of spells that support this combination behavior. Often times it's just better to drop a rock and fireball the area over and over again because of how powerful that combination is against every group of enemy. It's generally completely unecessary to create fog and then electrecute it to shock enemies. The combinations that, to an experience player, could turn losing fights into landslides early on cease to have much of an impact as spells just become giant blasts rather than interesting enviromental spells.  Conclusion:  Divinity is an amazing game. It has an engaging story, interesting and challenging combat system, and phenomenal characterization. Does it have it's errors? Sure, but that doesn't mean it still isn't one of the greatest RPGs ever made. I am a huge fan of the classic isometric RPGs and Divinity was such a phenomenal take on the genre that I can't help but appreciate it. However, due to it's overwhelming awesomeness every flaw stands out like a sore thumb and jerks you out of the experience. Such is the nature of great video games.  Ultimate verdict: 9/10

I actually enjoyed this game when it was in Early Access. I felt it had a lot of potential. To be honest, this and Darkout (another failed 'sandbox-style' game) are the games that got me interested in the genre.  But now, the game has been released from Early Access incomplete - there is no story to speak of, some NPC quests are still broken, some recipes are still broken, overall balance is lacking, some deep biomes/strata are broken/bugged/non-functional, and the game is poorly optimized. It should have never left Early Access.  Add on to that, there has been no news on the game in six months (as of this posting), said news being a vague-yet-ominous post about a future announcement yet to materialize, the official game page has turned into a GoDaddy parking page, and the developer has reportedly closed doors.  I wanted to believe in this game, for Laser-Guided Flying Space Sharks with Human Teeth if nothing else, but in this state, unfinished and apparently abandoned, I cannot recommend it to anybody.

The graphics - at least with TheWorseMod installed - are great. The feeling of driving around Chicago is incredible. The story - though clichéd - hits most of the right buttons for me. And the things you can do in the game are sufficiently varied that you can stave off boredom for a good long while.  This game, in short, could have been awesome.   If the main character had more emotions than a brick. If the driving controls weren't so awful. If street lamps, trash cans and other city 'furniture' didn't destroy all immersion by behaving as if they were made of paper. If money, and hence all equipment, weren't so easy to come by as to destroy all sense of achievement. If escaping the police were fun instead of a chore.   If the only likeable character didn't die for absolutely nothing.     As it is, the flaws outweigh the good stuff by a solid margin and I can't really recommend Watch_Dogs. But I'm still looking forward to seeing if its sequel can do better. Because if it manages this, it could be a really, really good game.

This game has a generally boring appearance, but a playthrough of the game will easily show that this game is not boring at all.  Things I like: •It is easy to align yourself with a specific ideology (capitalism, socialism, etc.). •The game is simplistic enough to understand, but still allows a general amount of micromanagement. •The game allows for easy modding and several of the mods look as if they were part of the game. •The DLC expands the game a great amount and is not very expensive.  Things I don't like: •The game feels a bit expensive and although I enjoy the game, I was skeptical of buying it. •The Extremism DLC is not expansive enough (i.e. it allows the ban of homosexuality if you choose, but not the ban of religion, which would be a sort of opposing idea). •It is ridiculuously easy to be assassinated in the game. You can only assume that you, as a leader of a country, have absolutely no protection and have a target on your forehead. For example, I decided that my country should have retinal scans for those entering the borders (it was a test) and I was assassinated the next turn. •The game is a bit simple when it comes to names for various groups. For example, if you are assassinated by an ethnic group, it will ALWAYS be the 'Black Power' group. This needs more diversity, as black people obviously do not represent all ethnic people.  Overall, I recommend this game, along with its DLC. However, I would wait for a Steam sale to purchase it at a discounted price, because I can't really imagine having a desire to purchase this game if it is over $15 USD.

Bought this game on sale to play it with my brother. And now I regret spending even those 6 or 7 euros on this game.  The good: - I really like the world design and the overall feel. You look around and you say - 'Yeah! That is how post-apocalyptic world should look like'. Honestly - the world in this game is big and looks amazing!  The bad (pretty much everything else): - The biggest issue with this game is it's unoptimizaed engine - poor fps, graphical glitches, etc - all this reduces the joy of playing to zero! - The second big issue is players movement and collision model in the game - it feel like you are controlling a tractor on two legs that is running around in a concrete maze - you constantly bump into things, you have troubles jumping, etc etc... it just feels so annoying, that makes the game almost unplayable - The world is big and looks good, but it is empty! Apart from the loot from the monsters the only thing I managed to find during my 2 hours gameplay - was a water bottle - that's it! - There are tons of bugs, and I mean tons! Sometimes weapon switching stops working, sometimes monsters attack you without animation and in a really weird way - it looks like a cardboard cutout is flying towards you. Sometimes you encounter monsters who ignore you and just stand there, without doing anything while your bullets do them no harm.  So overall verdict - in it's current state for me the game is close to unplayable and I have a bad feeling that it will remain this way. The game had potential, and if the current state would be marked as early access, I would have written the positive review, because how the game plays now is obviously an early access - but hey fix the optimization and the frame drops, make player move like a human and add more stuff to the world, to make it worth exploring and it would be a great game, right now it is just unfinished product.

A great way to scratch that BOI itch for all the people whove played that game well and beyond its completion. However theres some important differences to note, before people go in expecting an BOI all over again.    The devs put in that dodge feature and damn do they expect you to use it, expect this game to be ALOT more defensive than BOI. Youll be dodging, prolly even more than youll be attacking, and that can get tedius/frustrating.   Dont expect to get like, 3 items a floor. Bosses dont drop them, money is incredibly scarce and usually better spent refilling health, so odds are youll get only 1 item per floor and youll like it. (Although i should mention, you do level up from time to time, which is basically like obtaining an item. As you progress however, they become rather infrequent)   at time of writing, im about 2 hours and some change into the game, ive yet to encounter a single passive that changes my attack type. (*edit* Ok they do exist, got a bit farther, and ive found some.) There also arent any other classes to my knowledge. So dont expect a wildly different playstyle each time.   those are just the big differences, that wont be immediatley apparent in the screenshots. None of the games choices are bad, but alot of people might hear 'its just like BOI' and be sold right away. if the above details bother you, you should consider moving along.

The graphics are what makes this game. They are so unique and gritty i can't even begin to describe them. If they had used any other game engine for this game than i don't think it would be nearly as scary. The interior graphics are some of the best i have ever seen, they are extremely detailed and invoke a sort of terror that i've never experienced before, its almost as if you can feel the dry paint on the wall. This is one of the best horror games i've played in the last 15 years. The jumpscares are the best out of any horror game hands down. The only ugly part's visually are the cemetary which have no grass covering the countours on the ground. But the interiors more than make up for it. Once you get to the delacroix' house thats where the game really starts to pick up. The puzzles are enjoyable to if not for the loading screens.  But where did this little gem of a game come from? Games like this are what make me love being a pc gamer. It's unique, it uses its own game engine, it is not trying to appeal to the masses. And its just an all around experience that puts me in a surreal creeped out mindset. I like finding special games like this deep within steams library that i had no idea existed, its kind of like that special moment when you find a unique old wooden object at a yardsale that you could never even imagine finding at a big name brand store.  In other words its just a nice refreshing feeling when i play a game that is uniquely its own own game from the ground up.. I like playing games on new engines because its like a new feeling you havent experienced yet.. Just subtle differences like the way the mouse moves, the way the lighting looks, the way the characters move around, the way the textures look, the way your computer handles the performance all just come together to give each and every game its own unique fingerprint that are unlike any other game.. No two games feel exactly alike unless they use the same game engine. This is why i am just getting kind of sick of every game i boot up lately saying 'POWERED BY UNREAL ENGINE'. Playing games that all use the same engine is like hanging out with the same person everyday except they are wearing a new outfit.  This games awesome, its gritty, its creepy and has puzzles that require some serious intelligence.  Cons -The loading screens are wayy too frequent. -The pistol i finally found sounds very unsatisfying, there is no difference between a headshot and a body shot in terms of damage on a zombie. And the pistol does not fire as fast as you can click. There is also no blood or head popping effects from the pistols. Combat is just extremely unsatisfying and leaves a lot to be desired. But this game leaves its mark in atmosphere and its definately worth it in my opinion -You cant strafe left/right You can only run forward and turn your body with the mouse.  To make this game perfect all they would have to do is -Eliminate all of the redundant loading screens -Add a new louder/more satisftying gunshot sound to the pistol -Make headshots do increased damage with satisfying skull pops/blood splatters. (Not just with the mangum/shotgun) -One or two more zombie models, and maybe a nice automatic weapon like an AK after a while

Dustforce truly is a work of art. I don't mean that lightly. The way it's animated plus its music gives it that title in my book. I can find myself playing one level for an hour, maybe even hours at a time. The frustration of losing levels is filled with relaxation and calmness because of the ambient music. The way the levels are crafted is truly mindblowing. They had a system in which you had to perfect a level to earn a key, and you would use that key to earn more levels. In layman's terms, to earn more levels, you had to perfect current levels. They ditched that and added an XP system in which you gain more XP to earn a key the more levels you play and perfect. I'm very glad they added this system and was skeptical about buying it. But now that I did buy it, I do not regret it even for a second. One thing I really love about this game is how you don't have to unlock base levels. In a sense, this game is open world. That sense is that many levels are available to you, and you can choose whether you want to advance into more challenging levels, or stay where you're at. To get an S in completion, you have to sweep up EVERY leaf, and to get an S in Finesse, you have to maintain your combo throughout an entire level.   Another thing I absolutely enjoy about Dustforce is its expasive community. There are lots of Community Workshop maps for you to sharpen your skills on, and lots of people who can set a good example. I'm sure most of the people who get best times on all the levels have a Youtube channel, so you can probably catch them there. The community may or not be active, but it sure is good and friendly.  Overall, in terms of gameplay, music, artstyle, animation, and difficulty (it's not an easy game, but it's not that hard of a game either. It just depends on how much you want to play it), I rate this game:  10/10 BUY THIS GAME.

Out of all my games in my library, I have never been this immersed in a video game before. The world of the Witcher 3 is simply breathtaking in both beauty, size and scope. From my first fetch quest in Velen to assassinating the final boss of the Wild Hunt, a game has never taken me on such an amazing journey. Each mission is something in its self, with unique characters telling a story of either loss or betrayal, that really sticks with the player.   The game follows on from the Witcher 2, continuing the story Geralt of Rivia. The Northern Kingdoms are in a state of corruption whilst Geralt embarks on a personal journey to find his dear colleague and friend, Ciri. Upon finding out she is also being pursued by a evil army known as the 'Wild Hunt', it then becomes a race against time to see who can find her first. It's truly a gripping story and will have you at the edge of the seat right until the very end. The game play is also incredible, with combat requiring skills and finesse. While it is not perfect, it is certainly a lot of fun to master. There is a vast amount of options from heavy and light hits, dodges and a wide selection of magical abilities to stop your enemies in their tracks. While the keyboard and mouse layout is well thought out, I personally found it more enjoyable and at times easier to use an Xbox remote.   Graphics and visuals is where this game truly shines. Walking along the streets of Velen or journeying across the snowy mountain peaks of Skelliege reinforces all the reasons video games are a truly amazing medium and should be appreciated more in mainstream media. If your graphic settings are set to the max, the game is like looking at a photograph, they are that realistic. The game developers put detail into every aspect of the map. Just running in a random direction will lead you to a new and fully immersive destination with characters just waiting to give you a new quest. Sometimes I even found myself coming across a cave with new armor or weapon designs to help me in combat. At times I just put the remote back and watched the rain and thunder as they went through the virtual country side, the graphics are simply that stunning.   If I had to be honest I'm not the biggest fan of role playing games, but the Witcher 3 revitalizes the formula and what Projekt Red has done with this game is a massive feat not just in the video game world but the entertainment industry as a whole. It's sad to see Geralts journey come to an end, but based on the success of the franchise I'm sure he will not be out of action for too long. Regardless this game is a must buy for any gamer and if your already a fan, draw your sword and take another trip to Kaer Morhen.

Call of Duty: Infinite Warfare received a lot of negative feedback ever since the first trailer, which made me sceptical about buying it. In the end, as any die hard Call of Duty fan would do, I pre-ordered it anyway.  I have now spent over 70 hours on this game's Singleplayer and Multiplayer and I can safely say that I am very pleased with the game.  The Singleplayer has a great storyline and it is overall just really fun to play. The ending was amazing and off the beaten path compared to the endings in all the previous titles which just made it that much greater.  The Multiplayer has been nothing but a smooth experience. For once the Multiplayer in a Call of Duty game is working smoothly if you have good internet and a good setup to run the game. There are no hitmarker issues and the balancing is a lot better than the last few entries. The movement system has also been refined for the better. The only Multiplayer issue I have found is that sometimes lobbies can take a while to start, however that is not enough to fault the game.  Infinity Ward has done a good job here by trying to learn from the mistakes made by Treyarch in Black Ops 3. I am pleased :)

Elder Scrolls Online is an amazing game, best MMORPG I've ever played, (probably because I'm a huge Elder Scrolls fan). The combat is ok except that if you attack and the enemy you want to hit isn't outlined you'll miss. The One Tamriel update is amazing. It used to be if you wanted to travel to a friend but they were not in the same alliance as you, you couldn't, but now with One Tamriel you can. The gamepad mode is in beta but works great. While creating your character (unless you have the Imperial Edition or the thing from the crown store (forgot name) which let you be any race any alliance) you have a choice of 3 alliances with 3 races each. If you wanna be a heavy armored tank that deals all magic damage go for it, or a light armord swordsman that relies on healing abilites. You get to play your character your way.

So, what's Shan Gui, subtitled Sweet Osmanthus?  You're a young woman, a student going by the name of Han Hui, trying to find some childhood memories in a park in the mountains where you used to go. You meet a young girl, He Jia, that will guide you through the places, showing you the wonders around and maybe, make you smile again and keep the sadness at bay. Maybe you'll learn more about your own family too.  Shan Gui is a visual novel where you click and you click and you read and where you don't have any action done. In other words, apart the dialogues going around, with He Jia reflecting a bubbly teenager and Han Hui being lost in herself, you're doing nothing. No day to pass, no skills to improve, nothing of that. Just watch how a sad Han Hui overcomes her mood to be joyful again.  So why should I recommend a game with so much dialogues that it's more an interactive book than a game - when I indeed descended in flames another game for the same reason? First, it's translated in several languages and not in English only. It means that I can play it in French. Secondly, that visual novel is focused on the Purple Mountains and everything going with, which means a lot of educational content. Indeed, each link that you'll find will send you to Wikipedia and the pages related to the content, in your selected language. Wikipedia may not be an exact encyclopedia but it's still better than nothing.  The art is quite good apart that I don't really agree with the way the heroine's breasts are represented. You're under the impression that it's fan service - I'm not fan of big breasts. Being a woman isn't the main reason behind this dislike, but I find that so unrealistic by moments... I've seen some of my favorite characters with these and it's not suiting them. Han Hui isn't at least too much in the exaggeration but still, it's not something I appreciate in the art.  The soundtrack is also quite good. It's fitting the ambiance of this visual novel. You don't have any action, true, but if the music was a disaster, it would be more boring than you think.  And while you get some extras in the game or cards or achievements, I can't let go of the fact that I didn't have a choice. When I play a visual novel, I want to have the freedom of choosing my own path - well, mainly, deciding which I'll go through first, so that, when I'm done with the game, it's the ending I wanted the most that will be the ice on the cake.  Bah, for the price, consider it as a nice interactive book.

SteamWorld Heist  is a really good game with high polish and great execution, but much like  SteamWorld Dig  it suffers from not enough depth and complexity. Lots of potential wasted here, IMO.  Not to sound insulting, but Image & Form games often feel (to me) like one notch above games with mobile developement in mind. Overly simplified to a fault. When you build a game that begs to be compared and described as something like  XCOM , you have the potential for tons of customization and sandbox-like creativity. Let me explain further.  When you reduce a tactical game down to 2D, you already lose a bit of strategy, which is fine - to a certain extent this was made up for by the brilliant bullet-bouncing/aiming system. Thats great, and beyond the superb graphics and music, thats about where the greatness ends.  Leveling up could not be more simple - you have no choices, no skill tree, and have no options in bonuses you recieve.  It might as well be non-existant. You gain power as you win, and therefore can fight stronger/more enemies. It's a rub.  At first the loot you find is interesting until you realize that guns aren't  really  that functionally different, and it's a simple numbers game (ie. Gun A has +1, and Gun B has +2, so Gun B it is).  The weapon stats increase fairly linearly, so you don't get that awesome feeling of finding rare loot, or getting something early in the game.  Another note about loot - at the end of every level you have to 'open' your loot by clicking on it, which is fun for a second, but after a couple hours it feels like a mobile game. Yawn.  The hat collection is cool, and I like how you can strategize to shoot them off of enemies heads to get them. Nice. Are they functional? I don't believe they are, but I could be wrong.    I find it frustrating to give  SteamWorld Heist  a negative review, because it is really awesome and is  soooo close  to achieving greatness.  But alas, it is also so far. I want more. I want strategy like  XCOM . Whether it would be more base-building elements, better equipment, character customization, etc. All of this is possible in this game. In fact, the hard part of building a brilliant combat system is done. I think we just need more bells-and-whistles.   Pros:  +Awesome addicting combat that rewards solid strategy a la  XCOM  +Beautiful artwork, animation, and music +Gotta love that  SteamWorld  universe +Controls are tight regardless if you use keyboard and mouse or controller +Hat collection is cool   Cons:  -Everything can be solved with money, and this is no exception; if the game were ten bucks, I could overlook a lot, but that's not the state of things, so: -Overall things are too simple for an  XCOM -like game; loot is boring and linear, character customization verges on something mobile (as does a few features), no base building elements, etc.   At the end of the day, considering all the DLC,  SteamWorld Heist  costs well over $20, which is quite steep for something so one-dimensional.  Yes, the combat and artwork and music and atmosphere are brilliant, but after about 2-5 hours I think most people would find it repetitive and shallow.  Peace,

This game came out in 2015, chess with a unique turn and 40k modelling  The objective of Warhammer 40,000: Regicide depends on the game mode. Classic is normal chess but with Warhammer models and some cool kill cams and voice overs. Regicide the other game option allows chess to carry out with unique abilities dependant on class and level. Throughout the campaign you will play in this mode with a certain number of pieces scattered across the board and have to complete a primary and secondary (sometimes multiple) for experience and to move on.  The game is graphically awesome for chess.  Pro's: - Unique spin on Chess - If you can play Chess this adds a notch of skill (in regicide mode) - A campaign that are like small chess challanges - Unique Turn based strat - Sweet graphics and kill moves - Achievements and badges  Con's: - The roll system is flawed!! space marines are meant to shot better then orks... Yet when ever I play a AI with them I seem to always miss and they get 9/10 on 52% chance........... - Limited abilities for leveling up - No real customising units, only some standard skins - Load time seems a little excessive  Overall I am rating this negative because the regicide roll system is flawed and not consistent. I didn’t play this game for months in the hope to see this fixed. I played today and after 3 campaign missions I uninstalled the game and will not venture to re-install this. I love chess, I like the graphics BUT I can’t play the unique game without fear of destroying something of my PC.

World War II realism simulator, massive loss of death included! So basically, to sum this game up in a few sentences; it's a World War II themed game featuring both fronts of the war (pacific and european) that emphasizes on realistic encounters; if you get shot you will most likely start dieing in one of many gruesome ways (blacking out and dieing, bleeding to death, etc), there is a forced aim deadzone (which takes quite a bit of gameplay to get used to, but it's not that bad after) which can help to emphasize aimed gunplay and cautious close quarters encounters due to the extreme inaccuracy of hip fire, if you start to panic (i.e. getting shot at, watching your teammates die infront of you, getting shot and surviving, etc) you will start to visually lose focus and start to shake, making gameplay feel very realistic and punishing yet rewarding. I play this game mainly for it's immense immersion factor and for how much of a World War II enthusiast I am, it really dings my dong to be in the position of a German soldier fighting off those filthy communist scum defending my teams foothold.  Final verdict for this game: 10 dead communist scum out of 10

app_name: Plants vs. Zombies: Game of the Year
review_text: The perfect game.  The first time I played Plants vs. Zombies was around 2009 when it came out. Back then I didn't own the Steam version but that didn't stop me from completing the game and most of the mini-games and puzzles it offered. I found the game very easy to get into - yet astonishing because it offered such a great variety of clever ideas, puzzles, challenges - all that and more. At some point I reinstalled my system and along with that I lost the game and my progress.  Later on in 2011 I bought the Steam version. Some time had passed after the first play-through so I started anew with the game - and I wasn't disappointed. I loved every minute of it. The great thing with this game is that you can play it through so many times and it still doesn't fail to surprise you. Up to this day I still have those moments when I just feel the urge to play this game again - and then I go ahead and do play it. The fact that this game features Steam Cloud is just magnificent. I can continue playing on my laptop when I am not at home! And did I even mention the achievements? They are a game maker here, too.  Oh, and I also bought this game for my Windows Phone. This game was my savior during boring math classes. I beat the game - too bad I didn't pass the math course...

I love the kingdom management and I love how simple the game mechanics are. I just wish I had a bit more control over where the individual villagers go. After I had destroyed both portals on one side of my kingdom, I wish I had been able to move that half of my army to the otherside where I needed them.   But that's a small complaint. First of all, this is not a game. In a game you can fail, here you can't. There is nothing to do other than swim around and click at some objects which are easily identifyable and always repeated. This is not even an interactive movie because it totally lacks a plot and choices, Well, yes, a couple of things (to be generous) do happen, but you don't get neither involved nor affected by them. And you can't change the outcome whatever you do or don't do. Graphics aren't even that impressive, considering that everything is cartoonish and generally lacks detail. The animation is good though.  So why did I like it? Maybe because I'm an animalist. I enjoyed swimming and jumping out of the water followed by tens of small fish or by some big one (only in the last levels).  I also liked the expectation of something big coming up next, due to all the enthusiastic reviews everywhere, and even if at the end nothing big happened, I enjoyed the trip.  I think that to like this product you need both to feel relaxed when playing and to pay a reduced price (€5.99 in my case).

[Nah, ain't worthwhile] Even if a game telling about 1979's Black Friday was a nice idea, the game is extremely short. Dinner Date is a first person experience that puts you into someone's subconscious. You'll see what he sees and hear his thoughts as he waits for his date and you'll push keys. The key prompts float in little bubbles that have hints about what they'll do. Different keys do different things. You'll get to pour a glass of wine, look at the clock, check your left hand and gaze at your right hand. There are even such details like putting your spoon into soup, stirring it, put the spoon back down and so on. You'll hear the character talk, basically telling you which of the things to do next.  Going into the game I thought it would be another 'crazy hand simulator' like Surgeon Simulator or Ampu-Tea. Nope, you just press keys to do things when the prompts are up.  You have no control over movement or the camera, just over what the character does as he continues to worry about his date. He will wonder whether to drink another glass of wine and what else he could be doing. The dinner date is a quick experience, over in about 30 minutes. Trying to replay it again didn't lead me to think that there are different outcomes.  Dinner Date has a lot going for it, the voice acting is good, the environment is great, but its just a small kitchen with a table in it. You'll go to the window to have a cigarette, but that's it. This could be the demo of a bigger more detailed game, there is talent here, but I just didn't have fun playing the game. Plus there's not much here to get excited about. Its over quick. If a bobbing camera gets you motion sick, avoid this game.  Here's something to think about when telling a story. If its not the most interesting thing that happens to the character, why aren't you telling the more interesting story? Is it technical limitations such as having characters to interact with? Is this what leads the character up to his suicide or finding his true love after the game has ended? Is it witty banter amongst two characters blended with subconscious thought? No, but maybe that will happen in Dinner Date 2: The Reckoning. I'm sure that Luke Skywalker had moments where he just chilled in his bedroom thinking about a girl. That never made it into Star Wars.  Dinner Date was just watching something while I mash all keys at once since there's no sort of right or wrong. Other narrative experiences like Dear Esther and Gone Home at least give you movement control and environments to explore. Like the plot of Dinner Date, it stands in a category all its own.

Incredibly great game, still don't know why it is so unknown. But think it may be these reasons:  -The movement feels awkward. -The combat feels awkward. -Enemies have way more health than they should. -My guns doesn't feel powerful, i feel like i am using techno-peashooters. -The upgrade menu is counter intuitive, yes if you read carefully no mistakes are made, but you need to mouse over each item to show all information. (i spent half of the game using default 'weapon mode' just upgrading 'armor/ammo' before realizing that the first of each upgrade area were the actual weapons, i thought the 'weapon mode' were going to be unlocked at some point, not manually) -Explosive attacks goes through walls, I seriously kept dying by enemy missiles BEHIND COVER. -Still have no idea what the ♥♥♥♥ i am fighting against. (Edit3: Robots i guess? characters just kept talking about external internal AI and technobabble) -Ambience noises that tear my auditory system apart. When these ambience sounds start i take my headphone off for a moment. (edit: these appear to be level specific)  -When i was about to complain that enemies have too much health and weapons are weak, i got the energy area of effect gun and it is op, it stuns? or slows small enemies and then explode in an area, upgrade with ammo regen and you are op as hell. (edit: it still op as hell if enemies are all coming after you in a single corridor)  -Besides these points the game is actually really great. I'm actually enjoying it so far.  Edit1:  I just beat the first boss, more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t unavoidable explosives, i just beat him because the xray gun told me the target to shoot.  -I forgot to mention, the graphics are Sanctum 2 level of quality and polish, very beaultiful game. -It also has a comic book style of storytelling while loading like Sanctum 2. But this one is animated and voice acted.  Edit2:  -Also forgot to mention, the level design is mixed, seems good, but every enemy encounter follows this: 1: Arrive in an area. 2: The passage is closed behind you. cannot retreat. 3: Enemies overwhelm you, just a clusterf uck from everywhere. 4: If you manage to survive, collect loot and advance level. Goto 1 (AKA rinse and repeat)  Edit3:   I just beaten the game. -It is actually very well polished. lots of props plants and debris around. -Lots of detail everywhere. Very beautiful overall. -Very atmospheric. -Just the movement, combat and enemy that are awkward and feel unpolished. -The last boss was interesting and lame at the same time, i think my strategy was too good for it. -I am not even gonna try another playthrough, i can already predict that higher difficulty is the same but with less pickups and/or more enemy health.  I'm talking bad about this game but i feel it is worth playing it.
